The Frontier Pretraining Atlas · a field manual for the 10-trillion-parameter MoE
Contents0102030405060708091011121314151617181920·A·B·C·D
The Frontier Pretraining Atlas

What it takes to pretrain a 10-trillion-parameter MoE

A systems-and-data field manual · mid-2026 · MoE-only — dense is not relitigated.
~110kwords
24chapters + appendices
200+cited sources
100kGB200 worked example

Most writing about frontier models stops at the architecture diagram. This one keeps going — into the GB200 NVLink domains, the all-to-all that gates MoE throughput, the FP4 grouped GEMMs, the optimizer that finally broke the AdamW monoculture, the data taxonomies nobody photographs, and the fault-tolerance math that decides whether a 100,000-GPU run survives the month. The worked example throughout is concrete: a 10T-total / 200B-active Mixture-of-Experts model on 100,000 GB200 GPUs.

The reference bar for this book is the worklog-style technical post — the JAX Scaling Book, the DeepSeek-V3 report, Jianlin Su's kexue.fm — where every figure answers one question and every number names its denominator. Frontier labs disclose almost nothing, so claims here carry an explicit epistemic tier: confirmed (in a paper, datasheet, or source tree), leaked (credible reconstruction — SemiAnalysis, Epoch AI), and rumored (single-source, directional). Where a number is a marketing or sparse-peak figure, it is flagged.

How to read this

The chapters are self-contained and cross-referenced. Systems engineers building the run: 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 14 → 15, then the punch-list (20), with Appendices A and C at your elbow. Pretraining scientists: 8 → 9 → 10 → 11 → 12 → 13 → 16 → 18. Sizing the bet: the argument below, then 1 → 6 → 20 → Appendix B. Completists: front to back — it is designed to take days. Every figure states its regime and its evidence, because a number without a denominator is just a vibe.

▸The argument in brief

Ten claims, each expanded, sourced, and quantified inside:

  • The target sits at the frontier of the announced envelope, not beyond it — the bottleneck is whether your software extracts 35% MFU instead of 18%, and whether the run survives months of failure. (Ch 1)
  • At this scale you are communication-bound on the MoE all-to-all — keep expert parallelism inside the NVLink domain and overlap the dispatch/combine with compute. (Ch 5)
  • Networking became a protocol problem and the protocol changed: OpenAI's MRC — packet spraying, SRv6, microsecond failover — is the systems disclosure of the year. (Ch 3)
  • The MFU you actually get is 20–40%; anyone quoting 50%+ is using sparse peak or a dense Llama-style transformer. (Ch 6)
  • FP8 is the floor; FP4 QAT on the expert weights is the 2026 frontier, and it is real. (Ch 9)
  • The optimizer monoculture broke: Muon / MuonClip trained a trillion-parameter MoE with zero loss spikes. (Ch 10)
  • "Scaling laws" now means your own laws, fit on your own data, with quality-dependent exponents — not Chinchilla. (Ch 12)
  • Data curation is the highest-leverage, least-photographed work — taxonomies are a template, not a ceiling. (Ch 13)
  • The dataloader is a distributed storage system with a hard real-time SLA, and goodput — not MFU — is the real metric. (Ch 14)
  • The payoff is a build-order, not a parts list. (Ch 20)
At 100k GB200 you are communication-bound on the MoE all-to-all, not compute- or memory-bound. Almost every other decision follows from that one fact.

Built from primary sources — arXiv papers, official technical reports, framework source code, the JAX Scaling Book, a SemiAnalysis archive, Stas Bekman's ML-Engineering, and a live MaxText checkout (HEAD 2026-06-22) — with every load-bearing number put through an adversarial verification pass. Prose: CC BY 4.0. Design system adapted from the MiniMax-M3 Inference Atlas. Where a number is a marketing or sparse figure, it is flagged; where the open literature does not know, the book says so.

Chapter 01 · Who is training what, and what it costs

The mid-2026 frontier landscape: the runs, the silicon, the price tags, and where a 10T/200B bet sits

You are planning a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200 GPUs. As of mid-2026, almost nobody has done anything close — and the one party that has, did it at one-eighth your GPU count, one-twentieth your total parameters, and one-sixth your active budget. This chapter maps the whole distribution, run by run, with explicit confidence labels, and positions your target inside it.

~24 min read6 figuresgrounded in the MAI-Base-1 report & Epoch estimates

The single most useful fact for anyone planning a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200 GPUs is that, as of mid-2026, almost nobody has done anything close, and the one party that has trained a from-scratch trillion-parameter MoE on production GB200 silicon — Microsoft AI, with MAI-Base-1 / MAI-Thinking-1 — did it at one-eighth your GPU count, one-twentieth your total parameters, and one-sixth your active-parameter budget. The frontier you are joining is real, but the part of it that overlaps your exact target is a single 109-page technical report and a handful of leaked cluster-power numbers. Everything else is either (a) a closed Western run whose parameter counts are state secrets and whose compute must be inferred from energy and rack counts, or (b) a Chinese / Microsoft open(-ish)-weight run that publishes honest architecture tables but tops out an order of magnitude below your total-parameter target and 4–6× below your active-parameter target. This chapter maps that distribution run-by-run with explicit confidence labels, builds the comparison table you will return to repeatedly, and then positions your target inside it: which tier it sits in, who is nearest, and whether the configuration is plausible at all.

The labs telling you how to train a trillion-parameter MoE are not the labs at the top of the leaderboard, and the labs at the top are telling you nothing. Your recipe must be assembled from the documented tier and extrapolated to a scale none of them have published.
8,192GB200
MAI-Base-1: the only on-substrate GB200 trillion-MoE
100kGPU
your target cluster — ~12× MAI's
200Bact
~4–6× every shipped open MoE
0.45EF/s
dense FP8 peak, 100k GPUs (not 1.0)

01The bifurcation: closed-inferred vs. open-documented

By June 2026 the frontier has cleanly split into two epistemic regimes, and you must treat them differently.

The Western closed-weight tier — OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Anthropic, xAI — publishes essentially no architecture. Parameter counts, token counts, and FLOP budgets are absent from every GPT-5.x release note, the Gemini 3 Pro model card, and every Claude release. What is knowable is infrastructural: cluster GPU counts, datacenter power envelopes, and chip-purchase contracts, all of which leak through supply-chain reporting, satellite imagery of buildouts, and energy filings. From those you reconstruct compute via the standard identity (FLOPs ≈ 6 × active-params × tokens, plus an RL multiplier), and groups like Epoch AI publish methodologically transparent estimates with 90% confidence intervals. For this tier, treat every parameter number as rumor and every compute number as inferred unless it carries an Epoch-style derivation.

compute pretrain FLOP ≈ 6 · active-params · tokens (+ RL multiplier) The reconstruction identity for the closed tier. Note it tracks active params, not total — which is why trillion-total models with 32–37B active are small on a FLOP basis.

The open-documented tier — DeepSeek, Qwen/Alibaba, Moonshot's Kimi, Zhipu's GLM, MiniMax, and Microsoft AI — publishes real architecture tables, and in DeepSeek's and MAI's cases publishes (or all-but-publishes) the training recipe down to optimizer betas and FP8 cast points. This tier is where your actual engineering evidence lives. The irony worth internalizing before you spend a billion dollars of compute: the labs telling you how to train a trillion-parameter MoE are not the labs at the top of the capability leaderboard, and the labs at the top are telling you nothing. Your recipe must be assembled from the documented tier and extrapolated to a scale none of them have published.

02The GB200 reference point: MAI-Base-1

MAI-Thinking-1 / MAI-Base-1 ("Building a Hill-Climbing Machine," Microsoft AI, 2 June 2026) is the load-bearing document of this entire field manual, because it is the only public, detailed report of a from-scratch trillion-parameter MoE trained on 8,192 GB200 GPUs — your exact substrate, at 8% of your scale. Confirmed specifics:

sparsity
35B act / 1T total
decoder-only MoE, 8 of 512 experts/token in a compressed latent space, softmax gating.
layer pattern
dense ↔ high-sparsity MoE
alternating, first FFN layer dense — not uniform medium-sparsity. Ablated and defended.
attention
5:1 local:global
SWA-512 + RoPE-10k local, NoPE global → clean FlashAttention-4 + Ulysses CP.
tokens
30T + 3.55T mid
64K then 256K context phases, 256K final context.
  • 35B active / 1T total sparse decoder-only MoE, activating 8 of 512 experts per token in a compressed latent space with softmax gating.
  • Alternating dense-FFN and high-sparsity-MoE layers (first FFN layer dense), rather than the uniform medium-sparsity stacking most open MoEs use — an architecture choice the report ablates and defends.
  • Interleaved 5:1 local:global attention: sliding-window-512 with RoPE base 10k on local layers, NoPE (no positional encoding) on global layers, which is what lets them run FlashAttention-4 and Ulysses context parallelism cleanly.
  • 30T-token main pretrain + 3.55T tokens of mid-training (64K then 256K context phases), 256K final context.
  • Numerics: BF16 master weights, FP8 E4M3 for the forward GEMM, FP8 E5M2 for the data-gradient, BF16 weight-gradient with FP32 accumulation, delayed scaling.
  • Parallelism: expert-parallel 64 + ZeRO-2 for pretrain and mid-training phase 1, ZeRO-3 / FSDP for mid-training phase 2, context parallelism for the long-context phases.
  • Global batch 134M tokens; AdamW with β1 = 0.95, β2 = 0.925, peak LR 2e-4 → 2e-5 cosine, ~12B-token warmup. The unusually low β2 is itself a stability signal worth noting.
  • A custom "YOLO" training framework with Triton/CUDA/CuteDSL/CUTLASS FP8 grouped-GEMM kernels for the 512-expert layer, and a full "Cluster Environment" section dedicated to goodput.
yaml — MAI-Base-1 recipe, transcribed to a MaxText-style config (illustrative)
# --- architecture ---
num_experts: 512
num_experts_per_tok: 8          # 8 of 512 routed
first_ffn_layer_dense: true     # alternating dense <-> high-sparsity MoE
attention_local_global_ratio: "5:1"
local_attention: { type: sliding_window, window: 512, rope_base: 10000 }
global_attention: { type: nope }   # no positional encoding on global layers

# --- numerics (delayed scaling) ---
master_weight_dtype: bfloat16
fwd_gemm_dtype: float8_e4m3       # forward GEMM
dgrad_dtype:    float8_e5m2       # data-gradient
wgrad_dtype:    bfloat16          # weight-gradient, FP32 accumulation
fp32_accum: true

# --- optimizer / schedule ---
optimizer: adamw
adam_b1: 0.95
adam_b2: 0.925                    # unusually low -> stability signal
lr_peak: 2.0e-4
lr_end:  2.0e-5                   # cosine
warmup_tokens: 12_000_000_000     # ~12B
global_batch_tokens: 134_000_000  # 134M

# --- parallelism ---
phase_pretrain:    { expert_parallel: 64, zero: 2 }
phase_midtrain_p1: { expert_parallel: 64, zero: 2 }
phase_midtrain_p2: { zero: 3, fsdp: true, context_parallel: true }

Three disclosures are strategically decisive for your planning. First, MAI deliberately over-trains at 500–1000 tokens-per-active-parameter (TPP) — a 35B-active model on 30T tokens is ~857 TPP — versus Chinchilla-optimal ~20 and their own 100–200 TPP ablation ladder, trading training compute for inference cheapness. Second, no distillation, no LM-generated synthetic data, no open training sets — a deliberately clean-data stance that flags clean-token scarcity as the binding constraint at scale. Third, an attention-output zero-initialization trick to fix early MoE router imbalance, a cheap stability fix you should adopt on day one. Reported benchmarks: 52.8% SWE-Bench Pro, 97.0% AIME 2025, 87.7% LiveCodeBench v6. This is the recipe to clone and scale; the rest of the landscape tells you which knobs to turn as you 5–8× it.

The one report to read cover-to-cover

MAI-Base-1 is your exact substrate (GB200) at 8% of your scale, with the recipe published to optimizer betas and FP8 cast points. Clone it and scale: attention-output zero-init from day one, dense+MoE interleaving, FP8 E4M3 forward / E5M2 dgrad with FP32 accum, EP=64. Everything else in this chapter tells you which knobs to turn as you 5–8× it.

03The Western closed tier (compute inferred)

OpenAI GPT-5.x

GPT-5 launched 7 August 2025. Epoch AI's best estimate puts it at ~3e25 FLOP pretraining and ~5e25 FLOP total (RL contributing 10–200% of pretrain), built on an assumed ~100B active params and ≥30T tokens — and critically, below GPT-4.5's >1e26 FLOP (Epoch). The clearest public read on the frontier's strategic shift: OpenAI traded raw pretraining scale for a smaller, over-trained, RL-heavy model. By June 2026 the line has iterated through GPT-5.1 toward GPT-5.5, trained at the Abilene "Stargate I" site (Oracle/OCI, GB200 NVL72 racks, ~1.2 GW planned, energized September 2025). Circulating 3–5T total-param claims are rumor.

Google Gemini 2.5 → 3 / 3.1

Gemini 3 Pro released 18 November 2025 (LMArena 1501 Elo, HLE 37.5% no-tools, GPQA-Diamond 91.9%). The model card confirms a sparse, natively-multimodal MoE trained on Google TPUs via JAX/Pathways but discloses no parameter count, token count, or FLOPs — Google's consistent posture across the 2.5/3 line. Training ran on TPU v5e + v6e (Trillium) pods on the Jupiter fabric (>13 Pb/s bisection, >100k-chip pods). "Deep Think" is a test-time-compute mode, not a separate pretrain. Widely-guessed 500B+ total / 50–100B active is rumor. The relevance to you: Gemini proves a hyperscaler can run frontier MoE pretraining at >100k-accelerator scale, but on a non-NVIDIA fabric whose collective-communication economics differ fundamentally from your NVLink-domain GB200 racks.

Anthropic Claude Opus 4.x

Zero parameter/token/FLOP disclosure anywhere. The confirmable facts are infrastructural: Project Rainier, AWS's ~500,000 Trainium2 cluster, is online and training Claude; Anthropic states it now uses >1M Trainium2 chips and has contracted (Nov 2025, expanded 2026) for up to 5 GW of Trainium2/3 capacity, with Rainier providing "more than 5×" the training compute of prior Claude models (Anthropic). Opus 4.6 is the SWE-Bench-Pro comparison target that MAI and others cite. Architecture is entirely undisclosed. Like Gemini, this is a non-NVIDIA datapoint — a proof of frontier scale, not a transferable recipe.

xAI Grok 3/4 (Colossus)

The best-documented Western compute story. Colossus (Memphis) stood up 100k H100 in 122 days and doubled to ~200k mixed H100/H200/GB200 in 92 days; by December 2025 the mix was reportedly ~150k H100 + 50k H200 + 30k GB200, with Colossus 2 scaling toward ~110k GB200 NVL72 and ~1.1 GW and the broader site reportedly ~555k GPUs / ~2 GW by early 2026 (SemiAnalysis; leaked/estimated). The headline for Grok 4 (July 2025): xAI claims RL compute on the same order of magnitude as pretraining — a ~10× RL scale-up over Grok 3, RL ≈ 50% of the training budget (xAI) — the cleanest public signal that the frontier RL:pretrain ratio is approaching 1:1. Epoch AI estimates Grok 4 at ~246M H100-hours, ~310 GWh, median ~$490M, ~154,000 t CO₂, with large explicit uncertainty (Epoch). Colossus 2 is, crucially, the only ~100k-GB200-class cluster actually being stood up in the West — it is your closest real-world infrastructure analog, and the SemiAnalysis power-buildout reconstruction is the most concrete reference you have for the datacenter side of a 100k-GB200 deployment.

The RL:pretrain ratio is approaching 1:1

Grok 4's ~10× RL scale-up (RL ≈ 50% of budget) and GPT-5's ~5e25 total vs ~3e25 pretrain are the two cleanest signals that RL is no longer an afterthought. When you budget your own run, the RL stage is a first-class line item, not a tail.

04Meta Llama 4: the cautionary tale at your scale

Llama 4 (April 2025) shipped Scout (109B total / 17B active, 16 experts) and Maverick (~400B total / 17B active, 128 experts), both on >30T tokens across 200 languages (VentureBeat). But the model that matters to you is Behemoth — ~2T total / 288B active — Meta's frontier teacher, which as of mid-2026 is consistently reported shelved / never shipped, with mid-training MoE-routing and chunked-attention instabilities at 2T scale cited as why Meta lost confidence (leaked/rumor, but reported by multiple outlets). This is the single most important cautionary datapoint in the entire landscape for your target. Behemoth's 288B active is the only shipped-or-attempted active-parameter budget comparable to your 200B, and it did not ship — and it failed on exactly the two axes (routing stability, attention stability) that dominate trillion-parameter MoE risk. Read Behemoth not as "2T is impossible" but as "2T total with ~290B active, attempted without the MAI/Kimi stability stack, broke." Your job is to not repeat it.

The only 288B-active attempt did not ship leaked/rumor

Behemoth (2T/288B) is the single closest precedent to your 200B-active bet, and it was abandoned on routing + chunked-attention instability at 2T scale — the exact two failure modes that dominate trillion-MoE risk. Do not interpret this as "2T is impossible"; interpret it as "this scale, attempted without the MAI attention-zero-init / Kimi MuonClip stability stack, broke."

05DeepSeek V3 → V4: the efficiency frontier

DeepSeek defines the cheap-frontier datapoint everyone calibrates against. V3 (December 2024): 671B total / 37B active, 14.8T tokens, 2,788,000 H800 GPU-hours ≈ $5.576M marginal compute cost — all confirmed in the technical report. That run is also the most honest public MoE-at-scale efficiency anchor you have: derived from the published GPU-hours it lands at ~21.4% FP8 MFU (~42.9% BF16-equivalent), ~424 effective TFLOP/s/GPU over a full 14.8T-token run — a number that should weight far above any short MLPerf dense-405B sprint when you forecast your own sustained throughput.

V4 (released 24 April 2026, MIT): V4-Pro 1.6T total / 49B active, V4-Flash 284B / 13B active, both with 1M context, both reportedly trained on >32T tokens (leaked — DeepSeek published no token count, optimizer, hardware, or cost for V4) (Morph). The architectural pivot is the open-weight playbook for cheap 1M context: from V3's MLA, through V3.2's DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA), to V4's hybrid Compressed Sparse Attention + Heavily Compressed Attention with top-k compressed-token selection plus a 128-token uncompressed local window, KV compressed ~4× along sequence. V4-Pro-Max reportedly hits ~80.6% SWE-Bench Verified. For your long-context mid-training phase, DSA-lineage attention is the technique to lift; V4-Pro at 1.6T/49B is also the largest shipped open MoE total-param count, and still 6× below your total target.

Lift DSA-lineage attention for the long-context phase

V3 MLA → V3.2 DSA → V4 hybrid Compressed Sparse + Heavily Compressed Attention (top-k compressed-token selection + 128-token uncompressed local window, KV ~4× compressed along sequence) is the open-weight playbook for cheap 1M context. This is the technique to clone for your 256K+ mid-training phases.

06The Chinese open-weight cohort

ModelTotalActiveExpertsTokensNotable
Qwen3 (May 2025)235B22B8/token~36T119 langs, 262K ctx, Apache-2.0 (arXiv)
Qwen3-Max (Sept 2025)>1T——36Tproprietary
Qwen3.5-397B-A17B (2026)397B17B——reportedly beats Qwen3-Max at a fraction of cost
Kimi K2 (Moonshot, 2025)1T32B384 (8 routed + 1 shared)15.5TMuonClip, zero loss spikes (HF)
GLM-4.6 (Zhipu, Sept 2025)355B32B—23Tloss-free sigmoid-gate balancing
GLM-5 leaked744B———leaked
MiniMax-M2 (2026)229.9B9.8B—29.2T62-layer, 192K ctx, ~4% activation
 The open cohort: honest architecture tables, all an order of magnitude below your 10T total. Sources inline.

Two of these are decision-relevant beyond the table. Kimi K2 is the strongest existence proof that a non-AdamW optimizer can stabilize trillion-parameter MoE pretraining: 1T total / 32B active, 384 experts, 15.5T tokens, MuonClip optimizer, reported zero loss spikes at unprecedented scale (HF). MuonClip is a live alternative to MAI's AdamW + attention-zero-init recipe and the most important optimizer datapoint of the year. Alibaba's trajectory is the clearest statement of the prevailing efficiency trend: having crossed 1T with Qwen3-Max, they appear to be walking back from the 1T total toward sparser, cheaper configs (Qwen3.5-397B-A17B reportedly beating Qwen3-Max). And MiniMax-M2's ~4% activation ratio (9.8B/229.9B), alongside V4-Flash's 4.6% and Qwen3's 9%, marks the direction the entire field is moving: down in activation ratio, toward cheaper inference — which is the precise direction your 200B-active bet runs against.

— YOUR TARGET —10T200B20–60T100k GB2002.4e25–7.2e25†$B-scaleplan
 † Pretrain FLOP estimated as 6 × active-params × tokens; excludes RL. "n/d" = not disclosed.
Why the trillion-total models look "small" on FLOPs

On a FLOP basis (which tracks active params, not total) MAI-Base-1, DeepSeek-V3, and Kimi K2 are all small — ~3–6e24 — despite their trillion-parameter totals, because their active budgets are 32–37B. This is the central tension your config inverts: you are pushing the axis (active params) that actually drives both training FLOPs and inference cost.

08Positioning the 10T / 200B / 100k-GB200 target

Total parameters

Your 10T total is ~6× DeepSeek-V4-Pro (1.6T), ~5–10× Kimi K2 / Qwen3-Max (1T), and ~5× the shelved Llama-4 Behemoth (2T). It would be the largest dense-equivalent capacity ever attempted by a wide margin — roughly one order of magnitude above the largest shipped open MoE in total params. No one has publicly shipped past ~1.6T total; the only attempt past 2T (Behemoth) was abandoned. You are, in total-parameter terms, off the right edge of the entire distribution.

Active parameters — the real outlier

Your 200B active is the genuinely radical bet. It is ~4–6× the active budget of every shipped open MoE (V4-Pro 49B, K2 32B, MAI 35B, Qwen3 22B) and comparable only to Behemoth's 288B-active teacher, which did not ship. Meanwhile the field has spent all of 2025–2026 lowering activation ratios (MiniMax ~4%, V4-Flash 4.6%, Qwen3 9%). Your configuration is 2% sparsity in total terms (200B/10T — sparser than most) but a very heavy per-token compute load in absolute terms. Active params drive both training FLOPs and inference cost; choosing 200B active is a deliberate, expensive bet against the prevailing efficiency trend, justified only if you believe per-token capability at that active budget buys capability the sparser configs cannot reach. This is the decision to interrogate hardest before committing.

The one decision to interrogate hardest

200B active is the single contrarian bet in your whole plan. It is the only axis where you are off the distribution in a direction the field is actively retreating from, and the only comparable number was cancelled for instability. It is justified only if you believe per-token capability at 200B active buys something the sparser configs cannot reach.

Compute budget — corrected hardware math

Be careful with the GB200 NVL72 spec sheet: NVIDIA's headline 720 PFLOP/s FP8 / 360 PFLOP/s BF16 per 72-GPU rack are with-sparsity (2:4) figures — the GB200 NVL72 page footnotes "Dense is one-half sparse spec shown." For ordinary dense training FLOPs the meaningful peaks are 360 PFLOP/s FP8 and 180 PFLOP/s BF16 per rack, i.e. ~4.5 PFLOP/s dense FP8 / ~2.25 PFLOP/s dense BF16 per B200 GPU. Consequently a 100,000-GPU cluster is ~0.45 EFLOP/s dense FP8 peak, not the ~1.0 EFLOP/s you get only by counting sparsity. Derate this hard for MFU. Realistic sustained large-MoE FP8 MFU on GB200 in 2025–2026 sits in the ~20–35% band — the DeepSeek-V3 floor is ~21% FP8 and well-tuned dense frameworks ceiling near ~47% (SemiAnalysis) — with heavy routing and expert-parallel all-to-all dragging real MoE numbers toward the bottom of that range. At ~35% MFU on ~0.45 EFLOP/s dense FP8 you sustain roughly 0.16 EFLOP/s.

compute 100k × 4.5 PF/s = 0.45 EF/s dense FP8 × ~35% MFU ≈ 0.16 EF/s sustained The headline 720 PF/s/rack is a with-sparsity number; dense is one-half. Quote against 0.45, not 1.0, then derate hard for MFU.
forecast/gb200_throughput.pycorrected dense-FP8 mathillustrative
GPUS            = 100_000
DENSE_FP8_PFLOP = 4.5            # per B200; sparse spec (9.0) halved   # [1]
peak_ef         = GPUS * DENSE_FP8_PFLOP / 1e3        # PF -> EF = 0.45 EF/s

# sustained large-MoE FP8 MFU: ~20-35% band (V3 floor ~21%, ceiling ~47%)
for mfu in (0.21, 0.35):                                                # [2]
    print(f"MFU {mfu:4.0%}: {peak_ef * mfu:.3f} EFLOP/s sustained")
# MFU  21%: 0.095 EFLOP/s
# MFU  35%: 0.158 EFLOP/s   <- ~0.16 EF/s

def wallclock_days(tokens_T, active_B=200, sustained_ef=0.158):         # [3]
    flop = 6 * active_B * 1e9 * tokens_T * 1e12       # 6 * A * D
    return flop / (sustained_ef * 1e18) / 86_400      # compute-only LOWER bound

for T in (20, 30, 60, 85):
    print(f"{T:>3}T tok: {wallclock_days(T):4.1f} days (floor)")        # [4]
# 20T: 1.7 | 30T: 2.6 | 60T: 5.2 | 85T: 7.4

1Halve the spec sheet. NVIDIA's 9.0 PFLOP/s FP8 per B200 is a 2:4-sparsity number; dense training sees one-half — so 100k GPUs is 0.45 EF/s, not the ~1.0 EF/s you get only by counting sparsity.

2Derate hard for MFU. Sustained large-MoE FP8 MFU sits in the ~20–35% band — the V3 floor is ~21%, dense ceilings near ~47%; heavy routing + EP all-to-all drag real MoE toward the bottom.

36·A·D, active not total. Training FLOP ≈ 6 × active-params × tokens — it tracks the 200B active budget, not the 10T total. RL is excluded and stacks on top.

4A lower bound, not a schedule. These are compute-only floors; real wall-clock is multiples higher once checkpoint/restart, fault recovery, NVLink blast-radius, and the GB200 software ramp are added.

Training FLOPs ≈ 6 × active-params × tokens. For 200B active:

TokensPretrain FLOPvs. frontierWall-clock @ ~0.16 EF/s sustained
20T~2.4e25≈ GPT-5 pretrain~1.7 days*
30T~3.6e25~1.2× GPT-5 pretrain~2.6 days*
60T~7.2e25approaching GPT-4.5~5.2 days*
~85T~1.0e26GPT-4.5 territory~7.4 days*
 *Compute-only lower bounds; real wall-clock is multiples higher (see below).

*Compute-only lower bounds; real wall-clock is multiples higher once you add checkpoint/restart, fault recovery, the NVLink-domain failure blast-radius, optimizer-state churn, and the multi-month software-maturity ramp GB200 still demands. SemiAnalysis cautioned in late 2025 that no large-scale training run had yet completed on GB200 NVL72, blocked by software immaturity and NVLink-backplane reliability (the whole 72-GPU rack is one failure domain), and that GB200 must run ≥1.6× faster than H100 just to break even on TCO (SemiAnalysis). Treat the 1.6× break-even as the real bar, not the marketing FLOPs.

As of late 2025, no large run had finished on GB200 NVL72

SemiAnalysis flagged software immaturity and NVLink-backplane reliability — the whole 72-GPU rack is a single failure domain — as the blockers, and set ≥1.6× over H100 as the TCO break-even bar. Treat 1.6× as the real target, not the marketing FLOPs, and budget the goodput/determinism tooling MAI dedicates a whole "Cluster Environment" section to.

The strategic reading of this table: a single 30T-token run lands at roughly GPT-5-pretrain scale (~3e25) but with ~2× the active params, and pushing into GPT-4.5 territory (~1e26) requires ~85T tokens or a heavy RL stage on top — and with the frontier RL:pretrain ratio approaching 1:1 (Grok 4, GPT-5), the RL stage is no longer an afterthought to your budget.

TPP check — why you cannot over-train your way out

This is the cleanest constraint on the whole plan. MAI over-trained a 35B-active model to 500–1000 TPP (200T tokens would be needed at the top of that range). Your 200B active at even 1000 TPP would demand 200T tokens — flatly infeasible on the clean data MAI flags as scarce. At a sane 100–200 TPP you train on 20–40T tokens, i.e. mild over-training, decisively not the heavy-over-training regime MAI used. The lesson: your active budget is simply too large to over-train, so you cannot recover inference efficiency the way smaller-active models do. Above ~40–50T tokens data, not compute, becomes the binding constraint — clean-token scarcity bites long before your 100k GB200 cluster runs out of FLOP/s. Your run is data-bound, not compute-bound, which inverts the usual frontier intuition.

Your run is data-bound, not compute-bound

200B active at 1000 TPP would need 200T clean tokens — infeasible. At a sane 100–200 TPP you land at 20–40T tokens, only mild over-training. Above ~40–50T tokens, clean-token scarcity bites before the cluster runs out of FLOP/s. This inverts the usual frontier intuition: you cannot over-train your way to inference efficiency the way a 35B-active model can.

200T
tokens for 200B-act at 1000 TPP — infeasible
20–40T
sane 100–200 TPP regime → mild over-train
1.6×
GB200-over-H100 TCO break-even bar

Stability and recipe risk

The two largest publicly-attempted MoEs near your scale converged on the same lessons, and your run must adopt all of them: MuonClip-style optimization (Kimi: zero spikes at 1T/15.5T) or carefully-tuned AdamW with attention-output zero-init and global-batch load balancing (MAI); dense + MoE layer interleaving rather than uniform medium-sparsity; FP8 forward GEMMs with FP32 accumulation; and expert-parallel ≈ 64 as the practical EP degree. Behemoth's reported failure mode — routing plus chunked-attention instability at 2T total / 288B active — is the single biggest flag for going past ~2T total, and your 10T sits 5× beyond it. At 10T total you will need pipeline + expert + context parallelism stacked simultaneously, and goodput/determinism tooling (MAI dedicates an entire Cluster Environment section to it) will matter more to time-to-results than peak FLOPs ever will.

The non-negotiable stability stack

Adopt all of: MuonClip or AdamW + attention-output zero-init + global-batch load balancing; dense+MoE interleaving (not uniform medium-sparsity); FP8 forward GEMMs with FP32 accumulation; EP ≈ 64. At 10T total, stack pipeline + expert + context parallelism simultaneously, and invest in goodput/determinism tooling — it will gate time-to-results harder than peak FLOPs.

What tier you sit in

There are, functionally, three tiers in mid-2026. Tier 1, the shipped open MoEs, tops out at ~1.6T total / ~49B active (DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Kimi K2, Qwen3-Max). Tier 2, the inferred Western closed runs, is unknown in parameters but ~3e25–1e26 FLOP in compute (GPT-5, Grok 4, Gemini 3, Claude Opus 4.x). Tier 0, the abandoned frontier, contains exactly one entry: Llama-4 Behemoth at 2T/288B. Your target sits above all three on total parameters and in a tier of one on the active-parameter axis — the only company at 200B active is a model that was cancelled for instability. On compute you are squarely Tier 2 (a 30T-token run ≈ GPT-5 pretrain), which is reassuring: the FLOP budget is precedented even if the parameter configuration is not.

The plausibility verdict: the compute is plausible and precedented; the total-parameter scale is unprecedented but a smooth ~6× extrapolation of documented MoE practice; the 200B-active choice is the one genuinely contrarian, genuinely risky bet, and the recipe to de-risk it is MAI-Base-1 scaled up, with Kimi's MuonClip for spike-free optimization and DeepSeek's DSA-lineage sparse attention for the long-context phase. You are not doing something the field has proven impossible — Behemoth's cancellation is a stability-engineering failure, not a scaling-law wall — but you are doing it with thinner margins for error than any shipped run, on silicon that, as of late 2025, no one had yet completed a large run on.

The plausibility verdict in one line

Compute: plausible and precedented. Total-param scale: unprecedented but a smooth ~6× extrapolation. 200B active: the one genuinely contrarian, genuinely risky bet. The de-risking recipe is MAI-Base-1 scaled up + Kimi MuonClip + DeepSeek DSA-lineage attention. Behemoth's cancellation was a stability-engineering failure, not a scaling-law wall — but your margins are thinner than any shipped run, on silicon no one had finished a large run on as of late 2025.

Chapter 02 · The rack is the unit of compute

The 100k-GPU machine: GB200 NVL72, Blackwell numerics, and what an exaflop-class cluster actually is

The unit of compute is no longer the GPU, nor the eight-GPU node — it is the rack. For a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE run, almost every architectural decision follows from one fact: the GB200 NVL72 makes a 72-GPU, fully NVLink-coherent domain the atomic building block.

~24 min read7 figuresgrounded against the NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 spec + Blackwell microbenchmarks

The single most important fact about the machine you are about to commit to is that the unit of compute is no longer the GPU, and no longer the eight-GPU node. It is the rack. The GB200 NVL72 is engineered so that a 72-GPU, fully NVLink-coherent domain is the atomic building block, and almost every architectural decision in your 10T-total / 200B-active MoE run — where experts live, how large the expert-parallel group can be, where the all-to-all traffic spills from copper to optics, how the parameter and optimizer-state footprint shards — follows from that one design choice. This chapter establishes what the hardware actually is (not what the spec sheet markets), what Blackwell's FP4 numerics buy a trillion-scale MoE in 2026, what MFU and MBU you should realistically plan against, how GB200 compares to the credible alternatives (B300, TPU v7, Trainium3, Ascend/CloudMatrix), and what concretely changes versus a Hopper fleet for a run of this size.

GB200 NVL72 is not a faster H100 — it is a different unit of compute: a 72-GPU coherent rack whose NVLink domain and native FP4 reshape the parallelism and numerics of a trillion-scale MoE. Plan EP inside the rack, FP4 in the steady state with a higher-precision tail, and the build around power.
130TB/s
all-to-all NVLink domain across 72 GPUs
360PF/s
dense FP8 per NVL72 rack (not 720)
192GB
HBM3e per GPU · ~13.8 TB/rack
~250MW
facility power for 100k GB200

01The rack as the scale-up domain

A GB200 NVL72 packs 72 Blackwell GPUs — 36 GB200 superchips, each pairing two Blackwell dies with one Grace CPU — plus 36 Grace CPUs into a single liquid-cooled rack (NVIDIA). Each "GPU" here is itself a dual-die multi-chip module: two reticle-limited dies on one package, stitched together by a 10 TB/s NV-HBI die-to-die link and presented to software as one coherent device. This matters for your kernel authors — the chip you program against is already an internally-distributed system whose two halves communicate at bandwidth no external fabric can match, which is why CUTLASS/CuteDSL grouped-GEMM kernels for the expert layer should be written to keep an expert's weights resident on-package wherever the sharding allows.

The defining feature of the rack — the number to anchor every parallelism decision on — is NVLink5: 1.8 TB/s bidirectional per GPU (18 links × 100 GB/s each), aggregating through nine NVSwitch5 trays into a 130 TB/s all-to-all NVLink domain spanning all 72 GPUs (NVIDIA). The fabric is a copper backplane — roughly 5,000 cables, no optics inside the rack — which is exactly why the scale-up domain stops at 72 and why it is so cheap and reliable relative to optical scale-out. Compare to Hopper: an H100 NVLink4 node offers 900 GB/s per GPU across an 8-GPU domain (Spheron). Blackwell therefore delivers ~9× the per-GPU scale-up bandwidth across a domain that is itself 9× larger. For the expert-parallel all-to-all that dominates MoE communication, the entire EP group can live inside one NVLink domain up to EP=72 — versus EP≤8 on Hopper before the all-to-all spills onto InfiniBand at roughly a quarter of the bandwidth. This is the single largest systems lever Blackwell hands you, and the rest of this report's parallelism advice is downstream of it.

The one lever to anchor on

The 72-GPU, 130 TB/s flat NVLink5 domain lets the entire expert-parallel all-to-all live inside the rack up to EP=72 — versus EP≤8 on Hopper before spilling onto InfiniBand at ~1/4 the bandwidth. Every parallelism decision in your run is downstream of this one fact.

Memory follows the same rack-as-unit logic. Each GPU carries 192 GB of HBM3e, aggregating to ~13.4–13.8 TB of HBM per rack at ~576 TB/s (8 TB/s/GPU) (NVIDIA). The 36 Grace CPUs add ~17 TB of LPDDR5X, producing the marketed "~30 TB fast memory" figure — a coherent pool that changes how you think about expert placement and offload. For a 10T-parameter weight set plus optimizer state, the doubling of per-GPU capacity versus H100 (80 GB) or H200 (141 GB) directly shrinks the minimum sharding factor and relaxes the ZeRO/FSDP partitioning math relative to a Hopper-era plan.

02Power and cooling: the binding physical constraint

A fully loaded NVL72 draws ~120–132 kW of IT load (commonly cited near 120 kW, up to ~140 kW for higher-power SKUs), and it is liquid-cooled by mandate — direct-to-chip cold plates fed by ~45 °C facility water, with no air-cooling option for the GPU trays (Introl). Provisioned cooling capacity, including overhead, runs in the ~2.3–2.4 MW class per rack when you account for CDUs, secondary loops, and redundancy. The practical consequence for the build: you are not designing a GPU cluster that happens to need cooling; you are designing a 200+ MW thermal plant that happens to run GPUs. Megawatts, not silicon, are the gating resource.

You are building a thermal plant, not a GPU cluster

~120–132 kW IT per rack, liquid-cooled by mandate, ~2.3–2.4 MW of provisioned cooling per rack including overhead. At 100k GPUs that is a 200+ MW thermal plant. Power, not silicon, is the binding constraint — you can buy the GPUs faster than you can energize and cool them.

03Blackwell numerics: what FP4 actually buys a 10T/200B MoE

The most consequential change from Hopper is not raw FLOPs — it is that 4-bit floating point is now a training format, not merely an inference format, and the empirical case for betting a 10T-token run on it is, as of mid-2026, settled rather than speculative. Two microscaling formats matter, and you will use both.

MXFP8 (OCP)
block-32 · UE8M0 scale
The safe production default for the bulk of GEMMs. 50–60% throughput over BF16 with BF16-matching convergence, paired with stochastic rounding.
NVFP4
block-16 · E2M1 · two-level scale
E4M3 per-block micro-scale on FP32 per-tensor scale. The finer block + higher-fidelity scale is what makes it survive training, not just inference.

MXFP8 (OCP microscaling): blocks of 32 elements share a single UE8M0 power-of-two scale (NVIDIA NVFP4 blog). This is the safe production default for the bulk of your GEMMs. NVIDIA and multiple labs report 50–60% throughput gains over BF16 with BF16-matching convergence when you combine power-of-two block scaling with stochastic rounding. MXFP8 is the format you reach for when you want to de-risk a layer without dropping to 4-bit.

NVFP4: block size 16, element format E2M1, and a two-level scale — an E4M3 per-block micro-scale on top of an FP32 per-tensor scale (NVIDIA NVFP4 blog). The combination of a tighter block (16 vs. MXFP4's 32) and a higher-fidelity E4M3 scale (vs. MXFP4's E8M0) is precisely what makes NVFP4 survive training and not just inference. The finer granularity captures block-level dynamic range that MXFP4 throws away.

The load-bearing result — and the one you should treat as the template for your steady-state recipe — is NVIDIA's Pretraining Large Language Models with NVFP4, which trained a 12B Mamba-Transformer to 10T tokens, the exact horizon of your run, and held validation loss within ~1% of an FP8 baseline through the stable phase, widening to ~1.5% in the decay phase. The recipe is concrete and should be copied wholesale:

  1. Keep numerically sensitive layers in higher precision — final layers and embeddings stay in BF16/FP8; do not quantize them to 4-bit.
  2. Random Hadamard transforms applied before quantization to spread block-level outliers across the block, so a single large activation does not blow out the shared scale.
  3. 2D block scaling on weights, so the forward and backward passes see a consistent quantization of the same weight tensor (asymmetry here is a silent convergence killer).
  4. Stochastic rounding on the gradient path for unbiased low-precision gradients.
  5. A mixed-precision decay tail: switch the forward pass from NVFP4 back to BF16 at roughly the last 18% of tokens (in their run, at 8.2T of 10T), which cut the relative loss error from 1.5% down to 0.5% (NVIDIA NVFP4).
configs/nvfp4_recipe.pyNVFP4 steady-state recipeillustrative
quant_recipe = dict(
    fwd_dtype       = "nvfp4",      # [1] block-16 E2M1, E4M3 micro-scale on FP32 tensor-scale
    bwd_dtype       = "fp8_e4m3",   # [2] conservative backward + comms path
    hadamard_pre_q  = True,         # [3] spread block outliers before quantization
    weight_scaling  = "2d_block",   # [4] consistent fwd/bwd quantization of one weight
    grad_rounding   = "stochastic", #     unbiased low-precision gradients
    keep_bf16_layers = ("embed", "lm_head", "final_norm"),  # [5] sensitive carve-outs
)
# mixed-precision decay tail: flip the forward pass back to BF16 near the end
decay_tail_frac   = 0.18           # [6] last ~18% of tokens (8.2T of 10T)
fwd_dtype_at(step) = "bf16" if step > (1 - decay_tail_frac) * total_steps else "nvfp4"

1FP4 in the forward. Block-16 E2M1 with a two-level scale (E4M3 micro-scale on an FP32 per-tensor scale) is what survives training, not just inference.

2FP8 in the backward. The asymmetric forward/backward split — aggressive FP4 forward, conservative FP8 backward and comms — is the empirically validated MoE recipe.

3Hadamard before quant. Random Hadamard transforms spread block-level outliers so one large activation cannot blow out the shared scale.

42D weight scaling. Forward and backward must see a consistent quantization of the same weight tensor — asymmetry here is a silent convergence killer.

5Sensitive carve-outs. Final layers and embeddings stay BF16/FP8 — never quantized to 4-bit.

6Decay tail. Flipping the forward pass back to BF16 over the last ~18% of tokens cut the relative loss error from 1.5% to 0.5%.

For your 10T-total / 200B-active MoE, the engineering directive is: plan FP4 for the steady state and budget an explicit BF16/FP8 decay tail over the final ~15–20% of tokens. At 30T tokens that is roughly the last 5–6T tokens in higher precision — a non-trivial fraction of the compute budget, so size your throughput projections against a blended precision, not pure FP4.

For the expert GEMMs specifically, the empirically-validated recipe is asymmetric across the forward/backward split: aggressive FP4 in the forward pass, conservative FP8 in the backward and communication paths. The Practical FP4 Training for Large-Scale MoE Models work demonstrates exactly this on a 671B DeepSeek-class MoE — MXFP4 (E2M1, block-32, UE8M0 scale) forward GEMMs paired with FP8 (E4M3, block-128) backward — yielding +0.61% relative loss versus BF16 after 160B tokens alongside a 16.0% throughput speedup over BF16 and 12.5% over the best FP8 configuration (1157 → 1302 tokens/GPU/s), plus a 14.8% peak activation-memory reduction. The crucial subtext: that result was achieved on Hopper, which has no native FP4 tensor cores — the speedup came purely from the memory and bandwidth side. Blackwell's native FP4 tensor cores leave substantially more on the table, so treat the +16% Hopper number as a floor, not a ceiling, for what FP4 gives your expert layer.

FP4 forward, FP8 backward — the asymmetric MoE recipe

On a 671B DeepSeek-class MoE: MXFP4 forward + FP8-E4M3 backward gave +0.61% relative loss after 160B tokens, +16.0% throughput over BF16, and 14.8% lower peak activation memory — on Hopper, with no native FP4 tensor cores. The +16% is a floor for Blackwell, not a ceiling.

04Realized throughput: MFU and MBU reality on Blackwell

Spec sheets are not telemetry. The best independent grounding for your MFU/MBU assumptions comes from Microbenchmarking NVIDIA's Blackwell Architecture, which measured a B200 in isolation. The tensor cores essentially hit their marks: 96.2% of peak FP4 (7,700 TFLOPS), 96.3% of peak FP8 (3,851 TFLOPS), and 96.3% of peak BF16 (1,926 TFLOPS) in isolated GEMMs. The new warp-level matrix instruction, tcgen05.mma, has a single-instruction latency of 11.0–11.4 cycles versus Hopper's wgmma at 32–128 cycles — a 2.9–11.2× reduction that is a genuine, underappreciated win for the small, latency-bound expert GEMMs that dominate a high-sparsity MoE (your config activates a small fraction of experts per token, so individual expert matmuls are narrow and latency-sensitive).

The sobering counterpart is memory. The same study measured STREAM Triad HBM bandwidth at only 4.14 TB/s — 51.8% of the 8 TB/s spec (microbenchmark). Memory-bound kernels will not see anything close to peak bandwidth. The discipline this enforces: for the decode, attention, and all-to-all-bound phases, MBU (memory-bandwidth utilization), not MFU, is the governing metric, and a realized ~52% of nameplate HBM is the planning assumption, not 100%.

A critical correction on the headline FLOP numbers, because they are routinely misquoted: NVIDIA's marketed per-rack figures of 1,440 PFLOPS FP4 / 720 PFLOPS FP8 / 360 PFLOPS BF16 are with-sparsity (2:4) numbers, footnoted on NVIDIA's own page as "Dense is one-half sparse spec shown" (NVIDIA). For dense training FLOPs — what you actually compute against for 6 × active-params × tokens — the honest per-rack figures are ~360 PFLOPS dense FP8 and ~180 PFLOPS dense BF16, i.e. ~4.5 PFLOPS dense FP8 / ~2.25 PFLOPS dense BF16 per B200 GPU. Carry the dense numbers and state the sparse caveat explicitly; do not let a 2× sparsity marketing factor inflate your throughput model.

Quote dense, never sparse NVIDIA footnote

The marketed 1,440 / 720 / 360 PFLOPS per-rack figures are with-sparsity (2:4). For dense 6 × active-params × tokens accounting the honest numbers are ~360 PFLOPS dense FP8 (~4.5 PFLOPS/GPU) and ~180 PFLOPS dense BF16 (~2.25 PFLOPS/GPU). A 2× sparsity factor silently doubles a throughput model.

PrecisionPer B200 (dense)Per B200 (sparse)Per NVL72 rack (dense)Measured efficiency
FP4~10 PFLOPS~20 PFLOPS~720 PFLOPS96.2% of peak
FP8 / FP6~4.5 PFLOPS~9 PFLOPS~360 PFLOPS96.3% of peak
BF16~2.25 PFLOPS~4.5 PFLOPS~180 PFLOPS96.3% of peak
HBM BW8 TB/s nominal—~576 TB/s51.8% (STREAM Triad)
 Dense vs sparse per-GPU/per-rack peaks with measured efficiency. Source: NVIDIA GB200 NVL72, Blackwell microbenchmark.

End-to-end, a large MoE on GB200 in BF16/FP8 realistically lands in the ~30–45% MFU band, with heavy-routing MoE often at the lower end (~18–35%) because the all-to-all and grouped-GEMM overheads eat into the tensor-core duty cycle. FP8 MFU for large MoE rose roughly from ~29.5% to ~39.5% over 2024 as kernels matured, so ~35–40% FP8 is a defensible 2025–2026 planning figure — referenced against the dense peak, never the sparse one. Note the counterintuitive FP4 dynamic: switching to NVFP4 typically lowers nominal MFU, because the dequant, Hadamard, and scale-management overhead means you do not realize the full 2× FLOP advantage — yet it raises tokens/sec. The correct optimization targets are therefore tokens/GPU/s and $/token, not MFU. MFU is a diagnostic, not a goal; a run that drops MFU by 10 points while raising tokens/sec by 15% is a win.

Optimize tokens/GPU/s and $/token, not MFU

NVFP4 lowers nominal MFU (dequant + Hadamard + scale overhead means you don't realize the full 2× FLOP advantage) but raises tokens/sec. MFU is a diagnostic, not a goal — a run that drops MFU 10 points while raising tokens/sec 15% is a win.

05The accelerator landscape: MoE-relevant deltas

You are buying GB200, but a 100k-class commitment is large enough that the alternatives are a real decision, not a footnote — chiefly because the binding constraint is power and TCO, where the gaps are wide.

AcceleratorDense FP8Dense FP4HBM cap / BWScale-up domain & BW
H100 SXM~1,979 TFLOPS— (no FP4)80 GB HBM3 / 3.35 TB/s8-GPU, 900 GB/s NVLink4
H200 SXM~1,979 TFLOPS—141 GB HBM3e / 4.8 TB/s8-GPU, 900 GB/s
B200~4.5 PFLOPS~10 PFLOPS192 GB HBM3e / 8 TB/sNVL72, 1.8 TB/s
B300 (Ultra)~5 PFLOPS~15 PFLOPS288 GB HBM3e / 8 TB/sNVL72, 1.8 TB/s, ConnectX-8 800G
TPU v7 Ironwood4,614 TFLOPS(FP8-class)192 GB HBM3e / 7.38 TB/s9,216-chip pod, 1.2 TB/s ICI, 3D torus + OCS
Trainium32.52 PFLOPS (MXFP8)2.52 PFLOPS (=FP8)144 GB HBM3e / ~4.9 TB/sNL72×2 = 144 chips, 1.2 TB/s
Ascend 910C~0.78 PFLOPS BF16/die—128 GB HBM3 / 3.2 TB/sCloudMatrix 384 (UB all-to-all)
 MoE-relevant deltas across the credible 100k-class options. Dense figures throughout.

B300 / GB300 NVL72 (Blackwell Ultra) is the obvious upgrade path: 72× B300 with 288 GB HBM3e each (20 TB total HBM per rack), ~1.1–1.4 EFLOPS sparse FP4 (≈1.5× GB200), and ConnectX-8 at 800 Gb/s per GPU for scale-out, with each B300 rated ~15 PFLOPS dense FP4 at a 1,400 W TDP (NVIDIA GB300). For a 10T-parameter model the 50% larger HBM (288 vs. 192 GB) is the standout — it materially eases weight + optimizer-state placement and lets you keep more experts resident per GPU. If your procurement window allows, GB300 is the better substrate for a model this large; the HBM headroom alone changes the sharding calculus.

TPU v7 Ironwood is the only credible at-scale alternative, and it is a serious one. Per chip: 2,307 BF16 / 4,614 FP8 TFLOPS, 192 GB HBM3e, 7.38 TB/s, 1.2 TB/s bidirectional ICI, deployed in a 9,216-chip pod arranged as a 3D torus with optical circuit switching (OCS) — reconfigurable topology, no blast radius from a failed link, roughly 1.5 optical transceivers per chip at 800G routed through 48 144×144 OCS switches (Google; SemiAnalysis). SemiAnalysis estimates Ironwood at ~30–44% lower TCO than GB200/GB300. For very large expert parallelism the torus-plus-OCS design is arguably better than NVL72's 72-GPU copper island: you are not capped at a 72-device scale-up domain, and OCS lets you reconfigure the topology around failures rather than absorbing a blast radius. The cost is leaving CUDA — you trade the most mature kernel ecosystem on Earth for JAX/Pathways and Google's stack. For a single-vendor 100k commitment, Ironwood is the alternative that should actually keep you up at night on TCO grounds; the reason most labs still pick GB200 is software maturity and the CUDA moat, not raw efficiency.

Ironwood is the TCO number that should keep you up at night

SemiAnalysis estimates TPU v7 Ironwood at ~30–44% lower TCO than GB200/GB300, and the 9,216-chip 3D-torus + OCS pod escapes the 72-GPU scale-up cap entirely while reconfiguring around failures. The only thing keeping most labs on GB200 is software maturity and the CUDA moat — not raw efficiency.

Trainium3 (TSMC N3P) is a genuine cost challenger on paper — 2.52 PFLOPS FP8 per chip, 144 GB HBM3e, ~4.9 TB/s, NeuronLink 1.2 TB/s scale-up, with the Trn3 UltraServer scaling to 144 chips / 362 PFLOPS FP8 / 20.7 TB HBM / 706 TB/s (AWS). But it has a disqualifying property for your run: FP4 throughput equals FP8 throughput — there is no FP4 speedup (a systolic-array geometry choice, 512×128 vs. 128×128). For a run whose central efficiency bet is native 4-bit pretraining, a part that gives you nothing for going to 4-bit blunts the whole thesis. Trainium4 reportedly promises ≥6× FP4, which is the part to wait for; Trainium3 is a strong choice for an FP8 run and the wrong choice for a 4-bit one.

Trainium3 has no FP4 speedup — a disqualifier for a 4-bit thesis

FP4 throughput equals FP8 throughput (a 512×128 systolic-array geometry choice). For a run whose central efficiency bet is native 4-bit pretraining, that blunts the whole thesis. Trainium4 reportedly promises ≥6× FP4 — that is the part to wait for.

Huawei CloudMatrix 384 / Ascend 910C is the brute-force answer and the worst-case power reference point: ~300 PFLOPS BF16 and 49.2 TB HBM at the system level across 384 Ascend 910C dies, with a three-plane UB/RDMA/VPC fabric for all-to-all — but at ~4.1× the power and ~2.5× worse power-per-FLOP of a GB200 NVL72 (SemiAnalysis). It uses HBM3 with no FP4 path. Outside China, and outside the 4-bit regime, it is irrelevant to your decision — but it is a useful illustration of how far you can fall behind on power-per-FLOP if you optimize for raw HBM and bandwidth while ignoring the precision and efficiency frontier.

06Building a 100,000-GB200 cluster

100,000 GB200 GPUs is ~1,389 NVL72 racks. At ~120–132 kW IT per rack, and budgeting ~150–180 kW per rack all-in once cooling, networking, and PUE overhead are included, the facility lands at ~200 MW IT / ~250 MW facility power. The cleanest real-world reference is xAI's Colossus 2: SemiAnalysis reconstructs ~200 MW of cooling supporting roughly 110k GB200 NVL72 GPUs, while Colossus 1's ~300 MW carried ~200k Hopper plus ~30k GB200 (SemiAnalysis). For a clean 100k GB200 design, plan ~200 MW IT and ~250 MW at the facility meter.

Topology. With NVLink5 confined to 72 GPUs, racks group into pods of typically 8–16 NVL72 racks behind a scale-out layer (NDR/XDR InfiniBand or 800G Spectrum-X Ethernet), then a fat-tree or rail-optimized spine connects pods. The crucial design fact: because the coherent NVLink domain stops at the rack, the inter-rack network is the bottleneck for all pipeline-parallel and data-parallel traffic — everything that crosses a rack boundary runs at scale-out bandwidth, roughly a quarter or less of intra-rack NVLink. The standard compromise is 1:1 rail-optimized non-blocking connectivity at the pod level with a tapered (oversubscribed) spine across pods, exploiting the fact that DP gradient all-reduce traffic is more tolerant of taper than the EP all-to-all (which you have already confined inside the rack). Your parallelism layout should therefore map EP onto the NVLink domain, pipeline stages onto rails within a pod, and data parallelism across the tapered spine.

Capex order of magnitude. GB200 NVL72 racks run ~$3–3.5M each at street prices, putting ~$4.5–5B into 1,389 racks of compute alone, with networking, storage, and datacenter shell pushing the total to ~$6–8B — consistent with the ~$7B figure cited for the 200k-GPU Colossus build (Colossus). And the recurring cost is dominated by power: ~250 MW continuous is on the order of $120M+/yr in electricity at industrial rates before any cooling or staffing overhead. The strategic read is unambiguous — power, not silicon, is the binding constraint. You can buy the GPUs faster than you can energize and cool them.

1,389racks
NVL72 racks for 100k GPUs
$6–8B
total capex incl. network + shell
$120M+/yr
electricity at ~250 MW continuous

A reliability caveat you must price in. As of late 2025, SemiAnalysis reported that no large-scale training run had yet completed on GB200 NVL72, citing software and reliability immaturity, and put GB200 all-in cost-per-GPU at ~1.6–1.7× H100 — meaning the platform needs to clear a ~1.6× throughput-per-GPU break-even just to match Hopper economics (SemiAnalysis). By mid-2026 the picture has improved — the MAI-Thinking-1 report documents a from-scratch 1T-total MoE trained to 30T+ tokens on 8,192 production GB200 GPUs, which is the existence proof that the platform is now viable for serious runs — but the lesson stands: goodput and determinism tooling matter more than peak FLOPs at this scale. MAI devotes an entire "Cluster Environment" section to goodput for a reason. Budget for failure-domain isolation, fast checkpoint/restart, and silent-data-corruption detection as first-class engineering, not afterthoughts; on a ~250 MW machine, every percentage point of goodput is worth millions.

No completed large run on GB200 as of late 2025 SemiAnalysis

GB200 all-in cost-per-GPU is ~1.6–1.7× H100, so the platform must clear a ~1.6× throughput-per-GPU break-even just to match Hopper economics. Mid-2026's MAI-Thinking-1 (1T-total MoE, 30T+ tokens, 8,192 production GB200) is the existence proof it's now viable — but goodput, failure-domain isolation, fast checkpoint/restart, and SDC detection are first-class engineering, not afterthoughts.

07Compute budget for the 10T/200B target

Training FLOPs scale as 6 × active-params × tokens, so the 200B-active figure — not the 10T total — is what drives the compute bill. At dense FP8 peak, a 100k GB200 cluster is ~0.45 EFLOP/s (100,000 × ~4.5 PFLOPS dense FP8); the "~1.0 EFLOP/s FP8" figure that circulates is the sparse number and should not be used for dense training FLOP accounting. Working examples for 200B active:

compute Training FLOPs = 6 · active-params · tokens · · · wall-clock = FLOPs / (MFU · 0.45 EFLOP/s) 200B active drives the bill, not the 10T total. At ~40% MFU on ~0.45 EFLOP/s dense FP8: 20T tok → ~17 days, 30T → ~26 days, 60T → ~52 days.
python — dense-FLOP wall-clock budget (reproduces the table; quote dense, never sparse)
active_params = 200e9                 # 200B active drives the bill, not the 10T total
dense_fp8_per_gpu = 4.5e15            # dense FP8, NOT the 9e15 sparse figure
cluster_peak = 100_000 * dense_fp8_per_gpu   # ~0.45 EFLOP/s, NOT the ~1.0 sparse EFLOP/s
mfu = 0.40                            # defensible 2025-26 large-MoE FP8 planning figure

for tokens in (20e12, 30e12, 60e12):
    flops = 6 * active_params * tokens                 # 6 x active-params x tokens
    seconds = flops / (mfu * cluster_peak)
    print(f"{tokens/1e12:.0f}T -> {flops:.1e} FLOP, {seconds/86400:.0f} days")
# 20T -> 2.4e25 FLOP, 17 days | 30T -> 3.6e25, 26 days | 60T -> 7.2e25, 52 days
TokensTraining FLOPsWall-clock @ ~40% MFU, ~0.45 EFLOP/s dense FP8
20T~2.4e25~17 days
30T~3.6e25~26 days
60T~7.2e25~52 days
 Wall-clock for 200B-active at 40% MFU against the dense FP8 cluster peak.

A 30T-token run lands at roughly GPT-5-pretrain scale (~3e25 FLOP) but with ~2× the active parameters (Epoch AI). The over-training math is sobering for an active budget this large: a TPP (tokens-per-active-parameter) of even 100–200 implies 20–40T tokens, and chasing the 500–1000 TPP regime MAI used for its 35B-active model would demand 100–200T tokens — infeasible on clean data. With 200B active you are structurally in the mild-over-training regime, and data, not compute, becomes the binding constraint above ~40–50T tokens. The FP4 steady-state plus BF16 decay-tail recipe is what keeps the compute side tractable; the data side is a separate problem that no amount of GB200 solves.

Above ~40–50T tokens, data — not compute — is the wall

With 200B active you are structurally in mild over-training: TPP 100–200 implies 20–40T tokens; the 500–1000 TPP regime would demand 100–200T tokens, infeasible on clean data. FP4 + decay-tail keeps the compute side tractable; the data wall is a separate problem no amount of GB200 solves.

08What concretely changes versus Hopper for the 10T run

  1. Expert parallelism fits inside the rack. NVLink5's 72-GPU, 1.8 TB/s coherent domain keeps the EP all-to-all on-NVLink up to EP=72, versus EP≤8 on Hopper before spilling to InfiniBand at ~1/4 the bandwidth. This is the single largest architectural change, and it is why a Blackwell MoE beats a Hopper MoE by more than the raw FLOP ratio. Set EP to the NVLink domain and place experts to keep all-to-all on copper.
  2. FP4 is a training format now. NVFP4 (block-16, E4M3 scale) with Random Hadamard transforms, stochastic rounding, 2D weight scaling, and sensitive-layer carve-outs holds <1% loss to 10T tokens; budget a BF16/FP8 decay tail over the final ~18% of tokens. Hopper had no native FP4 tensor cores, so this lever simply did not exist before Blackwell.
  3. Per-GPU capacity doubles (192 GB vs. 80/141 GB, 288 GB on B300), shrinking the parameter-sharding factor and easing the 10T-weight + optimizer-state footprint. The coherent ~13.8 TB rack memory pool (~20 TB on GB300) changes where experts live.
  4. The bottleneck moves from FLOPs to bandwidth and power. Tensor cores hit ~96% of peak; HBM hits ~52% of nameplate; the win is gated by MBU and by the ~250 MW facility envelope. Optimize for tokens/GPU/s and $/token, plan the build around megawatts, and treat NVL72 EP-locality and the NVFP4 decay-tail recipe as the two highest-leverage decisions in the entire run.
The summary judgment

GB200 NVL72 is not a faster H100 — it is a different unit of compute, a 72-GPU coherent rack whose NVLink domain and native FP4 reshape the parallelism and numerics of a trillion-scale MoE. Plan EP inside the rack, FP4 in the steady state with a higher-precision tail, and the build around power. If your procurement timeline reaches GB300, take the 288 GB HBM. And keep one eye on Ironwood's TCO — it is the only number on the competitive board large enough to make a 100k-GB200 commitment something other than the obvious default.
