Chapter 03 · The network is the training system

Networking and collectives at 100k+ GPUs: MRC, Ultra Ethernet, Spectrum-X, NVLink domains, and the all-to-all problem

For a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200 GPUs the network is not a supporting subsystem — it is the training system. At this scale the model is a communication schedule with arithmetic bolted on, and the gap between a 38% MFU run and a 22% MFU run is almost entirely a function of how well you hide the collectives and how brutally one bad link can stall a synchronous step that 100,000 GPUs are all waiting on.

~28 min read6 figuresgrounded in OpenAI MRC + DeepEP/NCCL-GIN

The expert-parallel (EP) all-to-all that shuttles tokens to their routed experts dominates the inner loop; the data-parallel (DP) and tensor-parallel (TP) gradient/activation reductions dominate the outer loop; and the difference between a fast run and a stalled one is how well you hide these collectives and survive a single bad link. This chapter is about the physical fabric, the transport, and the collective software that make a synchronous all-to-all survivable and fast at this scale.

The architecture is two disjoint worlds, and you must reason about them separately. The scale-up domain is the NVLink5 / NVL72 NVSwitch fabric inside a rack: 72 Blackwell GPUs in one coherent NVLink namespace. The scale-out domain is the 800G RDMA Ethernet (or InfiniBand) plane that stitches ~1,389 NVL72 racks into a 100k-GPU job. The scale-up answer is settled — NVL72/NVLink5 — and the engineering interest is almost entirely in the scale-out plane and in the collective libraries that span the boundary. The central new artifact of mid-2026 is OpenAI's Multipath Reliable Connection (MRC), and the central recurring problem is the MoE all-to-all, so we organize around those.

MRC makes the packet fabric survivable; DeepEP/GIN/MegaMoE make the all-to-all invisible; NVL72 keeps the high-fanout hop on copper at 18× the scale-out bandwidth; and the routing config keeps the RDMA leg small enough that ~50% line-rate efficiency is still enough.
1.8TB/s
NVLink5 per-GPU, in NVL72
18×
NVLink : RDMA bandwidth ratio
256exp
routed experts, top-8 (V3-shaped)
6144F/B
FLOPs/byte to hide the all-to-all

01The scale-up domain: NVL72, NVLink5, and why 72 is the magic number

The GB200 NVL72 is the unit cell. Inside it, NVLink5 gives each Blackwell GPU 1.8 TB/s of bidirectional bandwidth — 18 links × 100 GB/s each, built on 224G SerDes — and the NVSwitch fabric delivers 130 TB/s of aggregate all-to-all bandwidth across the 72-GPU domain, presented as a single flat NVLink namespace (NVIDIA GB200 NVL72). That 1.8 TB/s per GPU is roughly 18× the 800 Gb/s (100 GB/s) per-GPU scale-out bandwidth a ConnectX-8 SuperNIC provides. This bandwidth asymmetry — a ~18:1 ratio between the intra-rack NVLink hop and the inter-rack RDMA hop — is the single most important number for MoE placement, and every routing and parallelism decision downstream is a consequence of it.

The one ratio to remember

NVLink is ~18× faster than RDMA per GPU (1.8 TB/s vs 100 GB/s). Keep the high-fanout part of the all-to-all on copper inside the NVL72 and let the 800G plane see only the irreducible inter-rack residue — every routing and parallelism decision in this chapter is downstream of that ratio.

The compute side deserves a correction that propagates into every roofline you draw. NVIDIA's headline GB200 NVL72 figures — 1,440 PFLOP/s FP4, 720 PFLOP/s FP8, 360 PFLOP/s BF16 per rack — are sparse (2:4 structured sparsity) numbers, footnoted on NVIDIA's own page as "Dense is one-half sparse spec shown." For dense training FLOPs, which is what you actually accumulate during pretraining, the real per-rack peaks are 360 PFLOP/s dense FP8 and 180 PFLOP/s dense BF16, i.e. ~4.5 PFLOP/s dense FP8 and ~2.25 PFLOP/s dense BF16 per B200 GPU (NVIDIA GB200 NVL72). A 100,000-GPU cluster is therefore ~0.45 EFLOP/s dense FP8 peak, not the ~1.0 EFLOP/s you get by counting sparsity. Quote the dense number, state the sparse caveat, and reference MFU (realistically 35–40% FP8 for a well-tuned large MoE in 2025–2026, often lower under heavy routing) against the dense peak — otherwise you over-provision compute relative to network and end up network-bound with idle tensor cores.

Quote dense, not sparse, or you will over-provision compute

The 720/360/180 PFLOP/s rack figures are 2:4-sparse; dense is one-half. Pretraining accumulates dense FLOPs, so a 100k-GPU cluster is ~0.45 EFLOP/s dense FP8, not ~1.0. Provision network against the dense number or your tensor cores sit idle while the fabric saturates.

The reason 72 is the magic number for MoE specifically: it is the largest expert-parallel group you can form where every dispatch/combine hop is an NVLink hop. DeepSeek-style node-limited (group-limited) routing, which caps each token to at most M=4 nodes (DeepSeek-V3), is explicitly designed so an expert group can be co-located inside one NVLink domain; the RDMA leg of the all-to-all then carries only cross-group traffic. On NVL72 you have far more headroom than the 8-GPU HGX nodes DeepSeek-V3 was tuned for, and the right move is to place an entire expert group — and ideally its redundant EPLB replicas — inside a single rack so the high-fanout part of the all-to-all stays on the 130 TB/s NVSwitch fabric and the 800G plane sees only the irreducible inter-rack residue.

02MRC: spray-and-route over RoCE, source-routed by SRv6

The load-bearing scale-out artifact for a 2026 100k-GPU build is OpenAI's Multipath Reliable Connection (MRC), published as arXiv 2605.04333 (50 authors across OpenAI and Microsoft) and contributed to the Open Compute Project as the MRC 1.0 spec (arXiv 2605.04333; OCP MRC 1.0). Understanding MRC is the fastest way to understand where the entire scale-out fabric debate landed by mid-2026.

MRC is not a new transport built from scratch. It extends RoCEv2's Reliable Connection (RC) semantics and keeps the RoCE verbs / queue-pair (QP) abstraction intact, so it slots into the existing NIC and NCCL software stack. Its one radical restriction is the enabler for everything else: an MRC connection supports only write and write-with-immediate — one-sided RDMA writes, no reads, no sends. Why does that unlock packet spraying? Because every data packet of a one-sided write already carries the RDMA virtual address and rkey of its destination. The receiving NIC can therefore DMA each arriving packet directly to its final memory location regardless of arrival order. There is no reorder buffer, because there is nothing to reorder — order is irrelevant when every packet is self-addressing. This is the precise mechanism that kills the central problem of naive multipath RDMA, where out-of-order arrival across sprayed paths forces a reorder buffer that destroys throughput (arXiv 2605.04333).

Spraying and routing. Each packet is tagged with a 32-bit Entropy Value (EV) striped across the UDP source port and the IPv6 flow label. A QP generates a pool of typically 128–256 EVs at startup and rotates through them packet by packet, so a single connection's bytes fan out across up to 256 physical paths. Path selection is static SRv6 source routing using micro-segment IDs (uSIDs, the uN flavor where each switch on the path is explicitly named in the segment list): every EV maps deterministically to exactly one physical path to the destination NIC. Switch forwarding tables are configured at install and essentially never change. This is the reliability thesis: the control plane is dumb and static, so there is no routing-protocol reconvergence to wait on when something breaks (arXiv 2605.04333).

Failover in microseconds. When a link or path fails, the NIC simply stops using the affected EV(s) — it removes them from the rotation. With 256 EVs, a single dead path costs you ~0.4% of your paths and is dropped in microseconds, so the job runs slightly degraded instead of stalling. The slow path — a full QP remap after a NIC↔T0 (top-of-rack) link is lost entirely — is the "few seconds" event, but it is rare and localized. Contrast this with conventional fabrics where a link flap triggers seconds of routing-protocol reconvergence during which a synchronous collective across 100k GPUs simply hangs. This microsecond failover is the actual product, and the reason MRC exists.

Loss and congestion handling, straight from the UEC playbook. MRC disables PFC (Priority Flow Control) entirely — no more PFC-deadlock and head-of-line-blocking pathologies that plague vanilla RoCEv2 at scale. Loss is handled with SACK fast retransmit; ECN is used purely as a load-balancing signal rather than a hard backpressure mechanism; and packet trimming forwards the headers of would-be-dropped packets as priority NACKs, so a sender learns about congestion-induced loss immediately rather than via timeout. This trim-and-spray-and-NACK design is lifted directly from the Ultra Ethernet Consortium's transport (§03).

mrc_qp.cMRC packet spray (illustrative)write-only RCtrimmed
// MRC connection: write-only verbs unlock reorder-free spray
struct mrc_qp {
    uint32_t ev_pool[256];   // 128–256 Entropy Values  // [1]
    int      ev_cursor;
    srv6_usid_path_t path_for_ev[256];  // static, set at install  // [2]
};

ssize_t mrc_post_write(struct mrc_qp *qp, void *src, size_t len,
                       uint64_t remote_addr, uint32_t rkey) {  // [3]
    for (size_t off = 0; off < len; off += MTU) {
        uint32_t ev = qp->ev_pool[qp->ev_cursor++ % 256];  // rotate per packet
        if (ev_is_dead(qp, ev)) continue;                  // failover: skip EV  // [4]
        pkt_t p = { .udp_src = ev & 0xffff,                // EV → UDP src + flow label
                    .ipv6_flow = (ev >> 16),
                    .srv6 = qp->path_for_ev[ev],           // deterministic path
                    .rdma_addr = remote_addr + off,        // self-addressing  // [5]
                    .rkey = rkey, .imm = WRITE_IMM };
        nic_tx(&p, src + off, MTU);                        // no reads, no sends
    }
}

1256 paths per QP. A pool of 128–256 EVs striped across UDP src port + IPv6 flow label fans one connection across up to 256 physical paths.

2Static SRv6. Each EV maps deterministically to one uSID source-routed path; forwarding tables are set at install and never reconverge.

3Write-only verbs. Only write/write-with-immediate — every packet carries the destination address, so order is irrelevant.

4Microsecond failover. A dead path just drops its EV from rotation (~0.4% of paths) — no reconvergence, the job runs slightly degraded.

5No reorder buffer. The NIC DMAs each self-addressing packet straight to its final memory location regardless of arrival order.

The measured numbers worth committing to memory (arXiv 2605.04333):

MetricMRC measured
ConnectX-8 point-to-point throughput~770 Gb/s (96% of 800G line rate)
T0-local (intra-leaf) latency5.09 µs
Cross-T1 (spine-crossing) latency6.54 µs
NCCL at 42,000 GPUs92 GB/s sustained
Single MRC QP (256 paths) vs RoCEv2beats 16 RoCEv2 QPs
Single ECMP-pinned RoCEv2 QP~50% of achievable throughput (hash collisions)
Topology scale (two-tier multi-plane Clos)131,072 GPUs, longest path = 3 switches
 The RoCEv2 comparison is the damning one — one MRC QP spraying 256 paths beats sixteen ECMP-pinned RoCEv2 QPs.

The RoCEv2 comparison is the damning one. A single RoCEv2 QP, pinned by ECMP hashing to one path, achieves only about half of achievable throughput because of hash collisions with other flows on the same path. One MRC QP spraying 256 paths beats sixteen RoCEv2 QPs. And the topology win is structural: the two-tier multi-plane Clos scales to 131,072 GPUs with a longest path of just 3 switches, versus the 5–7 switch hops of a conventional three-tier fat-tree, which is why the cross-T1 latency stays at 6.54 µs even at full scale.

Hardware and deployment. MRC's SRv6 + spray design is implemented across NVIDIA ConnectX-8 (800G), AMD Pollara (400G) and Vulcano (800G), and Broadcom Thor Ultra NICs, with the SRv6 forwarding handled by NVIDIA Spectrum-4/5 and Broadcom Tomahawk 5 switch silicon — explicitly multi-vendor, which is the point. It is deployed in production on OpenAI's OCI Abilene site and Microsoft Fairwater GB200 superclusters (OCP MRC 1.0). For a greenfield 100k build, MRC over Spectrum-X / Tomahawk-5 is the baseline scale-out transport, and the multi-vendor NIC/switch support is precisely what lets you dual-source and avoid being captive to a single supplier's lead times.

Baseline MRC over Spectrum-X / Tomahawk-5

For a greenfield 100k build, MRC over Spectrum-X / Tomahawk-5 is the scale-out transport. The multi-vendor NIC support (ConnectX-8, AMD Pollara/Vulcano, Broadcom Thor Ultra) is the lever that lets you dual-source at 100k-NIC volume and avoid single-supplier lead-time captivity.

03The fabric wars: InfiniBand vs RoCEv2 vs Ultra Ethernet

MRC is one point in a three-way contest. Here is the honest June-2026 comparison for the scale-out plane.

InfiniBand XDR (Quantum-X800)RoCEv2 / Spectrum-XUltra Ethernet (UEC 1.0) / MRC
Link rate800G800GbE800GbE
Small-msg latency~1 µs~1–2 µs~5–6.5 µs (MRC measured)
RoutingHW adaptive routingECMP (vanilla) / spray (Spectrum-X)Coordinated spray + SRv6 (MRC)
Flow controlCredit-basedPFC (deadlock-prone)LLR + CBFC (UET); PFC off (MRC)
In-network reductionSHARPv4 (14.4 TFLOPS, ~9× allreduce)nonenone (offload to SHARP if NVLink/IB hybrid)
Multi-vendorNVIDIA-onlymulti-vendormulti-vendor
Cost/porthighestmoderatemoderate
 The determinism advantage IB historically held over Ethernet is exactly what MRC was engineered to erase.

InfiniBand XDR via Quantum-X800 (the Q3400 switch, 144×800G ports) remains the deterministic gold standard: ~1 µs small-message latency, hardware adaptive routing, and crucially SHARPv4 in-network reduction delivering 14.4 TFLOPS of in-switch compute with claims of up to 9× traffic reduction on allreduce (NVIDIA SHARP). Its weaknesses are that it is NVIDIA-only and the most expensive per-port, and that the determinism advantage it historically held over Ethernet is exactly what MRC was engineered to erase.

Vanilla RoCEv2 is the cautionary tale: the ECMP hash-collision problem (a flow gets pinned to one path and collides) and PFC deadlock are the two pathologies that the entire spray-fabric movement exists to fix. Spectrum-X is NVIDIA's answer — RoCE plus adaptive routing and congestion control — and it is reported to come within ~5% of IB NDR on 8-node NCCL allreduce. Spectrum-X is the switch substrate MRC frequently runs on.

Ultra Ethernet (UEC 1.0, published June 2025) defines the Ultra Ethernet Transport (UET), which brings packet spraying with coordinated path selection (not bare ECMP hashing), Link Layer Retry (LLR) and Credit-Based Flow Control (CBFC) to give Ethernet InfiniBand-like lossless behavior, plus new sender- and receiver-driven congestion control (UEC 1.0). The cleanest way to position MRC is as OpenAI's productionized superset of UEC: it takes UEC's spray + trim + congestion-control machinery and adds two things UET handles differently — SRv6 source routing (deterministic per-EV paths instead of UET's coordinated-but-still-fabric-chosen paths) and the write-only-RDMA reorder-free trick (UET instead carries its own reordering model). Tom Herbert's protocol-engineer review of UET 1.0 is worth reading for the design tensions in UET's reordering and congestion-control internals that MRC's choices were specifically made to sidestep (Herbert, UET review).

Recommendation for the 100k GB200 build. The hyperscalers have already voted, and you should follow them: Ethernet-based spray fabrics (MRC / UEC / Spectrum-X), not InfiniBand, for the scale-out plane. The drivers are multi-vendor sourcing (no single-supplier captivity at 100k-NIC volume), blast-radius isolation, and the fact that MRC closes the determinism gap that historically justified IB's premium. Keep IB-style SHARP in the toolbox via the NVLink-SHARP path (§06), not via an IB scale-out fabric. The ~5 µs MRC latency floor vs IB's ~1 µs is a real regression, but for bandwidth-bound MoE all-to-all on large messages it is irrelevant — and the microsecond failover advantage at 100k GPUs dwarfs the per-message latency difference.

04Topology: rail-optimized fat-tree vs multi-plane (Oracle Acceleron)

Two structurally different scale-out topologies are on the table, and the choice interacts directly with MRC.

Rail-optimized fat-tree is the classic NVIDIA reference architecture. GPU i in every node connects to leaf switch i, organized into Scalable Units of 32 nodes, so that same-rail traffic crosses exactly one leaf and only cross-rail traffic climbs to the spine. With ConnectX-8 SuperNICs at 800 Gb/s/GPU, each NVL72 rack offers 57.6 Tb/s of scale-out bandwidth (72 × 800 Gb/s) (NVIDIA GB200 NVL72). The rail structure is a good match for collectives where ranks aligned on the same rail dominate the traffic, but it has the failure characteristic that a leaf or spine failure degrades the whole fabric until reconvergence.

Oracle Acceleron multiplanar is the structurally different bet and the most relevant non-NVIDIA primary source. Instead of one fabric, Acceleron splits each GPU's NIC across multiple fully-disjoint network planes, connecting a single GPU to several switches simultaneously (Oracle Acceleron). The payoffs are: (1) elimination of fate-sharing — a plane failure reroutes instantly onto surviving planes instead of degrading the whole job; (2) a flatter fabric with fewer tiers, which lowers latency, power, and cost; and (3) it is RoCEv2-based, so it composes with the spray transports above. OCI's Zettascale10 targets up to 800,000 GPUs / 16 zettaFLOPS, H2 2026, on this architecture.

The key insight is that multiplanar and MRC are complementary, not competing: MRC sprays within the redundant planes that Acceleron provides. Disjoint planes give MRC's microsecond EV-failover a richer substrate — when an entire plane degrades, you fail over to a wholly independent plane rather than just dropping EVs within one fabric. For a 100k greenfield build, baseline a disjoint multi-plane fabric (Acceleron-style) running MRC, against a single-plane rail-optimized fat-tree, and prefer the former. The fate-sharing elimination is worth the extra NIC ports and cabling complexity at this scale, because at 100k GPUs the probability that some fabric element is degraded at any given moment approaches one, and a single-plane fabric forces every such event into the job's critical path.

Prefer disjoint multi-plane + MRC over single-plane fat-tree

At 100k GPUs the probability that some fabric element is degraded approaches one. Disjoint planes (Acceleron-style) eliminate fate-sharing and give MRC's EV-failover a richer substrate — a whole-plane failure reroutes to an independent plane. The extra NIC ports and cabling are worth keeping every such event off the job's critical path.

05The contrast that matters: Google ICI + optical circuit switching

Everything above is packet-switched Ethernet. The architectural antithesis — and the forward-looking signal you should track — is Google's TPU fabric and its emerging GPU-side analogs.

TPU ICI (Inter-Chip Interconnect) is a 3D torus of optical-circuit-switched cubes: 4×4×4 = 64 TPUs per cube, up to 1.2 TB/s/chip, with MEMS-based optical circuit switches (OCS) — 2D mirror arrays / beam steering — reconfiguring the torus on a per-job basis. OCP's April 2026 OCS white paper and Google's own numbers cite ~40% power and ~30% cost reduction versus an equivalent electrical-packet switching tier, with the OCS handling only the inter-cube optical links while intra-cube links stay copper (OCP OCS white paper). The defining property is that circuit-switched means zero in-network congestion: you build a contention-free topology that exactly matches the collective, rather than statistically load-balancing a packet fabric and hoping spraying smooths the tail. The MEMS reconfiguration is slow (milliseconds), so it is used for job-granularity topology, not per-collective.

The genuinely forward-looking resource — and the one most directly relevant to MoE — is PCCL (arXiv 2509.15450, Cornell, Sep 2025) (PCCL). PCCL does per-collective-round topology reconfiguration on a photonic scale-up fabric, using MZI (Mach-Zehnder interferometer) switches on optical interposers that reconfigure in microseconds rather than the milliseconds of MEMS. The thesis is inverted from a decade of collective research: "collective-aware topologies, not topology-aware collectives." Instead of synthesizing a clever algorithm for a fixed topology, you reconfigure the topology to match each collective. On a 4×4×4 torus, PCCL is 7.5× faster on a 32 MB all-to-all and up to 2.5–3× faster on allreduce versus TACCL/TACOS/NCCL-class baselines, yielding 1.3× end-to-end training throughput.

The explicit motivation is MoE, and it names the exact problem this chapter circles: MoE "alternates between latency-sensitive AllToAll requiring low-diameter topologies and bandwidth-hungry AllReduce favoring ring-like high-diameter topologies" — no fixed topology serves both, costing 2–4× from congestion plus dilation. This is the deep structural reason the all-to-all problem is hard: the EP dispatch/combine wants a low-diameter, high-radix topology to minimize hop count for small scattered messages, while the DP/TP allreduce wants a ring-like high-diameter topology to maximize bisection bandwidth for large contiguous reductions. A packet fabric — MRC included — picks one compromise topology and lives with both being suboptimal.

The forward bet: photonic scale-up not yet productized

MRC and IB make the packet fabric survivable and fast-enough; they do not solve the all-to-all-vs-allreduce topology conflict at its root. PCCL/photonic scale-up does. For mid-2026 you build on MRC because photonic scale-up is not yet productized at 100k scale, but architect software (collective abstractions, expert placement) so a future photonic scale-up domain is a drop-in — that is where the all-to-all problem actually gets solved.

06The collective stack: NCCL, NVSHMEM, IBGDA, DeepEP, GIN, SHARP

The fabric is inert without the collective software that drives it. For a GB200 MoE there are three load-bearing layers, and they map onto the two domains (NVLink scale-up, RDMA scale-out) explicitly.

DeepEP — the device-initiated sparse all-to-all substrate

DeepEP is the reference EP communication library and the right mental model for dispatch/combine. The original (V1) design split into two kernel families. High-throughput (HT) / normal kernels (training and prefill) use NVSHMEM, run ~24 QPs, and do asymmetric-domain forwarding: a token destined for a remote expert goes over IB/RDMA to the matching in-node index on the destination rack, then hops over NVLink to the actual target expert — overlapping the RDMA and NVLink legs so both domains saturate concurrently. Low-latency (LL) kernels (decode) use a pure-RDMA full mesh over IBGDA (InfiniBand GPUDirect Async, where the GPU programs the NIC doorbell directly with no CPU in the loop), 8–16 QPs, with a hook-based comm/compute overlap that consumes zero SMs (DeepEP legacy docs). The whole HT path saturates both NVLink and RDMA with only ~20–24 SMs.

The V2 rewrite is the more important development for GB200. It unifies HT and LL kernels behind a single ElasticBuffer interface, drops SM consumption from 24 → 4–6 SMs, and — critically — replaces the NVSHMEM transport with an NCCL "Gin" backend, giving you NCCL's elasticity and fault tolerance under the same low-latency device-initiated all-to-all. V2 adds SM100 (Blackwell) support with measured 726/740 GB/s dispatch/combine over NVLink at EP8 (max-perf, 64 SMs) and 643/675 GB/s at the minimal-SM config; SM90/CX7 RDMA is ~90 GB/s at EP8×2 (DeepEP README). The NVIDIA-authored hybrid-ep branch is the one to track for GB200: it uses TMA copies and warp-level pipelining to minimize SM occupancy while saturating both NVLink and RDMA, plus experimental NIXL/UCX integration.

24→4–6
SM cost, DeepEP V1→V2
726GB/s
V2 SM100 NVLink dispatch, EP8
7.2TB/s
Blackwell NVLink-SHARP allreduce
Verify hybrid-ep SM100 status before committing verify

The NVIDIA-authored hybrid-ep branch is the most GB200-relevant DeepEP variant (TMA + warp pipelining, near-zero SM), but its documented GPU support still listed SM80/SM90 at survey time — confirm SM100 status before you build against it, because Blackwell is the whole point.

NCCL 2.28 GIN — DeepEP-style device-initiated RDMA, in NCCL

NVIDIA's NCCL 2.28 introduces a Device API with three modes (NCCL GIN, arXiv 2511.15076): LSA (Load/Store Accessible) for NVLink/PCIe, Multimem for NVLink-SHARP multicast/reduce, and GIN (GPU-Initiated Networking) for network RDMA. GIN has two backends: a GDAKI backend where DOCA GPUNetIO lets GPU threads program the NIC directly (the IBGDA idea, generalized), and a Proxy backend using lock-free GPU→CPU queues with 64-byte descriptors that works on any RDMA NIC. GIN is integrated with DeepEP and pplx-kernels, so you get NVSHMEM-class latency plus NCCL's collective ecosystem, elasticity, and fault tolerance. For a 100k-GPU run where fault tolerance is not optional, this is the strategically correct substrate — it lets you ride NCCL's mature failure-recovery while getting device-initiated all-to-all performance.

SHARP — in-network allreduce offload

SHARP offloads the allreduce side (the DP/TP gradient sync that overlaps with the EP all-to-all). NCCL 2.27 added both NVLink-SHARP and IB-SHARP; Blackwell NVLink-SHARP does 7.2 TB/s in-domain allreduce, 4× Hopper, and IB SHARPv4 on Quantum-X800 adds 14.4 TFLOPS of in-switch compute (NVIDIA SHARP). The architectural point for an Ethernet scale-out build: you lose IB-SHARP on the scale-out plane (you chose Ethernet), but you keep NVLink-SHARP inside each NVL72, which is where most of your high-bandwidth allreduce traffic should be confined anyway via TP-within-rack placement. Run the gradient allreduce hierarchically — NVLink-SHARP within rack, ring/tree allreduce across racks over MRC — and overlap it with the EP all-to-all of the next micro-batch.

07The MoE all-to-all bottleneck: quantifying it on this fabric

Now the central problem. Take DeepSeek-V3-class routing as the template: hidden dimension 7,168, 256 routed experts, top-8, FP8 dispatch / BF16 combine (DeepSeek-V3).

Volume math. Per token per MoE layer, dispatch moves the top-8 copies of the 7,168-dim activation outbound, and combine returns them inbound. The asymmetry is structural: dispatch in FP8 is 8 × 7,168 × 1 ≈ 57 KB up, while combine in BF16 is 8 × 7,168 × 2 ≈ 115 KB down. The general formula is 2 · top_k · d_model · bytes bidirectional per token per layer; FP8 dispatch halves the up leg. This is exactly why DeepEP reports higher combine bandwidth ceilings than dispatch — the combine leg moves twice the bytes. For a micro-batch of 8,192 tokens per EP-rank, dispatch+combine is ~1.4 GB of cross-fabric traffic per layer per rank, multiplied across ~58 MoE layers.

comms AllToAll bytes / token / layer = 2 · topk · dmodel · bytes Doubled for dispatch + combine. With topk=8, dmodel=7168: FP8 dispatch ≈ 57 KB up, BF16 combine ≈ 115 KB down. At 8,192 tokens/rank that is ~1.4 GB cross-fabric per layer per rank. The term that gates MoE MFU.

What the fabric actually delivers. This is where the dossier numbers turn from comforting to sobering. DeepEP's published internode RDMA throughput (H800 / CX-7, 50-node) is only 43–58 GB/s per GPU on dispatch/combine — i.e. the all-to-all sustains ~50–60% of the 400G NIC line rate, and that ceiling is set by spray efficiency and expert-skew, not raw link rate (DeepEP legacy docs). Worse, per-token internode bandwidth falls as expert count grows, because each token fans out to more remote NVLink domains: the LL pure-RDMA path goes from 77 µs / 98 GB/s at 8 experts to 194 µs / 39 GB/s at 256 experts, with combine latency 1.5–1.8× the dispatch latency. The normal-kernel internode RDMA peaks around 58 GB/s at EP32 and sags to ~50 GB/s at EP64.

On 800G ConnectX-8 with MRC spraying 256 paths you roughly double the absolute number — a ~90–110 GB/s/GPU envelope is plausible, consistent with the LL-kernel's 98–127 GB/s NVLink-assisted figures and DeepEP-V2's measured ~90 GB/s RDMA at EP8×2. But the fraction of line rate stays stubbornly at ~50–60%, because the bottleneck migrates from link bandwidth to all-to-all incast and load imbalance: many senders hit one receiver simultaneously (incast), and a hot expert receives disproportionate traffic (skew). Doubling the link rate does not double the effective all-to-all throughput when the limiter is the receiver's incast queue and the slowest (hottest) expert's serialization.

Concretely, at 100 GB/s/GPU effective: the ~115 KB combine per token per layer is ~1.15 µs of serialized fabric time per token per layer if unoverlapped. That number, multiplied by tokens and layers, is catastrophic if exposed. Which is the entire point: the game is overlap, not bandwidth.

The fraction of line rate, not the absolute, is the planning number

Doubling 400G → 800G roughly doubles the absolute GB/s but leaves the all-to-all stuck at ~50–60% of line rate, because the limiter is incast + expert skew, not link bandwidth. Provision for incast and skew, not for line rate — and remember the real game is overlap, not bandwidth.

08How the all-to-all is actually hidden

Three mechanisms, layered, hide these microseconds under expert compute.

DualPipe scheduling (V3). Each forward/backward chunk is decomposed into four phases — attention, all-to-all dispatch, MLP, all-to-all combine — with backward further split into backward-for-input and backward-for-weights, and a hand-tuned schedule overlaps the all-to-all of one micro-batch with the GEMMs of another (DeepSeek-V3). The bubble count drops to (PP/2−1)(F&B+B−3W) versus 1F1B's (PP−1)(F+B). The cost is two resident parameter copies (the bidirectional schedule), which is cheap because EP already shards the experts. The comms side reserves 20 SMs across 10 warp-specialized channels with custom PTX and auto-tuned chunk sizes to limit L2 interference — that 20-SM tax is exactly what DeepEP-V2 (4–6 SMs) and the V4 mega-kernel are built to eliminate.

The fundamental overlap condition. DeepSeek-V4 frames it crisply: you need roughly 6,144 FLOPs/Byte for the MoE compute to fully hide the communication (DeepSeek-V4). The expert GEMM does ~6·h·d FLOPs per token-expert against ~3·h bytes of communication, so the ratio is set by the expert intermediate dimension d. If your experts are too narrow relative to your network, the comm cannot be hidden no matter how clever the schedule — which is an argument for wider, fewer experts when the network is the binding constraint, and a direct input to your expert-sizing decision.

compute ratio to beat = (6 · h · d) FLOPs / (3 · h) bytes ≈ 6144 FLOP/byte Stay above this and the expert GEMM hides the all-to-all under compute. The ratio is set by the expert intermediate dimension d — narrow experts cannot hide comms, an argument for wider, fewer experts when the network binds.

Device-initiated overlap (DeepEP hook + GIN). DeepEP's hook mechanism issues the RDMA and returns, letting the grouped-GEMM proceed; the transfer completes asynchronously and consumes zero SMs. GIN's device-initiated puts do the same inside NCCL. This is what buries the ~1 µs/token/layer fabric time under the expert grouped-GEMM.

The V4 endgame — a single fused mega-kernel. DeepSeek-V4 (arXiv 2606.19348) replaces the DualPipe + DeepEP two-piece overlap with a single fused MoE mega-kernel ("MegaMoE", open-sourced in DeepGEMM) that fuses EP-dispatch → first FP8×FP4 expert GEMM → SwiGLU+requant → second FP8×FP4 GEMM → EP-combine into one symmetric-memory kernel (DeepSeek-V4). The mechanism is expert "waves": the current wave computes on the tensor cores while the next wave's tokens transfer over NVLink and finished experts' results are sent back, all concurrently — finer-grained than the prior Comet-style overlap. On SM100 it uses three warp roles (dispatch / UMMA-non-epilogue / epilogue), Tensor Memory, UE8M0 2D-TMA scale descriptors, and a MegaMoEScheduler that prioritizes NVLink-active tiles (DeepGEMM MegaMoE). Measured speedup is 1.50–1.73× general, up to 1.96× latency-sensitive over the non-fused baseline. For a 100k GB200 build this is the target: the fused kernel makes the all-to-all genuinely invisible by interleaving transfer and MMA at wave granularity rather than at kernel-boundary granularity.

Ship the V4 MegaMoE fused kernel as the overlap target

MegaMoE deletes DualPipe's 20-SM overlap tax and the two-piece dispatch/GEMM/combine handoff in one move, for 1.5–1.96×, by interleaving NVLink transfer and tensor-core MMA at wave granularity rather than kernel-boundary granularity. This is what makes the all-to-all genuinely invisible on GB200.

LL decode budgets. For the inference/decode side, the LL-kernel dispatch latencies of 77–194 µs and combine of 114–314 µs (8 → 256 experts) are the budget you must hide behind expert compute. If your per-expert compute is smaller than this, decode is comms-bound and you need EPLB replication of hot experts plus larger batched decode to amortize.

09What MRC actually buys the all-to-all: tail suppression

A crucial clarification: MRC's contribution to the all-to-all is not throughput — it is tail-latency suppression. At 100k GPUs a synchronous training step waits on the slowest all-to-all transfer across the whole job. In a conventional fabric, a single link flap spikes the p99.9 of these transfers to seconds during routing reconvergence, and that one stall propagates to all 100k GPUs. MRC's static SRv6 + EV-spray design means one dead path out of 256 costs ~0.4% of paths and is dropped in microseconds, so the p99.9 tail no longer spikes to seconds on a single link event (arXiv 2605.04333). For a synchronous 100k-GPU step, tail-latency suppression is worth more than peak bandwidth, and that is precisely the problem MRC was built to solve. You provision bandwidth with 800G NICs and spraying; you provision step-time stability with MRC's failover semantics.

MRC buys p99.9 tail suppression, not peak bandwidth

A synchronous 100k-GPU step waits on the slowest transfer. MRC keeps a single link event from spiking the p99.9 to seconds — you provision bandwidth with 800G NICs and spraying, and step-time stability with MRC's microsecond EV-failover. At this scale the second matters more.

10Placement and routing co-design: keeping the RDMA leg small

The networking and MoE-systems layers are not separable; the routing config determines the fabric load. The levers, in priority order for a 100k GB200 build:

  • Node-limited (M=4) routing caps each token's internode fan-out so an expert group fits one NVL72 domain; the RDMA leg then carries only cross-group residue (DeepSeek-V3). On NVL72's 72-GPU domain you have more headroom than the 8-GPU HGX assumption, so co-locate whole expert groups (plus replicas) in-rack.
  • Auxiliary-loss-free bias balancing keeps expert load even so the all-to-all incast stays bounded: a per-expert bias added to top-K selection scores only, nudged by ±γ (γ=0.001 for the first 14.3T tokens, 0.0 for the final 500B in V3). Without this, expert skew destroys the ~50–60% line-rate efficiency further. Watch DeepEP issue #427: LL dispatch can silently corrupt under heavily imbalanced load — exactly the early-training regime before the balancer converges (DeepEP #427).
  • EPLB redundant-expert replication at deployment is essentially mandatory at 100k scale: replicate the hottest experts so incast spreads across replicas. The newer LPLB solves per-batch dynamic redistribution as an LP over the redundant-expert topology via a single-SM interior-point method in ~100 µs/batch, fed by live DeepEP buffer stats (LPLB) — the research-frontier online variant.
  • Dropless ragged buffers sized at the min(ep_degree, global_experts/top_k) worst-case bound — never worse than 256/8 = 32× for a V3-style config — or ragged_buffer_factor≈3 as the near-dropless memory-bounded compromise (MaxText moe.py). In MaxText this rides jax.lax.ragged_all_to_all with offsets/sizes from get_all_to_all_params, dispatching the grouped GEMM through ragged_dot / megablox / tokamax.
Step-0 hazard — DeepEP #427 imbalance corruption

LL dispatch can silently corrupt activations under heavily imbalanced expert load — exactly the first few thousand steps before your auxiliary-loss-free bias balancer converges. Run the high-throughput path early, warm up the balancer aggressively, or use hash-routed early layers (immune to #427) as a clean warm-up.

The MaxText EP all-to-all is the most legible open reference for the JAX path. The dispatch/combine is built not from a dense one-hot all-to-all but from jax.lax.ragged_all_to_all, which moves only the valid (variable-length) per-expert token runs:

jax — MaxText EP dispatch/combine (illustrative; grounds in src/maxtext/layers/moe.py)
# per-shard group sizes -> ragged all-to-all offset/size metadata
def get_all_to_all_params(group_sizes, shard_id, ep_degree):
    base = group_sizes.reshape(ep_degree, -1)
    send_sizes = base[shard_id]            # rows we ship out
    recv_sizes = base[:, shard_id]         # rows we receive
    in_off  = jnp.concatenate([z, jnp.cumsum(send_sizes)[:-1]])
    out_off = jnp.concatenate([z, jnp.cumsum(recv_sizes)[:-1]])
    return in_off, send_sizes, out_off, recv_sizes

# dispatch leg: move only valid per-expert runs (no dense BSEC mask)
x_sorted, sort_idx, group_sizes = permute(x, top_k_indices)     # custom-VJP sort
p = get_all_to_all_params(group_sizes, shard_id, ep_degree)
x_disp = jax.lax.ragged_all_to_all(x_sorted, buffer, *p)        # NVLink + RDMA legs
y_local = gmm(local_permute(x_disp), w_experts, local_group_sizes)  # grouped GEMM
y_disp  = jax.lax.ragged_all_to_all(y_local, buffer, *p_rev)    # combine leg
y = unpermute(y_disp, sort_idx, combine_weights)               # apply un-biased gate

The unifying principle: every routing decision is a fabric-load decision. The network team and the model team must co-design, because a routing change that doubles internode fan-out doubles the RDMA leg, and a 50% line-rate fabric has no headroom to absorb that.

11Quantified recommendation for the 10T / 200B / 100k-GB200 run

Putting it together for the target system:

scale-up
NVL72 / NVLink5
1.8 TB/s/GPU, 130 TB/s/rack all-to-all. Confine TP + intra-group EP in-rack; NVLink-SHARP (7.2 TB/s) for in-rack gradient reduction.
scale-out
MRC over Spectrum-X / Tomahawk-5
~770 Gb/s/NIC, 57.6 Tb/s/rack, disjoint Acceleron-style multi-plane. Not single-plane InfiniBand.
collectives
DeepEP-V2 / hybrid-ep + MegaMoE
Fused dispatch-GEMM-combine, riding NCCL 2.28 GIN for elasticity; SHARP for in-rack allreduce.
  • Scale-up: NVL72 / NVLink5, 1.8 TB/s/GPU, 130 TB/s/rack aggregate all-to-all. Confine TP and intra-expert-group EP entirely inside the rack. Use NVLink-SHARP (7.2 TB/s in-domain allreduce) for in-rack gradient reduction.
  • Scale-out: 800G spray fabric — MRC over Spectrum-X / Tomahawk-5 on disjoint Acceleron-style multi-plane fabric, ~770 Gb/s/NIC achievable, 57.6 Tb/s/rack scale-out. Not single-plane InfiniBand. Two-tier multi-plane Clos, 3-switch longest path, scales past your 100k requirement toward the 131,072-GPU envelope.
  • Bandwidth budget: ~1.4 GB cross-fabric per layer per rank for a V3-class config at 8,192 tokens/rank; effective ~90–110 GB/s/GPU on the RDMA leg (~50–60% of 800G); the fraction of line rate, not the absolute, is the planning number — provision for incast and skew, not for line rate.
  • Collectives: DeepEP-V2 / hybrid-ep + DeepGEMM MegaMoE for the fused dispatch-GEMM-combine path, riding NCCL 2.28 GIN for elasticity and fault tolerance. SHARP for allreduce offload in-rack.
  • Overlap target: hit ≥6,144 FLOPs/Byte so the all-to-all hides fully; size experts wide enough that the expert GEMM dominates the dispatch/combine cost. The V4 fused mega-kernel's wave-level interleave (1.5–1.96× over non-fused) is the mechanism.
  • Reliability: MRC's microsecond EV-failover is the load-bearing property — at 100k GPUs you are buying p99.9 tail suppression, not peak bandwidth. Provision disjoint planes so plane-level failures also fail over instantly.
  • Forward bet: architect collective abstractions so a future photonic scale-up domain (PCCL-style per-collective-round reconfiguration, 7.5× all-to-all) drops in. That, not MRC, is where the all-to-all-vs-allreduce topology conflict ultimately dissolves.
The summary judgment

MRC makes the packet fabric survivable; DeepEP/GIN/MegaMoE make the all-to-all invisible; NVL72 keeps the high-fanout hop on copper at 18× the scale-out bandwidth; and the routing config keeps the RDMA leg small enough that ~50% line-rate efficiency is still enough. None of these individually solves the all-to-all problem — collectively, they make a synchronous 200B-active MoE step across 100,000 GPUs run without stalling. The root-cause solution to the topology conflict is photonic and still a generation away.

Sources

  • OpenAI MRC + SRv6 paper (arXiv 2605.04333): arxiv.org/abs/2605.04333
  • OCP MRC 1.0 spec: opencompute.org/documents/ocp-mrc-1-0-pdf
  • NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 (dense vs sparse peak, NVLink5, scale-out bandwidth): nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/gb200-nvl72
  • NCCL 2.28 GPU-Initiated Networking (GIN), arXiv 2511.15076: arxiv.org/abs/2511.15076
  • DeepEP legacy performance docs (V1 LL/HT bandwidth curves): DeepEP/docs/legacy.md
  • DeepEP README (V2, ElasticBuffer, NCCL Gin, SM100): github.com/deepseek-ai/DeepEP
  • DeepEP issue #427 (LL imbalance correctness): DeepEP#427
  • DeepEP hybrid-ep branch (NVIDIA, TMA + warp pipelining, NIXL/UCX): DeepEP/tree/hybrid-ep
  • PCCL: Photonic circuit-switched collectives (arXiv 2509.15450): arxiv.org/abs/2509.15450
  • Oracle Acceleron Multiplanar Networking: blogs.oracle.com/cloud-infrastructure/acceleron-multiplanar-networking
  • OCP Optical Circuit Switching white paper (April 2026): opencompute.org/documents/ocp-ocs-white-paper-april-2026
  • NVIDIA SHARP in-network computing: developer.nvidia.com/blog/nvidia-sharp-in-network-computing
  • UEC 1.0 specifications: ultraethernet.org/uec-progresses-towards-v1-0
  • Tom Herbert, UET 1.0 review: medium.com/@tom_84912 — UET review
  • DeepSeek-V3 paper (arXiv 2412.19437): arxiv.org/pdf/2412.19437
  • DeepSeek-V4 paper (arXiv 2606.19348): arxiv.org/html/2606.19348v1
  • DeepGEMM MegaMoE kernel internals (warp roles, TMEM, SymmBuffer, scheduler): deepwiki.com/deepseek-ai/DeepGEMM
  • DeepSeek LPLB (online LP load balancer): github.com/deepseek-ai/LPLB
  • MaxText moe.py (sparse_matmul, ragged_all_to_all, get_all_to_all_params): maxtext/src/maxtext/layers/moe.py

Chapter 04 · Laying the model across 100k GPUs

Parallelism strategy for a 10T/200B-active MoE: the five axes and the fabric that decides everything

The parallelism plan is the single decision that most determines whether your 100,000-GB200 cluster runs at 40% MFU or 18%. It is not one choice but a composition of five-to-six orthogonal axes mapped onto a strictly hierarchical fabric — and getting the mapping wrong doesn't cost a few points, it halves your throughput.

~28 min read6 figuresgrounded in MaxText configs/base.yml + layers/pipeline.py

The parallelism plan is the single decision that most determines whether your 100,000-GB200 cluster runs at 40% MFU or 18%. It is not one choice but a composition of five-to-six orthogonal axes — data/FSDP, tensor (with sequence-parallel variant), pipeline, expert, and context — each of which must shard a different part of the model state, mapped onto a strictly hierarchical fabric where intra-rack NVLink runs at 1.8 TB/s/GPU and inter-rack InfiniBand at roughly one-twentieth of that. Get the mapping wrong — put a high-volume collective on the wrong tier of the fabric — and you do not lose a few points of MFU, you lose half your throughput. This chapter does the memory math that rules out the naive plans, derives why each axis exists, and proposes a concrete layout with numbers, then grounds it in the user's actual codebase (MaxText) ici_/dcn_ axes and pipeline.py.

The parallelism plan is not one choice but the disciplined assignment of each axis to the one fabric tier and one slice of model state it was built for: EP×TP on NVLink, PP on InfiniBand, DP/FSDP across islands, CP folded onto attention. That single composition rule is the entire layout — everything else is justification.
120TB
resident Muon state (vs 160 TB Adam)
19.2PB
aggregate HBM · state is 0.6–0.8%
~20:1
NVLink : InfiniBand bandwidth cliff
≤72
EP×TP must fit one NVL72 domain

01Memory math: the optimizer state, not the weights, dictates the layout

The first thing to internalize is that a 10T-parameter model is not a capacity problem on this cluster. It is a sharding-and-bandwidth problem dressed up as a capacity problem. Walk the per-parameter byte budget for the steady state.

For a Muon-primary optimizer — which is the right call in mid-2026, since DeepSeek-V4 and Kimi-K2 both adopted Muon as the main optimizer and keep AdamW only on embeddings, output heads, and RMSNorm parameters (Muon-state quantization paper) — the resident per-parameter footprint is:

ComponentBytes/param (Muon)Bytes/param (AdamW)
Compute weights (bf16)22
Master weights (fp32)44
Gradient (bf16)22
Optimizer state4 (one fp32 momentum)8 (two fp32 moments m,v)
Total~12 B~16 B
 Muon's single momentum buffer halves the dominant optimizer-state term against AdamW's two moments.

For 10T parameters that is 120 TB (Muon) to 160 TB (Adam) of resident, non-replication-shardable state, plus an FP8 (E4M3) compute copy of the weights — the V3/V4 training format — adding 10 TB. The bf16 weights alone are 20 TB; in FP8, 10 TB. The decisive observation: the optimizer-and-master state is 60–75% of the resident footprint, and Muon's single momentum buffer (4 B/param vs AdamW's 8 B) is the cheapest 40-TB saving available — it halves the dominant term. The Scaling Book's own capacity note generalizes here: pure data parallelism with Adam's 10 bytes/param caps a model at ~9B parameters on a 96 GB chip (JAX Scaling Book, training chapter). The 10T model holds 1,100× that.

mem resident bytes ≈ N · (2bf16 + 4fp32 master + 2grad + opt)  →  12 B (Muon) · 10T = 120 TB opt = 4 B (Muon, one momentum) or 8 B (Adam, m+v). Plus a 10 TB FP8 compute copy. None of this is replication-shardable — it must be split across devices.

Now the aggregate picture. A GB200 carries 192 GB HBM3e per GPU at 8 TB/s (NVIDIA GB200 NVL72). 100,000 GPUs → ~19.2 PB aggregate HBM. The 120–160 TB of model+optimizer state is 0.6–0.8% of total HBM. There is no capacity wall. This is precisely why the problem is sharding and bandwidth: the binding constraints are (a) you must shard optimizer state so no device holds a meaningful fraction, (b) activation memory, not parameter memory, is what actually fills HBM per device, and (c) every collective must land on a fabric tier fast enough to hide under that layer's compute.

The one number to remember

The 120–160 TB of model+optimizer state is 0.6–0.8% of 19.2 PB aggregate HBM. There is no capacity wall — this is a sharding-and-bandwidth problem in disguise. The optimizer-and-master state is 60–75% of the resident footprint, so Muon's single momentum buffer is the cheapest 40-TB win on the table.

Activation memory is the real per-device pressure

Per the transformer accounting, a transformer block saves ~20 tensors/layer without rematerialization, which for a 4M-token batch at L=64, D=8192 is ~84 TB of activation memory (Scaling Book transformers). "Big-matmuls-only" remat saves ~7×; full-block remat saves only layer inputs but raises training FLOPs from 6Nθ to ~8Nθ (same) — a 33% compute tax you pay grudgingly and selectively. For a 10T MoE the activation footprint also carries the dispatched expert tokens (top_k copies of the routed activations) and the all-to-all staging buffers. This is why the layout must shard activations along sequence (SP-TP, CP) and pipeline-stage boundaries, not merely shard weights. A plan that shards 120 TB of optimizer state perfectly but leaves activations replicated will OOM on the first long-context microbatch.

Compute-time sanity check

Training FLOPs ≈ 6 × active-params × tokens (Scaling Book transformers). For 200B active over a 30T-token run: 6 · 2e11 · 3e13 = 3.6e25 FLOP. At 100,000 GB200, dense FP8 peak is ~4.5 PFLOP/s/GPU — note that NVIDIA's headline 720 PFLOP/s FP8 per NVL72 rack is the with-sparsity (2:4) figure; the page footnotes "Dense is one-half sparse spec shown," so dense rack FP8 is 360 PFLOP/s and the cluster dense FP8 peak is ~0.45 EFLOP/s, not ~1.0 (NVIDIA GB200 NVL72). At a realistic 40% MoE FP8 MFU: 1e5 · 4.5e15 · 0.40 = 1.8e20 FLOP/s → 3.6e25 / 1.8e20 ≈ 2.0e5 s ≈ 2.3 days of ideal compute, which in practice becomes weeks once all-to-all, pipeline bubble, checkpointing, and restart overheads land. The point of the parallelism plan is to keep that MFU near 40% rather than letting MoE communication drag it to 18%.

02Why pure FSDP (ZeRO-3) fails at this scale

FSDP/ZeRO-3 shards weights, gradients, and optimizer state across the DP axis, all-gathers the full weight shard just-in-time per layer in the forward pass, and reduce-scatters gradients in the backward. Its memory story is perfect — state ÷ X for FSDP degree X. Its communication story is what kills it at 100k.

The roofline is unambiguous: DP and FSDP are compute-bound only when per-device batch exceeds the network's arithmetic intensity C/W (Scaling Book training). On TPU v5p that threshold is ~2,550 tokens/chip over ICI and ~73,440 tokens/pod over the slower DCN. Recompute it for GB200: with NVLink W ≈ 1.8e12 B/s bidirectional and FP8 compute C ≈ 4.5e15 FLOP/s, C/W ≈ ~2,500 tokens/GPU — essentially the same regime as v5p (Scaling Book GPU chapter). FSDP's communication volume is batch-independent: it moves the full weight set every step regardless of how many tokens ride along. That is the fatal property. As you grow FSDP degree toward 100,000, the all-gather of the 10–20 TB weight set must complete inside each layer's compute window, and the per-device shard shrinks to tens of MB while the collective still moves the whole model.

Worse, FSDP at 100k crosses the fabric hierarchy. NVLink intranode is 1.8 TB/s/GPU; inter-rack RDMA is ~90 GB/s/GPU (DeepEP) — a ~20:1 cliff. A 100k-way all-gather necessarily spans thousands of NVL72 racks over InfiniBand, and its latency cannot hide behind one layer's compute. And FSDP cannot exploit MoE structure at all: it replicates the all-to-all-bound expert FFNs across the entire mesh and pays the weight-gather cost on all 10T parameters when only 200B are active per token. You would be all-gathering 50× more expert weight than any token touches.

FSDP's fatal property at 100k

FSDP communication volume is batch-independent — it moves the entire 10–20 TB weight set every step no matter how many tokens ride along. Pushed to 100k-way it spans thousands of racks over the 20:1 IB cliff and all-gathers 50× more expert weight than any token touches. The memory story is perfect; the comms story is fatal.

The fix is structural: confine FSDP/ZeRO to a fast island and offload the "shard the 10T" job to EP + PP. FSDP becomes the outer, elastic optimizer-sharding axis (ZeRO-1, gradients/optimizer only) across DP replicas — not the mechanism that shards the model.

03The five axes and what each must shard

Each axis exists because it shards a different state and rides a different fabric tier. The discipline is to assign each its natural job and no more.

EP — expert parallel
all-to-all dispatch/combine
Shards the bulk of 10T weights with no replication, no per-step weight all-gather. Lives on NVLink.
TP + SP-TP
model-dim + sequence shard
Attention/MLA/router/shared expert. Comm-bound past Y > F/(C/W) ≈ 8–16-way, NVLink only.
PP — pipeline
stage-boundary activations
Cheapest way to shard 10T across slow links — moves activations, not weights. Cost is the bubble.
CP — context parallel
ring/striped attention
Shards sequence + KV for 1M-token context. MLA/DSA compresses KV, so engage only at 64K→1M.
DP/FSDP + ZeRO-1
replicas + optimizer state
Elastic, fault-isolating outer axis. Gradient all-reduce is batch-amortized → tolerates slow top-of-fabric.
  • EP (expert parallel) — the load-bearing axis for a 10T MoE. Experts sit on disjoint device groups; routing becomes all-to-all dispatch → grouped-GEMM → all-to-all combine. EP shards the bulk of the 10T weights without replication and without per-step weight all-gather. All-to-all volume per layer is T·K·h·(EP−1)/EP (T local tokens, K top-k, h hidden) (Megatron-Core MoE). This is the only axis that natively matches MoE sparsity, and it must live on NVLink.
  • TP + sequence-parallel TP — shards attention and the dense parts (MLA up-projection, router, shared expert) along the model dimension; SP-TP additionally shards LayerNorm and activations along the sequence dimension to kill replicated-activation memory. TP becomes communication-bound past Y > F/(C/W), i.e. ~8–16 way in practice and only ever inside an NVLink domain (Scaling Book training). For a DeepSeek-class small expert F (~2,048), the GPU-chapter figure caps useful TP-on-experts at ~1–2 way (Scaling Book GPU chapter) — which is the whole argument for expert-sharding the FFN instead.
  • PP (pipeline) — partitions layers into stages; the cheapest way to shard 10T across slow links because it moves only stage-boundary activations (≈1.5·2BD/(W·L) per layer, negligible), not weights (Scaling Book training). The cost is the bubble.
  • CP (context parallel) — shards the sequence and KV for long context (V4 targets 1M tokens). Ring/striped attention. For an MLA/DSA model the KV is already compressed, so CP need only engage at the 64K→1M stages.
  • DP/FSDP outer + ZeRO-1 — replicates the (EP×TP×PP)-sharded unit and shards optimizer state across replicas. This is the elastic, fault-isolation axis. DP all-reduce of gradients is batch-amortized, so it tolerates the slow top-of-fabric.

Token-sharding vs expert-sharding: the central MoE choice

The FFN can be sharded two ways. Expert-sharding (EP) keeps each expert whole on a device and shuffles tokens to it (all-to-all dispatch/combine). Token-sharding (TP-on-experts) splits each expert's matrices and keeps tokens local (all-gather/reduce-scatter). DeepSeek and Megatron-Core both favor expert-sharding for the FFN because high TP "fragments small experts into inefficient shards" while low TP "underutilizes attention" (Megatron-Core MoE). The expert intermediate dim is small — 2,048 for DeepSeek-V3 (V3 report) — so a TP=8 split of a 2,048-wide GEMM leaves 256-wide tiles that under-fill the tensor cores. Expert-sharding keeps the GEMM full-width and pays only an all-to-all, which on NVLink is cheap (the all-to-all is one-quarter of an all-gather in the bandwidth-bound regime, since shards travel only part-way round the ring (Scaling Book sharding)).

Expert-shard the FFN — its F≈2,048 is too small to TP-split

A TP=8 split of a 2,048-wide expert GEMM leaves 256-wide tiles that starve the tensor cores. Keep each expert whole, shuffle tokens to it, and pay only an all-to-all — one-quarter the cost of an all-gather in the bandwidth-bound regime, and cheap on NVLink.

Megatron-Core's Parallel Folding resolves the attention-vs-expert TP conflict cleanly: it gives attention layers and MoE layers different TP/DP/EP meshes via a dual DeviceMesh — e.g. TP=8 with high CP on attention, TP=1 with high EP on experts — and does a zero-copy reshard at the boundary (Megatron-Core MoE). This is the production answer to "attention wants TP, experts want EP." MaxText expresses the same intent more coarsely through its separate tensor and expert logical axes plus the CP-as-EP custom_mesh_and_rule path (more in §07).

04Pipeline schedule: DualPipe vs zero-bubble vs 1F1B

With PP=16–32 stages on a 10T model, the bubble is real money. The schedules, ranked by sophistication:

ScheduleBubbleWeight copiesActivation stagesComm overlap
1F1B(PP−1)(F+B)1×PPnone
ZB-H1 (zero-bubble)~⅓ of 1F1B at equal peak mem1×PPpartial
ZB-H2≈0 (true zero bubble)1×higherpartial
DualPipe(PP/2−1)(F&B+B−3W)2×PP+1native
DualPipeVsame as DualPipe2×PP/2native
 Bubble, weight-duplication, activation-stage and overlap trade across the pipeline schedule zoo.

Zero-bubble (ZB paper) splits the backward into input-gradient (∂x) and weight-gradient (∂W) passes. ZB-H1 cuts the 1F1B bubble to ~1/3 at the same peak memory; ZB-H2 reaches a true zero bubble but demands more activation memory and enough microbatches to fill the schedule. Its virtue is no weight duplication; its weakness is that overlapping the EP all-to-all under compute is harder because the schedule wasn't designed around it.

DualPipe (DeepSeek DualPipe) is the V3 recipe: each forward and backward chunk is decomposed into four phases — attention, all-to-all dispatch, MLP, all-to-all combine — and a bidirectional schedule overlaps the all-to-all of one micro-batch with the GEMMs of another (V3 report). Bubble drops to (PP/2−1)(F&B+B−3W). The cost is 2× resident parameter copies (the bidirectional schedule), which at 10T is +20 TB resident — non-trivial but absorbable against 19 PB aggregate HBM, and DeepSeek argues it is cheap precisely because EP already shards experts. The communication side reserves 20 SMs across 10 warp-specialized channels with custom PTX (V3 report) — a tax that DeepEP-V2 and the V4 mega-kernel are explicitly trying to eliminate.

DualPipeV (DualPipe repo) is the under-discussed memory-saving variant: the "cut-in-half" V-shape keeps the same bubble with PP/2 devices and PP/2 activation stages (versus PP+1 for DualPipe), halving per-device activation memory. For a GB200 build where the EP all-to-all crosses NVL72 boundaries, DualPipe-family overlap is worth the weight duplication because it hides the InfiniBand and cross-rack traffic — DualPipeV is the practical choice.

Two adjuncts to hold in reserve

PipeOffload (arXiv 2503.01328) offloads activations to host memory, making deep PP at large microbatch counts memory-feasible without DualPipe's weight duplication — a real alternative if the +20 TB resident is unwelcome. And vocabulary parallelism (arXiv 2411.05288) addresses the timing imbalance the large embedding/output head injects into PP stages — material for a 10T model where the vocab projection (DV, counted once) is large enough to unbalance a zero-bubble or DualPipe schedule unless explicitly sharded.

05The fabric hierarchy and the composition rule

Treat the fabric as a strict three-tier hierarchy and assign each collective to exactly one tier:

TierBandwidthSpansCarry here
NVLink 5 (intra-NVL72)1.8 TB/s/GPU bidir, 130 TB/s rack bisection72 GPUsTP, EP all-to-all, SP-TP AG/RS
InfiniBand leaf/spine (intra-island)~90 GB/s/GPU RDMA (~800 Gb/s scale-out)racks in an islandPP stage-boundary activations
Top-of-fabric (inter-island)slowest tierislandsDP/FSDP gradient all-reduce (batch-amortized)
 One collective per fabric tier — the assignment that makes or breaks MFU.

The ~20:1 NVLink:IB cliff (DeepEP; NVIDIA GB200) is more extreme than the 4:1 cliff DeepSeek navigated on H800 (DeepSeek-V3 hardware retrospective), so the H800 lessons sharpen rather than soften: node-limited routing (cap fan-out), IB-then-NVLink forwarding to dedupe inter-node traffic, and overlapping IB with NVLink. The Scaling Book GPU chapter is blunt about the failure mode: cross-node all-to-all degrades to ~50 GB/s when it spans 2+ nodes (Scaling Book GPU chapter) — the single biggest GB200 MoE risk.

The composition rule that falls out:

EP × TP ≤ 72 (NVLink-resident). PP across racks within an island (InfiniBand). DP/FSDP across islands (top-of-fabric). CP folded into the attention mesh only.

Every O(activations·model) collective stays on NVLink; InfiniBand carries only O(activations) PP sends and batch-amortized DP all-reduce. This is non-negotiable — it is the difference between hiding the all-to-all and watching it serialize at 50 GB/s.

The single biggest way to lose half your MFU

Cross-node all-to-all degrades to ~50 GB/s once it spans 2+ nodes. Put TP, SP, or EP all-to-all over InfiniBand and you do not lose a few points — you serialize the dominant collective at one-twentieth of NVLink and watch your MFU collapse from 40% toward 18%.

06Proposed device layout for 100,000 GB200

100,000 GB200 ≈ 1,389 NVL72 racks. The concrete assignment:

Inside one NVL72 (72 GPUs): EP × TP

Use TP=8 for attention/MLA/shared-expert/router (the dense, model-dim-sharded parts), and EP up to 64 spanning the rest of the rack for the routed experts — both ride 1.8 TB/s NVLink, so the expert all-to-all and the attention AG/RS never touch InfiniBand. This mirrors DeepSeek's "256 experts over 8 H800 nodes, EP=64" (V3 report) but collapses it into a single rack, because the NVL72's 72-GPU NVLink domain is what 8 H800 nodes used to be — node egress jumps from ~400 GB/s (H100 8-GPU node) to ~3,600 GB/s (Scaling Book GPU chapter). For 10T/200B-active at ~6–8 experts/token, group experts so routing is rack-limited (the GB200 analog of DeepSeek's node-limited M=4 routing, where each token reaches ≤4 nodes chosen by per-group affinity sums (V3 report, hardware retrospective)): cap each token to ≤2–4 racks, send once over RDMA then fan out over NVLink to dedupe IB traffic. Keep an entire expert group inside one NVL72 domain so the RDMA leg carries only cross-group traffic.

The one architectural lever

The NVL72's 72-GPU flat NVLink domain is what 8 H800 nodes used to be — node egress jumps from ~400 GB/s to ~3,600 GB/s. Keep EP×TP ≤ 72 so the entire expert all-to-all and attention AG/RS ride NVLink and never touch InfiniBand. Everything else in the layout is downstream of this single decision.

A subtlety: TP=8 and EP=64 do not multiply to 72 on the same 72 GPUs — they are different meshes over the same rack (Parallel-Folding style). Attention runs TP=8 over 8-GPU NVLink subgroups with the experts collapsed; the MoE layer runs EP=64 with TP=1, resharding at the boundary. In MaxText's coarser model you instead set ici_tensor_parallelism=8 and ici_expert_parallelism=8 so ICI EP×TP = 64 ≤ 72, letting ici_fsdp_parallelism=-1 auto-fill the remainder of the rack (see §07).

Across racks within an island: PP

Put PP=16–32 stages across racks connected by one InfiniBand leaf/spine island. PP moves only stage-boundary activations over IB — the cheapest cross-rack traffic. Run DualPipeV so the EP all-to-all and PP sends overlap with compute; bubble ≈ (PP/2−1)(F&B+B−3W). With 61 decoder layers (DeepSeek-V3 scale) or more for a 10T model, PP=16–32 gives 2–4 layers/stage — comfortable for circular/interleaved scheduling.

Across islands: DP/FSDP outer + ZeRO-1

With EP·TP filling ~72 GPUs/rack and PP=16–32 racks/replica, one replica ≈ 1,150–2,300 GPUs; 100k GPUs → ~43–87 DP replicas. Shard optimizer state (ZeRO-1) across DP so each replica holds 1/N of the 120 TB Muon state (~1.4–2.8 TB/replica, trivially distributed within the replica's GPUs). DP all-reduce of gradients is the only full-model collective crossing the top of fabric, and it is batch-amortized — keep per-replica token batch above the DCN arithmetic-intensity threshold (the GB200 analog of ~73k tokens/replica (Scaling Book training)) to stay compute-bound. With ~43 replicas and a ~60M-token global batch you get ~1.4M tokens/replica — comfortably DP-safe.

CP for long context

Engage CP=2–8 only at the 64K→1M sequence stages, folded onto the attention mesh (Parallel-Folding style) so the MoE EP layout is untouched. MLA/DSA's compressed KV keeps CP all-gather volume low — and DSA's FP8 lightning indexer selecting top-2048 KV tokens per query reduces attention from O(L²) to O(Lk) (DeepSeek-V3.2 / DSA), so the CP traffic is on a compressed, sparse KV. This mirrors Llama-3 405B's 4D layout (TP=8, CP=16, PP=16, DP=4 on 16,384 H100 at 128K context (Llama-3)), but with EP added as the load-bearing fifth axis and the rack as the new "node."

Layout summary

AxisDegreeFabric tierShards
TP (+SP)8NVLink (intra-rack)attention/MLA, router, shared expert, activations along seq
EPup to 64NVLink (intra-rack)routed experts (bulk of 10T)
PP16–32InfiniBand (intra-island)layers across racks
DP/FSDP+ZeRO-1~43–87top-of-fabric (inter-island)replicas + optimizer state
CP2–8 (long-ctx)NVLink, folded on attentionsequence/KV
 The complete 5-axis layout for 10T/200B on 100k GB200.

07Mapping to MaxText ici_/dcn_ axes and pipeline.py

The user's actual codebase (MaxText) expresses this directly. Its logical mesh, from src/MaxText/configs/base.yml, is:

yaml — MaxText logical mesh axes (configs/base.yml)
mesh_axes: ['diloco', 'data', 'stage', 'fsdp', 'fsdp_transpose',
            'context', 'context_autoregressive', 'tensor',
            'tensor_transpose', 'tensor_sequence', 'expert', 'autoregressive']

with the hard ordering rule that DCN axes must appear earlier in the list than ICI axes, and the product of all ici_* degrees equals chips-per-slice (MaxText base.yml). On a GB200 build the mapping is ICI → NVLink rack domain, DCN → InfiniBand. The literal config:

src/MaxText/configs/base.ymlici_/dcn_ parallelismGB200 layout
# --- ICI = NVLink, inside one NVL72 rack ---
ici_tensor_parallelism: 8        # attention/MLA/shared-expert, NVLink   [1]
ici_expert_parallelism: 8        # ICI EP x TP = 64  <= 72/rack          [2]
ici_fsdp_parallelism: -1         # auto-fill the rack remainder          [3]
ici_pipeline_parallelism: 1
ici_context_parallelism: 1       # raise only for long-context phase

# --- DCN = InfiniBand, across racks/islands ---
dcn_pipeline_parallelism: 16     # PP across racks (the 'stage' axis)    [4]
dcn_data_parallelism: -1         # auto-shard DP across islands
dcn_expert_parallelism: 1        # never put EP all-to-all over IB       [5]
dcn_tensor_parallelism: 1        # 'never recommended' in base.yml
dcn_sequence_parallelism: 1      # 'never recommended'
dcn_tensor_sequence_parallelism: 1

1TP on NVLink. Tensor parallelism shards attention/MLA/router/shared-expert along the model dim — kept inside the rack so AG/RS never hits IB.

2EP×TP ≤ 72. ICI EP×TP = 8×8 = 64, comfortably under the 72-GPU NVL72 domain — the composition rule in literal form.

3Auto-fill. ici_fsdp_parallelism: -1 auto-shards the recommended ICI axis over the remainder of the rack.

4PP on the slow tier. The stage axis carries only stage-boundary activations over IB — the cheapest cross-rack traffic.

5Never EP/TP over DCN. The explicit # never recommended comments encode the ~50 GB/s cross-node all-to-all cliff.

This is the literal expression of "EP×TP on ICI, PP×DP on DCN." The base.yml defaults confirm the philosophy: ici_fsdp_parallelism: -1 (auto-shard the recommended ICI axis), dcn_data_parallelism: -1 (auto-shard the recommended DCN axis), and the explicit # never recommended comments on dcn_tensor_parallelism, dcn_sequence_parallelism, and dcn_tensor_sequence_parallelism (MaxText base.yml) — TP/SP over DCN is fatal precisely because of the ~50 GB/s cross-node all-to-all cliff. Note that EP is named via the expert axis (and TP via tensor, SP via tensor_sequence); the stage axis is pipeline. Because EP sits late in the mesh-axis list and PP (stage) sits early, the ordering rule naturally places PP on DCN and EP on ICI when you set the degrees as above.

Context-as-expert

MaxText exposes a CP_AS_EP path via custom_mesh_and_rule (utils/sharding.py) that folds context parallelism onto the expert mesh — useful when you want long-context CP to reuse the EP all-to-all machinery rather than carve out separate devices. For the long-context phase this is how you raise effective CP without disturbing the routed-expert layout.

Pipeline

src/MaxText/layers/pipeline.py implements a circular pipeline. The number of decoder layers equals num_stages × num_layers_per_pipeline_stage × num_pipeline_repeats. The relevant knobs from base.yml:

  • num_layers_per_pipeline_stage: 1 (default) — more layers/stage means easier comm overlap but fewer repeats;
  • num_pipeline_repeats: -1 — auto-set to num_decoder_layers / (num_pipeline_stages × num_layers_per_pipeline_stage);
  • num_pipeline_microbatches: -1 — auto-set to the number of stages; must be a multiple of the stage count; more microbatches → smaller bubble;
  • pipeline_delay_activation_forwarding: false — when true, delays activation forwarding one loop iteration to simplify XLA's overlap of the circular (last-stage→first-stage) transfer, doing it asynchronously.
data bubble frac = (num_stages − 1) / (num_repeats · num_microbatches + num_stages − 1) The circular-schedule arithmetic MaxText documents — the reason to push microbatch count up. Same family as 1F1B, not DualPipeV.

The bubble fraction MaxText documents is (num_stages − 1) / (num_pipeline_repeats × num_pipeline_microbatches + num_stages − 1) (MaxText base.yml) — the same circular-schedule arithmetic that motivates pushing microbatch count up. MaxText's circular pipeline is a 1F1B-family schedule, not DualPipeV; if you want native all-to-all/PP comm-compute overlap you either lean on pipeline_delay_activation_forwarding plus XLA's overlap, or you port a DualPipe-style bidirectional schedule. For the GB200 target, the pragmatic path inside MaxText is: maximize num_pipeline_microbatches, enable delayed activation forwarding, and rely on the EP all-to-all (jax.lax.ragged_all_to_all) overlapping with grouped-GEMM at the kernel level rather than at the schedule level.

Where the MoE sharding actually happens

MaxText's sparse_matmul path in layers/moe.py is the EP-sharded, dropless realization. EP cross-device routing uses jax.lax.ragged_all_to_all with offsets/sizes from get_all_to_all_params; the grouped GEMM dispatches across jax.lax.ragged_dot, the forked megablox mblx.gmm, or tokamax.ragged_dot. The dropless ragged buffer is sized at min(ep_degree, global_experts/top_k) × balanced_size for worst-case no-drop — never worse than 256/8 = 32× for a DeepSeek-style config, and once EP > 32 the bound is global_experts/top_k independent of EP (MaxText moe.py). The base.yml defaults are megablox: true, sparse_matmul: true, capacity_factor: -1.0 (dropless), ragged_buffer_factor: -1.0 (worst-case dropless buffer) (base.yml).

moe ragged buffer = min( |EP| , global_exp / topk ) × balanced_size  →  ≤ 256/8 = 32× Once |EP| > global_exp/topk the bound is global_exp/topk, independent of EP. Set ragged_buffer_factor ≈ 3.0 to cap activation memory at a near-dropless compromise.

At EP in the dozens-to-hundreds, set ragged_buffer_factor ≈ 3.0 to cap activation memory at a near-dropless compromise rather than provisioning the full 32× worst case, and enable the use_ring_of_experts / use_ragged_sort Pallas kernels (a2a_ragged_sort, ring_ragged_sort) which gather only the valid prefix instead of the padded buffer — the optimization that matters once EP is large and padded sort becomes pure waste.

Set ragged_buffer_factor ≈ 3.0 and enable ragged-sort

The 32× worst-case dropless buffer is far larger than real imbalance ever is. Set ragged_buffer_factor ≈ 3.0 for a near-dropless, memory-bounded compromise, and turn on the use_ragged_sort Pallas kernels so you sort only the valid prefix — the optimization that pays off once EP is large and the padded buffer dwarfs the real token count.

08Communication kernels and the FP8 dispatch path

The all-to-all is make-or-break, and the kernel substrate is where the layout's promises get cashed. DeepEP gives the measured numbers on Blackwell (SM100): NVLink dispatch/combine of 726/740 GB/s at EP8 (max-perf, 64 SMs) and 643/675 GB/s at the minimal-SM config; SM90/CX7 RDMA is 90/81 GB/s dispatch/combine (DeepEP). The ~20:1 NVLink:RDMA ratio in these numbers is exactly why EP must stay on NVLink. DeepEP supports FP8 dispatch with BF16 combine, halving the dispatch bytes, and the V2 rewrite drops SM consumption from 24 to 4–6 SMs while unifying the high-throughput and low-latency kernels behind a single ElasticBuffer and an NCCL "Gin" backend (DeepEP). Track the NVIDIA-authored hybrid-ep branch for GB200 — it uses TMA copies and warp-level pipelining to minimize SM occupancy while saturating both NVLink and RDMA (verify SM100 support before committing).

726GB/s
DeepEP SM100 NVLink dispatch, EP8
24→4–6
SM cost, V1→V2 low-latency path
~115KB
all-to-all / token / layer (BF16)
6144F/B
FLOPs/byte to hide MoE comm

Per-MoE-layer dispatch moves top_k × tokens activation rows of width d_model out and combine brings them back: bidirectional volume ≈ 2 · top_k · d_model · bytes per token, ~115 KB/token/layer in BF16 at top-8, d_model≈7168, dropping to ~86 KB with FP8 dispatch. The decisive ratio is ~6,144 FLOPs/Byte to fully hide MoE comm under compute (DeepSeek-V4) — design the per-GPU token batch so the expert GEMM FLOPs clear that bar.

comms AllToAll bytes / token / layer = 2 · topk · dmodel · bytes  →  ratio to beat ≈ 6144 FLOP/byte ≈115 KB at BF16 (top-8, d_model≈7168), ≈86 KB with FP8 dispatch. Stay above 6144 FLOP/byte and the expert GEMM hides the all-to-all under compute.

The endgame is the DeepGEMM MegaMoE fused kernel that V4 introduced: it fuses EP-dispatch → first FP8×FP4 expert GEMM → SwiGLU(+requant) → second GEMM → EP-combine into a single symmetric-memory kernel, overlapping NVLink transfers with tensor-core MMA via expert "waves," for 1.50–1.73× general / up to 1.96× latency-sensitive speedup over the non-fused baseline (DeepSeek-V4). Caveat: FP4×FP8 currently runs at FP8×FP8 speed on existing silicon — the FP4 expert weights are a memory/footprint win on GB200 today, not yet a compute win (V4). Megatron-Core's production realization (Flex dispatcher with DeepEP/HybridEP, TEGroupedMLP grouped-GEMM, permutation and router/aux-loss fusion) reports 1,233 TFLOPS/GPU on GB200 for DeepSeek-V3-685B (vs 1,048 on H100) and 974 (GB200) for Qwen3-235B (Megatron-Core MoE) — the empirical MFU envelope to design against. In JAX/MaxText, the equivalent stack is sparse_matmul + ragged_all_to_all + tokamax/megablox ragged_dot.

Two systems landmines at this scale

DeepEP's low-latency RDMA path floors at ~39 GB/s once you exceed ~256 experts (each token fans out to more remote domains), and DeepEP issue #427 reports low-latency dispatch can corrupt under heavily imbalanced expert load — precisely the early-training regime before the auxiliary-loss-free bias balancer (V3's ±γ=0.001 schedule, MaxText's routed_bias_update_rate) has converged (DeepEP; V3 report). FP4 caveat stated in V4

09Reliability forces DP to be the outer, elastic axis

The last constraint is not a roofline, it is the MTTF. At ~131k GPUs a training job's projected MTTF is ~14 minutes; SDC affects ~1/1000 machines with corruption events every 1–2 weeks; at 100k accelerators the checkpoint cadence approaches ~967/day (~every 1.5 min) (reliability analysis). GB200 shifts the unit of repair from server to rack. This is the systems reason DP/FSDP must be the outer axis: it is the only axis you can elastically resize — drop a failed replica, reform the mesh — without re-laying-out the EP/TP/PP sharded unit. The proposed layout deliberately confines a failure's blast radius to one PP-stage rack inside one DP replica.

~14min
job MTTF at ~131k GPUs
~967/day
checkpoint cadence (~every 1.5 min)
1/1000SDC
machines, corruption every 1–2 wk

Pair this with asynchronous/in-memory checkpointing. MaxText's common/checkpointing.py provides exactly the machinery: Orbax CheckpointManager, the EmergencyCheckpointManager and EmergencyReplicatorCheckpointManager (async/in-memory, replica-redundant copies), OCDBT with 2 GB target file size and 96 GB concurrent writes, and enable_autocheckpoint on preemption. ByteDance's infrastructure work (arXiv 2509.16293) documents the operational pattern — fast failure detection, in-memory checkpoint, elastic reconfiguration — that makes a DP-outer layout survivable at 10k+ GPUs and necessary at 100k.

Why DP must be the outermost axis

DP/FSDP is the only axis you can elastically resize — drop a failed replica, reform the mesh — without re-laying-out the EP/TP/PP sharded unit. At a ~14-minute MTTF you will exercise this constantly, so the layout deliberately confines a failure's blast radius to one PP-stage rack inside one DP replica.

10The opinionated bottom line

For 10T/200B on 100,000 GB200:

  1. Muon as primary optimizer (AdamW only on embeddings/heads/norms) to hold the resident state at 120 TB rather than 160 TB — the optimizer state is the binding memory term, not the weights.
  2. EP is the load-bearing axis. Shard the 10T weights with expert-parallelism, not FSDP. Keep EP×TP ≤ 72, entirely on NVLink.
  3. TP=8 for attention, EP up to 64 for experts, as separate Parallel-Folding meshes over the same rack; expert-shard the FFN (its F≈2,048 is too small to TP-split).
  4. PP=16–32 across racks on InfiniBand, scheduled with DualPipeV (or MaxText's circular pipeline with maximized microbatches + delayed activation forwarding) to overlap the all-to-all under compute.
  5. DP ~43–87 replicas across islands with ZeRO-1, as the elastic, fault-isolated outer axis — the only one you resize on failure.
  6. CP=2–8 folded onto attention only at the long-context (64K→1M) stages, riding MLA/DSA's compressed sparse KV.
  7. Never put TP, SP, or EP all-to-all over InfiniBand. The ~50 GB/s cross-node all-to-all cliff is the single biggest way to lose half your MFU.

In MaxText terms: ici_tensor_parallelism=8, ici_expert_parallelism=8, ici_fsdp_parallelism=-1, dcn_pipeline_parallelism=16-32, dcn_data_parallelism=-1, everything else dcn_*=1. That single config block is the entire layout, and the rest of this manual is the justification for why each number is what it is.

The whole layout in one config block

ici_tensor_parallelism=8 · ici_expert_parallelism=8 · ici_fsdp_parallelism=-1 · dcn_pipeline_parallelism=16–32 · dcn_data_parallelism=-1 · dcn_*=1. EP×TP on NVLink, PP×DP on InfiniBand and top-of-fabric, CP folded onto attention only at long context. Everything else in this chapter is the justification for those numbers.

Sources

  • DeepSeek-V3 hardware co-design retrospective (NVLink:IB cliff, IB-then-NVLink forwarding): arxiv.org/html/2505.09343v1
  • DualPipe / DualPipeV repository (bubble/memory comparison): github.com/deepseek-ai/DualPipe
  • Zero Bubble pipeline (ZB-H1/ZB-H2): arxiv.org/abs/2401.10241
  • PipeOffload (activation offloading for deep PP): arxiv.org/html/2503.01328v1
  • Vocabulary parallelism for balanced PP: arxiv.org/pdf/2411.05288
