  • Megatron-Core MoE / Parallel Folding / GB200 TFLOPS: arxiv.org/abs/2603.07685
  • JAX Scaling Book — training rooflines and parallelism: jax-ml.github.io/scaling-book/training
  • JAX Scaling Book — transformer math (FLOPs, params, activations, MoE deltas): jax-ml.github.io/scaling-book/transformers
  • JAX Scaling Book — sharding and collective costs: jax-ml.github.io/scaling-book/sharding
  • JAX Scaling Book — GPU chapter (NVL72 rooflines, cross-node all-to-all cliff): jax-ml.github.io/scaling-book/gpus
  • NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 (HBM, NVLink, dense vs sparse FLOPS): nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/gb200-nvl72
  • Muon optimizer state quantization (4 B/param momentum): arxiv.org/html/2509.23106v2
  • DeepSeek-V4 (MegaMoE fused kernel, FP8×FP4, Sqrt(Softplus) routing): arxiv.org/html/2606.19348v1
  • DeepSeek-V3.2 / DSA sparse attention: arxiv.org/abs/2512.02556
  • Llama-3 405B 4D parallelism reference: arxiv.org/pdf/2407.21783
  • Reliability at ~131k GPUs (MTTF, checkpoint cadence): arxiv.org/html/2502.12340v1
  • ByteDance robust LLM training infrastructure: arxiv.org/pdf/2509.16293

  • MaxText moe.py (ragged_all_to_all, ragged buffer sizing): maxtext/src/maxtext/layers/moe.py

Chapter 05 · The MoE layer is the run

MoE systems engineering: expert parallelism, all-to-all, and the fused mega-kernel

At a 10T-total / 200B-active scale spread across 100,000 GB200 GPUs, attention is a fixed cost — the MoE FFN is where the run lives or dies. This is the systems field manual for that layer: the all-to-all substrate, the overlap schedule, the routing and balancing machinery, and the grouped-GEMM kernel zoo.

~26 min read6 figuresgrounded in MaxText src/maxtext/layers/moe.py

The MoE layer is where a 10T-total/200B-active run lives or dies. Attention is a fixed, well-understood cost; the FFN, sharded into experts and scattered across 100,000 GB200 GPUs, is where you pay the all-to-all tax, where load imbalance silently wastes a third of your FLOPs, and where the difference between a naive dense-dispatch kernel and a fused symmetric-memory mega-kernel is a 1.5–1.96× throughput swing on the critical path (DeepSeek-V4). This chapter is the systems field manual for that layer: the communication substrate (DeepEP and its V2/hybrid-ep rewrites), the compute/comm overlap schedule (DualPipe and its successor the fused MegaMoE kernel), the routing and balancing machinery (node-limited gating, auxiliary-loss-free bias, EPLB/LPLB), the grouped-GEMM kernel zoo (MegaBlocks dropless, CUTLASS, Triton, DeepGEMM, Tokamax ragged_dot), and the dropless-vs-capacity tradeoff — grounded throughout in the user's actual codebase (MaxText src/maxtext/layers/moe.py), which implements every one of these primitives in JAX/Pallas and is the most legible open reference for the JAX/XLA path.

On a flat NVL72 domain with DeepEP V2 and MegaMoE the overlap acrobatics shrink — but the architectural bets (sparser routing, node-limiting, dropless ragged buffers, FP8/FP4 dispatch, bias balancing) transfer directly and are the load-bearing decisions for your run.
256exp
routed experts, top-8 (V3-shaped)
115KB
all-to-all / token / layer (BF16)
6144F/B
FLOPs/byte you must beat to hide comms
1.96×
MegaMoE speedup, latency-sensitive

The governing arithmetic, established up front so the rest of the chapter can refer back to it: for a DeepSeek-V3-shaped config (256 routed experts, top-8, d_model≈7168, expert intermediate ≈2048), dispatch moves top_k · tokens activation rows of width d_model to the experts and combine brings them back. Per token per MoE layer that is 2 · top_k · d_model · bytes of bidirectional all-to-all traffic — roughly 115 KB/token/layer in BF16, ~86 KB with FP8 dispatch + BF16 combine (DeepEP). The expert GEMM does 6 · h · d FLOPs per token-expert against 3 · h bytes of weight traffic, so the ratio you must beat to hide communication under compute is ≈6144 FLOPs/Byte (DeepSeek-V4). Everything below — node-limiting, FP8 dispatch, kernel fusion, overlap scheduling — is in service of staying above that line at EP spanning hundreds of NVL72 domains.

comms AllToAll bytes / token / layer = 2 · topk · dmodel · bytes Doubled for dispatch + combine. With topk=8, dmodel=7168: ≈115 KB at BF16 (2 B), ≈86 KB with FP8 dispatch + BF16 combine. The term that gates MoE MFU.

01Expert parallelism and the all-to-all volume problem

At your scale, EP is not optional and it is not small. A 256-expert layer with experts large enough to matter cannot be replicated; it must be sharded, and the shard count (the EP degree) climbs into the hundreds. The MaxText mesh exposes expert as a first-class parallelism axis alongside fsdp, tensor, sequence, pipeline, and context (in utils/sharding.py and configs/base.yml's ici_*/dcn_* axis lists), and the logical-axis rules route expert-sharded tensors onto it. The decisive systems fact is that EP turns the FFN into an all-to-all-bound operation, and the all-to-all volume is fixed by the model (2·top_k·d_model·bytes/token/layer) while the bandwidth available per token degrades as EP grows, because each token's top_k experts land on more distinct devices and more distinct NVLink domains.

DeepEP's own measurements make this concrete and they are the load-bearing numbers for capacity planning. The V1 low-latency (pure-RDMA, IBGDA) path does dispatch at 77µs / 98 GB/s at 8 experts, degrading monotonically to 194µs / 39 GB/s at 256 experts; combine runs 1.5–1.8× the dispatch latency (DeepEP legacy). The internode "normal" (high-throughput) RDMA path peaks near 58 GB/s at EP32 and sags to ~50 GB/s at EP64. The lesson is brutal and counterintuitive: per-token RDMA bandwidth falls as expert count rises, and the low-latency floor of 39 GB/s is well under a single CX7's ~50 GB/s line rate once you exceed ~128 experts. You do not get to assume your NICs run at line rate; the EP fan-out structurally prevents it.

This is the entire motivation for node-limited (group-limited) routing, covered in §04, and for the asymmetric-domain forwarding that DeepEP's high-throughput kernels implement: NVLink→RDMA→NVLink, where intra-NVL72 traffic rides the 130 TB/s flat NVLink fabric and only the genuinely cross-domain fraction of top_k touches the NIC. At EP=N spanning many NVL72 domains, per-GPU RDMA demand scales with the internode share of top_k, not all of it (DeepEP). On GB200 NVL72 you get a 72-GPU flat NVLink domain, which is the single biggest architectural lever you have: keep an expert group inside one NVL72 and the RDMA leg carries only cross-group traffic.

The one lever to remember

The GB200 NVL72 72-GPU flat NVLink domain is your biggest architectural lever. Keep an expert group inside one NVL72 and the RDMA leg carries only cross-group traffic — everything else in this chapter is downstream of that decision.

MaxText's EP all-to-all, in code

MaxText implements EP dispatch/combine not with a dense one-hot all-to-all but with jax.lax.ragged_all_to_all, which moves only the valid (variable-length) per-expert token runs. The flow in sparse_matmul is: get_topk → permute (argsort by flattened selected experts, then _sort_activations with a custom-VJP gather) → get_all_to_all_params (computes input_offsets/send_sizes/output_offsets/recv_sizes from per-shard group sizes via cumsum for the batch-sharded case) → ragged_all_to_all into a sized buffer → local_permute to regroup by local expert → grouped GEMM → ragged_all_to_all back → unpermute. The permute/unpermute/local_permute trio plus get_all_to_all_params is the EP communication layer, and reading it is the fastest way to internalize what dispatch/combine actually does at the tensor level. The custom-VJP sort (use_custom_sort_vjp=true, default) matters for throughput: its backward is itself a sort by argsort(sort_indices), avoiding a materialized scatter.

src/maxtext/layers/moe.pyRoutedMoE.sparse_matmulEP dispatch/combinetrimmed
def get_all_to_all_params(group_sizes, shard_id, num_expert_parallelism):  # [1]
    # per-shard group sizes -> ragged all-to-all offset/size metadata
    base = group_sizes.reshape(num_expert_parallelism, -1)
    send_sizes   = base[shard_id]                         # rows we ship out
    recv_sizes   = base[:, shard_id]                      # rows we receive
    input_offsets  = jnp.concatenate([z, jnp.cumsum(send_sizes)[:-1]])
    output_offsets = jnp.concatenate([z, jnp.cumsum(recv_sizes)[:-1]])  # [2]
    return input_offsets, send_sizes, output_offsets, recv_sizes

# inside sparse_matmul: dispatch leg
x_sorted, sort_idx, group_sizes = permute(x, top_k_indices)   # [3]
p = get_all_to_all_params(group_sizes, shard_id, ep_degree)
x_disp = jax.lax.ragged_all_to_all(x_sorted, buffer, *p)      # [4]
x_local = local_permute(x_disp, ...)                          # regroup by local expert
y_local = gmm(x_local, w_experts, local_group_sizes)          # grouped GEMM
y_disp  = jax.lax.ragged_all_to_all(y_local, buffer, *p_rev)  # combine leg
y = unpermute(y_disp, sort_idx, combine_weights)              # [5]

1Offsets, not one-hot. The metadata is computed from per-shard group sizes — no dense BSEC dispatch mask is ever materialized.

2Cumsum bookkeeping. input_offsets/output_offsets are exclusive cumsums so each expert's variable-length run lands contiguously in the receive buffer.

3Custom-VJP sort. permute argsorts by flattened expert id; its backward is a sort by argsort(sort_idx), avoiding a materialized scatter.

4Ragged, not dense. ragged_all_to_all moves only valid per-expert runs into a sized buffer — the volume the §05 ragged-buffer math bounds.

5Symmetric return. Combine is the same primitive reversed; unpermute scatters expert outputs back and applies the un-biased gating weights.

For the EP-in-the-hundreds regime MaxText adds ring-of-experts (use_ring_of_experts) with custom Pallas ragged-sort kernels (kernels/ragged/ragged_sort: a2a_ragged_sort, ring_ragged_sort, ring_ragged_unsort, gated by use_ragged_sort). The point of these is precise: a naive sort over the padded ragged buffer is pure waste when the buffer is sized for worst-case imbalance and the true occupancy is a small prefix. The ragged-sort kernels gather only the valid prefix, which is exactly the optimization that pays off when EP is large and the worst-case buffer dwarfs the real token count. The ring schedule additionally overlaps the all-to-all with the expert GEMM by streaming experts in a ring rather than doing one monolithic dispatch — the JAX/Pallas analog of DeepEP's overlapped channels.

02DeepEP V1 → V2 → hybrid-ep: the substrate to target on GB200

DeepEP V1 split into two kernel families: normal/high-throughput (NVLink intranode + RDMA internode, NVSHMEM-based, asymmetric-domain forwarding aligned to V3's group-limited gating, measured 153/158 GB/s dispatch/combine over NVLink at EP8) and low-latency (pure RDMA via IBGDA, hook-based comm/compute overlap consuming zero SMs). For a GB200 build neither V1 number is what you ship against — DeepEP V2 is.

The V2 rewrite (2025–2026) is the more important development, and three changes matter for the 10T run:

AspectDeepEP V1DeepEP V2
Kernel architectureSeparate high-throughput + low-latency familiesUnified behind a single ElasticBuffer interface
TransportNVSHMEMNCCL "Gin" backend
SM cost (low-latency-equiv path)24 SMs4–6 SMs
Blackwell (SM100)Not supportedSupported: 726/740 GB/s dispatch/combine over NVLink at EP8 (max-perf, 64 SMs); 643/675 GB/s at minimal-SM config
SM90 CX7 RDMA (EP8×2)~58 GB/s peak90/81 GB/s dispatch/combine
 Sources: DeepEP README. The SM-occupancy collapse 24→4–6 is the headline.

The SM-occupancy collapse from 24→4–6 SMs is the headline: every SM the communication library does not consume is an SM doing tensor-core MMA, and at 100k GPUs the aggregate of that 20-SM-class tax (see DualPipe below) is enormous. V2 matching or beating V1 while freeing ~18–20 SMs is the single largest free lunch in the current EP stack.

24→4–6
SM cost, V1→V2 low-latency path
726GB/s
V2 SM100 NVLink dispatch, EP8
39GB/s
low-latency RDMA floor at ≥128 exp

The branch to actually track for GB200 is hybrid-ep, NVIDIA-authored, which re-architects the NVLink+RDMA path using TMA copies and warp-level pipelining to minimize SM occupancy while saturating both NVLink and RDMA, with experimental NIXL/UCX transport integration (hybrid-ep). This is the most GB200-relevant DeepEP variant and the easiest to miss because most readers know only the main branch.

Caveat to verify before committing verify

As of the surveyed docs, hybrid-ep's stated GPU support still lists SM80/SM90 — confirm SM100 status before you build against it, because the whole point is Blackwell.

Two operational landmines. First, DeepEP issue #427: low-latency dispatch can silently corrupt under heavily imbalanced expert load (#427). This is not a tail risk for you — it is exactly the regime in the first few thousand steps, before your auxiliary-loss-free bias balancer has converged, when expert load is maximally skewed. If you run low-latency dispatch from step 0 you can corrupt activations precisely when the model is most fragile.

Step-0 hazard — #427 imbalance corruption

Mitigation: use the high-throughput path early, or warm up the bias balancer aggressively, or pin a correctness check on dispatch output during the first N steps. Second landmine — the 39 GB/s low-latency RDMA floor at ≥128 experts is a hard ceiling on decode-style latency-bound dispatch; design your serving EP topology to keep the internode fan-out small enough that you live on the high-throughput curve, not the low-latency floor.

03DualPipe and the move to fused mega-kernels

V3's compute/comm overlap rests on DualPipe, which decomposes each forward and backward chunk into four phases — attention, all-to-all dispatch, MLP, all-to-all combine — with backward further split into backward-for-input and backward-for-weights, so a hand-tuned schedule overlaps one micro-batch's all-to-all with another's GEMMs. The bubble count is (PP/2−1)(F&B + B − 3W) versus 1F1B's (PP−1)(F+B), a meaningful reduction. The cost is two resident copies of the parameters (the bidirectional schedule runs from both ends), which V3 argues is cheap precisely because EP already shards the experts, so the duplicated tensors are the comparatively small dense/attention params. The communication side reserves 20 SMs (10 warp-specialized channels, custom PTX, auto-tuned chunk size to limit L2 interference) (DeepSeek-V3).

MaxText's layers/pipeline.py provides the analogous machinery for the JAX path: a circular (looping) pipeline with num_pipeline_microbatches, num_layers_per_pipeline_stage, and pipeline_delay_activation_forwarding to overlap the activation send with the next stage's compute. It is not DualPipe's bidirectional schedule, but it is the same family of bubble-reduction techniques, and combined with the ring-of-experts EP overlap it covers the two axes (PP bubble and EP all-to-all) that DualPipe addresses jointly.

Treat DualPipe as the fallback, not the target

That 20-SM tax is exactly what the field is trying to eliminate, and it is being eliminated from two directions: DeepEP V2/hybrid-ep drops the comm-kernel SM cost to 4–6 (or near-zero with TMA pipelining), and DeepSeek V4 deletes the two-piece overlap entirely by fusing dispatch, GEMM, and combine into one kernel (§06). For a greenfield GB200 build in mid-2026, DualPipe-style manual overlap was a workaround for the H800's throttled interconnect and a non-fused kernel stack. On a flat NVL72 domain with DeepEP V2 the overlap problem is dramatically smaller, and with MegaMoE it largely disappears.

04Routing: node-limited gating and auxiliary-loss-free balancing

Two routing decisions dominate MoE systems behavior: how many distinct nodes a token can touch (which sets your RDMA bill) and how you keep experts balanced without an auxiliary loss that distorts the gradient.

Node-limited (group-limited) routing

Each token is restricted to at most M=4 nodes, selected by summing the top K_r/M expert affinities per node and keeping the M best nodes; experts are only eligible within those nodes (DeepSeek-V3). This directly caps the internode fan-out that drives the RDMA-bandwidth decay of §01. MaxText implements the symmetric construct in expert_group_mask and deepseek_routing: it reshapes the gate logits to (batch·seq, n_routing_groups, experts_per_group), takes a per-group score (sum of top-2 in-group affinities), selects topk_routing_group groups via jax.lax.top_k, one-hots them into a group_mask, and broadcasts that mask back over experts so out-of-group experts are masked out before the final top-k. The config knobs are n_routing_groups and topk_routing_group; setting n_routing_groups == -1 disables grouping.

jax — MaxText deepseek_routing group-mask (illustrative shape flow)
# gate_logits: [batch*seq, n_experts]  ->  group-limit fan-out before top-k
g = gate_logits.reshape(tokens, n_routing_groups, experts_per_group)
group_score = jax.lax.top_k(g, 2)[0].sum(axis=-1)            # sum of top-2 in-group
_, grp_idx = jax.lax.top_k(group_score, topk_routing_group)  # keep M best groups
group_mask = jax.nn.one_hot(grp_idx, n_routing_groups).sum(1)
mask = jnp.repeat(group_mask, experts_per_group, axis=-1)     # broadcast over experts
masked_logits = jnp.where(mask > 0, gate_logits, -jnp.inf)    # out-of-group -> -inf
top_w, top_i = jax.lax.top_k(masked_logits, top_k)           # final routing
Map one routing group to one NVL72 domain

For your 10T build, set the group structure so one routing group maps to one NVL72 domain — then node-limited routing with M chosen to match your domain count keeps the dominant traffic on NVLink and the RDMA leg sees only cross-group tokens.

Auxiliary-loss-free balancing (the bias trick)

Rather than an auxiliary load-balance loss (which fights the language-modeling gradient and degrades quality), V3/V4 add a per-expert bias b_i to the affinity scores for top-K selection only — the gating weight still comes from the original, un-biased affinity — and nudge b_i by ±γ per step based on whether expert i is over- or under-loaded (DeepSeek-V3). V3's schedule was γ=0.001 for the first 14.3T tokens, then γ=0.0 for the final 500B to let the distribution settle. This is the correct default for you: it decouples balancing from the loss landscape and costs nothing but a per-expert counter.

MaxText implements exactly this. calculate_load_balance_updates(top_k_indices, num_experts, rate) computes the per-expert over/under-load and the bias delta; should_update_load_balance gates whether it fires (it requires routed_bias and routed_bias_update_rate > 0.0 and not is_hash_routing); the bias is applied to pre_bias_logits only (preserving the un-biased gating weight) for the deepseek3/deepseek4 model names. The update rate is the config field routed_bias_update_rate (default 0.0) — note that the default is off, so you must set it (e.g. 0.001) and supply a decay schedule to get V3's behavior.

src/maxtext/layers/moe.pycalculate_load_balance_updatesaux-loss-free biastrimmed
def calculate_load_balance_updates(top_k_indices, num_experts, rate):  # [1]
    # count tokens routed to each expert this step
    counts = jnp.bincount(top_k_indices.reshape(-1), length=num_experts)
    mean   = counts.mean()
    over   = counts > mean                                   # [2]
    delta  = jnp.where(over, -rate, rate)                    # +/- gamma per expert
    return delta                                             # bias update, NOT a grad

def should_update_load_balance(cfg):                          # [3]
    return (cfg.routed_bias
            and cfg.routed_bias_update_rate > 0.0
            and not cfg.is_hash_routing)

# selection uses biased logits; gating weight uses the un-biased affinity:
sel_logits = pre_bias_logits + routed_bias                   # [4]
top_w, top_i = jax.lax.top_k(jax.nn.sigmoid(pre_bias_logits) , top_k)  # weight un-biased

1A counter, not a loss. The update is computed from routed-token counts each step — there is no gradient term fighting the LM objective.

2±γ nudge. Over-loaded experts get −rate, under-loaded get +rate. V3 used γ=0.001 for 14.3T tokens, then 0 for the final 500B.

3Default is off. routed_bias_update_rate defaults to 0.0 — you must set it and supply a decay schedule yourself.

4Bias on selection only. The bias shifts which experts win the top-k; the gating weight comes from pre_bias_logits, so quality is untouched.

A classic bug — bias is a control signal, not a parameter

Critically, the bias update is applied post-optimizer in the train loop (trainers/pre_train/train.py applies moe_bias_updates via update_state_param after the optimizer step), not folded into the gradient — it is a control signal, not a learned parameter, and conflating the two is a classic bug.

Hash routing for early layers (V4)

V4 uses deterministic hash routing in its earliest blocks: a token's expert is a fixed function of its id via a lookup table, with no gating network and therefore no load-balance loss and no balancing dynamics at all (DeepSeek-V4). MaxText already supports this end to end: is_hash_routing gates the routing in get_topk (a tid2eid lookup table via Tid2EidVar, applied to first_num_hash_layers), and — importantly — it gates the load-balance loss off (load_balance_loss is skipped when is_hash_routing, and should_update_load_balance returns false). The deepseek4-284b.yml config wires in the hash-routing layers. Hash routing's systems appeal is that it is perfectly balanced by construction and deterministic, so dispatch buffers can be sized exactly and there is no imbalance-correctness hazard (the #427 regime cannot occur for hash-routed layers).

EPLB and LPLB: absorbing residual imbalance at deployment

The bias balancer keeps the training-time distribution roughly flat but cannot make it perfectly flat, and at inference you face a fixed deployment with hot experts. EPLB (Expert-Parallel Load Balancer) replicates the hottest experts redundantly and does hierarchical node→intra-node→GPU packing (used when #nodes divides #expert-groups, else global replication). For a 100k-GPU deployment, EPLB-style replication of the hottest experts is essentially mandatory — a single hot expert on one GPU bottlenecks the entire EP group.

The frontier successor is LPLB (Linear-Programming Load Balancer), which formulates per-batch dynamic token redistribution as an LP over a redundant-expert topology and solves it with a single-SM Interior Point Method on cuSolverDx/cuBLASDx in ~100µs/batch, fed by live statistics from DeepEP's buffers (LPLB). EPLB handles static imbalance (the long-run hot-expert distribution); LPLB handles the per-batch imbalance that EPLB's static replication cannot. For your run, ship EPLB redundant replication as the baseline and evaluate LPLB as the online refinement — it consumes the DeepEP buffer stats you already have and adds ~100µs/batch, which is negligible against a multi-millisecond decode step.

A genuine edge underwatched

Almost nobody outside DeepSeek's repo watchers knows LPLB exists; it is a genuine edge — an online LP refinement that piggybacks on the buffer statistics you are already collecting.

05Grouped-GEMM: dropless vs capacity-factor, and the kernel zoo

The expert FFN is a grouped (ragged) GEMM over variable-sized token groups — one matmul per expert, with group sizes that depend on routing. There are two regimes, and the choice between them is one of the highest-leverage decisions in the whole stack.

capacity-factor
drop / pad, dense masks
CF=11 to match dropless quality — 11× wasted FLOPs. Economically absurd at 100k GPUs.
dropless block-sparse
128×128 blocked-CSR-COO
Dropless quality without the FLOP blowup. 1.8–2.4× over dense Megatron-LM.
DeepGEMM / MegaMoE
CUDA SM100, fused
The Blackwell production target; fused dispatch-GEMM-combine (§06).

Capacity-factor (dropping/padding). Fix expert_capacity = ceil(tokens_per_batch / num_experts) · capacity_factor, then dispatch and combine via dense one-hot masks; tokens beyond capacity are dropped, under-capacity experts are zero-padded. MaxText's dense_matmul path implements exactly this — generate_masks builds the dispatch/combine masks and the einsums BSM,BSEC->EBCM … EBCM,BSEC->BSM do the masked routing (the code paths gate on capacity_factor > 0). The pathology, documented by MegaBlocks, is decisive: matching dropless quality with padding required capacity factors as high as 11, and dropless improved validation-loss reduction 1.73× over CF=1 (MegaBlocks). A capacity factor of 11 means you compute 11× the necessary FLOPs to avoid dropping tokens — economically absurd at 100k GPUs.

Dropless block-sparse (MegaBlocks dMoE). Reformulate the block-diagonal expert matmul as a block-sparse operation with 128×128 blocks, encoded in a hybrid blocked-CSR-COO format with transpose indices so the column-iteration (DSD / backward) needs no materialized transpose. Each expert's token batch is padded only to a multiple of 128 (negligible). Speedups: 1.38×–4.35× over Tutel (padding-based) and 1.8–2.4× over dense Megatron-LM (MegaBlocks). This is the dropless quality win without the capacity-factor FLOP blowup, and it is the recommended Hopper path via the megablocks[gg] grouped-GEMM backend.

The recommendation is unambiguous: dropless

At your scale, throwing away tokens degrades quality and wasting FLOPs on padding is unaffordable; the ragged-buffer machinery (§5.1 below) gives you bounded-memory dropless without the dense-mask cost.

Kernel implementations to weigh

KernelStackNotes
CUTLASS grouped GEMMCUDAThe 128×128-tile baseline MegaBlocks benchmarked against; solid, general
NVIDIA / Triton grouped GEMMCUDA / TritonPortable, well-understood; Triton easier to tune per-shape
DeepGEMM / MegaMoECUDA SM100The Blackwell production target; fused dispatch-GEMM-combine (§06)
SonicMoE (arXiv 2512.14080)CUDADec 2025, Guo/Mishra/Cheng/Stoica/Dao; IO- and tile-aware, benchmarked on H100 and GB200 vs MegaBlocks and CUTLASS — the freshest non-DeepSeek grouped-GEMM work (SonicMoE)
Tokamax ragged_dotJAX/MosaicThe credible non-CUDA path, used in MaxText (§5.2 below)
 If you stay in CUDA the realistic 2026 choice is DeepGEMM MegaMoE for the fused path, SonicMoE for a standalone IO/tile-aware grouped GEMM.

If you stay in CUDA, the realistic 2026 choice is DeepGEMM MegaMoE for the fused path with SonicMoE as the strong contender for the standalone grouped GEMM if you want IO/tile-aware tuning the DeepSeek kernel doesn't expose. If you stay in JAX/XLA, the MaxText sparse_matmul + ragged_dot stack below is production-credible and most CUDA-centric readers have never examined it.

5.1 · The MaxText ragged buffer: bounded-memory dropless

The single most useful piece of engineering in MaxText moe.py for your purposes is get_ragged_buffer_size, which encodes the worst-case dropless buffer math exactly. The function:

  • With ragged_buffer_factor > 0 it returns balanced_size · factor — the ragged analog of capacity factor. This can drop tokens if the true distribution exceeds the buffer (the comment is explicit that factor=1.0 is dropless only in the perfectly balanced case).
  • With factor ≤ 0 (the default, ragged_buffer_factor: -1.0) it computes the worst-case dropless size as worst_case_factor = min(ep_degree, global_experts / top_k) times balanced_size.
moe worst_case_factor = min( |EP| , global_exp / topk ) Once |EP| > global_exp/topk the bound stops growing — for 256/8 it is 32×, independent of EP.

The inequality encoded in the comment is worth internalizing because it is the capacity-planning formula for your activation memory. The worst case is "all of an EP replica group's tokens route to a single shard," which is |EP|× over balanced — but only while local experts ≥ top_k. Once EP > global_exp/top_k there are fewer than top_k local experts per shard, so at most local_exp/top_k of the tokens can land on the hottest shard, and the imbalance factor becomes EP · local_exp / top_k = EP · (global_exp/EP) / top_k = global_exp / top_k, independent of EP. For DeepSeek's 256/8 config that bound is 256/8 = 32×, and it does not get worse no matter how far you scale EP. The buffer never needs to be more than 32× the balanced size.

Set ragged_buffer_factor ≈ 3.0

The engineering recommendation, which MaxText's own comment endorses: the 32× worst-case bound is far larger than real imbalance ever is, so set ragged_buffer_factor ≈ 3.0 as a near-dropless, memory-bounded compromise. You accept a vanishingly small drop probability (un-guaranteed) in exchange for ~10× less activation memory than the worst-case buffer. For a config that widens experts or changes top_k, recompute global_exp/top_k — that ratio, not EP, is your buffer bound. And pair the buffer with the ragged-sort Pallas kernels (use_ragged_sort) so you sort only the valid prefix, not the padded buffer.

5.2 · The MaxText grouped-GEMM dispatch, in code

sparse_matmul's inner gmm() dispatches the grouped GEMM three ways:

  1. jax.lax.ragged_dot — the XLA-native ragged matmul, with set_xla_metadata(ragged_dot_tiling=...) to pass tile hints. The tiling knobs are exposed as config (wi_tile_fwd_* / wo_tile_*, 6 forward configs).
  2. Forked megablox mblx.gmm — the quantized path, supporting lhs_quantize_dtype/rhs_quantize_dtype, weight_gather_axes, and a gmm-v2 variant (use_gmm_v2, currently incompatible with quantization). This is the path you take when running FP8/INT8 expert weights.
  3. tokamax.ragged_dot — implementation="mosaic", gated by use_tokamax_gmm. The Mosaic/Pallas ragged-GEMM, and the most actively tuned of the three for TPU-style tiling.
src/maxtext/layers/moe.pyRoutedMoE.sparse_matmul → gmm()three-way dispatchtrimmed
def gmm(self, lhs, rhs, group_sizes):                         # [1]
    gs = RaggedDotGroupSizes(                                  # real sizes + fallback
        real=group_sizes,
        even_split=(lhs.shape[0] // self.num_experts,) * self.num_experts)  # [2]
    if self.config.use_tokamax_gmm:                           # [3]
        return tokamax.ragged_dot(lhs, rhs, gs.real, implementation="mosaic")
    if self.config.megablox:                                  # default backend
        return mblx.gmm(lhs, rhs, gs.real,                    # quantized path
                        lhs_quantize_dtype=self.config.lhs_quantize_dtype,
                        rhs_quantize_dtype=self.config.rhs_quantize_dtype)
    with set_xla_metadata(ragged_dot_tiling=self.wi_tile_fwd):  # [4]
        return jax.lax.ragged_dot(lhs, rhs, gs.real)          # XLA-native ragged

1Grouped, not batched. Each expert sees a variable row count; group_sizes carries the per-expert run lengths.

2Even-split fallback. RaggedDotGroupSizes carries an (n//G,)*G fallback for when real sizes are unavailable (e.g. shape inference).

3Three backends. Tokamax Mosaic, forked-megablox (quantized, the base.yml default), or XLA-native ragged_dot.

4Tile hints. ragged_dot_tiling passes the wi_tile_fwd_*/wo_tile_* knobs (6 forward configs) through to XLA.

Group sizes flow through a RaggedDotGroupSizes object that carries both the real per-expert group sizes and an even-split fallback (inputs.shape[0]//num_groups,)*num_groups for when the real sizes aren't available (e.g. shape inference). The base.yml defaults are megablox: true, sparse_matmul: true, use_tokamax_gmm: false, use_gmm_v2: false — i.e. the forked-megablox path is the default backend. For your run on GB200 the megablox/CUDA path is moot (it's TPU-oriented), but the structure — ragged_dot with explicit group sizes and an even-split fallback, plus a quantized backend selector — is exactly what you want to replicate against DeepGEMM/CUTLASS in CUDA. The Tokamax RaggedDotGroupSizes + Mosaic stack is a real, non-CUDA grouped-GEMM option most CUDA-centric teams haven't evaluated and worth a bake-off if you have any TPU exposure.

06DeepSeek V4's fused MoE mega-kernel: the GB200 endgame

V4 (arXiv 2606.19348, April 2026; V4-Pro 1.6T total / 49B active, V4-Flash 284B / 13B active) replaces the DualPipe + DeepEP two-piece overlap with a single fused MoE mega-kernel — "MegaMoE," open-sourced in DeepGEMM (DeepSeek-V4). It fuses the entire MoE forward — EP-dispatch → first FP8×FP4 expert GEMM → SwiGLU (+requant to FP8) → second FP8×FP4 GEMM → EP-combine — into one symmetric-memory (SymmBuffer) kernel that overlaps NVLink transfers with tensor-core MMA inside a single launch.

The mechanism is expert "waves": the current wave computes while the next wave's tokens transfer and finished experts' results are sent back, all concurrently — finer-grained overlap than Comet, and far finer than DualPipe's chunk-level schedule. On SM100 the kernel uses three warp roles (dispatch / UMMA non-epilogue / epilogue), Tensor Memory (TMEM), UE8M0 2D-TMA scaling descriptors, and a MegaMoEScheduler that prioritizes NVLink-active tiles so the comm-bound work always has compute to hide behind (DeepGEMM MegaMoE). Measured speedup: 1.50–1.73× general, up to 1.96× latency-sensitive over the non-fused baseline (DeepSeek-V4). DeepSeek validated the kernel on both NVIDIA GPUs and Huawei Ascend NPUs — the strongest public signal that a frontier lab is engineering MoE serving for a dual-stack future under export pressure.

Other V4 systems facts relevant to your kernel choices:

  • Sqrt(Softplus(·)) affinity replaces V3's sigmoid gating — a small change with quality motivation, trivial to implement.
  • Hash routing for the first layers (deterministic, no aux loss), which MaxText already gates correctly (is_hash_routing turns off lb-loss; see §04).
  • FP4 QAT on expert weights plus FP4 on the indexer QK path — the first trillion-scale model trained with FP4 in the loop, directly relevant to GB200's native NVFP4 microscaling.
  • TileLang DSL for the kernels (sub-µs host launchers, Z3-checked indexing, bitwise-reproducible IEEE-754 intrinsics).
  • A pull-based all-to-all (each GPU reads remote activations rather than pushing) with FP32 local-sum reduce-scatter for gradients.
The FP4 caveat you must not gloss over stated in V4

V4 explicitly notes that FP4×FP8 currently executes at FP8×FP8 speed on existing silicon and only realizes the ~1/3 efficiency win on future hardware (DeepSeek-V4). On GB200 today, the FP4 expert GEMM is a memory-bandwidth and footprint win (half the weight bytes, so you fit more experts per GPU and move fewer bytes in dispatch/combine), not yet a compute win. Budget accordingly: FP4 buys you capacity and bandwidth on Blackwell, and the compute speedup lands on the next generation.

07Synthesis: the recommended MoE stack for 10T/200B on 100k GB200

Pulling the chapter together into a concrete build, anchored to your real cluster economics. A 100k-GB200 cluster delivers roughly 0.45 EFLOP/s dense FP8 peak (100,000 × ~4.5 PFLOP/s per B200 dense; the headline 720 PFLOP/s-per-rack figures are with-sparsity numbers, dense is one-half per NVIDIA's own footnote) (NVIDIA GB200 NVL72), and realistic large-MoE FP8 MFU sits in the 35–40% band — heavy routing pushes it lower. Every percent of that is gated by the MoE layer's communication and balancing, which is why the choices below matter.

Communication substrate

Target DeepEP V2 (ElasticBuffer, NCCL Gin backend, 4–6 SMs, SM100 support at 726/740 GB/s NVLink at EP8) and track the hybrid-ep branch for the TMA-pipelined near-zero-SM path — but verify its SM100 status before committing. Treat the 39 GB/s low-latency RDMA floor at ≥128 experts as a hard design constraint and the #427 imbalance-corruption bug as a step-0 hazard: run the high-throughput dispatch path until the bias balancer converges, or use hash-routed early layers (which cannot hit #427) as a clean warm-up.

Kernel

Ship the DeepGEMM MegaMoE fused kernel as the target — it deletes DualPipe's 20-SM overlap tax and the two-piece dispatch/GEMM/combine handoff in one move, for 1.5–1.96×. Bake it off against SonicMoE (GB200-benchmarked, IO/tile-aware) for the standalone grouped GEMM. If any part of the run is JAX/TPU, MaxText's sparse_matmul + ragged_all_to_all + Tokamax ragged_dot is the production-credible non-CUDA equivalent.

compute ratio to beat = (6 · h · d) FLOPs / (3 · h) bytes ≈ 6144 FLOP/byte Stay above this and the expert GEMM hides the all-to-all under compute. Node-limiting + FP8 dispatch + the fused kernel are all in service of this line.

Dropless and buffers

Commit to dropless. Size ragged buffers at the global_exp/top_k bound (≤32× for a V3-style 256/8 config, recomputed if you widen experts) for guaranteed no-drop, or set ragged_buffer_factor ≈ 3.0 for the near-dropless, ~10×-smaller-memory compromise MaxText recommends. Pair with the ragged-sort Pallas kernels so you sort only the valid prefix at high EP. Never run a capacity factor of 11 to chase dropless quality with padding — that is 11× wasted expert FLOPs.

Routing and balancing

Run auxiliary-loss-free bias balancing with routed_bias_update_rate ≈ 0.001 on a decay-to-zero schedule (V3 decayed at ~96% of training; remember MaxText's default is off and applies the update post-optimizer). Use node-limited M=4 routing with one routing group mapped to one NVL72 domain so the RDMA leg carries only cross-group traffic. Consider hash routing for the first layers (perfectly balanced, deterministic, immune to #427). At deployment, EPLB redundant-expert replication is mandatory for the hottest experts; evaluate LPLB for online per-batch redistribution (~100µs/batch, fed by DeepEP buffer stats).

Precision

Use FP8 dispatch / BF16 combine to halve the dispatch leg (~86 KB vs ~115 KB per token/layer). Plan for FP4 expert weights as a footprint/bandwidth win on GB200 — fitting more experts per GPU and moving fewer dispatch bytes — while remembering it is not a compute win until next-gen silicon. Keep the controlling ratio in view: you need ≈6144 FLOPs/Byte to fully hide MoE communication under compute, and node-limiting + FP8 dispatch + the fused kernel are all in service of staying above that line.

The meta-lesson

The deepest meta-lesson from the open reports is that most of the DualPipe/DeepEP overlap acrobatics were workarounds for the H800's throttled interconnect. On a flat NVL72 domain with DeepEP V2 and MegaMoE, the overlap problem shrinks dramatically — but the architectural bets (sparser routing, node-limiting, dropless ragged buffers, FP8/FP4 dispatch, bias balancing) transfer directly and are the load-bearing decisions for your run.

Sources

  • DeepEP README (V2, ElasticBuffer, NCCL Gin, SM100 bandwidth): github.com/deepseek-ai/DeepEP
  • DeepEP legacy docs (V1 low-latency/normal-kernel bandwidth curves): DeepEP/docs/legacy.md
  • DeepEP hybrid-ep branch (NVIDIA, TMA + warp pipelining, NIXL/UCX): DeepEP/tree/hybrid-ep
  • DeepEP issue #427 (low-latency imbalance correctness): DeepEP#427
  • DeepSeek-V3 (DualPipe, node-limited routing, aux-loss-free bias): arxiv.org/html/2412.19437v1
  • DeepSeek-V4 (MegaMoE fused kernel, FP4 QAT, hash routing, 6144 FLOPs/Byte): arxiv.org/html/2606.19348v1
  • DeepGEMM MegaMoE kernel internals (warp roles, TMEM, SymmBuffer, scheduler): deepwiki.com/deepseek-ai/DeepGEMM
  • MegaBlocks dropless dMoE (block-sparse, capacity-factor pathology): ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2211.15841
  • SonicMoE (IO/tile-aware grouped GEMM, H100 + GB200): arxiv.org/pdf/2512.14080
  • DeepSeek LPLB (online LP load balancer): github.com/deepseek-ai/LPLB
  • MaxText moe.py (sparse_matmul, ragged buffer, EP all-to-all, routing): maxtext/src/maxtext/layers/moe.py
  • MaxText base.yml (MoE defaults): maxtext/src/maxtext/configs/base.yml
  • NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 (dense vs sparse peak FLOPs): nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/gb200-nvl72

Chapter 06 · The framework decision is the run

Framework shoot-out: Megatron-Core vs NeMo vs MaxText/JAX vs TorchTitan vs DeepSpeed at frontier scale

The training framework is the single most consequential, hardest-to-reverse choice in a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE program. It fixes which parallelism mappings you can express, which precision recipes are production-validated, how the all-to-all overlaps — and, at 100,000 GB200 GPUs where mean-time-between-failure is measured in minutes, whether the run survives at all.

~30 min read5 figuresgrounded in Megatron-Core, MaxText src/maxtext/layers/moe.py

The framework decision dictates which parallelism mappings you can express, which precision recipes are production-validated, how the all-to-all is overlapped, and — at 100,000 GB200 GPUs where mean-time-between-failure is measured in minutes, not hours — whether your run survives at all. This chapter is an opinionated, evidence-weighted comparison of the five contenders that matter: Megatron-Core, NeMo / Megatron-Bridge, MaxText/JAX, TorchTitan, and the legacy DeepSpeed stack. The verdict is given up front and defended below: for a first frontier run on NVIDIA Blackwell, Megatron-Core (driven by Megatron-Bridge) is the lowest-risk EP engine today, MaxText/JAX is the strongest contender if your organization already has TPU-grade XLA muscle and wants quantization-as-config, TorchTitan is the best de-risking and rapid-iteration vehicle, and DeepSpeed is a legacy fallback you should not build a new 10T run on.

Whichever stack you pick, no framework has a published >2K-GPU MoE MFU curve — at 100k you are past every documented envelope, and the binding constraint is fault tolerance and silent-data-corruption resilience, not kernel throughput.
1048TF/s
Megatron DSV3-685B on GB200 (~23% MFU)
60%
of step time lost to unoptimized cross-node A2A
0.45EF/s
100k-GPU dense FP8 peak (not the ~1.0 sparse)
20–35%
realistic FP8 MoE MFU planning band

01A note on the denominator: dense FP8 peak, not sparse marketing

Before any MFU claim, fix the denominator. NVIDIA's headline GB200 NVL72 figures — 1,440 PFLOP/s FP4, 720 FP8, 360 BF16 per 72-GPU rack — are with-sparsity (2:4) numbers; the GB200 NVL72 product page footnotes them explicitly ("Dense is one-half sparse spec shown") (nvidia.com/.../gb200-nvl72). For dense training FLOPs the meaningful peaks are 360 PFLOP/s FP8 and 180 PFLOP/s BF16 per rack, i.e. roughly 4.5 PFLOP/s dense FP8 and 2.25 PFLOP/s dense BF16 per B200 GPU. A 100,000-GPU fleet is therefore ~0.45 EFLOP/s dense FP8 peak, not the ~1.0 EFLOP/s you get only by counting sparsity.

Every "TFLOPS/GPU" number quoted by Megatron, NeMo, MaxText and TorchTitan below is a dense-FLOP-equivalent achieved throughput; to convert to honest MFU you divide by 4,500 (FP8) or 2,250 (BF16). So Megatron-Core's 1,048 TFLOP/s/GPU for DeepSeek-V3-685B on GB200 (arxiv.org/abs/2603.07685) is ~23% of dense FP8 peak — and that is the best published Blackwell MoE number. The realistic large-MoE FP8 MFU band for 2025–2026 is ~35–40% for friendlier routing and ~18–35% for heavy fine-grained routing; nobody is near 50% on a 200B-active model, and any vendor pitch implying otherwise is quoting sparse peak or a dense Llama-style transformer.

The one number to plan against

Plan your token budget against ~20–35% FP8 MFU, not 50%. Convert every quoted TFLOP/s/GPU to MFU by dividing by 4,500 (FP8) or 2,250 (BF16). Anything implying 50% on a 200B-active MoE is quoting sparse peak or a dense model.

compute MFU = TFLOP/s/GPU ÷ dense-peak ; dense-peak = 4500 (FP8) · 2250 (BF16) GB200 dense FP8 = ½ × 720 PF sparse ÷ 72 GPU ≈ 4.5 PFLOP/s/GPU. Megatron's 1048 TF/s ÷ 4500 ≈ 23%.

02Megatron-Core: the EP-maturity leader

Megatron-Core is the de-facto reference implementation for frontier MoE, and the March 2026 NVIDIA report "Scalable Training of Mixture-of-Experts Models with Megatron Core" (arXiv 2603.07685, 45 authors, 88 pages) is the densest primary source in the field (arxiv.org/abs/2603.07685). It frames the problem as three coupled "walls" — memory, communication, computation — and argues MoE forces co-design because relieving one wall shifts pressure to another. The headline throughput, FP8 path, dense-BF16-reference:

ModelGB200 TFLOP/s/GPUGB300 TFLOP/s/GPUGB200 MFU (vs 4.5 PF dense FP8)
DeepSeek-V3-685B1,0481,233~23%
Qwen3-235B919974~20%
 Best published Blackwell MoE numbers, FP8 path. Source: Megatron-Core report.

For calibration: the prior Hopper generation of this stack reached ~47% MFU on H100 and a widely-cited ~368 TFLOP/s/GPU FP8 for DeepSeek-V3 on H100 (the latter is NVIDIA messaging, not verbatim in the abstract — treat as corroborating) (github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM). The GB200 1,048 figure is ~2.8× the H100 FP8 number, consistent with NVIDIA's separate MLPerf claim that GB200 NVL72 delivers 3.2× Llama-3.1-405B training over Hopper at equal GPU count, with GB300 a further 1.9× (developer.nvidia.com/.../3x-faster-training).

Parallel Folding: decoupling attention from experts

Parallel Folding is the architectural centerpiece, and it is essentially mandatory for fine-grained MoE. The classical Megatron constraint was world_size = TP×CP×PP×DP with EP ⊆ DP, forcing attention and expert layers to share a parallelism mapping. Parallel Folding (precursor arXiv 2504.14960) decouples them via a ProcessGroupCollection tracking two independent hierarchies: attention uses (tp, cp, dp, pp); MoE uses (ep, etp, expt_dp, pp) (arxiv.org/html/2603.07685v1). This resolves the dense-sparse mismatch directly: dense attention — especially MLA with its large up-projection — benefits from high TP, while fine-grained experts are fragmented by high TP and prefer ETP=1 with large EP.

The load-bearing data point lives in the folding precursor: on fine-grained Mixtral-8x22B-G8T8 (128 H100), folding lifts MFU from 17.1% to 28.8% (+68%), versus only 46.3%→49.3% for standard Mixtral (arxiv.org/pdf/2504.14960). The fine-grained regime — exactly the 10T/200B target with hundreds of small experts — is where folding matters most. FP8 stacks on top: Mixtral-8x22B reaches 631.7 TFLOPS (FP8+folding) vs 458.3 BF16, a 1.30× lift.

ETP=1, high EP for fine-grained experts

Parallel Folding lets attention run TP-heavy (MLA up-proj loves it) while experts run ETP=1 with large EP. On fine-grained Mixtral-8x22B this is a +68% MFU swing (17.1%→28.8%) — the single biggest architectural lever for the 10T/200B target.

EP placement and the dispatcher zoo

The EP-placement philosophy is the biggest single throughput lever, and it is topology-driven. The DeepSeek-V3 case study lands on EP=8, ETP=1, TP=4, PP=2, VPP=2, CP=1, micro-batch=1 on a GB200 NVL72, deliberately keeping EP entirely inside the 72-GPU NVLink domain so all-to-all never crosses the scale-out IB fabric (arxiv.org/html/2603.07685). The motivation is stark: unoptimized cross-node A2A can consume up to 60% of step time for DeepSeek-V3-style multi-node experts. A2A volume scales as T·K·h·(EP−1)/EP, so confining EP to one NVLink island (130 TB/s aggregate on NVL72) is the difference between a healthy run and a comm-bound one.

comms A2A volume ∝ T · K · h · (EP − 1) / EP Approaches T·K·h as EP grows. Confining EP inside one 130 TB/s NVL72 island is what keeps the (EP−1)/EP factor riding NVLink instead of the IB fabric.

Megatron-Core ships three dispatcher backends: AllGather (memory-heavy, tiny-EP only), AllToAll (NCCL P2P, the workhorse), and Flex — a unified backend wrapping DeepEP (DeepSeek's NVSHMEM+IBGDA kernels, for cross-node fine-grained) and HybridEP (NVIDIA's own TMA+IBGDA kernels tuned for NVL72-class topologies). For GB200 NVL72, NVIDIA explicitly recommends HybridEP over DeepEP (--moe-flex-dispatcher-backend hybridep), as it is integrated into Megatron-Core's fused all-to-all module (docs.nvidia.com/.../performance-guide). A practical wrinkle from the perf guide: use 64 of the 72 GPUs to avoid NVLink/network-domain partitioning complexity, and TP4+PP2 can beat TP8 when both fit memory.

Dropless, precision, and the three walls

Token-dropless (dMoE) is the default and the recommended frontier path — no capacity factor, no dropped tokens, implemented via token permutation + all-to-all so each expert sees a contiguous GEMM. The hard part is CUDA Graphs: dropless routing produces dynamic shapes, so full-iteration graph capture requires the newer sync-free kernels, MoE ECHO, and Paged Stashing machinery — recent and less battle-tested than the rest of the stack. Load balancing offers aux_loss, seq_aux_loss, global_aux_loss, Sinkhorn, Z-loss, and the DeepSeek-style auxiliary-loss-free per-expert bias (--moe-router-enable-expert-bias --moe-router-bias-update-rate 1e-3).

megatron-core — DSV3-shaped MoE launch flags (illustrative)
# EP inside the NVL72 domain; ETP=1 for fine-grained experts; HybridEP on GB200
--expert-model-parallel-size 8 \
--expert-tensor-parallel-size 1 \
--tensor-model-parallel-size 4 --pipeline-model-parallel-size 2 \
--moe-flex-dispatcher-backend hybridep \          # NVIDIA's TMA+IBGDA, recommended over DeepEP on NVL72
--moe-token-dispatcher-type flex \
--moe-grouped-gemm \                              # contiguous per-expert GEMM, dropless
--moe-router-enable-expert-bias \                 # aux-loss-free balancing
--moe-router-bias-update-rate 1e-3 \              # gamma; decay to 0 near end of training
--moe-router-padding-for-fp8 \                    # static-shape alignment for FP8 grouped quant
--overlap-moe-expert-parallel-comm \              # batch-level EP-A2A / compute overlap
--fp8-recipe blockwise                            # recommended Hopper production recipe

Precision is a first-class lever on all three walls. Via Transformer Engine + TEGroupedMLP: per-tensor FP8 (generic), blockwise FP8 (the recommended Hopper production recipe, loss "negligibly different" from BF16), MXFP8 (Blackwell microscaling), and NVFP4 (Blackwell 4-bit) (github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM/.../moe/README.md). NVIDIA's standalone NVFP4 result is the most concrete ceiling: 1.59× over BF16 on GB200 NVL72 (1,850 vs 1,165 TFLOP/s/GPU), MMLU 52.83% vs 52.73% BF16 on Llama-3-8B/1T tokens — but with two sharp caveats: the final four transformer layers must stay BF16, and the recipe is sensitive (AdamW ε=1e-8, LR 6e-4→6e-6, GBS=768) (developer.nvidia.com/.../nvfp4). MoE-specific FP8 plumbing includes grouped quantization fused with Grouped GEMM and padding/unpadding fusion (--moe-router-padding-for-fp8) to satisfy static-shape alignment.

The memory wall is attacked with dual-level fine-grained recomputation (selectively recompute MLA up-proj, layernorm, moe_act rather than whole layers), fine-grained activation offloading (per-layer host offload in forward, prefetch in backward, VPP-aware), and a precision-aware distributed optimizer (ZeRO-1-style, BF16 master weights/moments, ~50% optimizer-memory cut). The communication wall, beyond dispatchers, adds --overlap-moe-expert-parallel-comm for batch-level EP-A2A/compute overlap, DP reduce-scatter overlapped with backward, and 1F1B EP-A2A overlap on the roadmap.

Forward-tracking is Megatron's quiet superpower

The 2026 Q2 roadmap (Issue #4815) already targets DeepSeek-V4 (mHC, sqrt-softplus / Hash MoE scoring, CSA+HCA hybrid attention, Muon distributed optimizer with FP8 primary weights, MTP), Qwen3.5 (Gated Delta Rule kernels), HybridEP for GB200, DeepEP v2, and the ECHO/Paged-Stashing CUDA-graph trick (github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM/issues/4815). It tracks frontier architectures fastest of any open stack.

Where Megatron is worst at 100k

Four real risks. (1) Published benchmarks top out at "thousands of GPUs" / 1,024-node strong scaling — there is no published 100k-GB200 MFU curve, so frontier-scale claims are extrapolation, and the existing curves already shed ~8–15 MFU points going 128→1,024 GPUs. (2) DP-scaling of A2A and PP bubble: at 100k you need very large DP, and gradient all-reduce/reduce-scatter plus PP warmup/cooldown bubbles dominate. (3) Resiliency is bolted on, not native — NVRx fault tolerance, in-process restart, straggler detection, and async/local checkpointing live in the Megatron-Bridge/NeMo layer and require Slurm + ft_launcher, PyTorch ≥2.5.1, NCCL ≥2.26.2, torch_dist checkpoints (docs.nvidia.com/.../resiliency). (4) Silent data corruption: emerging work (LLM-PRISM, arXiv 2604.10390) embeds RTL-level GPU fault injection into Megatron-LM and shows SDC silently perturbs gradients — there is no first-class SDC detection in the core training loop (arxiv.org/html/2604.10390v1).

The real 100k bottleneck lives outside the compute kernels

Net: the compute/communication kernels are frontier-ready; the fault-tolerance and SDC-resilience envelope is the real 100k-GPU bottleneck, and it lives outside Megatron-Core proper — in the Megatron-Bridge/NeMo layer. Which is exactly why you drive Megatron-Core through Bridge, not raw.

03NeMo / Megatron-Bridge: orchestration, recipes, and a migration in flight

The most important fact for a 2026 planner is that the "NeMo 2.0" abstraction you may be anchored to is mid-migration. NeMo 2.0 was a code-first Pythonic rewrite that wrapped Megatron-Core as the engine but kept PyTorch Lightning as orchestration (Trainer, MegatronStrategy, callbacks) with config scattered across Lightning args, callbacks, and strategy params. As of 2026, NVIDIA has refactored this into Megatron-Bridge (NVIDIA-NeMo/Megatron-Bridge, latest v0.5.0, June 22 2026), a PyTorch-native training loop that drops Lightning entirely, consolidates everything into a single ConfigContainer, and pins Megatron-Core as a git submodule tracking main+dev via scripts/switch_mcore.sh, with bidirectional HuggingFace↔Megatron checkpoint conversion (github.com/NVIDIA-NeMo/Megatron-Bridge).

Critically, NVIDIA's own production MoE pretraining now runs on Megatron-Bridge: the Nemotron Nano 3 recipe (hybrid Mamba-2/attention/MoE, 128 routed + 1 shared expert, top-6, ~3.5B active / ~31.6B total, 25T tokens, aux-loss-free balancing) states "Pretraining is implemented using Megatron-Bridge" (docs.nvidia.com/nemotron/.../nano3/pretrain).

Megatron-Core
the engine
Token dispatch, grouped GEMM, parallel folding, FP8 — MFU is fundamentally a Megatron-Core property.
Megatron-Bridge
training loop / recipes
Model providers, mixed-precision presets, HF bridging, resiliency. Ergonomics on top.
NeMo-Run
launcher / orchestrator
Slurm/Skypilot/DGXCloud/Lepton executors, ft launcher, AutoConfigurator grid search.

The correct mental model for a fresh 10T/200B run starting in 2026: Megatron-Core = engine, Megatron-Bridge = training loop/recipes, NeMo-Run = launcher/orchestrator, with "NeMo 2.0" a transitional waypoint you should not build new large runs on. The Bridge layer adds what raw Megatron-Core lacks: (1) preset model providers and recipes (deepseek_v3_pretrain_config(), DeepSeek V2/V3/V4, Qwen3-MoE, Qwen3.5/3.6-VL, Gemma-4-VL 26B-A4B, OLMoE, Ling 2.0 MoE); (2) mixed-precision recipe presets (bf16_with_fp8_current_scaling_mixed, bf16_with_mxfp8_mixed, bf16_with_nvfp4_mixed); (3) checkpoint/HF bridging; (4) nvidia-resiliency-ext and AutoConfigurator integration; (5) NeMo-Run as the cluster launcher. The MoE math — token dispatch, grouped GEMM, parallel folding, FP8 — all lives in Megatron-Core, so MFU is fundamentally a Megatron-Core property; Bridge is ergonomics on top. The reference DeepSeek-V3 recipe uses TP2, PP16, EP64, ETP1, SP on, GBS 4096, seq 4096, MTP 1 layer (loss scale 0.1), selective recompute on a 1,024-GPU/128-node reference (docs.nvidia.com/.../deepseek_v3).

The resiliency stack: why a 100k run survives

The resiliency stack is the reason a 100k run survives, and it is the strongest in the open ecosystem. nvidia-resiliency-ext (NVRx) exposes five components:

  1. straggler detection (relative + per-GPU scores; does not stop by default, needs stop_if_detected=True);
  2. fault tolerance (section-based monitoring, in-job restart within the existing Slurm allocation, requires ft_launcher not torchrun);
  3. in-process restart (inprocess.Wrapper) — restarts within the same process via a while True: initialize→health_check→run→except→abort→rank_assignment loop, with a documented latency budget of destroy_process_group ~0.5s + rank reassignment ~0.5s + TCPStore barrier ~0.5s @ 16k ranks, total 1.5–3+ seconds vs minutes for a relaunch, built on NCCL 2.27's ncclCommShrink (nvidia.github.io/.../inprocess/usage_guide, developer.nvidia.com/.../nccl-2-27);
  4. async checkpointing (torch_dist only, 5–15× overhead reduction vs synchronous);
  5. local checkpointing (node-local SSD/RAM with configurable cross-node replication).
1.5–3s
NVRx in-process restart vs minutes for relaunch
5–15×
async-checkpoint overhead reduction
96%
Hierarchical-AllReduce scaling efficiency @ 21ms RTT

This is the most complete open resilience story for Blackwell, and it composes cleanly with the Megatron-Core engine — which is precisely why driving Megatron-Core through Megatron-Bridge, rather than raw, is the correct posture at 100k.

Maturity warnings you must internalize. In NeMo 25.09, DeepSeek subchannel/blockwise FP8 pretraining was broken; the FP8-current-scaling workaround left MTP loss non-converging (docs.nvidia.com/.../knownissues). The B200 precision table (Llama-3/Research-8B, 1T tokens) shows FP8-CS 1,547 TFLOP/s/GPU (1.33×), MXFP8 1,540 (1.32×), NVFP4 1,850 (1.59×), with fully-NVFP4 (F0L0) diverging and the working recipe keeping the final four layers BF16 (F0L4) (github.com/.../Megatron-Bridge/discussions/3460). Plan for FP8-CS or BF16 fallbacks during bring-up.

A 100k fleet spans multiple domains — plan the outer loop demonstrated @ 3,072 GPUs

A 100k-GB200 fleet almost certainly spans multiple NVLink/IB domains and possibly multiple datacenters. NVIDIA's Hierarchical AllReduce work (DP/PP across sites, ReduceScatter→cross-DC AllReduce→AllGather, ~1/N inter-DC bandwidth, 96% scaling efficiency and MFU 49% vs 51% single-site at 21ms RTT, projected to 500k+ GPUs) shows the path — but is demonstrated at only 3,072 GPUs (developer.nvidia.com/.../turbocharge-llm-training).

NeMo-Run rounds out the picture with Slurm (Pyxis)/Skypilot/DGXCloud/Lepton executors, torchrun and ft launchers, and the AutoConfigurator grid search over TP/PP/CP/EP/MBS/activation-checkpointing-layers (docs.nvidia.com/.../autoconfigurator).

04MaxText / JAX: can XLA:GPU compete on GB200?

MaxText (AI-Hypercomputer/maxtext) is a pure-Python/JAX reference trainer that leans almost entirely on XLA ("largely optimization-free"), and it is the user's actual codebase for this program — so its specifics deserve precise treatment. As of the Feb 2026 src-layout restructure it ships Flax-NNX models, Orbax checkpointing, Optax/Tunix optimizers, Grain data loading, and a GSPMD logical-axis surface in src/maxtext/configs/base.yml that exposes mesh_axes = ['diloco','data','stage','fsdp','fsdp_transpose','context','context_autoregressive','tensor','tensor_transpose','tensor_sequence','expert','autoregressive'], each independently mappable to ICI vs DCN via ici_*_parallelism/dcn_*_parallelism (github.com/.../configs/base.yml). Recommended defaults are ici_fsdp_parallelism=-1 (auto) and dcn_data_parallelism=-1; dcn_tensor_parallelism and dcn_sequence_parallelism are flagged "never recommended." Sharding is GSPMD-by-annotation (logical axis rules + jax.sharding), with shard_map reserved for kernels and hand-written collectives.

The MoE path is the center of gravity and is unusually rich. In the user's actual codebase (MaxText), src/maxtext/layers/moe.py implements RoutedMoE with a get_topk() that dispatches routing variants covering the full frontier lineage: DeepSeek-V3 sigmoid + learnable-bias (aux-loss-free balancing via routed_bias_update_rate), DeepSeek-V4 HASH routing (a tid2eid lookup table, Tid2EidVar, first_num_hash_layers), Llama4 sigmoid gate, and Gemma sigmoid. Expert parallelism is realized through permute()/unpermute()/local_permute()/get_all_to_all_params() implementing the EP all-to-all, plus an optional use_ring_of_experts scheme built on jax.lax.ragged_all_to_all with custom Pallas ragged-sort kernels (a2a_ragged_sort, ring_ragged_sort, ring_ragged_unsort) and a ragged_buffer_factor knob trading token-drop vs memory (capacity_factor = -1.0 selects the dropless worst-case buffer). The grouped-matmul sparse_matmul() has three backends — Tokamax ragged_dot (use_tokamax_gmm), Megablox GMM, and jax.lax.ragged_dot — with explicit tiling knobs (wi_tile_*/wo_tile_*). DeepSeek-V3 is a first-class config, not a fork.

src/maxtext/layers/moe.pyRoutedMoE.get_topk → sparse_matmulrouting + EP all-to-all + grouped GEMMillustrative
class RoutedMoE(nnx.Module):
  def get_topk(self, gate_logits, tid):                        # [1]
    if self.config.is_hash_routing:                            # DeepSeek-V4 first layers
      return self.tid2eid[tid], jnp.ones_like(tid, jnp.float32)
    # DeepSeek-V3: sigmoid affinity + learnable per-expert bias (selection only)
    sel = jax.nn.sigmoid(gate_logits) + self.routed_bias       # bias is NOT a grad param
    return jax.lax.top_k(sel, self.config.num_experts_per_tok)

  def sparse_matmul(self, x, w, top_k_indices):
    x_sorted, sort_idx, gs = permute(x, top_k_indices)         # [2]
    p   = get_all_to_all_params(gs, self.shard_id, self.ep)    # offsets/sizes, not one-hot
    xd  = jax.lax.ragged_all_to_all(x_sorted, self.buffer, *p) # [3] EP dispatch
    xl  = local_permute(xd, ...)                               # regroup by local expert
    yl  = self.gmm(xl, w, local_group_sizes)                   # [4] 3-backend grouped GEMM
    yd  = jax.lax.ragged_all_to_all(yl, self.buffer, *p_rev)   # EP combine
    return unpermute(yd, sort_idx, combine_weights)            # un-biased gating weight

1Full frontier lineage in one method. Hash routing (V4), sigmoid+bias (V3), Llama4/Gemma gates — selected by config, no fork.

2Permute, not one-hot. permute argsorts by flattened expert id; get_all_to_all_params builds offset/size metadata — no dense BSEC mask.

3Ragged all-to-all. ragged_all_to_all moves only valid per-expert runs into a sized buffer (the ragged_buffer_factor knob bounds it).

4Three backends. gmm() picks Tokamax Mosaic ragged_dot, Megablox GMM (quantized), or XLA-native jax.lax.ragged_dot with wi_tile_* hints.

Attention is equally complete: attentions.py/attention_op.py/attention_mla.py provide MHA/GQA and a full MLA class (q_lora_rank, kv_lora_rank, qk_nope_head_dim, qk_rope_head_dim, v_head_dim), with kernel dispatch hardware-branched — TPU splash (Pallas) vs GPU cudnn_flash_te (Transformer Engine cuDNN fused attention). The model configs in configs/models/ are exactly the frontier set: deepseek3-671b.yml (256 experts top-8, 1 shared, 61 layers, 3 dense-first, MLA q_lora 1536/kv_lora 512, YaRN rope_factor 40, 163840 ctx, routed_scaling_factor 2.5), deepseek4-284b.yml (hash-routing layers), llama4-17b-128e.yml, and qwen3-480b-a35b.yml. Precision spans AQT int8/intmp, fp8/nanoo_fp8, and the newer Qwix path (use_qwix_quantization, with N:M sparsity), with AQT being deprecated in favor of Qwix mid-flight.

The optimizer/loop in optimizers.py and trainers/pre_train/train.py is notably mature for resilience: AdamW and Muon (optax.contrib._muon.muon), a skip_step_on_spikes() wrapper (rolling-mean ± scaling_factor·std, Bessel-corrected, preserving opt state), z-loss, MoE LB-loss aggregation, post-optimizer MoE bias updates, host-offload of params, gradient accumulation, and clipping. Checkpointing uses Orbax with EmergencyCheckpointManager / EmergencyReplicatorCheckpointManager (async/in-memory) and OCDBT — a genuinely strong in-framework resilience primitive.

The Blackwell evidence is real but thin

The strongest GPU source is NVIDIA's "Train Models Faster with JAX and MaxText Using NVFP4 on NVIDIA Blackwell": on GB200/GB300, NVFP4 vs FP8 yields 1.44× (405B/GB200) and 1.73× (405B/GB300), with Llama-3.1-405B hitting 2,241 TFLOP/s/GPU (GB200, NVFP4) and 3,633 (GB300, NVFP4) (developer.nvidia.com/.../train-models-faster-with-jax-and-maxtext-nvfp4). That 2,241 figure on GB200 is higher than Megatron's 1,048 — but it is a 405B dense model, not a 200B-active MoE, and the config is pure FSDP=128 (zero TP/PP/EP) at seq 8192. It proves XLA:GPU + GSPMD can hold a 405B-dense run on data/FSDP sharding inside one NVL72 domain; it does not prove the expert all-to-all scales.

GPU FP8/NVFP4 is delivered via Transformer Engine (quantization=te_nvfp4/te_nvfp4_no_rht), applied to MLP GEMMs only, attention higher-precision, +0.026-nat loss gap over 10k steps — so the GPU training precision path is TE-owned, while Qwix/AQT is the TPU-native path. The second decisive source is NVIDIA's Feb 2026 "Accelerating Long-Context Model Training in JAX and XLA": on a GB200 NVL72 running Llama-3-8B, an NVSHMEM-backed CollectivePermute beats NCCL by +36.3% at 256K seq (CP=16/TP=2, 32 GPUs: 366.94→500.22 TFLOP/s), selected by a new XLA pass, CollectiveBackendAssigner, that picks NCCL vs NVSHMEM per-collective and "always uses NVSHMEM for CollectivePermute" (requires JAX 0.6.2+) (developer.nvidia.com/.../accelerating-long-context-model-training-in-jax-and-xla). This shows XLA:GPU collective infra now reaches into on-stream NVSHMEM P2P — but the published win is CP-attention, not expert all-to-all.

The honest JAX-on-GPU risk verdict

Strengths. The math works (2,241–3,633 TFLOP/s/GPU on 405B with NVFP4 is competitive Blackwell utilization), the DeepSeek-V3 architecture is already GPU-and-TPU-tested, sharding/quantization/remat are config edits not kernel rewrites, and MaxText even reimplements DeepSeek's FP8-comms trick in pure JAX — deepseek_batchsplit_fp8.py's quantized_psum_scatter does a BF16 reduce-scatter forward and a quantized FP8 all-gather backward via custom VJP (github.com/.../deepseek_batchsplit_fp8.py).

Risks specific to 100k GB200.

  • (a) published GPU benchmarks top out at 128 GPUs (405B FSDP) and 64 GPUs (8B context-parallel) — there is no public MaxText-on-GPU result even at 1k GPUs, so multi-NVL72 / cross-DCN GSPMD scaling for a 200B-active MoE is externally unvalidated.
  • (b) Pathways — the single-controller, elastic, fault-tolerant runtime that makes 9,216-chip TPU Ironwood runs survivable — is documented almost exclusively for TPU; Google Cloud's GTC 2026 GB200/GB300 (A4X) story leans on Vertex AI + NVIDIA's NeMo/TensorRT-LLM/Dynamo, with no Pathways/MaxText-on-GPU mention. On a 100k-GPU NVIDIA cluster you would likely run multi-controller JAX (IFRT) and own your restart/elasticity layer — though MaxText's in-framework Emergency checkpointing and skip_step_on_spikes soften this.
  • (c) GPU FP8/NVFP4 is TE-dependent, so you inherit TE's coverage and gaps (GPU flash rejects attention sinks, QK-Clip / record_max_logits, and the decode stage per the code's validate_gpu_flash_attention).
  • (d) The MoE expert all-to-all on GPU — the hardest collective for a 200B-active model — has no published NVSHMEM/XLA result; only CP-attention does.
  • (e) Tokamax GPU kernels are self-described "heavily under development," H100-and-newer only.

The XLA-flag asymmetry tells the maturity story bluntly: TPU has dozens of mature overlap flags (xla_tpu_enable_async_collective_fusion*, SparseCore offload, even an xla_tpu_enable_sdc_checker) versus a handful of GPU flags plus the emerging NVSHMEM path.

Prefer MaxText on Ironwood; treat MaxText-on-GB200 as co-engineering

Net: JAX+XLA can match Megatron on per-GPU Blackwell throughput and is architecturally ahead on quantization ergonomics, but on a 100k-GB200 cluster the unproven pieces are large-scale GPU GSPMD, GPU expert all-to-all, and the resilience/orchestration layer that on TPU is Pathways. For this program — where MaxText is the incumbent codebase — the pragmatic stance is to run MaxText on TPU (Ironwood) where Pathways exists, and treat MaxText-on-GB200 as a co-engineering effort with NVIDIA's JAX-Toolbox team rather than a turnkey path.

05TorchTitan: the composable, PyTorch-native challenger

TorchTitan (arXiv 2410.06511, ICLR 2025) is a thin, model-readable trainer built entirely on first-party PyTorch distributed primitives: DTensor (Shard/Replicate/Partial placements over a DeviceMesh), FSDP2 (per-parameter fully_shard, DTensor-native), TP/SP/async-TP, torch.distributed.pipelining, Context Parallel, DCP checkpointing, Flight Recorder, and torch.compile (arxiv.org/abs/2410.06511). The thesis is composability: every technique is a DTensor transformation over a named mesh, so 1D→4D parallelism is a config change. The original H100 numbers (Llama-3.1 8B +65.08% at 128 GPUs; +12.59% adding 2D TP at 70B/256; +30% adding 3D PP at 405B/512) were dense and tested only to 512 GPUs — the central caveat for a 100k reader.

MoE/EP is now first-class and mirrors Megatron's folding. The mesh axes (parallel_dims.py) are dp_replicate, dp_shard, cp, tp, pp, ep plus a separate ETP degree. Routed expert FFNs live on a sparse sub-mesh (EP × expert-FSDP), distinct from the dense attention/shared-expert sub-mesh (FSDP × TP) — exactly the Parallel Folding decoupling. The MOE_SHARDING.md contract is precise: routed expert weights are Shard(0) on EP, MoE input redistributes Shard(1)→Shard(1), output is Partial→Shard(1) reduced once at the MoE boundary, with a LocalMapConfig converting DTensor→local so the grouped-GEMM runs on plain local tensors (github.com/.../MOE_SHARDING.md). Three EP all-to-all backends: a naive PyTorch all-to-all (degrades badly — ~50% of step time at EP=32), DeepEP (NVSHMEM+IBGDA, where dispatch-backward is combine), and MinimalAsyncEP (SymmetricMemory-native all-to-all-v via symm_mem.empty()/rendezvous(), enabling microbatch overlap without the DeepEP dependency).

The load-bearing Blackwell number is the PyTorch blog "Enabling up to 41% faster pre-training… DeepSeek-V3 on B200": DSV3-671B, 256× B200, seq 8192, EP=32, ETP=1, TP=2, PP=2, full AC — BF16+naive-EP = 651 tok/s; BF16+DeepEP = 859 (+32%); MXFP8-grouped-GEMM+DeepEP = 918 (+41%) (pytorch.org/blog/enabling-up-to-41-faster-pre-training). The decisive lesson: the DeepEP win (+32%) dominates the MXFP8 win (+9% incremental) — comms, not precision, is the lever.

AMD-side, TorchTitan+Primus-Turbo hit 96% scaling efficiency 128→1,024 GPUs on DSV3-671B with DeepEP capping EP comm at ~18% of runtime (vs ~50% naive) (pytorch.org/blog/efficient-moe-pre-training-at-scale-with-torchtitan) — proof the stack is genuinely vendor-portable, a non-obvious de-risking option for a GB200 buyer. The largest published dense run is Llama3-70B on 1,856 B200 GPUs (HSDP2, CP=2) with MXFP8 holding 1.22–1.28× over BF16 and only ~5% efficiency loss 4→188 nodes (pytorch.org/blog/accelerating-2k-scale-pre-training) — still ~18× below target.

Precision is delegated to torchao: Float8 tensorwise/rowwise with FSDP2 fp8 all-gather, MXFP8 (native torch._scaled_grouped_mm, ~28% over BF16 dense, ~1.2–1.8× on MoE grouped GEMMs), and an NVFP4 path that is QAT/inference-mature but not yet a validated end-to-end pretraining recipe as of mid-2026 — the precision frontier to watch, not to bet the cluster on. The comms substrate is SymmetricMemory (NVLS multimem_all_reduce/all_gather, NVSwitch v3+), on which async-TP is built (up to 29% fwd / 8% e2e on Llama3-8B) — but async-TP and the SymmetricMemory all-to-all are intra-node/NVSwitch-bound, and SGLang's testing found pure-SymmetricMemory all-to-all does not beat NCCL for large payloads after correctness fixes (github.com/sgl-project/sglang/issues/22583), which is why DeepEP remains the production multi-node EP path. On GB200 NVL72 the entire 72-GPU rack is one NVLink domain, which structurally expands the regime where SymmetricMemory/multimem and async-TP win — design EP inside an NVL72 domain.

Comms is the lever — ship DeepEP before chasing precision

The +41% TorchTitan result is +32% DeepEP, +9% MXFP8. On a GB200 buy, get the EP all-to-all onto DeepEP/HybridEP first; precision is the smaller, second-order win. And keep EP inside the NVL72 domain so SymmetricMemory/async-TP/multimem actually pay off.

Fault tolerance is the gating concern, and torchft is the credible PyTorch-native answer. torchft (meta-pytorch) gives checkpoint-free recovery: a global Lighthouse quorum server + per-replica-group managers run quorum-as-transaction (commit-or-rollback per step), and recovering HSDP replica groups pull weights from healthy peers via async P2P, not disk. The demo survived 2,000+ synthetic failures (one per 15s) on 300 L40S GPUs with 82.3% step efficiency at 60s-MTBF, and DiLoCo/Streaming-DiLoCo semi-sync gave 4,000 vs 1,200 tok/s on Ethernet (pytorch.org/blog/fault-tolerant-llama-training). But the demo is 1B params on 300 GPUs — three orders below target — and the FT path runs across replica groups (HSDP outer), adding a semi-sync outer loop you must validate against MoE convergence. The SPARe paper (arXiv 2603.00357) addresses the exact 100k+-GPU regime (arxiv.org/pdf/2603.00357).

TorchTitan's unproven edges at frontier scale

Bottom line: production-real for MoE to ~1K GPUs on both NVIDIA and AMD, the most composable and readable option, and where DTensor/SymmetricMemory/torchao innovations land first — but EP throughput parity with Megatron's GB200 TFLOPS, any published run beyond ~2K GPUs, and torchft at >10K-GPU MoE scale are all unproven.

06DeepSpeed: the legacy fallback

DeepSpeed pioneered ZeRO (stages 1–3), ZeRO-Offload/Infinity, and an early MoE path, and remains widely deployed. For a new 10T/200B run on 100k GB200, it is the wrong primary choice and warrants only a short verdict. Its ZeRO sharding is conceptually subsumed by Megatron-Core's precision-aware distributed optimizer and TorchTitan's FSDP2 + HSDP, both of which compose ZeRO-style sharding with the 5D parallelism and folded-EP that DeepSpeed's MoE never matched. DeepSpeed's MoE lacks the production-grade dropless dispatcher zoo (DeepEP/HybridEP), the fine-grained recompute/offload taxonomy, native MXFP8/NVFP4 grouped-GEMM training, and — decisively — the Blackwell-tuned NVL72 EP placement and modern resiliency (NVRx in-process restart, torchft quorum) that the other stacks now ship. There is no recent DeepSpeed GB200 MoE TFLOPS number remotely competitive with Megatron's 1,048 or TorchTitan's 918 tok/s DSV3 result.

Do not architect a frontier run on DeepSpeed

Keep it only as a fallback if a dependency forces it; do not architect a frontier run on it. Its ZeRO sharding is subsumed by Megatron's distributed optimizer and TorchTitan's FSDP2+HSDP, and it never matched the folded-EP, dropless dispatcher zoo, or Blackwell resiliency the other stacks ship.

07Head-to-head and recommendation

DimensionMegatron-Core (+Bridge)NeMo/Bridge layerMaxText/JAXTorchTitanDeepSpeed
Best published Blackwell MoE1,048 TF/s DSV3 (GB200)(= Megatron)2,241 TF/s 405B dense FSDP918 tok/s DSV3, 256×B200none competitive
Folded EP (attn≠expert mapping)Mature (Parallel Folding)inheritsyes (mesh axes)yes (sparse sub-mesh)no
NVL72-tuned dispatcherHybridEP (TMA+IBGDA)inheritsNVSHMEM via XLA (CP only)DeepEP / SymmetricMemoryno
FP8 / MXFP8 / NVFP4 trainingall three, validatedpresets + known bugsTE-owned on GPU; Qwix on TPUtorchao; NVFP4 not e2edated
Largest published GPU scale"thousands""thousands"128 (GPU) / 9,216 (TPU)1,856 dense / 1,024 MoE—
Native resilienceNVRx (in Bridge layer)NVRx full stackEmergency ckpt; Pathways=TPUtorchft (quorum, ckpt-free)basic
Debuggability / readabilitymediummediumhigh (XLA, config-edits)highest (DTensor)low
Frontier-arch trackingfastest (V4/Qwen3.5)fastestfast (V4 hash, DSA, mHC)mediumslow
 The five contenders across the eight dimensions that decide a 100k-GB200 MoE program.

Recommendation for the 10T/200B/100k-GB200 target. Run the production training on Megatron-Core driven by Megatron-Bridge, with HybridEP confined to NVL72 domains, ETP=1 with high EP for the fine-grained experts, TP-heavy folded attention for MLA, blockwise FP8 at bring-up migrating to MXFP8/NVFP4 (final four layers BF16), and the NVRx full resiliency stack (in-process restart + local/async checkpointing) as the non-negotiable substrate. This is the lowest-risk EP engine with the only credible Blackwell MoE throughput numbers and the most complete open resilience story.

Use TorchTitan as the de-risking and rapid-iteration vehicle — prototype recipes there (torchao precision, DTensor readability, fast 1→1K-GPU iteration) and run a head-to-head 1K-GB200 bake-off against Megatron using DSV3-685B as the reference, gating the 100k decision on EP-comm scaling inside NVL72 and torchft hardening. MaxText/JAX is the credible alternative only if your org has TPU-grade XLA expertise: it is architecturally ahead on quantization-as-config and already implements the DeepSeek-V4 lineage (hash routing, MLA, FP8 comms, DSA) in the user's actual codebase — but on a 100k GPU cluster you inherit unproven large-scale GSPMD, an unbenchmarked GPU expert all-to-all, and the absence of Pathways, so prefer it on Ironwood TPU and treat MaxText-on-GB200 as co-engineering. DeepSpeed is out for a new build.

The risks you are actually buying

Whichever you pick, no framework has a published >2K-GPU MoE MFU curve — Megatron caps at "thousands," TorchTitan at ~1,024–1,856, MaxText at 128 on GPU. At 100k you are past every documented envelope, the binding constraint is fault tolerance and silent-data-corruption resilience, not kernel throughput, and a multi-domain/multi-DC fleet will force Hierarchical-AllReduce-style outer loops nobody has demonstrated past ~3K GPUs. Budget for co-engineering with your silicon vendor, validate FP8/FP4 convergence on a 100B-token slice before committing the full token budget, and treat the ~20–35% dense-FP8 MFU band — not 50% — as your planning number.
