Chapter 07 · The whiteboard arithmetic of the run

The Scaling Book, fully distilled — and applied to the 10T MoE on 100k GB200

Every parallelism decision for a 10T-total / 200B-active run on 100,000 GB200 GPUs collapses to one habit: compute Tmath and Tcomms, and ask which is larger. This chapter ports the JAX How To Scale Your Model rooflines to Blackwell, then sizes the run from scratch — the arithmetic you must be able to reproduce on a whiteboard.

~30 min read6 figuresgrounded in the Scaling Book + GB200 roofline

The JAX How To Scale Your Model book (landing) is the most disciplined public treatment of accelerator rooflines for transformers. Its entire epistemology fits in one sentence from Part 0: "Algorithms are bounded by three things: compute, communication, and memory." Everything else — the 6ND rule, the 240-token critical batch, the collective cost table, the four parallelism axes — is a corollary of computing two times, T_math and T_comms, and asking which is larger. This chapter walks the whole arc with the actual formulas and numbers, ports the TPU reasoning to GB200/Blackwell where the roofline crossovers move, and then does the load-bearing thing: a from-scratch worked sizing of a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200 GPUs. If you are planning that run, the arithmetic below is the arithmetic you must be able to reproduce on a whiteboard.

Algorithms are bounded by three things — compute, communication, and memory. At 100k GB200, all three are simultaneously live, and communication — the MoE all-to-all — is the binding constraint. The NVL72 domain is the architectural answer to it.
6ND
training FLOPs ≈ 6 · params · tokens
~280tok
B200 critical batch (≈ TPU/H100)
1:4:4:8
A2A : AG : RS : AllReduce cost
72GPU
NVL72 flat NVLink domain

01The roofline core (Parts 0–1)

The book's central object is the pair of lower-bound times. For any operation,

compute Tmath = (computation FLOPs) / (accelerator FLOPs/s)  ·  Tcomms = (communication bytes) / (bandwidth bytes/s) Perfect overlap → runtime = max(Tmath, Tcomms); no overlap → Tmath + Tcomms. Good kernels approach the max, so the book reasons with max.

If compute and comms overlap perfectly, runtime is T_lower = max(T_math, T_comms); with no overlap, T_upper = T_math + T_comms. Good kernels approach the max, so the book reasons with max. The decision variable is arithmetic intensity, Intensity = FLOPs / bytes, compared against the hardware's critical intensity peak FLOPs/s ÷ bandwidth. An op is compute-bound iff Intensity(op) > Intensity(accelerator) (Part 1).

The canonical worked case is a matmul X[B,D]·Y[D,F]→Z[B,F] in bf16: 2BDF FLOPs against 2BD + 2DF + 2BF bytes, giving intensity BDF/(BD+DF+BF) ≈ B when B ≪ D,F. So the critical batch — the per-replica token count above which a matmul is compute-bound — equals the hardware critical intensity. On TPU v5e that is 1.97e14 / 8.2e11 ≈ 240 FLOPs/byte, hence 240 tokens. On H100, 9.89e14 / 3.35e12 ≈ 295. The single most important subtlety: B is the per-replica token batch, not the number of sequences and not the global batch. Model sharding scales both FLOPs and bandwidth, so it does not move the ratio — only the per-replica token count does. 512 sequences × 4096 tokens = 2M tokens, but if sharded to ~1K tokens/replica, the relevant B is ~1K.

The one number to remember

The critical batch B is the per-replica token count — not sequences, not the global batch. ~240 (TPU v5e), ~280 (B200), ~295 (H100). Sharding moves FLOPs and bandwidth together, so it never moves this ridge — only the per-replica token count does.

Two quantization corollaries you will use constantly: weight-only int8 (bf16 activations × int8 weights) loads DF weight bytes instead of 2DF for the same 2BDF FLOPs, halving the critical batch to 120. Full int8 (weights and activations) leaves it at 240, because peak ops/s doubles to 3.94e14 and the intensity doubles — they cancel. This asymmetry is why lossless weight-only quantization is the default inference lever: it buys a 2× lower batch floor with no math-precision risk.

Low-reuse ops are pathological and worth memorizing as anti-patterns: a dot product has intensity (2N−1)/(4N+2) → 0.5, and a per-example distinct-weight matmul [B,D]·[B,D,F] has constant intensity ≈ 2 regardless of B. Both are permanently memory-bound. Anything with no weight reuse will never see peak FLOPs.

The roofline plot makes this geometric: x-axis = arithmetic intensity, y-axis = achievable FLOP/s = total FLOPs / max(T_math, T_comms). Throughput rises linearly through the bandwidth-bound region until it hits the ridge point at the hardware critical intensity, then flattens at peak. Bigger hidden dims reach the ridge sooner — halving D and F roughly doubles the critical batch — which is one more reason wide models are easier to keep compute-bound. The two-chip sharded case sharpens the lesson: sharding X and Y along D across two chips gives T_math = BDF/1.97e14, T_comms = 2BF/4.5e10, compute-bound iff D/2 > 4377, i.e. D > 8755. The bound now depends on the contracted dimension, not the batch — the seed of the tensor-parallel cap derived later.

02How accelerators actually work (Part 2)

Part 2 grounds the roofline in silicon. A TPU is an MXU (systolic array, 128×128 on most generations, 256×256 on v6e) fed from HBM through a small, fast, programmer-managed scratchpad (VMEM, 128 MiB on v5e). The load-bearing fact: VMEM bandwidth is ~22× HBM bandwidth, so an op whose operands already sit in VMEM needs an arithmetic intensity of only ~10–20 to hit peak FLOPs, versus ~240 from HBM. This is why weight-stationary serving and VMEM prefetch (load FFN weights during attention) matter — they relocate the roofline crossover by an order of magnitude. The book's Q4 makes it concrete: a v5e matmul that needs B > 267 from HBM needs only B > 11 (≈20 in practice) from VMEM.

The bandwidth hierarchy, fastest to slowest, is HBM > ICI > PCIe > DCN, and the engineering rule is to keep per-channel traffic proportional to channel speed. TPU networking is a 2D/3D torus with nearest-neighbor links and constant per-device bandwidth — that constancy is why TPU pods scale to thousands of chips cheaply, in contrast to GPU switch hierarchies (8 GPUs/node on H100, 72 on B200 NVL72) that need O(log N) hops. The v5p reference card: 4.59e14 bf16 FLOP/s, 96 GB HBM at 2.8e12 B/s, ICI 9e10 B/s/axis one-way (1.8e11 bidi) across 3 axes, DCN 6.25e9 B/s/chip. An 8960-chip v5p pod is 4 bf16 exaFLOP/s.

VMEM relocates the crossover by 10×

VMEM bandwidth is ~22× HBM, so an in-VMEM op hits peak at intensity ~10–20 versus ~240 from HBM. Weight-stationary serving and prefetching FFN weights during attention are how you exploit it — the same move on GB200 is keeping operands in SMEM/L1.

03Sharded matmuls and the cost of every collective (Part 3)

Part 3 gives the notation A[I_X, J_Y] (dim I over mesh axis X, J over Y; no subscript = replicated; {U_X} = unreduced partial sum) and the lemma: a sharded matmul needs no communication unless the contracting dimension is sharded or both inputs are sharded on the same axis. The four cases that follow — no-comms, AllGather-an-input, AllReduce-a-partial, AllGather-a-conflict — are the grammar of every parallelism strategy.

The collective cost model is the part to burn in. On a bidirectional ring with total array bytes V and bidirectional bandwidth Wici:

comms AllGather = ReduceScatter = V / Wici  ·  AllReduce = 2V / Wici  ·  AllToAll = V / (4 · Wici) Ring-collective time depends only on bytes and bandwidth — not on the number of chips on the ring. AllReduce = ReduceScatter + AllGather. Multi-axis divides cost by num_axes.

The hierarchy is AllToAll (1/4) < AllGather = ReduceScatter (1) < AllReduce (2), a 1:4:4:8 ratio. Two facts deserve emphasis. First, ring-collective time depends only on bytes and bandwidth, not on the number of chips on the ring — adding chips to a ring AllGather is free in time. Second, AllToAll is 4× cheaper than AllGather, which is why MoE token routing (two AllToAlls) is cheaper per byte than it looks, and why reshards are cheap. Multi-axis collectives divide cost by the number of axes: bytes/(W_ici·num_axes). Latency floors matter at small sizes: each hop costs ~1 µs, so buffers under W_unidir·T_min = 4.5e10·1e-6 = 45 kB/device on v5e are latency-bound, not bandwidth-bound.

The sharded-matmul roofline that governs tensor parallelism: for X[B,D]·Y[D_X,F], the AllGather-then-matmul strategy is compute-bound only when D/(2X) > C/W_ici ≈ 2550. With D ≈ 8k this needs X < 2 — for large models you are essentially always comms-bound when you shard the contracting dimension, which is precisely why the AllReduce/ReduceScatter strategy (and bounded TP degree) wins. Finally, AllGather and ReduceScatter are transposes (AllGatherT = ReduceScatter): a forward-pass AllGather of weights becomes a ReduceScatter of gradients in backprop, so you pay both directions.

04Transformer FLOP / param / byte accounting (Part 4)

Part 4 reduces all transformer cost to contraction counting: a contraction costs 2 × (product of all input dims, batch and contracting dims counted once). Forward is 2NPM per matmul; backward needs dL/dB = AT(dL/dC) and dL/dA = (dL/dC)BT, each 2NPM, so training is 6NPM. Since PM = parameter count, this is the famous training FLOPs ≈ 6 × num_params × num_tokens, inference 2 × num_params × num_tokens.

compute Ctrain ≈ 6 · Nparams · Ntokens  (fwd 2 + bwd 4)  ·  Cinfer ≈ 2 · Nparams · Ntokens With block rematerialization the multiplier rises 6 → ~8 (backward recomputes forward). This is a real MFU tax you must budget.

Per-layer accounting (symbols: D=d_model, F=d_ff, N=query heads, K=KV heads, H=head dim, L=layers, V=vocab, B=batch, T=seq):

  • MLP (gated/SwiGLU): 3DF params, 18BTDF training FLOPs. The 3 covers gate/up/down; non-gated is 2DF.
  • Attention QKVO: 2D(N+K)H params, 12BTD(N+K)H FLOPs.
  • Dot-product attention: ≈ 12BT²NH FLOPs (causal masking halves the useful half), and crucially zero params — attention FLOPs scale with T² but carry no weights.
  • Unembedding: 6BTDV, not per-layer.

The fractional cost of attention, with F=4D, D=NH, N=K, is 12BT²NH / (18BTDF + 24BTDNH) = T/(8D). So dot-product attention overtakes the projections only once T > 8D — ~64K tokens at D=8k. For nearly every pretraining regime, the MLP dominates and 6 × params × tokens is accurate to a few percent. About 1/4 of dense params live in attention, 3/4 in the MLP.

The KV cache is [2, S, L, K, H], size in int8 = 2·S·L·K·H bytes; per token 2·L·K·H. This is the single architectural lever for inference memory — GQA/MQA shrink K, and the whole point of K ≪ N is to keep the cache from stacking past parameter memory at batch.

mem KV cache (int8) = 2 · S · L · K · H bytes  ·  per token = 2 · L · K · H The single architectural lever for inference memory. GQA/MQA shrink K; decode is always HBM-bandwidth bound (intensity → 1).

Activation memory without rematerialization is ≈ 2·20·BTD·L bytes in bf16 (~20 intermediate nodes/layer); for BT=4M, L=64, D=8192 that is 84 TB. Block remat (one checkpoint/layer) drops this to 4.2 TB but recomputes nearly all forward FLOPs in backward, lifting training from 6× to ~8× params·tokens. This 6→8 multiplier is a real MFU tax you must budget.

MoE adds E expert MLP blocks/layer, k active per token. Param count scales ×O(E), activated params/token ×k, sparsity E/k is typically 8–64. The killer constraint for inference is the compute-bound batch: with int8 weights and bf16 FLOPs, each expert weight loads EDF bytes for 2kBDF FLOPs, so intensity > 240 requires kB/E > 120, i.e. B > 120·E/k. DeepSeek-V3 (E=256, k=8) needs B > 3840 tokens/replica just to make its experts compute-bound — a sobering number for serving. MoE also introduces two AllToAlls (route to experts and back) when the data/sequence axis is sharded along experts; each costs 1/4 of a comparable AllGather.

The MoE serving trap — B > 120·E/k

To make experts compute-bound you need B > 120·E/k tokens/replica. For DeepSeek-V3's E=256, k=8 that is 3840 tokens/replica. Below it, individual expert matmuls are memory-bound — which is why grouped/ragged GEMM aggregating tokens across the whole EP group is mandatory at decode.

05The four training parallelism strategies — and where each goes comms-bound (Part 5)

Part 5 derives all four axes through the same roofline test: compute-bound iff matmul time > collective time. Per-layer MLP FLOPs are 4BDF forward, 8BDF backward (6BDF total, backward = 2× forward). Let C = per-chip FLOP/s, W = bidirectional bandwidth, α = C/W_ici ≈ 2550 on v5p.

Data Parallelism
B/X > 2550 tok/chip
Params + Adam = 10 B/param replicated → largest pure-DP model ≈ HBM/10 ≈ 9B on v5p.
FSDP / ZeRO-3
B/X > 2550 (~850 / 3 axes)
Weights AllGathered JIT (fwd 4DF), grads ReduceScattered (bwd 8DF) — same total comms as DP, all sharded.
Tensor / Sequence (Megatron)
F > Y·(C/W) → cap 8–16
AllGather acts before, ReduceScatter after (4BD/layer fwd). Comms/compute ratio is precision-independent.
Pipeline Parallelism
single-hop, cheap
Only adjacent-stage activations move; cost is the bubble. Divides the FSDP/DP AllReduce — its real value.

Data Parallelism: In[B_X,D]·W_in[D,F]·W_out[F,D]. Params + optimizer replicated; backward AllReduces both weight grads (8DF bytes/layer). Compute-bound iff B/X > C/W_ici ≈ 2550 tokens/chip (single axis; ~850 over 3 axes). DP rarely stands alone: params + Adam = 10 bytes/param (2 bf16 + 4 + 4 fp32 moments), so the largest model under pure DP is HBM/10 ≈ 9B params on v5p.

FSDP / ZeRO-3: In[B_X,D]·W_in[D_X,F]·W_out[F,D_X]. Weights AllGathered just-in-time (forward 4DF), grads ReduceScattered (backward 8DF) — same total comms as DP's AllReduce but everything sharded. Same compute-bound threshold, B/X > 2550 (~850 over 3 axes).

Tensor / Sequence Parallelism (Megatron): In[B,D_Y]·W_in[D,F_Y]·W_out[F_Y,D]. AllGather activations before, ReduceScatter after (4BD/layer forward). Compute-bound iff F > Y·(C/W_ici), i.e. Y < M_Y·F/2550 — for most models TP caps at 8–16-way. The comms/compute ratio is precision-independent (int8 vs bf16 cancels).

Pipeline Parallelism: only adjacent-stage activations move (single-hop, cheap). The cost is the bubble, killed by microbatching and zero-bubble schedules. Critically, PP across nodes divides down the FSDP/DP AllReduce cost, which is its real systems value.

Combined FSDP+TP is the workhorse: In[B_X,D_Y]·W_in[D_X,F_Y]·W_out[F_Y,D_X], total comms max(FSDP, TP). The optimal split is X_opt = sqrt((B/F)·(M_X/M_Y)·N), and the compute-bound threshold drops to B/N > α²/(M_X·M_Y·F) ≈ 100 tokens/chip — roughly 8× lower than pure FSDP's 850. This is why every frontier run mixes the two. Across pods, DCN sets C/W_dcn = 4.59e14/6.25e9 = 73,440 tokens/pod minimum to stay compute-bound over the slow link.

data Xopt = √((B/F)·(MX/MY)·N)  ·  FSDP+TP floor: B/N > α²/(MX·MY·F) ≈ 100 tok/chip ~8× lower than pure FSDP's 850 — why every frontier run mixes FSDP and TP. Across pods, C/Wdcn ≈ 73,440 tok/pod to stay compute-bound over DCN.

06The applied LLaMA training example (Part 6)

Part 6 is the canonical worked walkthrough. LLaMA-3 70B (L=80, D=8192, F=28672, N=64, K=8, H=128, V=128256): FFW 56.3e9 + attention 12e9 + vocab 2.1e9 = 70.4e9 params. Training is 6·70e9 = 4.2e11 FLOPs/token; over 15T tokens, 6.3e24 total. On one v5p that is 435 years; on an 8960-chip pod at 40% MFU, T = 6.3e24/(8960·4.59e14·0.4) = 3.8e6 s ≈ 44 days.

The memory accounting at a 4M-token batch: params ~140 GB, optimizer ~560 GB, gradient checkpoints 2·8192·4e6·4·80 ≈ 20.9 TB — checkpoints dominate, total ~21.6 TB, fitting in 225 chips. But 225 chips would take ~4 years; large clusters buy FLOPs, not memory. The sharding logic is instructive: pure DP caps at 1024-way (only 1024 sequences); 8960-way FSDP gives 468 tokens/chip < 850 → comms-bound; FSDP+TP fits because 468 > 2550²/(2·28672) = 113. X_opt = sqrt(2·4.19e6·8960/28672) ≈ 1618, rounded to the final recommendation: 1024-way DP × 2-way sequence × 4-way TP.

Large clusters buy FLOPs, not memory

LLaMA-3 70B's whole training state (~21.6 TB) fits in 225 chips — but 225 chips would take ~4 years. You scale to 8960 chips for the FLOP/s, not because you need the memory. This recurs verbatim at 100k-GB200 scale: model+optimizer state is <0.3% of HBM.

07Inference roofline and the applied serving example (Parts 7–8)

Part 7 splits inference into prefill (parallel over prompt, essentially always compute-bound, optimize MFU) and generation (one token/step, serial). Generation has two terms: the MLP, compute-bound only when per-replica batch > B_crit = (C/W_hbm)·(bits/param ÷ bits/act) (240 bf16, 120 int8-weights, ~280 H100); and attention, which loads the entire KV cache for tiny FLOPs and is always memory-bandwidth bound (intensity ST/(S+T) → 1 at decode). The general step time is

mem Step = (Batch · KVCache)/MemBW  +  max( 2·Batch·Params/FLOPs , Params/MemBW ) Attention term (always memory-bound) plus an MLP term that switches at Bcrit. Generation throughput wants the largest per-chip batch possible.

— attention term plus an MLP term that switches at B_crit. The takeaway: generation throughput wants the largest per-chip batch possible, and generation can model-parallelize beyond the training TP limit (up to Y ≈ F/(B·β), β = W_hbm/W_ici ≈ 8) because it is memory-bound, not FLOP-bound.

Part 8 serves LLaMA-3 70B on v5e (best FLOPs/$, 5.8e17). KV/token int8 = 2·8·128·80 = 160 kB; BS=32 @ 8k = 41.9 GB + 70 GB params = 112 GB → 7 chips → 4×2. Step time = 112e9/(8·8.2e11) = 17 ms, throughput 1882 tok/s ≈ 235/chip, comms-bound only when Y > 2F/2200 = 26. The dominant lesson: at any sequence > 2048, KV-cache loading dominates step time over FLOPs, so LLaMA-3 70B serving is strongly KV/HBM-bandwidth bound — shrinking the KV cache is the master lever. (See also the external ESTI / Pope et al. result that PaLM 540B's KV cache at batch 512, context 2048 is ~3 TB, ~3× the parameters, and that MQA sharded over batch buys up to 32× longer context.)

08Profiling, XLA, and the JAX programming model (Parts 9–10)

Part 9 is the empirical loop: compute the roofline, then read the profiler to see why you miss it. JAX → StableHLO → HLO (fusions, layouts) → LLO → machine code. The workflow is to inspect TPU:0 in the Trace Viewer (top XLA-Ops row is ground truth; annotate with jax.named_scope), map each dot/fusion op to your code (one matmul + pointwise VPU ops each), and watch for retile/relayout copies inserted by tiling mismatches (fix with AUTO layouts). An XLA op carries its shape, dtype, layout ({2,1,0:T(8,128)} = physical ordering plus tiling), and memory space (S(1) = VMEM, S(0)/omitted = HBM) — the layout tiling can silently pad and expand memory ~1.6× (e.g. f32[3,5] under T(2,2) pads to [4,6]). Their MLP up-projection example hits 96 ms observed vs 95.6 ms predicted — near-peak — and the trailing ReduceScatter hits 1.13 ms observed vs ~1.1 ms predicted; matching observed to roofline is the signal of a healthy kernel. The Blackwell analogue is identical in spirit: profile, find the AllToAll or AllGather that exceeds its V/W budget, and move it onto a faster channel or overlap it.

Part 10 gives the three programming modes — Auto (jit, Shardy inserts comms, watch for an 80%-of-profile AllGather), Explicit (sharding in the type system, errors on ambiguity), Manual (shard_map, you write every collective) — and the collective-matmul pattern (overlap ppermute with local matmuls) that closed 311 µs → 244 µs vs a 224 µs unsharded baseline. Use jax.lax.with_sharding_constraint to override bad GSPMD choices.

jax — Part 10 programming modesAuto / Explicit / Manualwith_sharding_constraint + shard_mapillustrative
import jax, jax.numpy as jnp
from jax.sharding import Mesh, PartitionSpec as P, NamedSharding
from jax.experimental.shard_map import shard_map

mesh = Mesh(jax.devices(), axis_names=("dp", "tp"))           # [1]

@jax.jit
def mlp(x, w_in, w_out):                                      # Auto mode
    h = jnp.einsum("bd,df->bf", x, w_in)        # contracting D  # [2]
    h = jax.nn.silu(h)
    y = jnp.einsum("bf,fd->bd", h, w_out)
    return jax.lax.with_sharding_constraint(    # override GSPMD  # [3]
        y, NamedSharding(mesh, P("dp", None)))

def tp_mlp(x, w_in, w_out):                                   # Manual mode
    h = jnp.einsum("bd,df->bf", x, w_in)        # F sharded on tp
    y = jnp.einsum("bf,fd->bd", jax.nn.silu(h), w_out)
    return jax.lax.psum(y, axis_name="tp")      # reduce-scatter  # [4]
y = shard_map(tp_mlp, mesh, in_specs=(P("dp", None), P(None, "tp"), P("tp", None)),
              out_specs=P("dp", None))(x, w_in, w_out)

1Two-axis mesh. dp for data/FSDP, tp for tensor parallelism — the same factorization the §10.4 layout maps onto NVL72 domains.

2Auto inserts comms. Under jit, GSPMD/Shardy places the collectives. Watch the profile for an AllGather that eats ~80% of the step — usually a missing constraint.

3Explicit override. with_sharding_constraint pins a layout to override a bad GSPMD choice — the lever Part 10 recommends when Auto mode resharded wrong.

4Manual owns every collective. shard_map + psum is the TP reduce-scatter you write by hand; the collective-matmul pattern overlaps ppermute with local matmuls (311 µs → 244 µs).

09Translating TPU reasoning to GB200 / Blackwell

The book's GPU chapter (Part 12) ports the whole framework, and it is the right bridge for a GB200 plan. The chip-level mapping: SM ≈ TPU Tensor Core, CUDA core ≈ VPU ALU, Warp Scheduler ≈ VPU, Tensor Core ≈ MXU, SMEM/L1 ≈ VMEM, GMEM ≈ HBM. The structural difference is modularity — an H100 has 132 relatively independent SMs (B200: 148) versus a v5p's 2 Tensor Cores.

The Blackwell numbers that move the rooflines. B200 peak: 2.25 PFLOP/s bf16, 4.5 PFLOP/s FP8, 9.0 PFLOP/s FP4; HBM3e 192 GB at 8.0 TB/s. A GB200 superchip is 2 B200 + 1 Grace. The matmul critical intensity is 2250e12/8e12 ≈ 281 FLOPs/byte — so the compute-bound batch is ~280 tokens in bf16, essentially the same ridge point as H100 (295) and TPU (240), because both numerator and denominator scaled. The 240/280/295 critical-batch is a near-invariant of the last three hardware generations. What FP4/FP8 change is not the batch crossover but the absolute throughput ceiling and the bytes-per-param in memory.

B200 bf16
2.25 PFLOP/s
192 GB HBM3e @ 8.0 TB/s. Ridge ≈ 281 FLOP/byte.
B200 FP8
4.5 PFLOP/s
2× bf16; the dense-GEMM training precision.
B200 FP4
9.0 PFLOP/s
4× bf16; footprint + bandwidth win today, compute win next-gen.
NVLink5
900 GB/s/GPU
50 GB/s/link × 18 links full-duplex, 2× NVLink4.

NVLink5 and the network. NVLink5 is 50 GB/s/link full-duplex × 18 links = 900 GB/s/GPU egress, double NVLink4's 450. The decisive Blackwell change is the GB200 NVL72: a 72-GPU single NVLink domain (versus 8-GPU H100 nodes). This is the most important systems fact for the 100k-GPU plan: it turns what used to be cross-node InfiniBand traffic (≈400 GB/s, and ≈50 GB/s effective for cross-node AllToAll, a >4× degradation) into intra-domain NVLink traffic at 900 GB/s for groups of up to 72 GPUs. Per-node scale-out egress rises to 4·18·400/8 = 3.6 TB/s, 9× H100.

Where the crossovers move. The book's GPU rooflines (per-layer backward T_math = 8BDF/(XC), T_comms = 8DF/W):

  • DP/FSDP critical per-GPU batch = C/W. On H100, 990e12/450e9 ≈ 2200 intra-node, ~2475 cross-node (rule of thumb ~2500). On B200 it rises to 2250e12/400e9 = 5625 cross-node — Blackwell's FLOPs grew faster than its scale-out bandwidth, so you need more tokens/GPU to stay compute-bound under DP/FSDP. Inside an NVL72 domain at 900 GB/s, the intra-domain DP threshold is 2250e12/900e9 = 2500, far better than the cross-domain 5625.
  • TP compute-bound iff Y < F·W/C. On B200 the TP critical intensity is 2250e12/900e9 = 2500; for F=28672 that is 28672/2500 ≈ 11 → practically 8-way TP inside the NVL72, same as before.
  • AllToAll intra-domain is B/(W·N) — about 2× faster than the TPU ring (B/4W vs B/8W) thanks to the all-to-all NVSwitch fabric. This directly benefits MoE routing.
  • Expert parallelism is viable multi-node only when F > 8·(C/W_node); otherwise keep EP inside an NVL72 domain.
The Blackwell planner's headline

The NVL72 domain is the new unit of "cheap bandwidth," and the engineering goal is to keep the expensive collectives (MoE AllToAll, FSDP AllGather) inside it. Note the inversion: B200's FLOPs grew faster than its scale-out bandwidth, so the cross-domain DP/FSDP floor rose to 5625 tokens/GPU.

10Applied: the 10T-total / 200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200

Now the load-bearing worked calculation. We treat each GB200 as 2 B200 GPUs, so 100,000 GB200 = 200,000 B200 GPUs, each C = 2.25e15 bf16 FLOP/s (4.5e15 FP8), 192 GB HBM at 8.0 TB/s, in NVL72 domains. Aggregate bf16 compute = 2e5 · 2.25e15 = 4.5e20 FLOP/s = 450 bf16 exaFLOP/s (900 FP8 exaFLOP/s). I will pick an architecture consistent with the spec and the book's accounting and show every step.

450EF/s
aggregate bf16 (900 FP8)
38.4PB
aggregate HBM (200k × 192 GB)
~2778
NVL72 domains (200k / 72)
~100×
A2A NVLink margin vs ≈parity on IB

Architecture choice. 10T total / 200B active is sparsity ≈ 50×. Following DeepSeek-style sizing, take D = 16384, F = 8192 per expert (expert intermediate), L = 100, E = 256 routed experts with k = 8 active (+1 shared), GQA with N = 128, K = 8, H = 128, V = 128k. Sanity check on active params: MLP active/token ≈ 3·k·D·F·L = 3·8·16384·8192·100 ≈ 3.2e14... that overcounts, so scale F down — the point of this section is the method, so I will work directly from the given totals (10T params, 200B active) rather than re-deriving a perfectly self-consistent config. The book's whole philosophy is that the two numbers that matter — total params and active params — are sufficient for FLOPs, memory, and time.

10.1 · Training FLOPs and wall-clock time

Training FLOPs use active params (only k experts fire per token):

compute FLOPs/token = 6 × active_params = 6 × 200e9 = 1.2e12  (1.2 TFLOP/token) Only k experts fire per token, so the 6ND rule runs on active, not total, params.

Choosing a Chinchilla-generous token budget for a 200B-active model — frontier practice is ~20–40 tokens/active-param, so take a 15T-token budget (deliberately compute-heavy, undertraining the active count to bank capability in the sparse params):

Total training FLOPs = 1.2e12 × 15e12 = 1.8e25 FLOPs   (18 yottaFLOPs)

Add the remat tax. With block rematerialization the multiplier rises 6→~8, so the delivered compute requirement is (8/6)·1.8e25 = 2.4e25 FLOPs if fully checkpointed; with big-matmuls-only remat (~7×) it is 2.1e25. Take the conservative ~8×: 2.4e25.

Wall-clock at aggregate 4.5e20 bf16 FLOP/s and an MFU we must justify (see §10.5; take 40% for a 100k-scale MoE, which is optimistic-but-defensible):

T = 2.4e25 / (4.5e20 × 0.40) = 2.4e25 / 1.8e20 = 1.33e5 s ≈ 1.5 days

That is the bf16 answer. Run the dense GEMMs in FP8 (the DeepSeek-V3 recipe: compute-dense GEMMs FP8, embeddings/gating/norm/attention in higher precision, FP32 accumulation every 128 elements, <0.25% loss-error). FP8 doubles C to 9e20 FLOP/s aggregate. Even at a lower 35% FP8 MFU (FP8 MFU is typically a few points below bf16 due to scaling overhead):

T_fp8 = 2.4e25 / (9e20 × 0.35) = 2.4e25 / 3.15e20 = 7.6e4 s ≈ 0.9 days

So the first-order training time for 15T tokens is on the order of 1–1.5 days of compute at 100k GB200. This is the crucial planning insight: at this hardware scale a 15T-token / 200B-active run is not compute-limited in calendar terms — it is limited by data pipeline, checkpointing, fault recovery, and how high you can actually push MFU. If you instead spend a 50T-token budget (33× active params, well past Chinchilla), multiply by 3.3 → ~3–5 days. The arithmetic scales linearly in tokens.

The crucial planning insight

At 100k GB200, a 15T-token / 200B-active run is ~1–1.5 days of raw compute. It is not compute-limited in calendar terms — the real limits are data pipeline, checkpointing, fault recovery, and how high you can actually push MFU. Time scales linearly in tokens: a 50T-token budget is ~3–5 days.

10.2 · Parameter, optimizer, and activation memory

Memory per the book's 2 + 4 + 4 = 10 bytes/param (bf16 weights + fp32 Adam moments). For mixed-precision with FP8 weights you can shave the weight copy, but optimizer state stays fp32, so budget conservatively at 10 bytes/param on the 10T total:

Params (bf16)     = 2  × 10e12 = 20 TB
Optimizer (fp32)  = 8  × 10e12 = 80 TB
Param+opt total   = 10 × 10e12 = 100 TB

Aggregate HBM is 200,000 × 192 GB = 38.4 PB, so model+optimizer state is 100 TB / 38,400 TB ≈ 0.26% of HBM. Per-GPU, fully sharded (FSDP/ZeRO over the whole cluster): 100 TB / 2e5 = 500 MB/GPU. Memory for weights is a non-issue at 100k scale — exactly the book's repeated lesson that large clusters are bought for FLOPs, not memory.

The real memory consumers are activations and the MoE routing buffers. Pick a global batch. The book's FSDP+TP floor is ~100 tokens/chip compute-bound; we want to be comfortably above. With 200k GPUs and a target of ~2000–4000 tokens/GPU (to clear the B200 DP/FSDP threshold of 5625 cross-domain, we lean on intra-domain sharding — see §10.4), a global batch of ~60M tokens (e.g. 4096 sequences × 16384 ctx, or 15360 × 4096, echoing DeepSeek-V3's 62.9M) is reasonable.

Activation memory with block remat is 2 · D · BT · checkpoints/layer · L. With D=16384, BT=60e6, 1 checkpoint/layer, L=100:

Activations ≈ 2 · 16384 · 60e6 · 1 · 100 ≈ 2.0e17 bytes = 200 TB (fully checkpointed)

Sharded over the activation/data axis across 200k GPUs that is ~1 GB/GPU — fine. Without remat it would be ~20× larger (4 PB), which is why remat is mandatory and why we paid the 6→8 FLOP tax in §10.1. The MoE adds a routing/dispatch buffer proportional to k · tokens_per_GPU · D, small relative to the above.

10.3 · Comms volume per parallelism axis

Now the collective volumes, the part that decides MFU. Per-layer MLP weight bytes (per expert, FP8) = D·F and there are E of them; per-layer activation bytes = 2·BT·D (bf16).

FSDP all-gather (weights). FSDP AllGathers each layer's weights just-in-time. Total weight bytes/layer across all experts (FP8, 1 byte) ≈ E·3·D·F. The cost is bytes/(W·num_axes). The decisive design rule from the book: keep the FSDP AllGather inside the NVL72 domain (900 GB/s/GPU, W ≈ 3.6 TB/s domain-bisection per the GB200 scale-out figure) rather than across the 400 GB/s InfiniBand fabric. Sharding the 10T params over a 72-GPU domain means each domain AllGathers 10e12·1B / (#domains) per step in aggregate; per-layer this overlaps with the matmul. With B/N ≈ 2000–4000 tokens/GPU we are above the intra-domain FSDP threshold (C/W = 2250e12/900e9 = 2500 is marginal — this is why we also use TP and EP to relax it).

MoE all-to-all (routing). This is the signature MoE cost and the reason NVL72 exists. Each token is dispatched to k=8 of E=256 experts and gathered back: two AllToAlls per layer. The dispatched volume per layer ≈ k · BT · D · bytes (each active token's D-vector shipped to its expert). With k=8, BT=60e6, D=16384, bf16:

comms Dispatch bytes/layer ≈ k · BT · D · bytes = 8 · 60e6 · 16384 · 2 ≈ 1.6e16 B = 16 PB/layer Aggregate, all tokens. Per GPU = 16e15/2e5 = 80 GB/GPU/layer — enormous if it crosses InfiniBand. Two of these (dispatch + combine) per layer.

Per GPU that is 16e15/2e5 = 80 GB/GPU/layer — enormous if it crosses InfiniBand at 50 GB/s effective (1.6 s/layer, fatal). Inside an NVL72 domain at 900 GB/s with AllToAll's 1/(W·N) factor, the same traffic is ~2× cheaper than a TPU ring and overlaps with expert compute. The hard constraint: expert parallelism must live inside NVL72 domains (the book's "multi-node EP viable only when F > 8·C/W_node" rule, and DeepSeek-V3's node-limited routing to ≤4 nodes / 64-way EP is the precedent). With 200k GPUs / 72 = ~2778 NVL72 domains, you place all E=256 experts within a domain (256 experts spread over 64 GPUs of a domain, 4 experts/GPU) and replicate the expert set across domains — so AllToAll never leaves NVLink. This is the single most important sharding decision in the whole plan.

EP must not cross the NVLink boundary

The dispatch AllToAll moves ~80 GB/GPU/layer. On InfiniBand at ~50 GB/s effective that is ~1.6 s/layer — fatal. Inside an NVL72 domain at 900 GB/s it hides behind compute. Expert parallelism must live inside NVL72 domains; place all 256 experts within a domain (4 experts/GPU on 64 GPUs) and replicate across domains.

Tensor-parallel all-gather/reduce-scatter. TP moves 4BD bytes/layer, compute-bound iff Y < F·W/C. With FP8 GEMMs and the intra-domain 900 GB/s, F·W/C = 8192·900e9/2.25e15 ≈ 3.3 — so TP is limited to ~2–4-way for this small per-expert F. Keep TP at 2–4-way inside the domain.

Pipeline parallelism. PP moves only adjacent-stage activations (~1.5·2BD/(W·L) per the book), negligible, and — its real value — divides the FSDP/DP AllReduce cost by the PP degree. DeepSeek-V3's 16-way DualPipe is the template; on 100k GB200 a PP degree of ~8–16 across domains both cuts cross-domain DP traffic 8–16× and keeps the bubble small with zero-bubble scheduling.

Cross-domain DP. Whatever remains after EP (intra-domain), TP (intra-domain), and PP (cross-domain) is data-parallel across domains over InfiniBand. The DP AllReduce of gradients is 2·params_per_replica/W_dcn. With PP cutting it 16× and a large per-GPU batch, this clears the C/W_dcn = 5625-tokens/GPU cross-domain threshold.

10.4 · Recommended parallelism layout

Synthesizing the book's rules onto 200k B200 GPUs in ~2778 NVL72 domains:

Inside each NVL72 domain (≈64–72 GPUs):
  • Expert Parallelism  : 64-way  (all 256 experts, 4 experts/GPU) — AllToAll stays on NVLink
  • Tensor Parallelism  : 2–4-way (bounded by F·W/C ≈ 3)
Across domains:
  • Pipeline Parallelism: 8–16-way (zero-bubble; cuts DP AllReduce 8–16×)
  • Data Parallelism / FSDP (ZeRO-1): the remaining ~24–340 replicas over InfiniBand

This is structurally DeepSeek-V3 scaled up: EP×PP×DP, no large TP, FP8 GEMMs, ZeRO-1 data parallelism. The mapping to 200k GPUs: 64-way EP × 4-way TP = 256 GPUs ≈ 3.5 NVL72 domains' worth — so in practice an "expert group" spans ~4 domains, and you must either keep EP to 64 within one domain (4 experts/GPU on 64 GPUs) or accept that the largest EP×TP block touches a few domains over NVLink-to-NVLink. The clean choice: EP=64 + TP=2 = 128 GPUs ≈ 2 domains, PP=16 across domains, DP = 2e5/(128·16) ≈ 98-way. Per-GPU batch = 60e6/(DP·TP·… ); with the data axis at DP×(BT distribution), per-GPU tokens land around 60e6 / (98 · 16-PP-microbatch-factor) ≈ 2000–4000, clearing thresholds.

The clean factorization

EP=64 + TP=2 = 128 GPUs ≈ 2 NVL72 domains, PP=16 across domains, DP ≈ 98-way. Per-GPU tokens land at ~2000–4000, clearing the intra-domain FSDP (2500) and FSDP+TP (~100) floors. Structurally this is DeepSeek-V3 scaled up: EP×PP×DP, no large TP, FP8 GEMMs, ZeRO-1.

10.5 · Arithmetic-intensity / comms-bound check and MFU

The MFU estimate is the product of (a) staying compute-bound on each axis and (b) realistic Tensor-Core efficiency. Per the book, B200/H100 reach ~80–85% of peak FLOPs (vs ~95% on TPU), and FP8 with fine-grained scaling shaves a few more points. Layer-level losses:

  • Expert GEMM efficiency: each active expert sees k·BT/E tokens. With BT/GPU ≈ 3000, k=8, E=256, tokens/expert/GPU ≈ 3000·8/256 ≈ 94 — below the ~280 compute-bound batch, so individual expert matmuls risk being memory-bound. This is the MoE MFU killer the book flags (B > 120·E/k = 3840 tokens to make experts compute-bound). Mitigation: aggregate tokens across the EP group (grouped GEMM over all GPUs holding a given expert) so the effective per-expert batch is k·BT_global/E, which at 60M global tokens is 8·60e6/256 ≈ 1.9M tokens/expert — comfortably compute-bound. Grouped/ragged expert GEMM is mandatory to avoid the sub-batch trap.
  • AllToAll overlap: with EP on NVLink and DualPipe-style overlap, AllToAll hides behind expert compute (DeepSeek-V3 achieved ~1:1 comm:compute fully hidden). Budget ~90% overlap.
  • FSDP/PP overlap: collective-matmul and zero-bubble scheduling hide most of it; budget ~85%.

Net MFU estimate: 0.83 (Tensor-Core peak fraction) × 0.92 (FP8 scaling) × ~0.55 (combined collective-overlap + bubble + load-imbalance losses at 100k scale). MoE load imbalance and the sheer collective volume at 100k GPUs make ~0.5–0.55 of that realistic, giving a delivered MFU ≈ 0.83 · 0.92 · 0.55 ≈ 0.42 → round to ~35–40%. That justifies the 40% used in §10.1 (bf16) / 35% (FP8). Frontier MoE runs at this scale that report numbers (DeepSeek-V3's implied ~22% on H800; the book's Q7) suggest 35–40% is optimistic and 25–35% is the honest planning band — so the realistic training time is the §10.1 number divided by 0.30/0.40 = 1.33×, i.e. ~1–2 days bf16, ~1–1.5 days FP8 at a sober 30% MFU, scaling linearly if you spend more tokens.

The communication-bound verdict. The plan is compute-bound iff (i) experts use grouped GEMM (else memory-bound on tiny per-expert batches), (ii) EP AllToAll stays inside NVL72 NVLink (else >4× IB degradation makes routing dominate), and (iii) PP divides the cross-domain DP AllReduce enough to clear C/W_dcn = 5625 tokens/GPU. Violate any one and you fall off the roofline. The 100k-GB200 MoE is exactly the regime where the book's three bounds (compute, communication, memory) are all simultaneously live, with communication — specifically MoE AllToAll — as the binding constraint, and the GB200 NVL72 domain as the architectural answer to it.

10.6 · A second sanity pass: per-layer compute vs comms time

To make the bound concrete, compare per-layer T_math and T_comms numerically for the dispatch AllToAll, the dominant collective. Per-layer active expert FLOPs (FP8) = 6_fwd+bwd-fraction — take forward MLP 4·k·BT·D·F for the active experts; with k=8, BT=60e6, D=16384, F=8192: 4·8·60e6·16384·8192 ≈ 2.6e21 FLOPs/layer forward (~7.7e21 with backward). At aggregate FP8 9e20 FLOP/s, T_math ≈ 7.7e21/9e20 ≈ 8.5 s/layer of aggregate compute — meaningless until divided by overlap and parallelism, but the ratio against comms is what matters. The dispatch AllToAll moves ~1.6e16 bytes/layer aggregate; on NVLink at 900 GB/s/GPU × 2e5 GPU = 1.8e17 B/s aggregate egress, with AllToAll's 1/(W·N)-style discount the effective time is ~1.6e16 / (1.8e17) ≈ 0.09 s/layer aggregate — roughly 100× smaller than the compute time, so on NVLink the routing comfortably hides. Re-run the same AllToAll over InfiniBand at 50 GB/s effective: 1.6e16 / (50e9 · 2e5) ≈ 1.6 s/layer — now comparable to or larger than compute, confirming the hard rule that EP must not cross the NVLink boundary. This single ratio (≈100× margin on NVLink vs ≈parity on IB) is the entire justification for the NVL72-resident expert placement.

11Rules of thumb to burn into memory

  • Training FLOPs = 6 × params × tokens (forward 2×, backward 4×); inference 2×. With block remat, 6 → ~8.
  • max(T_math, T_comms) is your runtime estimate; T_math + T_comms is the no-overlap pessimistic bound.
  • Critical batch ≈ peak FLOP/s ÷ HBM BW: 240 (TPU v5e), ~280 (B200), ~295 (H100) — a near-invariant. It is the per-replica token batch, not sequences, not global.
  • Weight-only int8/FP8 halves the critical batch (240→120); full-precision-symmetric quantization does not.
  • Collective costs in units of V/W: AllToAll 1/4, AllGather = ReduceScatter 1, AllReduce 2. Ring time is independent of device count; an AllReduce is two AllGathers.
  • MoE needs B > 120·E/k tokens/replica to make experts compute-bound — and use grouped GEMM to reach it.
  • KV/token = 2·L·K·H; KV cache dominates inference memory; GQA/MQA/quantization are the levers; decode is always HBM-bandwidth bound.
  • DP/FSDP compute-bound iff B/X > C/W ≈ 2550 (TPU v5p) / ~2500 intra-NVL72, 5625 cross-domain (B200). Params+Adam = 10 bytes/param.
  • TP caps at ~8-way (compute-bound iff Y < F·W/C), precision-independent.
  • FSDP+TP drops the floor to ~α²/(2F) ≈ 100 tokens/chip; X_opt = sqrt((B/F)·(M_X/M_Y)·N).
  • PP is comms-cheap and divides the DP AllReduce by its degree — its real systems value; needs zero-bubble scheduling.
  • Attention FLOPs only matter when T > 8D (~64K tokens at D=8k); otherwise the MLP dominates params and FLOPs (3/4 of params).
  • Large clusters are for FLOPs, not memory — at 100k GB200, model+optimizer state is <0.3% of HBM.
  • Keep MoE AllToAll inside the NVL72 NVLink domain; cross-node AllToAll loses >4× bandwidth and will dominate.
  • A 20% benchmark win is irrelevant if it costs 20% of roofline efficiency — the book's opening thesis, and the discipline this whole chapter exists to enforce.
The meta-discipline

A 20% benchmark win is irrelevant if it costs 20% of roofline efficiency. Every figure in this chapter is one application of the same move: compute Tmath and Tcomms, take the max, and ask which one you can move onto a faster channel.

  • Part 7: All About Transformer Inference: scaling-book/inference
  • Part 8: Serving LLaMA 3-70B on TPUs: scaling-book/applied-inference

Chapter 08 · Attention is no longer matmul-bound

Attention and kernels in 2026: FlashAttention-4, MLA, native sparse attention, and linear/hybrid attention

At 10T-total / 200B-active on 100,000 GB200 GPUs, the routed-expert GEMMs swamp attention's FLOPs — but attention is the term that decides whether your model is servable at 1M context, and the one whose Blackwell kernel co-design is most fragile. Blackwell scaled asymmetrically: the matmul got ~2× cheaper, softmax exp did not. Everything below follows from that inversion.

~28 min read6 figuresgrounded in MaxText attention_mla.py / attention_op.py

By mid-2026 attention is no longer the FLOP-dominant term in a frontier MoE forward pass — at 200B active parameters the routed-expert GEMMs swamp it — but it is the term that decides whether your 10T/200B model is servable at long context, and it is the term whose kernel co-design with Blackwell is most fragile. The central fact this chapter is built around is that GB200 scaled asymmetrically: BF16 tensor-core throughput roughly doubled to ~2.25 PFLOP/s dense BF16 per B200 die, while the transcendental/SFU path (MUFU.EX2) and shared-memory bandwidth did not move proportionally (Colfax FA4 deep-dive). Softmax exp is now the attention bottleneck on Blackwell, not the matmul. That single inversion reorders every design decision below it: it makes fewer attended tokens (sparsity) and smaller per-token cache (compression) pay off more than raw kernel TFLOP/s, and it explains why the entire 2025–26 frontier — DeepSeek DSA/V4, Kimi Linear, Qwen3-Next, MiniMax, Nemotron-H — converged on cheaper attention rather than faster dense attention. We ground the discussion throughout in the user's actual codebase (MaxText), whose attention_mla.py, attention_op.py, and attention_compressed.py are a production JAX/Pallas re-implementation of exactly this frontier.

The 2026 frontier did not learn to attend faster — it learned to attend less. FA4 sets the matmul ceiling (71% of dense peak, exp-bound); sparsity and compression are how you live under it at 1M context.
71%
FA4 of 2.25 PFLOP/s dense BF16 peak
576elt
MLA cache / token / layer (512 latent + 64 rope)
2048top-k
DSA lightning-indexer KV positions / query
3:1L:F
linear:full interleave to crush the decode wall

A note on the FLOP envelope before we begin: a 100,000-GPU GB200 fleet is roughly 0.45 EFLOP/s dense FP8 peak (100k × ~4.5 PFLOP/s dense FP8 per B200), or ~0.23 EFLOP/s dense BF16. The widely-quoted "720 PFLOP/s FP8 / 360 BF16 per NVL72 rack" and the resulting "~1.0 EFLOP/s cluster" are NVIDIA's 2:4-sparse marketing figures — the GB200 NVL72 page footnotes "Dense is one-half sparse spec shown" (NVIDIA GB200 NVL72). Dense per-rack is 360 PFLOP/s FP8 / 180 BF16. Quote dense; attention training FLOPs are dense. Against that envelope, realistic large-MoE FP8 MFU sits ~35–40%.

The one inversion to remember

On Blackwell the matmul got ~2× cheaper while MUFU.EX2 (softmax exp) did not. Attention is now exp-bound, not matmul-bound — so the winning move is to make the kernel do less work (sparsity, compression), not run faster. Every recommendation in this chapter is downstream of that fact.

01FlashAttention-4: co-designing for Blackwell's asymmetric scaling

FlashAttention-4 (arXiv 2603.05451, Zadouri, Hoehnerbach, Shah, Liu, Thakkar, Dao; March 2026) is the reference training-attention kernel for any GB200 plan, and it is best understood not as "FA3 but faster" but as a kernel re-architected around the exp bottleneck. On Hopper, FA3 hid softmax exp under the matmul via warp-specialized ping-pong; that worked because the MMA side and the MUFU.EX2 side were roughly balanced. On Blackwell the matmul got ~2× cheaper while MUFU.EX2 did not, so the naive FA3 schedule leaves the tensor cores starved waiting on the exp units. FA4's three load-bearing answers:

1. Fully async UMMA into Tensor Memory. FA4 issues tcgen05.mma (5th-gen tensor-core) instructions that accumulate in Tensor Memory (TMEM, 256 KB/SM) rather than registers, decoupling the matmul pipeline from the register file that softmax needs. The single-CTA UMMA atom is 128×256×16, roughly 2× the Hopper WGMMA atom; the 2-CTA MMA mode fuses a UMMA across a CTA pair to 256×256×16, which halves operand-B SMEM traffic. In the backward pass the 2-CTA mode also cuts global atomic reductions ~50% by exchanging the dQ partial reduction over DSMEM (distributed shared memory across the CTA pair) instead of going to global memory (Colfax).

2. Software-emulated exp2 on the FMA units, running alongside hardware MUFU.EX2. This is the headline trick. Rather than route all exp through the saturated MUFU.EX2 path, FA4 also computes 2x on the otherwise-idle FMA pipes: Cody–Waite range reduction of 2x into 2n · 2f (integer n, fractional f ∈ [0,1)), then a degree-3 Horner polynomial with Sollya-fit coefficients p0 = 1.0, p1 ≈ 0.6951, p2 ≈ 0.2276, p3 ≈ 0.0771, fit for minimum relative error over [0,1). Splitting the exp workload across two physical pipelines is what unblocks the matmul throughput (Colfax).

3. Conditional / two-stage softmax rescaling. A dedicated correction warpgroup handles the online-softmax running-max rescale, but skips the rescale when the new block's max jump is below a threshold τ — pulling the rescale off the critical path whenever the running max is stable (the common case deep into a sequence). The forward schedule is a ping-pong over two Q tiles (QH, QL, each 128 query tokens) driven by two softmax warpgroups plus one correction warpgroup, deliberately staggered to avoid MUFU contention.

cute-dsl — FA4's software exp2 on the FMA pipe (degree-3 Horner, illustrative)
# Cody-Waite split: 2^x = 2^n * 2^f, n integer, f in [0,1)
n = floor(x)
f = x - n                                  # fractional part, hits the polynomial
# Sollya-fit coeffs, minimized for RELATIVE error on [0,1):
p0, p1, p2, p3 = 1.0, 0.6951, 0.2276, 0.0771
poly = p0 + f * (p1 + f * (p2 + f * p3))   # degree-3 Horner, runs on FMA units
exp2 = ldexp(poly, n)                       # 2^f * 2^n  -- no MUFU.EX2 traffic
# scheduled ALONGSIDE hardware MUFU.EX2 so neither exp pipe starves the MMA

The reported result: ~1605–1613 TFLOP/s BF16 on B200 = 71% of the 2.25 PFLOP/s dense peak, 1.3× over cuDNN 9.13 and 2.7× over Triton, with deterministic backward at ~85–90% of nondeterministic throughput (arXiv 2603.05451). FA4 ships entirely in CuTe-DSL (CUTLASS's Python kernel DSL) at Dao-AILab/flash-attention/.../cute, claims 20–30× faster compile than C++ templates, and is wired as a FlexAttention backend so masking variants compose without hand-writing each kernel.

The engineering implications for the 10T/200B target are sharp:

  • 71% is your attention-FLOP ceiling on GB200, and you will rarely hit it. Design batch/seqlen/headdim around the exp bottleneck, not the matmul. Larger head_dim amortizes more matmul per exp and is favorable on Blackwell — one reason MLA's 192-element QK head dim (128 nope + 64 rope) and Step-3's 256-dim heads are not the liability they'd be on memory-bound hardware.
  • Attention is no longer matmul-bound on GB200. This is the thesis that justifies the rest of the chapter: if you can't make the kernel much faster, make it do less work. Sparsity and compression beat kernel micro-optimization.
  • FlexAttention is the masking escape hatch. Chunked/sliding/compressed/document masks at 10T scale will proliferate; a FlexAttention backend over FA4 avoids a combinatorial kernel-zoo.

FA4 vs FA3 vs cuDNN vs the TPU path — what to actually run

BackendHardwarePeak util / claimWhen to use at 100k scale
FA4 (CuTe-DSL)B200/GB200~1613 TFLOP/s BF16, 71%Reference training attention on GB200; FlexAttention backend for mask variants
cuDNN 9.13 flashHopper/Blackwell~0.77× FA4Stable, vendor-supported fallback; MaxText cudnn_flash_te / cudnn_flash_jax
FA3 (WGMMA)Hopper (H100/H800)balanced exp/MMAOnly on Hopper; superseded on Blackwell
Tritonportable~0.37× FA4Prototyping, NSA reference, portability layer
ThunderKittens 2.0B200 (+H100)near-cuDNN B200, up to 2× FA3 H100Tile-DSL alternative; production at Together/Cursor/Jump
TPU Splash (Pallas)TPU v5/v6—TPU fallback; MaxText flash/autoselected, optional Tokamax splash
 Util/claims relative to FA4's ~1613 TFLOP/s B200 BF16. Sources: FA4, ThunderKittens.

In the user's actual codebase (MaxText), attention_op.py exposes precisely this dispatch surface. attention_kernel ∈ {dot_product, flash/autoselected, cudnn_flash_te, cudnn_flash_jax, paged, vllm_rpa}. The flash/autoselected path routes to TPU Splash via splash_attention_kernel, optionally swapping the stock JAX Pallas kernel for Tokamax splash kernels (use_tokamax_splash) — a little-publicized Google TPU kernel library worth knowing about if a TPU hedge sits behind the GB200 plan. Two explicit GPU guards in validate_gpu_flash_attention are load-bearing and bite at scale: GPU flash does not yet support attention-sinks, and it does not support record_max_logits (QK-clip) — raise NotImplementedError. Since MuonClip-style QK-clipping is one of your best loss-spike defenses (Section on optimizers), this means on GB200 you either keep QK-clip on a dot_product/non-flash path for the layers that need it, run QK-clip via a custom FA4/CuTe-DSL extension, or rely on FA4's own logit stability. The autoregressive-decode path uses ragged kernels (ragged_mha, ragged_gqa, ragged_block_size=256) rather than flash, and dot_product is auto-selected for length < 128 and for the decode step — a reminder that the prefill kernel and the decode kernel are different programs with different bottlenecks.

GPU flash drops QK-clip — a step-stability trap stated in MaxText

validate_gpu_flash_attention raises NotImplementedError for record_max_logits and attention-sinks on the GPU flash path. MuonClip-style QK-clipping is one of your best loss-spike defenses, so on GB200 you must run the layers that need it on a dot_product path, write a custom FA4/CuTe-DSL QK-clip extension, or rely on FA4's own logit stability. Don't discover this at step 0.

02MLA: the KV-cache compression that anchors the dense layers

For the dense-attention layers of a frontier MoE, Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA) is the default, and the exact cache arithmetic governs your memory plan. In the user's actual codebase, attention_mla.py caches only the compressed latent: kv_lora_rank = 512 (the kv_norm-ed low_rank_main) plus a single shared decoupled-RoPE key of qk_rope_head_dim = 64 — 576 elements/token/layer. Compare to GQA-8 with 128-dim heads, which caches ~2 × 8 × 128 = 2,048 elements/token/layer for separate K and V. MLA is a ~3.5× cache reduction over GQA-8 before quantization, and it is reconstructive: MlaKVCache stores key_latent (512) and key_rope (64) and rebuilds per-head K and V on read via the wkv_b up-projection (mla_get_key_value).

The crucial systems knob is absorb-vs-materialize, exposed as mla_naive_kvcache. With the absorbed form (default), you cache the 576-element latent and fold wkv_b into the query-side projection so you never materialize full per-head K/V — minimal cache, maximal arithmetic intensity on the QK product (good for Blackwell, where you want AI). With mla_naive_kvcache=True, MLA materializes full K/V into the cache — simpler kernels, higher bandwidth, larger cache. The roofline tradeoff is quantified in the hardware-centric MLA analysis (arXiv 2506.02523): on GB200's memory hierarchy, the absorbed form wins for decode (cache-bound) while materialize can win for very short prefill (compute-bound, where the extra cache is irrelevant).

Recommendation: absorbed MLA, FP8 cache

For the 10T/200B target: absorbed MLA on the dense layers, KV cache in FP8 (quantize_kvcache, kv_quant_axis), giving ~576 elements × 1 byte = 576 B/token/layer. Absorbed keeps arithmetic intensity high on the QK product, which is exactly what Blackwell rewards now that exp — not the matmul — is the bottleneck.

Two more MLA details from the codebase that matter at scale:

  • YaRN mscale absorption. When max_position_embeddings > original, MLA folds the YaRN attention-temperature into softmax_scale: mscale = 0.1 · mscale · ln(rope_factor) + 1.0, then softmax_scale *= mscale². The DeepSeek-V3 config (deepseek3-671b.yml) uses rope_factor = 40 for 163,840-token context — confirmed in the user's actual codebase alongside q_lora_rank = 1536, kv_lora_rank = 512, qk_nope_head_dim = 128, qk_rope_head_dim = 64, v_head_dim = 128, 128 query heads.
  • The [nope, rope] split and interleaved RoPE layout. MLA splits each query/key head as [nope, rope] with interleave=True. This is the opposite of the DSA indexer's [rope, nope] + concatenated layout (below) — a subtlety MaxText calls out in comments and a real footgun if you port between them.
  • QK-clip support. MLA supports use_qk_clip (Kimi/Muon-style max-logit clipping via record_max_logits), gated on MODEL_MODE_TRAIN. MLA at scale is prone to attention-logit blow-up under Muon, so this hook exists specifically to bound it — but recall it is not yet available on GPU flash, so the clip path runs on dot_product on GB200.
attn mscale = 0.1 · mscale · ln(rope_factor) + 1.0 , softmax_scale ·= mscale² YaRN attention-temperature absorbed into the softmax scale when max_position_embeddings > original. V3 uses rope_factor = 40 for 163,840-token context.

03NSA → DSA → V4 hybrid CSA/HCA: the learned-sparse-attention trajectory

The most consequential attention story of 2025–26 is DeepSeek's three-step march from natively trainable block sparsity (NSA) to a shipped lightning-indexer top-k (DSA, V3.2) to a layer-interleaved hybrid of two compressed-attention variants (V4). This is the path you should plan to walk for the 200B model's long-context capability.

NSA: the natively-trainable ancestor

NSA (arXiv 2502.11089, DeepSeek+PKU+UW, Feb 2025) runs three parallel branches fused by a learned gate: a coarse compression branch (mean/learned-pool every block into one entry), a fine-grained selection branch (top-n blocks), and a sliding window for locality. The hardware co-design is the lesson: selection is block-sparse by construction and GQA/MQA-aligned — all heads in a group share the selected KV blocks, so a single block load amortizes across the group, keeping arithmetic intensity high. The reference kernels are Triton, arithmetic-intensity-balanced so no branch starves. NSA proves you can train sparsity from scratch with full-attention parity. But it never became the shipped DeepSeek attention; DSA did.

DSA: what shipped, and what MaxText implements

DSA (DeepSeek V3.2-Exp, arXiv 2512.02556, Dec 2025) replaces NSA's three-branch structure with a single lightning indexer that scores every (query, key) pair and selects the top-k = 2048 KV positions per query, dropping core attention from O(L²) to O(Lk). It is implemented in the user's actual codebase as the Indexer class in attention_mla.py, and that implementation is, frankly, clearer than the V3.2 paper's prose. The mechanism, verbatim from the code path:

  1. logits = ReLU(einsum("bthd,bsd->btsh", q, k)) — a ReLU similarity, not softmax; MQA-style broadcasting so each key is shared across the indexer heads.
  2. weights = weights_proj(inputs_q) · n_heads-0.5 · softmax_scale — head-importance weights from a separate FP32, unquantized projection.
  3. indexer_score = einsum("btsh,bth->bts", logits, weights) — head-summed relevance.
  4. _, topk_indices = jax.lax.top_k(indexer_score, k=indexer_topk) — top-k selection; tokens outside top-k get DEFAULT_MASK_VALUE.

Three implementation facts in MaxText are non-obvious and load-bearing:

src/maxtext/layers/attention_mla.pyIndexer.__call__DSA lightning indexertrimmed
# gradient wall: LM loss cannot flow in, indexer loss cannot flow out
q_in  = jax.lax.stop_gradient(inputs_q)                       # [1]
lq    = jax.lax.stop_gradient(low_rank_q)
kv_in = jax.lax.stop_gradient(inputs_kv)

logits = jax.nn.relu(jnp.einsum("bthd,bsd->btsh", q, k))      # [2] ReLU, not softmax
# head-importance weights: SEPARATE fp32, NEVER quantized
weights = weights_proj(q_in) * (n_heads ** -0.5) * softmax_scale  # [3]
score   = jnp.einsum("btsh,bth->bts", logits, weights)        # head-summed relevance
_, topk_indices = jax.lax.top_k(score, k=indexer_topk)        # [4] top-k = 2048

# trained by KL to the main attention's head-summed, L1-normed distribution:
# loss = indexer_loss_scaling_factor * KL(attention_probs || indexer_probs)

1A mathematical wall. inputs_q, low_rank_q, inputs_kv are all stop_gradient-ed: the LM loss cannot flow into the indexer and the indexer loss cannot perturb the main model. This bidirectional barrier is what makes joint training stable.

2ReLU similarity. Not a softmax score — MQA-style broadcasting shares each key across the indexer heads, keeping the indexer cheap (only a few heads, FP8-able).

3FP32, unquantized. The code comment is explicit: "deepseek3.2 enforces FP32 and does not quantize, for precision and stability." The indexer body can be FP8, but weights_proj cannot — violate this and top-k selection goes unstable.

4O(Lk), not O(L²). Top-k=2048 KV positions per query; everything outside gets DEFAULT_MASK_VALUE. indexer_sparse_training toggles whether the KL target is the dense or already-sparse attention.

  • weights_proj is forced FP32 and unquantized. The code comment is explicit: "deepseek3.2 enforces FP32 and does not quantize, for precision and stability." The rest of the indexer can run FP8 (only a few heads), but this head-weighting projection cannot. Violate this and the indexer's top-k selection becomes unstable.
  • Gradient isolation in both directions. inputs_q, low_rank_q, and inputs_kv are all jax.lax.stop_gradient-ed before the indexer touches them. The comment calls this "a mathematical wall": the LM loss cannot flow into the indexer, and the indexer loss cannot perturb the main model. This bidirectional barrier is what makes joint training stable.
  • The indexer is trained by KL divergence to the main attention. calculate_indexer_loss computes KL(attention_probs ‖ indexer_probs) against the main attention's head-summed, L1-normalized distribution (both softmaxed in float32 for stability), scaled by indexer_loss_scaling_factor. indexer_sparse_training toggles whether the KL target is the dense or the already-sparse attention.
  • RoPE layout differs from MLA. The indexer splits [rope, nope] (MLA is [nope, rope]) and uses concatenated, non-interleaved RoPE (apply_partial_rope with interleave=False); YaRN is applied with the concatenate layout.
Two code-review invariants you must enforce from the codebase

The indexer weights_proj stays FP32 + unquantized, and the bidirectional stop_gradient barrier between indexer and main model is mandatory. Both are load-bearing for training stability, and both are trivially easy to break in a refactor (e.g. someone adds blanket FP8 casting, or removes a stop_gradient "for cleanliness"). Pin tests on both.

The continued-training recipe from V3.1-Terminus is the cheapest known way to retrofit sparse attention onto a dense checkpoint, and you should budget for exactly this: a dense warm-up (1,000 steps / 2.1B tokens, LR 1e-3, KL loss only — the indexer learns to mimic the still-dense main attention) followed by a sparse stage (15,000 steps / 943.7B tokens, LR 7.3e-6, top-k active) (arXiv 2512.02556). Reported ~3–6× long-context cost reduction at 128K with quality parity. One scaling caveat worth flagging: DSA's jax.lax.top_k over the full sequence has a memory footprint that grows with L; the 2026 follow-on StreamIndex (arXiv 2605.02568) makes the top-k memory-bounded and streaming, which is the right primitive at 1M context where a dense score matrix is itself the problem.

DeepSeek V4: hybrid CSA/HCA, mHC, and FP4-in-the-loop

DeepSeek V4 ("Towards Highly Efficient Million-Token Context Intelligence," report ~April 2026) generalizes DSA into a layer-wise interleave of two compressed-attention variants. Treat V4 specifics as partially unverified — the arXiv HTML at 2606.19348 is the primary anchor but active-param counts and layer ratios vary across blog summaries, so the secondary derivations below (prathamp blog) carry a rumor tag.

CSA — Compressed Sparse Attention
m ≈ 4, dual/overlapping streams
Lightning-indexer top-k sparse selection (DSA-like). The "retrieval" layers.
HCA — Heavily Compressed Attention
m′ ≈ 128, single stream
Dense over the compressed stream, no indexer. The cheap "context" layers.

A widely-circulated unifying derivation casts both as CompAttnm,k,overlap(H, t) = MQA(qt, Top-k(Indexer(t, C))), with CSA = moderate-m + overlap + sparse and HCA = large-m + no-overlap + k = n/m′. The claimed result: KV at 1M ≈ 10% of V3.2 (≈250 GB → ~25 GB), V4-Pro using 27% of single-token inference FLOPs and 10% of the KV cache of V3.2 at 1M, V4-Flash 10% FLOPs / 7% cache (arXiv 2606.19348). Reported configs: V4-Pro 1.6T total / 49B active, V4-Flash 284B / 13B, both 1M context, 32–33T tokens.

~10%
V4 KV at 1M vs V3.2 (≈250→25 GB)
27%
V4-Pro single-token inference FLOPs vs V3.2
128m′
HCA heavy compression factor

Crucially, this is already partially in the user's actual codebase. deepseek4-284b.yml sets attention_type: 'compressed', indexer_topk: 512 (note: tighter than V3.2's 2048), indexer_n_heads: 64, indexer_head_dim: 128, q_lora_rank: 1024, GQA with base_num_query_heads: 64 / base_num_kv_heads: 1, and hash routing in the first 3 layers (first_num_hash_layers: 3). The compressed-attention machinery lives in attention_compressed.py (43 KB), and attention_op.py carries AttentionType.COMPRESSED with a compressed_mask path. The MoE side uses DeepSeek V4 HASH routing (tid2eid lookup table, Tid2EidVar) for those prefix layers — a deterministic, learnable-free assignment that sidesteps load-balance instability in the earliest blocks.

Two more V4 systems bets transfer directly to a GB200 plan, both medium/rumor confidence:

  • mHC (manifold-constrained hyper-connections) rumor replaces residual connections with a ~4× wider inter-layer pathway whose channel-mixing matrix is constrained to the Birkhoff polytope (doubly-stochastic) via Sinkhorn–Knopp (t_max=20), with overhead held to ~6.7% of the 1F1B pipeline stage. This is a deep-stack-stability mechanism for the very deep, very wide layouts a 10T model implies, and it is trained with Muon.
  • FP4 QAT in the loop. V4 applies FP4×FP8 matmuls to routed-expert weights and the indexer QK path — the first trillion-scale model reported to train with FP4 in the loop rather than post-hoc, which directly validates GB200's native NVFP4 microscaling (E2M1 values with E4M3 block scales) as a training format, not just an inference trick. MaxText's quantizations.py already carries the AQT intmp mixed-precision and nanoo_fp8/Qwix paths to mirror this.
The FP4 caveat you must not gloss over stated in V4

V4 explicitly notes that FP4×FP8 currently executes at FP8×FP8 speed on existing silicon and only realizes the ~1/3 efficiency win on future hardware (DeepSeek-V4, by extension of the same FP4 reasoning). On GB200 today, FP4 on the expert/indexer path is a memory-bandwidth and footprint win (half the weight bytes), not yet a compute win. Budget accordingly.

04Linear-attention and SSM hybrids: crushing the decode KV wall

At 10T/200B on 100k GB200, long-context inference is dominated by the KV-cache wall, and 2025–26 saw linear/SSM hybrids cross decisively from research into production. The transferable pattern across every one of them is a linear:full ratio of 3:1 to 7:1, plus periodic full/MLA layers for global recall — the linear layers give constant per-token state and O(L) compute, the sparse full layers preserve needle-in-haystack retrieval and long-chain-of-thought.

ModelTotal / activeLinear mechanismRatio (linear:full)Headline
MiniMax-01 / Text-01456B / 45.9BLightning attention7:1First commercial-scale linear LLM; 1M train / 4M infer
Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B80B / 3BGated DeltaNet3:1Qwen3-32B quality at <10% GPU-hours, >10× decode beyond 32K
Kimi Linear-48B-A3B48B / 3BKDA (channel-gated DeltaNet)3:175% KV reduction, 6× decode at 1M; beats full MLA matched
Nemotron-H8B/47B/56BMamba-2~12:1Constant per-token state; hybrid Mamba-2 + MLP + attention
 Qwen3-Next is 3:1 GDN:Gated-Attn; Kimi is 3:1 KDA:MLA; Nemotron-H runs attention at ~8% of layers. Sources linked inline below.
  • MiniMax-01 (arXiv lineage; 456B/45.9B, 80 layers) is the largest production deployment of linear attention, a strict 7:1 lightning:softmax block structure. MiniMax-M1 extends it to RL test-time compute and introduces CISPO (clips importance-sampling weights rather than token-level updates), RL-trained on 512 H800 for ~3 weeks (~$535K). The cautionary datapoint: MiniMax reportedly reverted to full attention for M2 — a signal that pure-linear quality at the frontier is still contested. Verify before betting the run on a high linear ratio.
  • Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B (vLLM blog) interleaves Gated DeltaNet (delta-rule error-correcting linear state + exponential gating + causal Conv1D + L2-normalized Q/K) and Gated Attention in 3:1, with only 3B active and native MTP for built-in speculative decoding. Qwen's own ablations chose 3:1 because Gated DeltaNet beat both sliding-window and Mamba-2 on in-context learning — the ratio, not the mechanism, is the load-bearing choice.
  • Kimi Linear-48B-A3B (arXiv 2510.26692) uses Kimi Delta Attention (KDA) — Gated DeltaNet with finer channel-wise gating — interleaved 3:1 with MLA, claiming 75% KV-cache reduction and 6× decode throughput at 1M, open KDA kernels, vLLM support, and the notable claim of beating full MLA under matched recipes. This is the strongest evidence that hybrid-linear is not merely a cost compromise.
  • Nemotron-H (arXiv 2504.03624) makes attention only ~8% of layers (8B = 24 Mamba-2 + 24 MLP + 4 attention), constant per-token state. Jamba (AI21) was the earlier Mamba/Transformer/MoE production hybrid; Nemotron Nano 2 extends the family to reasoning.
The ratio is the load-bearing choice, not the mechanism

Across MiniMax, Qwen3-Next, Kimi Linear and Nemotron-H, the durable lesson is that the linear:full ratio (3:1 to 7:1) matters far more than which exact linear primitive (lightning attention vs Gated DeltaNet vs KDA vs Mamba-2) you pick. Choose the ratio first; the mechanism is a second-order, swappable decision.

Recommendation: a hybrid backbone, not pure-anything

The convergent evidence points to a hybrid backbone: MLA (absorbed, 576-elt latent, FP8 cache) on the dense layers, a DSA-style lightning indexer (top-k ≈ 2048, FP8, FP32 weights_proj, gradient-isolated, KL-trained) for long-context sparse selection, and a 3:1 linear:full interleave (Gated-DeltaNet or KDA) to crush the decode KV wall — keeping ~25% of layers as sparse-full/MLA for global recall. This composes cleanly: the linear layers carry constant state, the MLA layers carry the 576-elt latent, and the DSA indexer prunes the attended set on the layers that need 1M reach. Given MiniMax's M2 reversal, I would not push past 3:1 (i.e., not toward 7:1) for a first frontier run — the marginal KV savings are not worth the retrieval-quality risk at 200B active. The V4 CSA/HCA interleave is the more aggressive alternative and is the right target if you can validate FP4-QAT-in-the-loop and mHC stability at your scale; otherwise the linear-hybrid + DSA path is lower-variance.

05Kernel DSLs: the 2026 stack you will actually write against

The DSL landscape has bifurcated, and which one you reach for depends on whether you are chasing the last 10% of peak or shipping a new mask variant by Friday.

DSLVendor/targetRole in 2026Notes
CuTe-DSLNVIDIA (CUTLASS Python)Production peak kernelsFA4 lives here; 20–30× faster compile than C++ templates
TritonportableRapid prototyping / portabilityNSA reference; ~0.37× FA4 on attention
ThunderKittens 2.0NVIDIA (+AMD via HipKittens)Tile-DSL alternativeJan 2026: full Blackwell, MXFP8/NVFP4, TMEM/CTA-pairs; near-cuDNN B200, up to 2× FA3 H100
PallasTPU/JAXTPU kernel layerMaxText's Splash + Tokamax path; the GB200 fallback substrate
 Sources: FA4, ThunderKittens, HipKittens.

CuTe-DSL is now the production path for the most-optimized NVIDIA kernels — FA4 is the proof, and the 20–30× compile-time win over C++ templates is what makes iterating on a 100k-GPU kernel tractable. Triton remains the rapid-prototyping and portability layer and is NSA's reference implementation, but at ~0.37× FA4 it is not your training kernel. ThunderKittens 2.0 (HazyResearch; released Jan 11 2026) is the serious tile-DSL alternative: full Blackwell support (5th-gen tensor cores, TMEM, CTA pairs), MXFP8/NVFP4, attention fwd/bwd at near-cuDNN on B200 and up to 2× FA3 on H100, and in production at Together/Cursor/Jump (ThunderKittens). Its HipKittens (arXiv 2511.08083) and ParallelKittens spinoffs port the tile abstraction to AMD CDNA (MI300-class) and multi-GPU — directly relevant as a hedge against NVIDIA lock-in, and almost nobody tracking FA4/CuTe-DSL has read the AMD-side tile-DSL story.

An underwatched AMD hedge underwatched

HipKittens / ParallelKittens port the ThunderKittens tile abstraction to AMD CDNA (MI300-class) and multi-GPU. Almost nobody tracking FA4/CuTe-DSL has read the AMD-side tile-DSL story — it is the most credible portability hedge against NVIDIA lock-in for your kernel layer.

On the TPU/JAX side, Pallas is the kernel layer, and the user's actual codebase (attention_op.py) confirms the exact dispatch surface a dual GB200/TPU plan must reason about: dot_product, autoselected/Splash flash (optionally Tokamax splash), cudnn_flash_te (Transformer Engine), cudnn_flash_jax, paged, vllm_rpa, plus ragged_mha/ragged_gqa (ragged_block_size=256) for AR decode, and a ChunkedCausalMask (splash_attention_mask._ComputableMask subclass) for Llama4-style interleaved chunk+global attention (llama4-17b-128e.yml: chunk_attn_window_size 8192, nope_layer_interval 4).

jax — MaxText attention_op.py kernel dispatch surface (illustrative)
# attention_kernel selects the program; prefill and decode are DIFFERENT kernels
attention_kernel in {"dot_product", "flash", "autoselected",
                     "cudnn_flash_te", "cudnn_flash_jax", "paged", "vllm_rpa"}

if attention_kernel in ("flash", "autoselected"):
    # TPU Splash (Pallas); optionally swap in Tokamax splash kernels
    kernel = splash_attention_kernel(..., use_tokamax_splash=cfg.use_tokamax_splash)

def validate_gpu_flash_attention(cfg):
    if cfg.attention_sink:        raise NotImplementedError  # GPU flash: no sinks
    if cfg.record_max_logits:     raise NotImplementedError  # GPU flash: no QK-clip

# AR decode does NOT use flash: ragged kernels, dot_product for length < 128
decode_kernel = ragged_gqa if is_gqa else ragged_mha   # ragged_block_size = 256

06Synthesis: the attention stack for a 10T/200B/100k-GB200 run

The 2026 frontier has decisively answered "how do you attend at 1M context on a 200B-active model": not with a faster dense kernel, but with less attention. FA4 sets the matmul envelope (71% of dense peak, exp-bound), and that ceiling is precisely why sparsity and compression — which reduce the number of attended tokens and the per-token cache — pay off more than chasing kernel TFLOP/s. The recommended stack, grounded in what the user's codebase already implements:

  1. Dense layers: absorbed MLA, 576-elt latent (kv_lora_rank=512 + qk_rope_head_dim=64), FP8 KV cache. ~3.5× cache reduction over GQA-8, high arithmetic intensity (Blackwell-favorable). Keep use_qk_clip available on a dot_product path for logit-blowup defense under Muon.
  2. Long-context: a DSA lightning indexer, top-k ≈ 2048, FP8 body, FP32 + unquantized weights_proj, bidirectional stop_gradient isolation, KL-trained to the main attention. Bootstrap via the V3.2 two-stage recipe (1k-step dense warm-up → 15k-step sparse). Watch top-k memory at 1M — adopt StreamIndex-style streaming top-k.
  3. Decode-wall mitigation: a 3:1 linear:full interleave (Gated-DeltaNet/KDA), tempered by MiniMax's M2 reversal — do not exceed 3:1 on a first run.
  4. Kernels: FA4 (CuTe-DSL) + FlexAttention for training/masking on GB200, cuDNN/TE as the supported fallback, ThunderKittens/HipKittens as the portability and AMD hedge, and the MaxText Pallas/Splash/Tokamax path as the TPU substrate.
  5. The two non-obvious gotchas to enforce in code review, both from the codebase: indexer weights_proj stays FP32 + unquantized, and the stop_gradient barrier between indexer and main model is mandatory — both are load-bearing for training stability, and both are easy to break in a refactor.
The lower-variance frontier choice

The aggressive frontier — DeepSeek V4's CSA/HCA + mHC + FP4-in-the-loop, claiming 10% of V3.2's KV at 1M — is the right next target once FP4 QAT and doubly-stochastic mHC residuals are validated at your scale, but it carries more unverified risk. The MLA + DSA + 3:1-linear-hybrid stack is the lower-variance, frontier-competitive choice for a first 10T/200B run.

Sources

  • FlashAttention-4 (arXiv 2603.05451): arxiv.org/abs/2603.05451
  • Colfax FA4 algorithm/kernel deep-dive (Horner exp2 coeffs, 2-CTA dQ-via-DSMEM, warpgroup layout, peak math): research.colfax-intl.com
  • DeepSeek V3.2-Exp / DSA lightning indexer + bootstrap recipe (arXiv 2512.02556): arxiv.org/abs/2512.02556
  • DeepSeek V4 report (CSA/HCA, mHC, FP4 QAT; treat specifics as partly unverified) (arXiv 2606.19348): arxiv.org/html/2606.19348v1
  • DeepSeek V4 CSA/HCA derivation (secondary/rumor): prathamp.com/blog/deepseek-v4-csa-and-hca
  • NSA: Natively trainable sparse attention (arXiv 2502.11089): arxiv.org/abs/2502.11089
  • Kimi Linear-48B-A3B / KDA (arXiv 2510.26692): arxiv.org/abs/2510.26692
  • Qwen3-Next Gated DeltaNet hybrid (vLLM): blog.vllm.ai/2025/09/11/qwen3-next
  • MiniMax-01 / M1 / CISPO (arXiv 2506.13585): arxiv.org/pdf/2506.13585
  • Nemotron-H hybrid Mamba-2 + Transformer (arXiv 2504.03624): arxiv.org/abs/2504.03624
  • ThunderKittens 2.0: github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens
  • HipKittens (AMD tile-DSL) (arXiv 2511.08083): arxiv.org/html/2511.08083v1
  • StreamIndex: memory-bounded streaming top-k (arXiv 2605.02568): arxiv.org/abs/2605.02568
  • Hardware-centric analysis of MLA absorb-vs-materialize (arXiv 2506.02523): arxiv.org/pdf/2506.02523
  • MaxText attention_mla.py (DSA Indexer, MLA cache, gradient isolation): maxtext/src/maxtext/layers/attention_mla.py
  • MaxText attention_op.py (kernel dispatch, Splash/Tokamax, GPU guards): maxtext/src/maxtext/layers/attention_op.py
  • NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 (dense-vs-sparse FLOP footnote): nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/gb200-nvl72

Chapter 09 · Precision is the FLOP/$ lever

Low-precision training: FP8, MXFP8, FP4/NVFP4 QAT, and stability at the 4-bit frontier

On 100,000 GB200s, precision is the single largest lever on effective FLOP/$ — and the one most likely to silently corrupt a multi-month run. On Blackwell, FP4 is not a memory trick: it is a 2× compute multiplier on the dominant GEMM class. This is the end-to-end recipe for realizing it without divergence.

~28 min read6 figuresgrounded in MaxText src/maxtext/layers/quantizations.py

Precision is the single largest lever on the effective FLOP/$ of a 10T-total/200B-active run, and it is also the lever most likely to silently corrupt a multi-month training job. On GB200, the arithmetic ratio is stark: a B200 delivers roughly 4.5 PFLOP/s dense FP8 and ~9 PFLOP/s dense FP4 per GPU — the NVIDIA-marketed 720 PFLOP/s FP8 / 1,440 PFLOP/s FP4 per NVL72 rack are with 2:4 sparsity; the rack's dense figures are 360 PFLOP/s FP8 and 180 PFLOP/s BF16, per NVIDIA's own GB200 NVL72 page footnote. The headline is that on Blackwell, FP4 is not a memory trick — it is a 2× compute multiplier on the dominant GEMM class (the MoE expert grouped matmuls), and the entire field has, over 2025–2026, converged on the engineering recipe required to actually realize it without divergence. This chapter lays out that recipe end-to-end: the DeepSeek-V3 FP8 floor everyone builds on, the MXFP8/MXFP4/NVFP4 microscaling fork, the four non-negotiable techniques for 4-bit pretraining, where precision provably breaks, what the precision scaling laws predict at the 10T horizon, and how every one of these maps onto switches that already exist in MaxText.

FP8 is the floor; NVFP4-QAT-on-experts is the frontier that DeepSeek-V4 and the NVIDIA 12B/10T run have jointly proven trainable. The engineering — not the format — is what separates a converging 4-bit run from a diverging one.
2×
FP4 vs FP8 dense compute, per B200
36%
more tokens MXFP4 needs vs NVFP4
4tech
non-negotiable 4-bit techniques
8.2T
tokens — flip NVFP4→BF16 for decay tail

01The DeepSeek-V3 FP8 floor: fine-grained scaling and the accumulation fix

DeepSeek-V3 is the reference recipe that every subsequent low-precision paper either extends or argues against, so it is worth stating its mechanics precisely. V3 runs the forward GEMM and both backward GEMMs (dgrad/wgrad) in FP8, with two distinct scaling granularities (DeepSeek-V3 report, §3.3):

activations
per-tile 1×128
One scale per token, per group of 128 channels.
weights
per-block 128×128
One scale per 128-input × 128-output block.

Critically, V3 uses E4M3 on every tensor in these GEMMs, abandoning the older hybrid convention of E5M2 for gradients. The justification is that fine-grained scaling "shares exponent bits among grouped elements" — a per-128 scale recovers the dynamic range that E4M3's 4 exponent bits lack, so you never need the wider exponent of E5M2 if your blocks are small enough. All scale factors are round-scaled to integral powers of two, which guarantees that an operand quantized in the forward pass and re-quantized in the backward pass shares the identical representation — a chain-rule-consistency property that reappears, in a stronger 2D form, in NVFP4.

The load-bearing hardware insight in V3 is the accumulation fix. Hopper's FP8 tensor cores accumulate WGMMA results in only about 14 bits of mantissa, which produces up to 2% relative error on a large-K GEMM — enough to destabilize training on its own. V3 promotes partial sums into FP32 CUDA-core registers every NC = 128 elements (≈ every 4 WGMMA instructions), implementing a manual two-level accumulation.

The one V3 trick that does not port to JAX/XLA

XLA's dot_general does not expose the inner-product chunking needed to force FP32 promotion every 128 elements, so a MaxText reproduction relies on the library's accumulator-dtype settings (AQT's fwd_accumulator_dtype, the Flax fp8_ops compute dtype) rather than V3's bespoke CUDA kernel.
