On Blackwell this concern largely evaporates: the 5th-gen tensor cores accumulate FP8 in higher precision and the microscaling formats are hardware-native, so the manual NC trick becomes unnecessary — but the principle (never trust the tensor core's native accumulator for a long-K MoE GEMM) carries forward to FP4 directly.

The other half of the V3 recipe is the precision-breaks map — the list of operators deliberately not quantized:

ComponentV3 precisionWhy it stays high
Embedding / output headBF16/FP32Vocabulary-projection logits are wide-dynamic-range and feed the loss directly
MoE gating / routerBF16/FP32Top-k/argmax is discontinuous; one mis-quantized gate flips an expert assignment
All normalization (RMSNorm)BF16/FP32Norms compute reciprocal sqrt of a sum; quantization error compounds
All attention operatorsBF16/FP32Softmax logits and QKᵀ products are dynamic-range-sensitive
Optimizer master weightsFP32Small-update accumulation needs full mantissa
Optimizer gradient accumulatorsFP32Summing many micro-batch grads in FP8 loses tail bits
Optimizer 1st/2nd momentsBF16Tolerable; the memory savings are large
 Source: DeepSeek-V3, §3.3. This map is essentially invariant across every later FP4 paper.

This map is essentially invariant across every later FP4 paper. The router in particular is non-negotiable, and the reason is structural rather than empirical: routing is a discrete decision, and the gradient of a discrete decision does not smooth out quantization noise the way a dense matmul does. This is precisely why MaxText scopes its FP8/FP4 quantization rules to decoder/.*layers.* and routes them only over the op names ("dot_general", "gmm", "ragged_dot") — the router's GateLogit einsum in moe.py is not in that set, so it stays in the working dtype by construction (MaxText, quantizations.py). The gmm/ragged_dot ops in that list are exactly the grouped/ragged expert GEMMs — the parameter bulk and the only place where low precision pays for itself.

02The microscaling fork: MXFP8, MXFP4, and why NVFP4 broke from the OCP spec

The OCP Microscaling (MX) v1.0 spec is the normative definition of block-scaled low precision: a block of k = 32 elements shares a single E8M0 scale — an 8-bit, sign-less, power-of-two value with bias 127, i.e. scale = 2(s − 127) (OCP MX v1.0 spec). The spec defines MXFP8 (E4M3 or E5M2 elements), MXFP6, MXFP4 (E2M1 elements), and MXINT8. MaxText ships the ground-truth MXFP4 decoder in dequantize_mxfp4.py (the gpt-oss checkpoint layout): FP4 is E2M1 with the value set {0, ±0.5, ±1, ±1.5, ±2, ±3, ±4, ±6}, two nibbles packed per uint8, 32 values per block, and scales = 2.0 ** (scales_e8m0 − 127) (MaxText). That 16-value codebook with a max magnitude of 6 is the entire numerical budget of a 4-bit weight; everything in FP4 training is about keeping the interesting values away from the {±4, ±6} clipping cliff and away from the {±0.5} underflow floor.

prec MXFP4 codebook (E2M1) = { 0, ±0.5, ±1, ±1.5, ±2, ±3, ±4, ±6 } 16 values, max magnitude 6. The whole numerical budget of a 4-bit weight — keep interesting values off the ±6 clip cliff and the ±0.5 underflow floor.

NVFP4 — NVIDIA's Blackwell-native format — deliberately breaks the MX spec in two ways that matter (NVFP4 pretraining paper, §3):

  1. 16-element micro-blocks, not 32. Smaller blocks mean a single outlier pollutes the shared scale for fewer neighbors.
  2. A two-level scale: a per-block E4M3 scale (not a power-of-two E8M0) multiplied by an FP32 per-tensor scale.

The E4M3 block scale is the consequential choice. Because it is not rounded to a power of two, it can place the block's max value precisely at a representable E2M1 point, preserving the ±4 and ±6 codes that MXFP4's UE8M0 power-of-two rounding tends to clip. With a 16-element block, at least 6.25% of values (the per-block max) land at near-FP8 fidelity. The empirical payoff is direct and large: on an 8B/1T-token run, MXFP4 shows ~2.5% relative loss error vs ~1.5% for NVFP4, and MXFP4 needs 36% more tokens (1.36T vs 1.0T) to match NVFP4's loss (§5).

The one number to remember: 36% more tokens

For a 10T-token budget that 36% tax is the difference between a 10T and a 13.6T run — on a 100k-GB200 cluster, the cost of choosing the wrong 4-bit format is measured in millions of GPU-hours. The recommendation is unambiguous: if you train in 4-bit, train in NVFP4, not MXFP4. MXFP4 is the right format only for the comms/storage path on hardware that cannot do native NVFP4 (§05).

The format choice is exposed directly in MaxText. quantizations.py wires three TransformerEngine recipes (MaxText):

MaxText recipeTE classBlock sizeNotes
te_mxfp8MXFP8BlockScaling32OCP-spec MXFP8, E8M0 scale
te_nvfp4NVFP4BlockScaling128 today# TODO(set this to 16 when unfused RHT is supported)
te_nvfp4_no_rhtNVFP4BlockScaling(disable_rht=True)128RHT toggled off
 Source: MaxText quantizations.py. The block-128 default and its TODO are the production-readiness detail no paper discloses.
A production detail no paper discloses in MaxText HEAD

The block_size for te_nvfp4 returns 128, with an explicit TODO to drop it to 16 once unfused Random Hadamard Transform is supported — a concrete signal that the native NVFP4 16-element block in JAX is gated on fused-RHT support, and that the no-RHT path exists precisely to ablate the technique §04 will show is mandatory. As of HEAD, a MaxText NVFP4 run is operating at a coarser-than-native 128 block and will tighten to 16 when the kernel lands.

03The four mandatory techniques for 4-bit pretraining

The NVFP4 pretraining paper is the load-bearing primary source for the 4-bit go/no-go decision because it is the longest publicly documented 4-bit run: a 12B hybrid Mamba-Transformer trained to 10T tokens — exactly the horizon the target run sits at. Its central finding is blunt: pure NVFP4 GEMMs diverge early, and convergence requires all four of the following techniques, none optional (NVFP4 paper, §4):

1 · selective BF16
~16% of linear layers
First 2 + last 8 blocks of the 12B. Final layers feed the loss.
2 · RHT
16×16 Hadamard, Wgrad input only
Gaussianizes block outliers. 4×4 degrades; 128×128 barely helps.
3 · 2D scaling
16×16 on weights
Chain-rule consistency: one weight representation, fwd + bwd.
4 · stochastic rounding
gradients only
Unbiased grad quant. SR on fwd is explicitly detrimental.

1. Keep ~16% of linear layers in BF16. For the 12B, that is the first two blocks plus the final eight blocks. Ablations show stability holds with only the final four blocks in high precision, but the final layers are the sensitive ones — the LM-head-adjacent representations whose errors flow straight into the loss. This is the same precision-breaks intuition as V3's "keep the output head high," generalized to a band of layers rather than a single op. For the 10T/200B target, hold the first ~2 and the last ~8 decoder layers (scaled to total depth) in BF16, and let the middle run NVFP4. In MaxText this is exactly the per-layer regex mechanism that the mixed-precision JSON configs (e.g. dense_llm_subchannel.json) already express — w_bits/a_bits/tile_size keyed by a layer-name regex — so "first 2 + last 8 BF16, middle NVFP4, router/norms/attention always high" is a configuration, not a code change.

2. Random Hadamard Transform on Wgrad inputs only. A 16×16 Hadamard matrix multiplied by a single fixed random ±1 sign vector shared across all layers and all of training. The RHT Gaussianizes block-level gradient outliers so a single spike does not dominate the per-block scale. The dimensions matter: 4×4 degrades, 128×128 gives only marginal gains over 16×16 — so 16×16 is the sweet spot, and it is applied only to the inputs of the weight-gradient GEMM, not the forward or dgrad GEMMs. This is the technique gated behind the te_nvfp4 / te_nvfp4_no_rht toggle in MaxText; the fact that the native 16-block is waiting on fused RHT support tells you the RHT must be fused into the quantization kernel to be free.

3. 2D 16×16 block scaling on weights (vs 1×16 for activations and gradients). The motivation is chain-rule consistency, the same principle V3 enforced via power-of-two rounding but stronger: a weight quantized one way in the forward GEMM and a different way in the backward GEMM produces an inconsistent gradient. 2D scaling forces a single quantized weight representation shared across the forward and backward passes.

4. Stochastic rounding on gradients only. SR makes gradient quantization unbiased — the rounding error has zero expectation, so it averages out over an accumulation. Crucially, applying SR to forward activations or weights is explicitly detrimental: it injects variance where you want a deterministic value. This asymmetry is exactly what MaxText encodes. In quantizations.py, AQT's set_stochastic_rounding(...) defaults to vjp_lhs_stochastic_rounding=False, vjp_rhs_stochastic_rounding=False and is configurable per-tensor (MaxText) — i.e. the framework lets you turn SR on for the gradient GEMMs and leave it off for the forward, which is precisely the NVFP4 prescription.

jax — MaxText AQT: SR on gradient GEMMs only (the NVFP4 asymmetry)
# aqt.jax.v2 — stochastic rounding is per-tensor, OFF by default on the forward
cfg = config_v4(fwd_bits="e4m3", dlhs_bits="e5m2", drhs_bits="e5m2")
aqt.set_stochastic_rounding(
    cfg,
    vjp_lhs_stochastic_rounding=True,    # gradient GEMM input  -> SR ON  (unbiased)
    vjp_rhs_stochastic_rounding=True,    # gradient GEMM weight -> SR ON
    implementation="jax.uniform",
)
# forward stays deterministic: fwd_lhs/fwd_rhs SR remain False by default

The end-of-training behavior is the most important practical detail for the target run. NVFP4 loss tracks FP8 to <1% through the stable phase but widens to >1.5% during LR decay — the model becomes precision-sensitive exactly when the learning rate shrinks and updates become small relative to quantization noise. The mitigation is a precision switch: flipping NVFP4→BF16 at 8.2T tokens (82% of training) drops relative loss error from 1.5% to 0.5%, at a cost of only ~6% of total compute spent in high precision (NVFP4 paper). And the downstream result is real parity, not just loss parity: MMLU-Pro 62.58% (NVFP4) vs 62.62% (FP8).

Plan the decay-phase switch into the LR schedule up front

For a 10T-token plan this dictates a concrete schedule: run NVFP4 for the bulk of training and budget a BF16 (or FP8) tail for roughly the final ~15–20% / the LR-decay phase. The decay-phase precision switch is the single highest-leverage stability intervention in 4-bit pretraining, and it must be planned into the LR schedule and the checkpoint cadence from the start, not bolted on.

04Where precision breaks: router, norms, logits, and the stability map

Synthesizing V3 and the NVFP4 work, the precision-sensitivity ranking is stable across the field and should be treated as a hard constraint, not a tuning knob:

Tensor / opSafe precisionFailure mode if quantized too low
MoE router / gate logitsBF16/FP32Discontinuous top-k flips expert assignment; load-balance collapse
RMSNorm / all normalizationBF16/FP32Reciprocal-sqrt error compounds; activation blowup
Attention logits / softmax inputFP32 accumulationLogit explosion (the Muon failure mode); NaN softmax
Embedding + LM headBF16/FP32Direct loss corruption; wide vocab dynamic range
First ~2 + last ~8 decoder blocksBF16Early/late representation sensitivity (NVFP4 ablation)
Forward/backward expert GEMMsNVFP4 (w/ recipe)— (this is where 4-bit pays off)
Gradients (Wgrad input)NVFP4 + RHT + SROutlier-dominated block scale → divergence
 The precision-sensitivity ranking, synthesized across DeepSeek-V3 and the NVFP4 paper. A hard constraint, not a tuning knob.

Two failure modes deserve emphasis because they intersect with the optimizer chapter. First, attention-logit explosion: low precision in the QKᵀ path amplifies the runaway-logit pathology that MuonClip was designed to bound (Kimi K2's per-head logit cap at τ=100, arXiv 2507.20534). The defense is to keep attention out of FP4 entirely — V3 keeps "all attention operators" high-precision, and MaxText exposes float32_qk_product and float32_logits flags in base.yml to force the QK product and the softmax input to FP32 even when the rest of the layer is low-precision (MaxText). For the target run, set both to true if attention is anywhere near the precision floor.

Second, router/load-balance interaction: the aux-loss-free balancing in moe.py (calculate_load_balance_updates via routed_bias_update_rate, applied post-optimizer as a bias update) depends on accurate per-expert assignment counts; quantizing the gate would corrupt the very signal the balancer integrates, which is why the router is excluded from the quant scope.

Keep skip_step_on_spikes armed at 4-bit

A complementary stability tool in the codebase is skip_step_on_spikes() in optimizers.py, which computes a rolling mean ± scaling_factor·std (Bessel-corrected) over the loss and zeroes any update exceeding the threshold while preserving optimizer state (MaxText). At 4-bit, where quantization noise raises the baseline variance of the loss, this guard is doubly valuable: a transient precision-induced spike (a block scale momentarily clipping) gets skipped rather than poisoning the moment estimates. Budget for it.

05FP4 on Hopper without FP4 tensor cores

Not every part of a real fleet is Blackwell, and even on GB200 the expert-parallel all-to-all is a place where FP4 helps for bandwidth independent of compute. The Hopper-FP4 paper is the answer for both. Hopper has FP8 tensor cores but no native FP4 instructions, so the design keeps core MoE GEMMs in FP8 while compressing activations and the expert-parallel all-to-all to MXFP4 (32-element blocks, UE8M0 scale, RTNE rounding — explicitly not stochastic, since SR's benefit is a backward-pass-only property and this is a forward/comms compression). The clever bits:

  • A scaling-aware direct FP8↔FP4 conversion done by bitwise integer-domain remapping (exponent adjust plus a 1-bit mantissa left-shift), avoiding an FP4→BF16→FP8 round-trip.
  • Hierarchical scale alignment mapping four 32-element MXFP4 blocks onto one 128-element FP8 block, with the target scale = max of the four sub-block scales.
  • The combine phase keeps BF16 accumulation.

On DeepSeek-V3 671B (MLA), 256 Hopper GPUs, 160B tokens, this yields +0.61% relative loss (MXFP4) vs +0.29% (FP8) against BF16, throughput 1157→1302 tok/GPU/s (+12.5%), and −14.8% peak activation memory (−11.8 GB) (arXiv 2603.02731). The crucial caveat: there is no FP4 matmul speedup on Hopper — this is purely a memory-and-communication play. The 12.5% throughput gain comes from smaller all-to-all payloads and reduced activation memory enabling larger micro-batches, not from faster math.

The lesson transfers to Blackwell

For the 10T/200B/GB200 target the lesson transfers even though the hardware is Blackwell: the expert-parallel all-to-all is the dominant cross-rack traffic in a wide-EP MoE, and compressing the dispatched tokens to FP4 (NVFP4 on Blackwell, where the conversion is hardware-native) shrinks that payload roughly in half. On a 100k-GPU NVL72 fabric where EP spans many racks, halving all-to-all bytes is a first-order win even before the FP4 compute speedup. MaxText's MoE path (permute()/unpermute()/get_all_to_all_params(), the ring-of-experts Pallas ragged kernels) is where this compression would live; quantizing the dispatched activations to FP4 before the all-to-all and dequantizing on arrival is the GB200 analog of the Hopper paper's comms compression.

06DeepSeek-V4: FP4 QAT lands in production

DeepSeek-V4 is the existence proof that FP4 in the training loop is production-viable at trillion scale, and its design is the single most relevant signal for the target build. V4 applies FP4 quantization-aware training to MoE expert weights (FP4×FP8 matmuls) and to the CSA "lightning indexer" QK path, with FP8 for everything else, FP8 KV storage, and BF16 only on the RoPE dims (V4 HF discussion; V4 report). The indexer's precision dropped from FP8 in V3.2 → MXFP4 in V4, with QAT holding accuracy. Reported configs: V4-Pro 1.6T total / 49B active, V4-Flash 284B / 13B active, both 1M context, trained on 32–33T tokens.

Two architectural decisions in V4 are directly actionable:

1. The QAT-on-experts pattern. Experts are the parameter bulk, they tolerate aggressive quantization better than any other component, and they benefit most from FP4 storage plus FP4×FP8 compute. Everything precision-sensitive (router, indexer-output, norms, attention) stays at FP8 or higher. For a 200B-active model this is the obvious partition: the experts are where ~90% of the parameters live, so FP4 on experts captures nearly all the memory and compute savings while leaving the fragile 10% untouched.

2. FP4 as a storage/comms format that decodes into the FP8 GEMM. V4's economically important claim is a lossless FP4→FP8 dequantization that reuses the existing FP8 pipeline unchanged — FP4 is the storage representation, and it decodes into the same FP8 matmul, anticipating FP4×FP8 GEMMs up to ⅓ faster on future hardware (treat the exact speedup as medium-confidence; HF blog). This is exactly the pattern MaxText anticipates: the V4 "store FP4, compute FP8" model maps onto MaxText's AQT mixed-precision path where weights are held at one bit-width and the GEMM accumulates at another, and onto the Qwix dot_general_qt config where lhs/rhs_qtype=float8_e4m3fn with dlhs/drhs_grad_qtype=float8_e5m2 defines the compute precision independent of storage.

The FP4 caveat you must not gloss over ⅓-speedup medium-conf

V4 anticipates FP4×FP8 GEMMs up to ⅓ faster on future hardware — treat the exact figure as medium-confidence. On GB200 today, FP4-on-experts is realized first as a footprint and bandwidth win (half the weight bytes, more experts per GPU, fewer dispatch bytes). The reason V4 uses QAT rather than post-hoc quantization is not stylistic — it is forced by the scaling laws, as the next section shows.

07Precision scaling laws and the QAT escape hatch

Kumar et al.'s "Scaling Laws for Precision" (ICLR 2025) fit, on 465+ runs up to 1.7B params / 26B tokens, an effective-parameter law treating precision and parameter count as interchangeable contributors to capacity (arXiv 2411.04330):

data Neff(N, P) = N · (1 − e−Pw/γw) · (1 − e−Pa/γa) · (1 − e−Pkv/γkv) Weight, activation, and KV precision effects independent and multiplicative, with small single-digit γ constants. The exact γ live in Appendix K and were not machine-extractable — treat the precise γ as medium-confidence.

The two headline results both bear on the target run:

  • Compute-optimal training precision is ~7–8 bits, roughly independent of compute. Taken at face value this says BF16 is wasteful (you are paying for mantissa bits the model cannot use) and naive ≤4-bit is also sub-optimal (it forces a disproportionate model-size inflation to recover the lost effective parameters). This is the case against unmitigated 4-bit.
  • Post-train-quantization degradation grows with the data-to-parameter ratio: δPTQ ∝ (DγD/NγN) · e−Ppost/γpost. Plainly: the more tokens you train on, the more fragile the model becomes to later quantization. For a 10T-token/200B-active model — an aggressive 50:1 token-to-active-param ratio — this is a direct red flag. PTQ to INT4/FP4 after a 10T-token run will degrade super-linearly.

This single result is why V4 moved to QAT for FP4 experts rather than quantizing post-hoc, and it is the reason the target run should treat any "we'll quantize for inference later" plan with deep suspicion.

But the pessimistic 4-bit reading must be reconciled against the empirical NVFP4 success — a 12B trained to 10T tokens in 4-bit matching FP8 downstream. The resolution (synthesis by Wang) is that Kumar et al.'s law characterizes unmitigated low precision. The NVFP4 recipe — RHT + 2D scaling + SR + selective BF16 + the decay-phase precision switch — recovers near-FP8 quality, which is a partial refutation of the "4-bit is expensive" conclusion in the engineered-recipe regime. And the PTQ-fragility result is not a refutation at all; it is precisely the argument for QAT.

The clean decision the two laws hand you

Train in 4-bit (NVFP4 QAT on experts) with the full recipe; never PTQ a long-horizon model to 4-bit. The QAT path sidesteps the δPTQ fragility, and the engineered NVFP4 recipe sidesteps the unmitigated-4-bit penalty. The 7–8-bit "optimum" from the isolated law is what FP8-everywhere-except-experts already targets; pushing experts to 4-bit via QAT is a throughput optimization that the QAT-specific scaling laws (e.g. arXiv 2505.14302) show is recoverable, not the capacity loss the PTQ law would imply.

08The MaxText precision stack: three engines, one config surface

MaxText exposes three quantization stacks (plus TransformerEngine) in quantizations.py, and understanding which to reach for is part of the recipe (MaxText):

StackEntry pointRoleKey knobs
AQT (aqt.jax.v2)config_v4(fwd_bits="e4m3", dlhs_bits="e5m2", drhs_bits="e5m2")Int8/int-MP and FP8 with sub-channel tiling_tiling_fn/TiledDotGeneral (V3-style 1×128/128×128), set_stochastic_rounding(...) per-tensor, ConstantCalibration static power-of-two bounds
Qwix (dot_general_qt)fp8_fullTraining-time FP8 pathlhs/rhs_qtype=float8_e4m3fn, dlhs/drhs_grad_qtype=float8_e5m2, absmax calibration, optional N:M SparsityRule (weight_sparsity_n/m)
Flax fp8_opsFp8DirectDotGeneralOp, Fp8EinsumDelayed-scaling (TE-classic)amax_history_length=1024, _overwrite_with_gradient scale variables
TransformerEnginete_mxfp8 / te_nvfp4 / te_nvfp4_no_rhtReal TE block-scaling recipesMXFP8BlockScaling (block 32), NVFP4BlockScaling (block 128→16), RHT toggle
 Source: MaxText quantizations.py. Four config surfaces over the same dot_general/gmm/ragged_dot op set.

A few mappings make the recipe concrete. The annotated dispatch below is the load-bearing function — it shows where each engine attaches and how V3-style tiling, SR asymmetry, and the NVFP4 block live in one config surface:

src/maxtext/layers/quantizations.pyget_quant_configthree engines + TEtrimmed
def get_quant_config(cfg):                                    # [1]
    if cfg.quantization == "fp8_full":                        # Qwix training-time FP8
        return qwix.dot_general_qt(
            lhs_qtype=jnp.float8_e4m3fn, rhs_qtype=jnp.float8_e4m3fn,
            dlhs_grad_qtype=jnp.float8_e5m2, drhs_grad_qtype=jnp.float8_e5m2)  # [2]
    if cfg.quantization == "te_nvfp4":                        # Blackwell-native NVFP4
        return NVFP4BlockScaling(block_size=128)              # [3] TODO: 16 w/ fused RHT
    # AQT: V3-style sub-channel tiling + per-tensor stochastic rounding
    q = config_v4(fwd_bits="e4m3", dlhs_bits="e5m2", drhs_bits="e5m2")
    q = q.with_tiling(TiledDotGeneral(tile_size=128))         # [4] 1x128 act / 128x128 w
    q = set_stochastic_rounding(q, vjp_lhs=True, vjp_rhs=True)  # SR on grads only
    return q

# scope: only decoder layers, only the expert/dense GEMM ops
QUANT_SCOPE = re.compile(r"decoder/.*layers.*")
QUANT_OPS   = ("dot_general", "gmm", "ragged_dot")            # [5] router excluded

1One surface, four engines. AQT, Qwix, Flax fp8_ops, and TransformerEngine all hang off the same quantization config string — swap the recipe without touching the model.

2Storage ≠ compute. lhs/rhs_qtype sets the operand dtype; the *_grad_qtype E5M2 sets the gradient-GEMM precision independently — the same "store one width, compute another" pattern V4 ships.

3Block 128 today. The native 16-element NVFP4 block is gated on fused RHT; until then the JAX path runs a coarser 128 block.

4V3 tiling as a partial. The 1×128 activation / 128×128 weight granularity V3 hand-rolled is a tiling_fn with the right tile_size — and SR is enabled only on the VJP (gradient) GEMMs.

5Router stays high by construction. Quant routes only over dot_general/gmm/ragged_dot inside decoder/.*layers.*; the GateLogit einsum is not in that set, so it never quantizes.

  • V3-style tiling is the AQT _tiling_fn/TiledDotGeneral path: the 1×128 activation / 128×128 weight granularity that V3 hand-rolled is a tiling_fn partial with the appropriate tile_size. The mixed-precision JSON (dense_llm_subchannel.json) shows the regex-per-layer mechanism — e.g. query w4/tile_size=128, key w4/tile_size=256 — which is exactly the machinery to express "experts NVFP4, router/attention/norms BF16, first-2/last-8 blocks BF16."
  • Delayed scaling (the classic TE algorithm) lives in fp8_ops with amax_history_length=1024 and _overwrite_with_gradient scale variables — a tracked amax history that lags the current tensor by up to 1024 steps. For a stable long run this is fine; for the LR-decay phase where dynamic range shifts, the per-tensor current-scale AQT/Qwix path or a shorter history is safer.
  • NVFP4 on Blackwell is the te_nvfp4 recipe, currently at block 128 pending the fused-RHT kernel that unlocks the native 16-block. The NVIDIA "Train Models Faster with JAX and MaxText Using NVFP4 on Blackwell" blog documents this integration end-to-end and is the bridge between the NVFP4 paper and the MaxText/JAX stack the target run will use.
  • KV-cache quantization is orthogonal and already wired: quantize_kvcache + kv_quant_axis (default heads_and_dkv) in base.yml. V4's "FP8 KV with BF16 on RoPE dims" maps onto this — quantize the non-RoPE KV, keep the rotary dims high.

09Concrete recommendation for the 10T/200B/100k-GB200 run

Putting the recipe together into a single prescription:

  1. Baseline everything in FP8 (E4M3 forward, fine-grained scaling), experts in NVFP4 QAT. This mirrors V4's "FP4 on experts, FP8 elsewhere" partition and captures the ~2× FP4 compute multiplier on the dominant GEMM class while keeping the precision-sensitive 10% safe. On GB200's dense ~9 PFLOP/s FP4 vs ~4.5 PFLOP/s FP8 per B200, the expert GEMMs are where the throughput is won.
  2. Use NVFP4, not MXFP4, for compute. The 36%-more-tokens penalty of MXFP4 is unaffordable at 10T scale. Reserve MXFP4 only for the expert-parallel all-to-all comms compression if profiling shows the fabric is the bottleneck (the §05 pattern, RTNE not SR).
  3. Apply the full four-technique NVFP4 recipe: ~16% of layers BF16 (first ~2 + last ~8 of the decoder, scaled to depth), 16×16 RHT on Wgrad inputs with a fixed shared sign vector, 2D 16×16 weight block scaling, SR on gradients only. In MaxText, that is te_nvfp4 (not te_nvfp4_no_rht) plus the AQT set_stochastic_rounding asymmetry, scoped via the per-layer regex.
  4. Keep router, all norms, embeddings, LM head, and all attention operators out of FP4 — out of the dot_general/gmm/ragged_dot quant scope. Set float32_qk_product and float32_logits true. Keep FP32 master weights and FP32 gradient accumulators; BF16 optimizer moments are fine.
  5. Plan a decay-phase precision switch. Run NVFP4 for ~80–85% of tokens, then flip experts to FP8/BF16 for the LR-decay tail. This recovers ~1% relative loss for ~6% of compute — the highest-leverage single intervention. Wire it into the LR schedule and checkpoint cadence up front, not as an afterthought.
  6. Never plan to PTQ this model to 4-bit for inference. At 50:1 tokens-to-active-params the δPTQ fragility is severe; if 4-bit inference is wanted, the experts are already QAT-FP4 and decode losslessly into FP8, exactly as V4 does. INT4 weight-only QAT on MoE in post-training (the Kimi K2-Thinking pattern, ~594GB / ~2× decode) is the inference move, applied as QAT, not PTQ.
  7. Keep skip_step_on_spikes armed with a tuned scaling_factor to absorb the elevated loss variance that 4-bit introduces, and treat any sustained loss-gap widening (the >1.5% decay-phase signal) as the trigger to advance the precision switch.
The strategic read

FP8 is the floor, NVFP4-QAT-on-experts is the frontier that DeepSeek-V4 and the NVIDIA 12B/10T run have jointly proven trainable, and the engineering — not the format — is what separates a converging 4-bit run from a diverging one. For a build at this scale, the precision recipe is not an optimization to bolt on later; it is a first-class architectural decision that shapes the LR schedule, the layer-precision map, the optimizer config, and the comms layout from day one.

Sources

  • DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report (FP8 fine-grained recipe, accumulation fix, precision-breaks map): arxiv.org/abs/2412.19437
  • Pretraining Large Language Models with NVFP4 (four-technique recipe, NVFP4 vs MXFP4, decay-phase switch): arxiv.org/abs/2509.25149
  • Practical FP4 Training for Large-Scale MoE Models on Hopper (FP8↔FP4 conversion, comms compression): arxiv.org/abs/2603.02731
  • DeepSeek-V4 report and config discussion (FP4 QAT on experts + indexer): arxiv.org/html/2606.19348v1, HF discussion, HF blog
  • Scaling Laws for Precision (Kumar et al., ICLR 2025): arxiv.org/abs/2411.04330
  • A One-Stop Guide to Scaling Laws in LLM Quantization (Wang synthesis): mr-philo.github.io
  • OCP Microscaling Formats (MX) v1.0 specification: opencompute.org — MX v1.0
  • NVIDIA — Train Models Faster with JAX and MaxText Using NVFP4 on Blackwell: developer.nvidia.com/blog
  • Kimi K2 Technical Report (MuonClip, logit-cap stability relevant to attention precision): arxiv.org/html/2507.20534
  • NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 product page (dense vs sparse FLOP footnote): nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/gb200-nvl72
  • MaxText: quantizations.py, dequantize_mxfp4.py, optimizers/optimizers.py, layers/moe.py, configs/base.yml

Chapter 10 · The optimizer is an economics decision

Optimizers and hyperparameter transfer at scale: Muon, MuonClip, Shampoo/SOAP, muP, and schedules

For a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200s, the optimizer is not a tuning knob — it is a systems-and-economics decision that propagates into your token budget, your inter-node communication volume, your optimizer-state memory (~tens of TB), and your stability envelope across a multi-month run.

~28 min read6 figuresgrounded in MaxText optimizers/optimizers.py & utils/muon_utils.py

The frontier has, over 18 months, converged hard: every trillion-scale MoE shipped or rumored in 2025–2026 — Moonlight, Kimi K2, DeepSeek-V4 — runs Muon on the 2D hidden weights and AdamW on the embeddings, head, and norms, with a logit-stability patch (QK-Clip or RMSNorm-on-QK) bolted on. This chapter argues that stack is correct for your target, quantifies the real (and over-claimed) efficiency gains, and grounds every choice in the user's actual codebase (MaxText), whose optimizers/optimizers.py and utils/muon_utils.py already implement exactly this split via the optax.contrib._muon.muon transform.

Do not justify Muon with the 2× number. The honest case is ~10–15% token savings plus large-batch robustness plus halved optimizer-state memory plus a stability story battle-tested to 15.5T tokens with zero spikes — and at 100k GB200s the last three are worth far more than the first.
1.95×
Moonlight token-efficiency claim
10–15%
honest iso-loss token saving (Essential AI)
~20TB
bf16 Muon momentum at 10T params
<1%
Newton-Schulz FLOP overhead

01Muon: steepest descent under the spectral norm

Muon (MomentUm Orthogonalized by Newton-Schulz, Keller Jordan, Oct 2024) is the practical instantiation of one idea: the right geometry for a 2D weight matrix is the spectral norm, and steepest descent in that norm orthogonalizes the update. Concretely, for each 2D hidden weight, Muon maintains an SGD-momentum buffer M (β=0.95 default) and replaces the raw update with O = NS(M) ≈ UVᵀ, the orthogonal factor of M's SVD, computed without an SVD via five Newton-Schulz iterations of the quintic φ(X) = aX + bXXᵀX + c(XXᵀ)²X with the now-canonical coefficients (a,b,c) = (3.4445, −4.7750, 2.0315), all in bf16 (Jordan).

optim φ(X) = a·X + b·XXᵀX + c·(XXᵀ)²X,  (a,b,c) = (3.4445, −4.7750, 2.0315) Quintic Newton-Schulz iteration. φ′(0) = a ≈ 3.44 is maximized so small singular values are amplified toward 1 fast — not tuned to converge to the identity.

The coefficient choice is the part most readers get wrong. They are deliberately not tuned to make φ converge to the identity on [0,1]. Instead φ′(0) = a ≈ 3.44 is maximized so that small singular values — which dominate a freshly-accumulated momentum matrix — are amplified toward 1 as fast as possible. Jordan tolerates a spectral error of ε ≈ 0.3 in the resulting near-orthogonal matrix without measurable loss degradation, which is precisely why 5 steps suffice rather than the 15–30 a convergent iteration would need (Jordan). The FLOP overhead of the NS iterations is <1% of training compute (0.7% at NanoGPT width m=768, projected ~0.5% at a Llama-405B-scale m=16,384 with B=16M tokens). At your 200B-active scale the matmul-heavy NS iterations are trivial against the MoE GEMMs — the cost of Muon is communication and memory, not FLOPs (§07).

The one thing to remember about Muon

Muon's cost is communication and memory, not FLOPs. The Newton-Schulz iterations are <1% of compute; the all-gather of the momentum matrix and the optimizer-state footprint are what bite at 100k-GPU scale. Optimize the optimizer for the network, not the math.

Muon is matrix-only by construction. Embeddings, the unembedding/classifier head, RMSNorm gains, and all biases are 1D or have a privileged basis where orthogonalization is meaningless or harmful, and are handed to AdamW. This is not a heuristic — it is the same split Jeremy Bernstein's modular/spectral-norm framework predicts, and it is exactly what MaxText encodes. In utils/muon_utils.py, transform_logic returns None (→ routed to AdamW) for any parameter path containing scale, bias, embedding, or logits_dense, and otherwise emits a MuonDimensionNumbers with hand-specified reduction/output-axis tuples per weight class.

Weight classPath matchMuonDimensionNumbers (reduction, output)
MoE expert (wi_0, wi_1, wo)MoeBlock_0((-2,), (-1,))
Attention out-projself_attention + out((0,-2), (-1,))
QKV / MLA wq_b,wkv_bself_attention + query/key/value/wq_b/wkv_b((0,), (-2,-1))
Standard 2D weightfall-through((0,), (-1,))
 MaxText utils/muon_utils.py — per-class axis tuples (the user's actual codebase).

The per-class tuples matter because MaxText's weights carry a leading layer axis when scan_layers=True (the L in the comments) and the expert/attention reshapes have non-trivial layouts; orthogonalization must contract the matmul axes, not the layer or expert-batch axes. Note that MLA's down-projections wq_a/wkv_a are deliberately excluded from the QKV rule — they are low-rank compression factors, not the head-shaped projections Muon is designed for.

src/maxtext/utils/muon_utils.pytransform_logicmatrix-only splittrimmed
def transform_logic(path):                                   # [1]
    name = "/".join(str(k) for k in path)
    if any(s in name for s in ("scale", "bias",              # [2]
                               "embedding", "logits_dense")):
        return None                                          # -> AdamW sub-optimizer
    if "MoeBlock_0" in name:                                  # [3]
        return MuonDimensionNumbers(reduction=(-2,), output=(-1,))
    if "self_attention" in name and "out" in name:
        return MuonDimensionNumbers(reduction=(0, -2), output=(-1,))
    if "self_attention" in name and any(                     # [4]
        q in name for q in ("query","key","value","wq_b","wkv_b")):
        return MuonDimensionNumbers(reduction=(0,), output=(-2, -1))
    return MuonDimensionNumbers(reduction=(0,), output=(-1,)) # standard 2D

1One function decides the split. Returning None routes a parameter to AdamW; anything else goes to Muon with explicit axis numbers.

21D & privileged-basis params. scale/bias/embedding/logits_dense have no spectral geometry to exploit — orthogonalization would be meaningless or harmful.

3Reductions contract matmul axes. With scan_layers=True a leading layer axis L must be preserved — the tuples contract the GEMM axes only, not the layer or expert-batch axes.

4MLA wq_a/wkv_a excluded. The down-projections are low-rank compression factors, not head-shaped projections, so they fall through rather than matching the QKV rule.

02The two fixes that made Muon scale (Moonlight)

Out-of-the-box Muon — as it won NanoGPT speedruns — does not scale to billions of parameters. Moonshot's Muon is Scalable (Liu et al., arXiv:2502.16982) diagnosed two failures, both fixable with zero retuning of the base LR:

fix 1 · weight decay
decoupled λ = 0.1
Plain Muon lets weight norms grow unbounded; singular values of W drift and the orthogonalized update stops being well-conditioned.
fix 2 · update-scale match
0.2 · √max(A,B)
NS output RMS ≈ 1/√max(A,B) is shape-dependent; rescale every matrix to effective RMS ≈ 0.2 so one shared LR works.
  1. Add weight decay. Plain Muon lets weight norms grow unbounded; the singular values of W drift and the orthogonalized update stops being well-conditioned. Moonlight applies decoupled AdamW-style decay λW with λ = 0.1 for every training stage.
  2. Per-parameter update-scale matching. The NS output O has RMS ≈ 1/√max(A,B) for an A×B matrix — a shape-dependent update magnitude wildly different from AdamW's empirically-observed update RMS of 0.2–0.4. Without correction, a single LR cannot serve matrices of different shapes.
optim Wₜ = Wₜ₋₁ − ηₜ · ( 0.2 · Oₜ · √max(A,B) + λ·Wₜ₋₁ ) Moonlight's update rule. The 0.2·√max(A,B) factor rescales every matrix's effective update RMS to a constant ≈ 0.2, so a single shared LR — reused verbatim from an existing AdamW recipe — works across all shapes (arXiv:2502.16982).

In MaxText this is the muon_consistent_rms config (base.yml default None, recommended 0.2). When set to a float, the optax muon transform applies the constant-RMS rescaling; when None, it falls back to width-transfer scaling √max(1, fan_out/fan_in) — the muP-flavored path (§05). The other knobs are muon_beta=0.95, muon_weight_decay (set to 0.1 for a real run; base default is 0), inheriting adam_b1/b2, adam_eps, mu_dtype, and adam_weight_decay for the AdamW sub-optimizer that handles the excluded params (the user's actual codebase, MaxText).

Two defaults are off — you must set them

muon_consistent_rms defaults to None (falls back to width-scaling) and muon_weight_decay defaults to 0. For a real run you must set muon_consistent_rms=0.2 and muon_weight_decay=0.1 explicitly, or you get neither of Moonlight's two scaling fixes.

Moonlight itself is a DeepSeek-V3-small-style MoE — 2.24B activated / 15.29B total, 64 experts top-2, 5.7T tokens — and its headline claim launched the field: Muon reaches compute-optimal loss at ~52% of AdamW's training FLOPs (~1.95× token-efficiency), fitted over 399M–1.5B scaling-law runs (arXiv:2502.16982).

03The "2×" claim, interrogated

The 1.95× figure is the most over-cited and most fragile number in the optimizer literature, and for a 100k-GPU capital commitment you must not budget against it. Essential AI's Practical Efficiency of Muon for Pretraining (arXiv:2505.02222) is the decisive adversarial check. Sweeping 100M–4B params, two data distributions, and batch sizes from 128K to 16M tokens — and crucially tuning AdamW as carefully as Muon — they find Muon needs only ~10–15% fewer tokens for iso-loss, not ~50%. The gap from 1.95× to 1.1× is almost entirely a baseline-tuning artifact: Moonlight froze an AdamW recipe and ported it, handing Muon an under-tuned opponent.

So why still run Muon? Because Essential AI's durable finding is the one that actually maps to your hardware. The data-efficiency ratio R_L(B) of Muon over AdamW is non-decreasing in batch size and stays above 1 deep into the over-large-batch regime, out to 16M-token batches (arXiv:2505.02222). AdamW's relative efficiency collapses once you push past its critical batch size; spectral-norm descent degrades gracefully. At 100k GB200s your data-parallel width forces enormous global batches (K2 used 67M tokens, DeepSeek-V4 peaks at 75–94M; §06) — exactly the regime where AdamW falls off the cliff and Muon does not.

The real reason to adopt Muon

The reason to adopt Muon is large-batch robustness, not a mythical 2× token saving. Budget for ~10–15% fewer tokens and treat large-batch resilience as the real prize — it is what lets you actually exploit 100k-GPU data-parallel width.

The mechanism has a recent first-principles backing. Spectral Scaling Laws of Muon (MIT, arXiv:2606.04058, June 2026) shows the singular-value quantiles of momentum matrices follow clean power laws in model size with layer-dependent exponents: mid-depth layers degrade as ~M−0.25 (so a fixed 5-step NS keeps them well-orthonormalized at much larger scale), but final-layer projections degrade as steeply as ~M−0.96, meaning the output projections are the first to outrun a 5-step iteration and need more polishing as you scale. This is the predictive justification for DeepSeek-V4's two-stage NS below — and a warning that a single global ns_steps is a scale-dependent choice.

04MuonClip, QK-Clip, and the V4 alternative

Muon excites the one failure mode AdamW largely suppresses: exploding attention logits. Because Muon equalizes the update RMS across all singular directions of Wq and Wk, it actively drives the product of their singular values upward, and the pre-softmax score S = QKᵀ/√d grows unbounded. Kimi K2 (arXiv:2507.20534) reports max logits "rapidly exceeding 1000" under vanilla Muon at a 9B-activated proxy scale; past ~O(10³) the bf16 softmax saturates and diverges.

MuonClip = Muon + weight decay + consistent-RMS + QK-Clip. QK-Clip is a post-step surgical rescale, not a forward-pass modification. After each optimizer step, for every head h whose max logit S_max^h exceeds threshold τ=100, compute γ_h = min(1, τ/S_max^h) and split it across the two projections: Wq^h ← γ^α·Wq^h, Wk^h ← γ^(1−α)·Wk^h with α=0.5 (each gets √γ). It is per-head and conditional — only offending heads are touched — and self-extinguishing: K2 reports the cap stops binding after ~30% of steps, logits self-decay to a normal range, and τ never needs retuning. Result: 15.5T tokens, zero loss spikes (arXiv:2507.20534). For MLA the handling is component-specific: the non-RoPE q^c, k^c each get √γ, the decoupled per-head RoPE query q^r gets the full γ, and the shared RoPE key k^r is left untouched to avoid cross-head contamination.

optim γ_h = min(1, τ / S_max^h),  Wq^h ← √γ·Wq^h,  Wk^h ← √γ·Wk^h  (τ=100, α=0.5) Per-head, conditional, post-step. Only heads whose max logit exceeds τ are rescaled; self-extinguishing — the cap stops binding after ~30% of K2's steps.

MaxText ships this exactly, in utils/qk_clip_utils.py (the user's actual codebase, MaxText): _scale_from_max_logits returns min(1, τ/(s_max + 1e-6)); _clip_mla_weight scales the nope head-slice of wq_b/wkv_b by √scale, scales the q^r tail of wq_b by the full scale, and leaves the V-slice of wkv_b untouched. Max logits are sown from the attention op (attention_mla.py, record_max_logits=use_qk_clip), and a config guard (types.py) forbids use_qk_clip together with attn_logits_soft_cap — QK-Clip must read raw dot products, so tanh-capping them first would corrupt the measurement.

src/maxtext/utils/qk_clip_utils.py_clip_mla_weightper-head QK-Cliptrimmed
def _scale_from_max_logits(s_max, tau):                       # [1]
    return jnp.minimum(1.0, tau / (s_max + 1e-6))            # γ_h, per head

def _clip_mla_weight(wq_b, wkv_b, s_max, tau):                # [2]
    scale = _scale_from_max_logits(s_max, tau)               # [B,H]
    rt    = jnp.sqrt(scale)
    wq_b  = wq_b.at[..., nope_slice].multiply(rt)            # q^c : √γ
    wkv_b = wkv_b.at[..., k_nope_slice].multiply(rt)         # k^c : √γ
    wq_b  = wq_b.at[..., rope_slice].multiply(scale)         # q^r : full γ  [3]
    # k^r (shared RoPE key) and V-slice of wkv_b: untouched   # [4]
    return wq_b, wkv_b

1Conditional by construction. min(1, τ/s_max) is exactly 1 for well-behaved heads, so only offending heads are rescaled — and +1e-6 guards the divide.

2Component-specific for MLA. The non-RoPE q^c/k^c slices each absorb √γ — the standard symmetric split.

3Decoupled RoPE query gets full γ. q^r has no symmetric partner to share with, so it takes the whole scale.

4Shared key left alone. The shared RoPE key k^r is untouched to avoid cross-head contamination; tanh soft-cap is forbidden so the max-logit read is on raw scores.

DeepSeek-V4 (report ~Apr 2026) reaches the same end by a different route, and the differences are load-bearing for a GB200 build:

  • It discards QK-Clip and instead applies RMSNorm directly on attention queries and KV entries, bounding logits structurally in the forward pass rather than surgically after each step. (This is the OLMo-2/Gemma-3 lineage: Gemma-3 itself dropped attention soft-cap in favor of QK-norm.)
  • Two-stage Newton-Schulz: 8 steps at (3.4445, −4.7750, 2.0315), then 2 steps at (2, −1.5, 0.5) — an aggressive amplifier followed by a near-identity polisher. This is precisely the remedy Spectral Scaling Laws predicts the final-layer projections need at scale (arXiv:2606.04058).
  • Update RMS rescaled to 0.18 (not Moonlight's 0.2) to reuse its AdamW LR.
  • Optimizer split: AdamW for the embedding module, prediction head, mHC static biases/gating factors, and all RMSNorm weights; Muon for everything else; λ=0.1 for both.

V4 scales: V4-Flash 284B/13B-active, 32T tokens, peak batch 75.5M; V4-Pro 1.6T/49B-active, 33T tokens, peak batch 94.4M (report). MaxText's deepseek4-284b.yml config (hash routing layers, use_qk_norm) is the closest in-repo template (the user's actual codebase, MaxText).

Recommendation for 10T/200B — logit stability

Both stability paths work. RMSNorm-on-QK (V4) is the cleaner default — it is a forward-pass invariant, composes trivially with FP8 attention kernels, needs no per-step max-logit reduction across the mesh, and is already a supported flag (use_qk_norm) in your codebase. QK-Clip (K2) is the better choice only if you are forced into vanilla Muon without QK-norm and need a retrofit, or want the empirical comfort of K2's exact 15.5T-token zero-spike result (use τ=100). For the NS schedule, adopt V4's two-stage 8+2 at the trillion scale — your output projections are deep into the M−0.96 regime and a flat 5-step iteration under-orthonormalizes them.

05muP, u-µP, and the awkward overlap with Muon

muTransfer (Tensor Programs V, Yang & Hu) gives width-invariant optima via abc-parameterization: hidden-layer LR ∝ 1/fan_in, init variance ∝ 1/fan_in, with constants tuned on a small proxy and transferred to the full model. The subtlety here is that Muon already implements much of muP's per-layer LR scaling for free. Bernstein's derivation shows that dualizing the gradient in the correct (spectral) norm is the muP LR scaling; the √max(A,B) factor in Moonlight's rule and the √max(1, fan_out/fan_in) width-scaling in MaxText's consistent_rms=None path are the same object muP would prescribe.

Do not double-count the width factor

Naive double-application — full muP on top of consistent-RMS Muon — double-counts the width factor and breaks transfer. Resolve it explicitly: either use Muon's consistent_rms=0.2 (a single global LR, width handled internally) or Essential AI's telescoping muP-for-Muon, which accounts for all muP error sources at O(C log N) overhead and demonstrates clean LR and coupled-weight-decay transfer up to 3.7B params at seqlen 8192 (final sweep λ=0.027, η₀=0.0612, 1.61 nats) (arXiv:2505.02222). Do not stack both.

For the AdamW-governed params (embeddings, head, norms) muP still earns its keep, and the version to actually adopt at this scale is u-µP (Blake et al., Graphcore, arXiv:2407.17465, ICLR 2025). u-µP fuses Unit Scaling with µP so all weights init at unit variance, hyperparameters become independent (transfer error 0.005 vs µP's 0.03), and every HP defaults to 1 except a single global η — collapsing the sweep from a multi-dimensional grid to ~9 LR-only runs. Decisively for GB200: u-µP trains stably in FP8 at 7B with near-BF16 loss, where standard µP diverges from gradient underflow (arXiv:2407.17465). On Blackwell's FP8/FP4 tensor cores that is not a nicety — it is the difference between a usable and a diverging low-precision run, and it pairs naturally with MaxText's float32_logits / fp8 paths in layers/quantizations.py.

0.005vs 0.03
u-µP vs µP transfer error
~9runs
LR-only sweep (was a grid)
7B
stable FP8 train, near-BF16 loss

The one caveat to state loudly: depth transfer remains the weak axis for all of these. Every clean transfer claim above — telescoping muP, u-µP, vanilla µP — is width and batch transfer. Depth transfer (more layers) is not reliably solved; mHC-style residual normalization (the V4 doubly-stochastic Hyper-Connections trick) is doing structural work that HP transfer alone cannot. For a 10T MoE, fix your depth early on the proxy, transfer width/batch/LR/λ, and validate the final depth with at least one full-width short run before committing the cluster.

Depth transfer is unsolved stated caveat

All clean transfer results are width-and-batch only. Fix depth early on the proxy, transfer width/batch/LR/λ, and validate the final depth with at least one full-width short run before committing the cluster. mHC residual normalization is doing structural work HP transfer alone cannot.

Concrete transfer recipe for the 10T/200B target

1. Pick proxy width ~1/16–1/32 of target, same depth as target, same seqlen. 2. u-µP parameterization; sweep ~9 global-LR runs to find η₀. 3. Pick λ via Power Lines (§06), not by sweeping — it transfers as a power law. 4. Transfer η₀, λ, init to full width with Muon consistent_rms=0.2; do not re-apply µP width scaling on the Muon params. 5. One full-width, short-horizon validation run before the real launch.

06Batch size, weight decay, and the WSD schedule at 10T tokens

The load-bearing scaling law for HP selection here is Power Lines (Bergsma et al., Cerebras, arXiv:2505.13738), which reframes weight decay through the AdamW EMA timescale τ = B/(η·λ·D) — the fraction of past iterations effectively averaged into the final weights. Its fitted laws:

QuantityLawUse
Optimal EMA timescaleτ_opt ∝ (D/N)−0.52 = TPP−0.52derive λ from it
Optimal weight decayλ_opt = B/(c_τ·η·D)·TPP0.52set λ, don't sweep
Optimal batchB_opt ∝ D0.4target throughput batch
Critical batchB_crit ∝ D0.5data-parallel ceiling
Over-batch penaltyD = D_min·(1 + B/B_crit)token cost of going wide
 Power Lines fitted laws (arXiv:2505.13738). TPP = tokens-per-parameter = D/N.

The penalty term is the one that prices your data-parallel ambitions: running at B = B_crit costs 2× the minimum tokens (arXiv:2505.13738). For a 10T-token budget, B_crit ∝ D0.5 lands in the tens of millions of tokens, consistent with the observed K2 (67M) and V4 (75–94M) peak batches. This is exactly where Muon's persistence past B_crit (§03) becomes the lever that lets you exploit 100k GB200s: AdamW would pay a steep extra-token penalty to run that wide, Muon pays less. Pragmatically: target B somewhat below B_crit at the throughput sweet spot, accept the (1 + B/B_crit) token penalty as the cost of parallelism, and let Muon absorb the over-batch regime. McCandlish's gradient-noise-scale gives a cheap online B_crit proxy but is explicitly distrusted as a sole guide — pair it with a direct empirical CBS measurement (the Ai2 protocol) on the proxy.

Schedule: WSD over cosine. Use warmup-stable-decay. The constant stable phase lets you branch short decays off a single trunk run to probe the scaling law continuously — invaluable when one full run costs weeks of 100k-GPU time. The river-valley-landscape analysis (arXiv:2410.05192) explains why the sharp final decay recovers the loss a cosine would have spent the whole run earning. K2's concrete instance: constant 2e-4 for 10T tokens, then cosine to 2e-5 over the final 5.5T (arXiv:2507.20534).

MaxText supports this natively. base.yml exposes lr_schedule_type: 'wsd' with warmup_steps_fraction (linear warmup), a stable phase, then a wsd_decay_steps_fraction (default 0.1) decay of wsd_decay_style linear or cosine down to learning_rate_final_fraction (default 0.1) of peak (the user's actual codebase, MaxText). For the 10T run: warmup ~1–2% of steps, a long stable trunk, and a 10–20% terminal decay; branch your scaling-law probes off the stable phase rather than running independent cosine schedules. Complement with batch-size warmup — ramp B during early training while the gradient noise scale is small — to avoid wasting the expensive large batch on the easy early loss.

yaml — MaxText base.yml WSD knobs for the 10T run
lr_schedule_type: "wsd"            # warmup-stable-decay over cosine
learning_rate: 2.0e-4              # constant stable-phase peak (K2-shaped)
warmup_steps_fraction: 0.015       # ~1.5% linear warmup
wsd_decay_steps_fraction: 0.20     # 10-20% terminal decay window
wsd_decay_style: "cosine"          # cosine | linear terminal shape
learning_rate_final_fraction: 0.10 # decay to 2e-5 = 0.1 * peak
# branch short decays off the stable trunk to probe the scaling law
Branch probes off the stable trunk

The whole point of WSD at 100k-GPU scale: a single long stable phase lets you fork short terminal decays to read the scaling law continuously, instead of paying for independent cosine runs that each cost weeks. Pair with batch-size warmup so you don't waste the expensive large batch on the easy early loss.

07Optimizer-state memory and sharding at 10T

Muon's first structural win at this scale is memory: it stores one momentum buffer vs AdamW's two (μ + ν), halving optimizer state on the matrix params. But "halved" is still enormous. At 10T total parameters even bf16 momentum is ~20 TB, and a naive fp32 master copy doubles that — it must be sharded, and the sharding strategy interacts with the NS iteration.

ApproachComm volume / stepUnshards full matrix?Notes
Distributed Muon (Moonlight §A)(1, 1.25]× of distributed AdamWYes (transient DP all-gather for NS)ZeRO-1 partition; all-gather full matrix, NS in bf16, scatter back
Dion (arXiv:2504.05295)O((m+n)r) factors onlyNoamortized rank-r power iteration + error feedback (β=0.05); composes with FSDP+TP+DDP
AdamW (baseline)standard all-reducen/a2× the state of Muon
 Optimizer-state sharding strategies. The full-matrix all-gather is Distributed Muon's bottleneck; Dion never unshards.

Distributed Muon (Moonlight) uses ZeRO-1: partition the momentum across the DP axis, all-gather the full matrix only transiently to run the NS iteration, do NS in bf16, scatter back. Communication lands in (1, 1.25]× of distributed AdamW — acceptable, but the all-gather of the full momentum matrix is the bottleneck at GB200 scale and grows with model width (arXiv:2502.16982).

Dion (Microsoft, arXiv:2504.05295) is the more scalable answer and the one to seriously evaluate for 100k GPUs. It replaces Newton-Schulz with amortized rank-r power iteration (warm-started right basis V, error feedback Mₙₑ𝓌 ← M − β·UWᵀ, β=0.05), exchanging only O((m+n)r) low-rank factors instead of O(mn), and — critically — never unshards the full matrix (1D/2D-sharded variants with distributed Cholesky-QR exchanging only O(r²) scalars). It composes with FSDP+TP+DDP simultaneously, and larger models tolerate lower rank (a 3B model matches full-rank Muon at r = d/4). For your DP-sync-bound regime, Dion turns the O(mn) all-gather into orders-of-magnitude less traffic — the difference between Muon being free and Muon being a network tax.

Dion is the difference between free and a network tax

In a DP-sync-bound regime at 100k GPUs, the O(mn) full-matrix all-gather Distributed Muon needs for Newton-Schulz is the optimizer's cost. Dion exchanges only O((m+n)r) low-rank factors and never unshards — turning that all-gather into orders-of-magnitude less traffic. Evaluate it seriously.

Two further upgrades worth ablating:

  • NorMuon (arXiv:2510.05491) adds per-neuron second-moment normalization after orthogonalization — Muon's orthogonalized updates have highly non-uniform per-neuron norms, letting a few neurons dominate. It reports +11.3% over Muon (and +21.7% over Adam) at 1.1B with comparable memory under FSDP2. A plausible drop-in once the base stack is validated.
  • Shampoo/SOAP as the AdamW-replacement alternative: SOAP (arXiv:2409.11321) runs AdamW in the eigenbasis of Shampoo's preconditioner (formally, Shampoo with the ½ power equals Adafactor in that eigenbasis), and beats both AdamW and Shampoo in large-batch LLM pretraining in iterations and wall-clock. SOAP is a serious contender, but it carries full L/R preconditioner state (more memory than Muon's single momentum buffer) and a heavier per-step eigendecomposition. At 10T params, Muon's halved state and <1% FLOP overhead make it the more economical second-order-flavored choice; reserve SOAP for if Muon's large-batch advantage fails to materialize on your data.

08Spike guarding and the integrated train step

No amount of optimizer theory survives 100k GPUs over months without a runtime backstop, because most trillion-scale spikes are late-emergent (they appear only at high token counts a proxy never reaches) and some are not optimizer pathologies at all — OLMo-2 traced many to data batches with long repeated n-grams, and SDC can silently corrupt a node's gradients with a loss curve "identical" to a healthy one.

MaxText's skip_step_on_spikes (optimizers/optimizers.py, ported from OLMo-core's skip_step_optimizer) is the production guard. It wraps the inner optax optimizer (AdamW, Muon, or muon+skip), keeps rolling buffers of the last skip_step_interval=128 losses and grad-norms with Bessel-corrected std, and if value − rolling_mean > scaling_factor·std (default scaling_factor=6.0) for either loss or grad-norm, it zeros the update via lax.cond so the spike batch never enters the optimizer's moments — momentum is not poisoned (the user's actual codebase, MaxText). Two subtleties in the implementation matter: it still appends the spike value to the buffer ("so spikes can become the new baseline," preventing a permanent self-clamp), and it warms up for max(2, interval//2) steps before enforcing, so early-training transients don't trip it. This is strictly superior to global gradient clipping, which still lets a corrupted direction through at clipped magnitude; skip-step rejects the whole batch.

src/maxtext/optimizers/optimizers.pyskip_step_on_spikes6σ outer guardtrimmed
def update(value, grad_norm, buf, count):                     # [1]
    mean = buf.mean()
    std  = buf.std(ddof=1)                                    # Bessel-corrected
    spike = ((value     - mean) > cfg.scaling_factor * std) | (   # 6.0σ
             (grad_norm - gn_mean) > cfg.scaling_factor * gn_std)
    warming = count < max(2, cfg.skip_step_interval // 2)     # [2]
    do_skip = spike & ~warming
    updates = jax.lax.cond(do_skip, lambda u: zeros_like(u),  # [3]
                           lambda u: u, inner_updates)
    buf = roll_append(buf, value)                             # [4] append even spikes
    return updates, buf

1Rolling 128-step window. Mean/std over the last skip_step_interval=128 losses and grad-norms, Bessel-corrected (ddof=1).

2Warmup before enforcing. The first max(2, interval//2) steps don't trip the guard, so early-training transients aren't mistaken for spikes.

3Zero, don't clip. A 6σ outlier in either loss or grad-norm zeros the whole update via lax.cond — momentum is never poisoned, unlike grad-clipping which lets a bad direction through.

4Spikes still update the baseline. The spike value is appended to the buffer so a genuine new regime can become the baseline — preventing a permanent self-clamp.

The full train step composes the optimizer with the other stability machinery (trainers/pre_train/train.py): z-loss (z_loss_multiplier, set ~1e-4 to bound the output-softmax log-partition and bf16 roundoff), MoE load-balance aggregation, and aux-loss-free router-bias updates applied post-optimizer via update_state_param — the DeepSeek-V3 sign(avg_load − count)·rate nudge that keeps experts balanced outside the gradient path, the correct default at 256+ experts. Gradient clipping (gradient_clipping_threshold=1.0) and gradient_accumulation_steps round it out. Note the layering: skip-step is the outer guard, z-loss/QK-norm are preventive, router-bias is post-hoc — and skip-step doubles as your SDC containment layer, stopping a corrupted batch from poisoning Adam/Muon momentum before the bad node is quarantined.

The layering of the integrated train step

skip-step is the outer guard, z-loss/QK-norm are preventive, router-bias is post-hoc. skip_step_on_spikes wraps the inner optax optimizer (interval 128, 6σ); z-loss (~1e-4) and QK-norm prevent spikes structurally; the aux-loss-free router-bias nudge is applied post-optimizer via update_state_param. skip-step also doubles as SDC containment.

09The recommended stack for 10T/200B on 100k GB200

Pulling the chapter together into a concrete build:

ComponentChoiceRationale
Matrix optimizerMuon, β=0.95, λ=0.1, consistent_rms=0.2 (or 0.18 à la V4)half AdamW state, graceful past B_crit
Newton-Schulz8+2 two-stage (V4), bf16final-proj M−0.96 needs polishing at scale
1D params (emb/head/norm)AdamW, λ=0.1, mask norms from decayorthogonalization meaningless on these
Logit stabilityRMSNorm-on-QK (default) or QK-Clip τ=100 (retrofit)bounds the dominant trillion-scale failure
HP transferu-µP, ~9 LR-only runs, no double width-scaling on MuonFP8-stable, near-zero transfer error
Weight decayλ from Power Lines τ_opt ∝ TPP−0.52, not swepttransfers as a power law
Batch sizenear B_crit ∝ D0.5 (tens of M tokens), batch-warmupexploits 100k-GPU DP width
ScheduleWSD, long stable trunk + 10–20% terminal decaybranchable scaling-law probes
State shardingDion (rank-r, never unshards) if DP-sync-bound, else Distributed Muon ZeRO-1O((m+n)r) vs O(mn) comm
Runtime guardskip_step_on_spikes (interval 128, 6σ) + z-loss 1e-4 + aux-loss-free router biasspike + SDC containment
 The recommended optimizer stack for 10T/200B on 100k GB200 — every row already wired into MaxText today.
The single most important framing

Do not justify Muon to your stakeholders with the 2× number. The honest case is ~10–15% token savings (arXiv:2505.02222) plus large-batch robustness plus halved optimizer-state memory plus a stability story (MuonClip/RMSNorm-on-QK) battle-tested to 15.5T tokens with zero spikes (arXiv:2507.20534). At 100k GB200s and 10T tokens, the second and third of those are worth far more than the first, and all four are already wired into the optimizer stack the user's codebase ships today.

Sources

  • Keller Jordan — Muon (NS coefficients, <1% FLOP overhead, ε≈0.3): kellerjordan.github.io/posts/muon
  • optax _muon.py (the muon transform MaxText calls): github.com/google-deepmind/optax/.../_muon.py
  • Muon is Scalable / Moonlight (1.95×, weight decay + consistent-RMS, Distributed Muon): arxiv.org/html/2502.16982v1
  • Essential AI — Practical Efficiency of Muon (10–15% iso-loss, large-batch R_L(B), telescoping muP): arxiv.org/html/2505.02222v1
  • Kimi K2 / MuonClip (QK-Clip τ=100, 15.5T tokens zero spikes, WSD 2e-4→2e-5): arxiv.org/html/2507.20534v1
  • DeepSeek-V4 report (RMSNorm-on-QK, 8+2 NS, FP4 QAT, V4-Flash/Pro scales): puiching-memory.github.io/.../deepseek-v4
  • Power Lines — weight decay & batch size (τ=B/(η·λ·D), B_crit ∝ D0.5): arxiv.org/html/2505.13738v2
  • Spectral Scaling Laws of Muon (layer-dependent exponents, M−0.96 final-proj): arxiv.org/html/2606.04058
  • u-µP (Graphcore; unit scaling + µP, FP8-stable 7B, ~9 LR runs): arxiv.org/html/2407.17465v1
  • Dion (Microsoft; rank-r power iteration, never unshards): arxiv.org/html/2504.05295v3
  • NorMuon (per-neuron 2nd-moment normalization, +11.3% over Muon): arxiv.org/abs/2510.05491
  • SOAP (AdamW in Shampoo's eigenbasis, large-batch pretraining): arxiv.org/abs/2409.11321
  • River-valley landscape / WSD analysis: arxiv.org/html/2410.05192
  • OLMo-core skip_step_optimizer (the source of skip_step_on_spikes): github.com/allenai/OLMo-core/.../skip_step_optimizer.py
  • MaxText qk_clip_utils.py (per-head QK-Clip, MLA component split): maxtext/.../utils/qk_clip_utils.py
  • MaxText optimizers.py (muon split, skip_step_on_spikes): maxtext/.../optimizers/optimizers.py

Chapter 11 · Instability is a budgeted line item

Training stability and failure modes for trillion-scale MoE

At 10T-total / 200B-active on 100,000 GB200 GPUs, a divergence that forces a rollback costs order $105 per recoverable hour — and the unrecoverable mode, silent weight drift, has no checkpoint to return to because you never saw it happen. This is the field manual for engineering against instability from step zero: the taxonomy, the mitigation stack, the router decision, the Kimi K2 zero-spike mechanism, and the exact MaxText knobs that implement all of it.

~28 min read6 figuresgrounded in MaxText qk_clip_utils.py / optimizers.py / max_utils.py

At 10T total / 200B active on 100k GB200s, instability is not a tail risk you patch reactively — it is a budgeted line item you engineer against from step zero. A single divergence that forces a rollback to the last good checkpoint costs, conservatively, hours of a fleet whose dense FP8 peak is ~0.45 EFLOP/s (100k B200 dies at ~4.5 PFLOP/s dense FP8 each; the headline 720 PFLOP/s-per-rack figures NVIDIA publishes are 2:4-sparse, and dense is exactly half that, so do not budget against them). At ~35–40% large-MoE FP8 MFU, every recoverable hour is order $105 in opportunity cost, and the unrecoverable failure mode — silent weight drift to a worse optimum — has no checkpoint to roll back to because you never saw it happen. The governing fact of the modern literature, from Wortsman et al.'s small-scale proxies (arXiv:2309.14322) through Kimi K2's 15.5T-token zero-spike run (arXiv:2507.20534), is that the instabilities that kill trillion-scale runs are late-emergent: they surface only at high token counts or high learning rate that a small-scale proxy with identical hyperparameters never reaches. The job is therefore preventive instrumentation plus structural fixes, with a runtime backstop for the residual. This chapter gives the taxonomy, the mitigation stack, the router-stability decision, the Kimi K2 zero-instability mechanism dissected, and the exact MaxText knobs that implement all of it.

Trillion-scale stability is a composition property, not a single trick: each mitigation closes exactly one failure family, and the run is stable only when all five are closed simultaneously. Diagnose by family, instrument the canaries, and never let a runtime guard quietly absorb a root cause.
5families
mechanistically distinct instabilities
15.5T tok
Kimi K2 zero-spike, zero-instability
1000+
max attention logit under vanilla Muon
1/15
bad nodes ABFT checksums caught

01A five-family taxonomy of trillion-scale instability

Every loss spike you will see decomposes into one of five mechanistically distinct families. Conflating them is the most common operational error: z-loss does nothing for attention-logit explosion, QK-Clip does nothing for a poisoned data batch, and gradient clipping does nothing for silent data corruption. Diagnose the family first.

FamilyMechanismPrimary monitored signalTargeted mitigation
(1) Attention logit explosionS = QKᵀ/√d grows unbounded via Wq·Wk singular-value product; softmax saturates, head gradient vanishes, bf16 exponent rounding divergesper-head max attention logitQK-norm / QK-Clip / soft-cap
(2) Router collapse / dead expertsSelf-reinforcing favoritism → load imbalance, dropped tokens, stragglers; plus router-logit grad spikesexpert-load entropy, max/min load ratio, dropped-token fractionaux-loss-free bias balancing
(3) Gradient-norm spikesOften a symptom; frequently a data pathology (repeated n-grams) not an optimizer oneglobal grad-norm rolling z-scoreskip-step-on-spike + dedup
(4) bf16 underflow/overflow(4a) log-Z drift → bf16 logit rounding; (4b) Adam second-moment underflow in fp16log-Z / z-loss valuez-loss; fp32 optimizer state
(5) Embedding/output-head norm growthUntied output embeddings and final logits grow unbounded, coupling into (1) and (4)output-logit RMS, embedding normz-loss + final-logit soft-cap
 Five mechanistically distinct families. The cardinal rule: diagnose the family before reaching for a mitigation — each fix closes exactly one.

Family (1), attention logit explosion, is the dominant trillion-scale failure and the single most-monitored stability scalar. The pre-softmax score grows because nothing in standard scaled-dot-product attention regularizes the product of the Wq and Wk singular values. Once max-logit exceeds ~O(10³), the softmax saturates, gradients vanish for that head, and bf16 rounding of the exponent triggers divergence. Wortsman et al. first showed this is reproducible in small models at high LR and is the same instability seen at scale (arXiv:2309.14322). The Kimi K2 team reports that under vanilla Muon, max attention logits "rapidly exceed 1000" at a 9B-activated proxy scale (arXiv:2507.20534). This is critical for our target because Muon — the near-mandatory optimizer at this scale — actively amplifies family (1): its Newton-Schulz orthogonalization equalizes update RMS across all singular directions, which is precisely the dynamic that drives the Wq·Wk product to grow. Choosing Muon for its large-batch data-efficiency persistence (arXiv:2505.02222) means you have signed up for QK-side logit control as a non-negotiable.

The one scalar to remember

The per-head max attention logit is your canary — the earliest-firing, highest-signal stability scalar, and on a Muon run the one most likely to move. Wire it to a hard alert at >100–500. Everything in §03 exists to keep it bounded.

Family (2), router collapse, is structurally unique to MoE and gets worse with expert count. At 256+ experts with expert parallelism (EP) sharded across thousands of GB200s, a slightly-favored expert receives more tokens, its gradients improve faster, the router favors it more, and the rest atrophy. Beyond the quality loss of dead experts, this produces catastrophic load imbalance: stragglers stall the all-to-all, and tokens overflow expert capacity and are dropped. A distinct sub-mode is gradient-norm spikes from the router logits themselves — the gate is a tiny matrix whose logits can blow up independently of the experts, so a grad-norm spike does not always implicate the experts. For a 200B-active design with the DeepSeek-V3-style ~256–384 fine-grained experts, this family is co-equal with (1) in priority.

Family (3), gradient-norm spikes, is most often the symptom the skip-step machinery keys on rather than a root cause. OLMo-2's most actionable finding traced many spikes to specific data batches containing long repeated n-gram sequences — a data pathology, not an optimizer pathology (arXiv:2501.00656). The engineering implication is two-layered: aggressive corpus-level deduplication offline, plus a runtime spike guard that rejects the occasional pathological batch that slips through.

Family (4), bf16 numerics, splits cleanly. (4a) is the softmax normalizer log-Z drifting far from zero so output logits round badly in bf16 — the original motivation for z-loss. (4b) is Adam second-moment / update underflow, which is largely solved by keeping optimizer state in fp32 (mu_dtype=float32). On GB200, the FP8 attention and GEMM paths re-introduce overflow risk and make max-logit control mandatory rather than optional; if you push to FP4 on the Blackwell tensor cores, the dynamic-range margin shrinks further.

Family (5) is the slow structural leak: untied output embeddings and final logits grow without bound and couple into both (1) and (4). Gemma-2's final-logit soft-cap (30.0) and z-loss both target this surface (arXiv:2408.00118).

02z-loss: the bf16-numerics and output-head anchor

z-loss adds λ·(log Z)² to the cross-entropy, where Z is the softmax partition function, penalizing drift of the log-partition from zero. In MaxText this is implemented directly inside the stabilized cross-entropy at src/maxtext/utils/max_utils.py (cross_entropy_with_logits), which computes log_z = logsumexp(logits) and adds z_loss · square(log_z), surfaced through the z_loss_multiplier config (default 0.0). The docstring states both purposes verbatim: keep the logits from drifting too far from zero so bf16 roundoff stays bounded, and encourage the logits to be normalized log-probabilities. The canonical coefficient is 10⁻⁴·log²Z — OLMo-2 uses it (arXiv:2501.00656), and the PaLM/Chinchilla-era runs used the same value. This is the fix for families (4a) and (5).

prec Ltotal = LCE + λ · (log Z)² , Z = Σv exp(logitv) , λ ≈ 10⁻⁴ Penalizes drift of the log-partition from zero so bf16 roundoff of output logits stays bounded. Applies to the output softmax only — orthogonal to attention-logit control.
jax — MaxText z-loss inside cross_entropy_with_logits (src/maxtext/utils/max_utils.py)
# logits: [..., vocab].  z_loss == z_loss_multiplier (config, default 0.0)
log_z      = jax.nn.logsumexp(logits, axis=-1)            # the softmax log-partition
log_softmax = logits - log_z[..., None]
xent       = -jnp.sum(targets * log_softmax, axis=-1)     # stabilized cross-entropy
total      = xent + z_loss * jnp.square(log_z)            # +1e-4 * (log Z)^2
The load-bearing caveat — z-loss is NOT your attention-stability story

z-loss is applied to the output softmax only; it does nothing for attention logit explosion (family 1). Engineers routinely set z_loss_multiplier=1e-4, watch the output-logit RMS stabilize, and assume they are protected against divergence — then a head's pre-softmax score blows past 1000 and the run dies anyway. z-loss and QK-side intervention are orthogonal and both required. Set z_loss_multiplier=1e-4 as a default and treat it as table stakes, not as your attention-stability story.

03The QK-side stack: QK-norm, soft-caps, and QK-Clip

Three techniques attack family (1) at different points in the forward/update path. They are largely alternatives, and the choice has real consequences at scale.

QK-norm
RMSNorm Q,K pre-dot
Cheapest structural option, free at inference, FA-compatible. Bounds activations, not the weight SV product — softer under Muon.
attention soft-cap
C·tanh(logits/C)
Gemma-2 legacy. Breaks the fused-softmax path; Gemma-3 removed it. Survives only as the final-logits cap.
QK-Clip (MuonClip)
post-hoc weight rescale
Trillion-scale state of the art. Per-head, conditional, becomes a no-op once logits settle. The K2 default with Muon+MLA.

QK-norm applies RMSNorm to Q and K per head before the dot product, bounding ‖q‖ and ‖k‖ so the logit cannot grow via activation magnitude. MaxText gates this with use_qk_norm in src/maxtext/layers/attentions.py. OLMo-3 uses a global variant (a single RMSNorm over the flattened heads·dim) where other implementations use per-head. The crucial OLMo-2 result is that QK-norm and reordered-norm yield no stability gain in isolation — only together do they tame gradient-norm growth and spikiness (arXiv:2501.00656). QK-norm is the cheapest structural option, costs nothing at inference, and is compatible with FlashAttention; it is the default choice for a dense-attention model and is what Gemma-3 switched to after dropping soft-caps. Its limitation: it bounds the activation magnitudes but not the weight-singular-value product directly, so under Muon's orthogonalized updates it is a softer guarantee than QK-Clip.

Attention soft-cap (Gemma-2) replaces the raw logits with C·tanh(logits/C), using C=50.0 for attention and C=30.0 for the final head (arXiv:2408.00118). MaxText implements this inside the attention kernels (splash_attention_kernel.py, jax_flash_attention.py) and exposes attn_logits_soft_cap / final_logits_soft_cap. The drawback is decisive for a 100k-GB200 build: tanh-capping is incompatible with the fused-softmax path in Flash/Splash attention in some configurations, and you cannot afford an unfused softmax at this throughput. Gemma-3 itself removed the attention soft-cap in favor of QK-norm. Treat soft-cap as legacy; the only place it survives in a modern recipe is the final-logits cap as a cheap belt-and-suspenders for family (5), and even there z-loss subsumes most of its value.

QK-Clip (MuonClip) is the trillion-scale state of the art and the right default if you run Muon with MLA. Rather than altering the forward pass, it post-hoc rescales the projection weights after each optimizer step. Per head h, define S_max^h = (1/√d)·max over batch,i,j of Qᵢ^h·Kⱼ^hᵀ; if S_max^h > τ, compute γ_h = min(1, τ/S_max^h) and apply Wq^h ← γ^α Wq^h, Wk^h ← γ^(1−α) Wk^h with α=0.5 so each projection absorbs √γ (arXiv:2507.20534v1). Only the heads exceeding τ are touched — "only a small subset of heads exhibit exploding logits" — so it is per-head and conditional, becoming a no-op once logits settle. For MLA, the decomposition matters: the non-RoPE components q^C and k^C are each scaled by √γ, the decoupled per-head RoPE query q^R is scaled by the full γ, and the shared RoPE key k^R is left untouched to avoid cross-head coupling (clipping a shared component would contaminate every head). Kimi K2 used τ=100.

attn Smaxh = (1/√d) · maxb,i,j Qih·Kjhᵀ ; γh = min(1, τ/Smaxh) ; Wqh ← √γ · Wqh, Wkh ← √γ · Wkh Per-head, conditional, post-optimizer. α=0.5 splits γ symmetrically across the two projections. A no-op once Smaxh ≤ τ.

MaxText implements exactly this in src/maxtext/utils/qk_clip_utils.py. The key routines:

  • _scale_from_max_logits(max_logits_batch, τ) computes s_max = max over batch then returns min(1.0, τ/(s_max + 1e-6)) — the γ factor, per head, with an epsilon guard.
  • _clip_mla_weight(layer_name, param, scale, qk_nope) splits the kernel into a head slice [..., :qk_nope] (the nope/non-RoPE part) and a tail. The nope slice is multiplied by √scale. For wq_b, the tail is the q^R RoPE slice and is multiplied by the full scale; for wkv_b, the tail is the V slice and is left untouched (tail_new = tail).
  • calculate_max_logit_metric extracts the global max logit across all layers from sown intermediates, supporting both the Linen sow shape (array,) and the NNX nnx.Intermediate(array) shape.
src/maxtext/utils/qk_clip_utils.pyMuonClip / QK-ClipMLA-aware rescaletrimmed
def _scale_from_max_logits(max_logits_batch, tau):            # [1]
    s_max = jnp.max(max_logits_batch, axis=0)                 # per-head max over batch
    return jnp.minimum(1.0, tau / (s_max + 1e-6))            # gamma, eps-guarded

def _clip_mla_weight(name, param, scale, qk_nope):           # [2]
    nope, tail = param[..., :qk_nope], param[..., qk_nope:]
    nope_new = nope * jnp.sqrt(scale)                         # non-RoPE: sqrt(gamma)
    if name == "wq_b":                                       # [3]
        tail_new = tail * scale                               # decoupled q^R: full gamma
    else:                                                    # wkv_b
        tail_new = tail                                       # shared k^R / V: UNTOUCHED
    return jnp.concatenate([nope_new, tail_new], axis=-1)

# applied post-optimizer, only to heads exceeding tau; no-op once logits settle

1γ, not a gradient. The scale is read from sown max-logits with a +1e-6 epsilon guard and clamped to ≤1 — a multiplicative weight rescale, never a loss term.

2nope gets √γ. The non-RoPE head slice [..., :qk_nope] on both Wq and Wk absorbs √scale, splitting γ symmetrically (α=0.5).

3RoPE asymmetry. The decoupled per-head q^R tail takes the full γ; the shared RoPE key k^R (and the V slice in wkv_b) is left untouched so clipping cannot contaminate every head.
