A config guard in types.py forbids use_qk_clip together with attn_logits_soft_cap, because QK-Clip must read the raw dot products to compute S_max — a soft-cap would clamp the very signal QK-Clip needs to measure. The max logits are sown from the attention op (attention_mla.py, record_max_logits=use_qk_clip), and _check_attention_type enforces that QK-Clip is only valid for attention_type='mla'.

Recommendation for the 10T/200B target

If your attention is MLA (it should be, for KV-cache economy at 200B active) and your optimizer is Muon (it should be), run QK-Clip with τ=100 as the primary family-(1) defense, and add per-head QK-norm only if you observe residual spikiness in the proxy sweeps. Do not stack soft-cap on top — the guard will reject it, and rightly so.

04Residual-stream stability: init scaling, sandwich norm, and DeepSeek-V4 mHC

Residual-stream growth across depth is the structural analog of logit growth, and at 100+ layers it is its own failure surface. The classical fixes are 1/√(2L) residual scaling (so the per-layer contribution shrinks with depth) and sandwich/post-norm placement, which bound the residual's growth rate. These remain the baseline and you should ship them: sandwich norm (RMSNorm before and after each sublayer's residual add) plus depth-scaled init is cheap insurance.

The 2026 advance is mHC — Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections (arXiv:2512.24880, Xie et al., Dec 31 2025), shipped in DeepSeek-V4-Pro (1.6T total / 49B active) and V4-Flash (284B / 13B active). Plain Hyper-Connections (ByteDance) widen the residual stream by an expansion rate n to give parallel mixing paths, but the learned mixing matrix breaks the identity-mapping property: the paper reports a loss surge at ~12k steps in a 27B model with the composite gain magnitude peaking near ~3000 — a residual explosion correlated with gradient-norm instability. mHC constrains the residual mixing matrix H^res ∈ ℝ^{n×n} to be doubly stochastic (rows and columns non-negative and summing to 1 — the Birkhoff polytope), enforced by 20 Sinkhorn-Knopp projection iterations. Double-stochasticity guarantees the spectral norm ‖H^res‖₂ ≤ 1, making each composite layer non-expansive and conserving the feature mean, so the residual neither explodes nor collapses with depth — recovering the identity-residual benefit while permitting rich cross-path mixing. The bound on composite gain drops from HC's ~3000 to mHC's ~1.6. At n=4 (a 4× wider residual stream) the overhead is only 6.7% of training time, with gating init α=0.01. MaxText ships an mHC-lite variant in src/maxtext/layers/mhc.py (sinkhorn(t, iters=20), get_permutation_matrices over the Birkhoff vertices, expand/reduce broadcast), following the mHC-lite note's explicit permutation-matrix parameterization rather than the full Sinkhorn-projected form.

~3000→1.6
composite gain bound, HC → mHC
20iters
Sinkhorn-Knopp projection per step
6.7%
training-time overhead at n=4

For the 10T/200B target this is a genuine decision point. mHC is recent (the primary paper is dated Dec 31 2025) and DeepSeek-V4 is the only public production validation. A community reimplementation exists, which lets you ablate the n=4 / 20-iter Sinkhorn / α=0.01 choices on a proxy before committing 100k GPUs.

The recommendation — validate, then adopt DeepSeek-V4 validated

Validate mHC on a 9B–27B proxy at the full target depth (depth is the weak transfer axis — see §07), and if it reproduces the residual-norm flattening, adopt n=4; the 6.7% overhead buys you out of an entire failure family. If you cannot validate in time, fall back to sandwich norm + 1/√(2L) init, which is well-trodden and safe.

05skip-step-on-spike: the runtime backstop and momentum-poisoning subtlety

Structural fixes prevent families (1), (4), (5), and the residual mode. They do not prevent a single pathological data batch (family 3) or a silently corrupted node (§08) from injecting one catastrophic update. The runtime backstop is skip-step-on-spike, and MaxText's implementation in src/maxtext/optimizers/optimizers.py (skip_step_on_spikes) is a faithful port of OLMo-core's skip_step_optimizer with the critical correctness property intact.

The mechanism: the wrapper keeps rolling buffers of the last interval losses and grad-norms, computes their mean and Bessel-corrected std (_compute_rolling_stats, using N−1 to align with torch.std), and on each step checks (loss − mean) ≤ scaling_factor·std AND the analogous condition for grad-norm. If either is violated, it zeros the update via lax.cond — do_update calls the inner optimizer, skip_update returns zero updates and the unchanged inner state. The default skip_step_scaling_factor is 6.0 and skip_step_interval is 128.

src/maxtext/optimizers/optimizers.pyskip_step_on_spikesport of OLMo-core skip_step_optimizertrimmed
mean_l, std_l = _compute_rolling_stats(losses)       # Bessel-corrected (N-1)  # [1]
mean_g, std_g = _compute_rolling_stats(grad_norms)
ok = ((loss - mean_l) <= scaling_factor * std_l) & \
     ((gnorm - mean_g) <= scaling_factor * std_g)    # default scaling=6.0

is_warmup = (count + 1) < min_history               # max(2, interval//2)      # [2]
updates, new_state = lax.cond(                       # [3]
    ok | is_warmup,
    lambda: inner.update(grads, state, params),      # do_update
    lambda: (zeros_like(grads), state))              # skip_update: state UNCHANGED

new_losses = losses.at[idx].set(loss)                # spike still appended      # [4]

1Bessel-corrected std. _compute_rolling_stats uses N−1 to align exactly with torch.std, so the 6σ band matches the OLMo-core reference.

2Warmup guard. A warmup of max(2, interval//2) steps prevents the guard firing before the buffer has enough history to give a meaningful std — avoiding spurious early skips.

3Zero, not clip. On a spike, skip_update returns zero updates and the unchanged inner state — the corrupt batch never enters Adam's or Muon's moment buffers.

4Spike becomes baseline. The skipped loss is still appended so a legitimately shifted regime (data-mix change, WSD decay onset) is not rejected forever.

The subtlety that makes this strictly superior to global gradient clipping: zeroing the update means the spike batch never enters Adam's (or Muon's momentum buffer's) moments. A clipped-but-nonzero update still injects a corrupt direction into the first-moment EMA, where it persists for ~1/(1−β₁) steps and biases every subsequent update; clipping limits the magnitude but not the poisoning. skip-step rejects the whole batch, so momentum is never contaminated. Two further details from the MaxText source matter operationally:

  • The spike value is still appended to the rolling buffer (new_losses = losses.at[idx].set(loss)) even when the step is skipped — "so spikes can become the new baseline." This prevents the guard from getting stuck permanently rejecting a legitimately shifted loss regime (e.g., after a data-mix change or a WSD decay phase begins).
  • A warmup of max(2, interval//2) steps (is_warmup = (count + 1) < min_history) prevents the guard from firing before the buffer has enough history to give a meaningful std, avoiding spurious early skips.
Recommendation — treat skip-step as containment, not cure

Enable skip_step_on_spikes with interval=128, scaling_factor=6.0. Monitor the fraction of skipped steps as a first-class health metric (§09) — a rising skip rate is your earliest warning that data quality or hardware is degrading. If it fires repeatedly, find and fix the root cause (a bad shard, a degrading node) rather than letting it silently absorb a steady stream of corruption.

06Router stability: aux-loss versus aux-loss-free bias

The MoE router is the other co-dominant failure surface, and MaxText supports both regimes.

Aux-loss balancing is the Switch/GShard approach. MaxText's load_balance_loss (src/maxtext/layers/moe.py) computes num_experts² · mean(density · density_prob) where density is the fraction of tokens dispatched per expert and density_prob the mean routing probability, weighted by load_balance_loss_weight (default 0). Its drawback, isolated cleanly by the Auxiliary-Loss-Free Load Balancing paper (arXiv:2408.15664), is interference gradients: the balance term is in the gradient path, so it trades model quality for load balance on every step.

Aux-loss-free bias balancing (DeepSeek-V3) is the recommended default at trillion scale. MaxText's calculate_load_balance_updates (src/maxtext/layers/moe.py) computes expert_counts = bincount(top_k_indices), average_load = total_tokens / num_experts, direction = sign(average_load − expert_counts), and returns direction · rate. This nudges a per-expert routing bias by ±routed_bias_update_rate; the bias steers the top-k selection but is not in the gradient path, so balance is decoupled from the loss surface entirely (arXiv:2412.19437). No interference gradients, no quality tax.

For 200B active across 100k GPUs there is a second-order choice that matters: global (cross-DP) balancing of the bias statistics versus purely sequence-local balancing. Per-sequence balance over-constrains the router and hurts expert specialization — you want experts to specialize, which means accepting per-sequence imbalance as long as the global batch is balanced. DeepSeek-V3 uses global bias balancing plus a negligible-weight complementary sequence-wise aux loss purely as a guardrail against any single sequence's pathological imbalance. Replicate that: aux-loss-free global bias as the primary mechanism, with load_balance_loss_weight set to a tiny value (DeepSeek-V3 territory) as a sequence-level safety net, not as the main balancer.

Aggregate the bias statistics across the DP mesh — don't re-introduce locality

Aggregate the bias-update statistics across the DP mesh before applying — a per-shard update would re-introduce the locality problem you are trying to avoid. The bias is a control signal applied post-optimizer, not a learned parameter; conflating the two re-couples balance to the loss surface.

07The Kimi K2 zero-instability mechanism, dissected

Kimi K2 (1.04T total / 32B active, 384 experts top-8) was pretrained on 15.5T tokens with zero loss spikes and zero training instability (arXiv:2507.20534). This is the single most important existence proof for the trillion-scale stability stack, and the claim is not luck — it is the composition of four interlocking pieces:

  1. MuonClip's QK-Clip preventively caps the one failure mode Muon amplifies. Muon's orthogonalized updates equalize update RMS across singular directions, which is exactly what drives the Wq·Wk product growth that produces attention logit explosion. QK-Clip (τ=100) clamps it before it can compound. The two are co-designed: you adopt Muon for its large-batch persistence, and QK-Clip is the tax you pay for it.
  2. Weight decay 0.1 plus consistent-RMS scaling (the 0.2·√max(A,B) factor that rescales every matrix's update to RMS≈0.2) bounds weight norms globally, so no single projection drifts.
  3. A constant-then-decay WSD schedule (constant 2e-4 for 10T tokens, then cosine to 2e-5 over the final 5.5T) avoids the late-training LR transients that classically trigger spikes — there is no cosine mid-run LR ramp to perturb a settled basin.
  4. 67M-token batches sit near the critical batch size where Muon's data-efficiency advantage persists.

The empirical signature is the tell that the system found a stable basin rather than being permanently clamped: logits pin at τ=100 early in training, then decay on their own to a normal operating range with no retuning of τ. A system that were merely being held down by a hard clamp would stay pinned; the self-decay means the weights relaxed into a configuration where the logits are naturally bounded and the clip stops binding (after ~30% of steps it becomes a no-op). For the 10T/200B target, this is the blueprint: Muon + QK-Clip(τ=100) + λ=0.1 + consistent-RMS + WSD, and watch for the pin-then-decay signature in your max-logit trace as confirmation the basin is stable.

One forward-looking note: DeepSeek-V4 reaches the same zero-spike end state by a different route — it discards QK-Clip and instead applies RMSNorm directly to attention queries and KV entries (structural QK-norm rather than post-hoc clip), pairs it with a two-stage Newton-Schulz (8 aggressive steps then 2 near-identity polishing steps, the latter to handle the steep M^−0.96 spectral degradation of final-layer projections), and rescales update RMS to 0.18 (arXiv:2606.04058). Both stacks work. QK-Clip is the more battle-tested public recipe (15.5T tokens, fully described, with a correct MaxText implementation); QK-norm is structurally simpler and FlashAttention-compatible.

Do not mix the two QK stacks blindly

If you value reproducing a known-good run, take K2's QK-Clip; if you value forward-pass simplicity and are willing to ablate, take V4's QK-norm. They are alternatives that attack family (1) at different points — pick one deliberately and validate it, do not stack them.

08Silent data corruption: the failure with no checkpoint

At 100k GB200s, silent data corruption (SDC) is a first-class stability risk and the deepest reason skip-step and gradient-replay exist. Meta's Llama-3 54-day run saw 466 interruptions (419 unexpected, 78% hardware), with ~6 attributed to SDC; Google reports SDC "every week or two" in Gemini training (arXiv:2502.12340v1). Over a multi-month run on 100k GPUs, permanent-fault probability approaches certainty, and the controlled 2026 SDC study using 15 known-bad production nodes is sobering:

  • SDC is silent: corrupted nodes can show loss curves "identical" to healthy ones, and do not necessarily produce NaNs — so aggregate statistics are unreliable as a detector.
  • ABFT checksums detected only 1 of 15 bad nodes. Checksum-based detection is nearly useless here.
  • Observed submodule corruption rates reach 2.89e-2 (forward attention) with mismatch severity up to 3.79e11×, and worst-case gradient noise-to-signal of 5.1%.
The failure with no checkpoint — treat "mysterious late spikes" as a hardware problem

The effect is not a clean crash; it is slow weight drift to a worse optimum, occasionally surfacing late as a loss spike — which is why "mysterious late spikes" should be treated as a hardware-detection problem, not only an optimizer one. The only reliable detection is recomputation: shadow DP replicas comparing gradients during all-reduce, or deterministic replay (Gemini's hot-standby scanners).

skip-step alone will NOT catch SDC — the "new baseline" behavior is a liability here

For the 10T/200B build, budget explicitly for cross-DP gradient comparison plus periodic golden-value stress tests, and treat skip-step-on-spike as the containment layer that prevents an SDC-corrupted batch from poisoning Adam/Muon momentum before the offending node is quarantined. The skip-step buffer's "spike becomes the new baseline" behavior is a liability here — a slowly drifting corruption can normalize into the rolling mean, so skip-step alone will not catch SDC; you need the recomputation layer on top.

09What to monitor on 100k GB200s

Log these primary scalars every step. Each maps to a failure family and has an alert threshold.

ScalarFamilyAlert / action
Per-head max attention logit(1)Alert >100–500; drives QK-Clip. Watch for the pin-then-decay signature
Global grad-norm + rolling z-score(3)Drives skip-step at mean + 6σ
Expert-load entropy / max-min load ratio per MoE layer(2)Router-collapse early warning; track dropped-token fraction
log-Z / z-loss value(4a)bf16 drift; should stay near 0
Residual-stream RMS by depthresidualmHC / residual-explosion check; should be flat across depth
Fraction of skipped steps(3)/SDCIf rising, data or hardware is degrading — investigate, do not absorb
 Six primary scalars, each mapped to a failure family. The max attention logit is the canary; the skip-step fraction is the systemic-degradation alarm.

Two operating principles. First, the max attention logit is your canary — it is the earliest-firing, highest-signal scalar, and on a Muon run it is the one most likely to move. Wire it to a hard alert. Second, a rising skip-step fraction is never benign. skip-step is designed to absorb the occasional bad batch silently; if it is firing more than transiently, you have a systematic problem — a corrupt shard, a degrading node, or a router that is starting to collapse — and the guard is masking it. Page on the skip rate, not just on outright divergence.

10Recommended stability stack for 10T / 200B / 100k GB200

Composing the chapter into a single configuration:

optimizer
Muon (2D) + AdamW
Muon for all 2D hidden matrices; AdamW for embeddings, head, RMSNorm gains. λ=0.1, RMS≈0.18–0.2, mu_dtype=float32.
family (1)
QK-Clip τ=100 (MLA)
or QK-norm on the V4 route. Never stack with attn_logits_soft_cap — MaxText forbids it.
family (4)/(5)
z_loss=1e-4
optional final_logits_soft_cap=30.0 as belt-and-suspenders.
family (2)
aux-loss-free global bias
calculate_load_balance_updates + negligible-weight sequence aux loss as guardrail.
  • Optimizer: Muon for all 2D hidden matrices (MoE experts, attention projections), AdamW for embeddings, unembed/head, and RMSNorm gains; λ=0.1 both; consistent-RMS≈0.18–0.2; mu_dtype=float32.
  • Family (1): QK-Clip with τ=100 on MLA (use_qk_clip, MLA-only), or QK-norm if you adopt the V4 route. Never stack with attn_logits_soft_cap — MaxText forbids it.
  • Family (4)/(5): z_loss_multiplier=1e-4; optional final_logits_soft_cap=30.0.
  • Residual: sandwich norm + 1/√(2L) init as baseline; mHC n=4 if validated on a depth-matched proxy.
  • Family (2): aux-loss-free global bias balancing (calculate_load_balance_updates) with a negligible-weight sequence-level aux loss as guardrail.
  • Runtime backstop: skip_step_on_spikes, interval=128, scaling_factor=6.0.
  • Schedule: WSD — long constant phase for scaling-law probing, sharp final cosine decay.
  • SDC: cross-DP gradient comparison + golden-value stress tests + hot-standby replay; quarantine on mismatch.
The unifying claim

Trillion-scale stability is a composition property, not a single trick — validated by Kimi K2's 15.5T-token zero-spike run: each mitigation closes exactly one failure family, and the run is stable only when all five are closed simultaneously. Diagnose by family, instrument the canaries, and never let a runtime guard quietly absorb a root cause.

Sources

  • Kimi K2 / MuonClip / QK-Clip: arxiv.org/abs/2507.20534, arxiv.org/html/2507.20534v1
  • OLMo-2 (z-loss, QK-norm, n-gram spikes): arxiv.org/abs/2501.00656
  • Wortsman et al., small-scale proxies (attention/output logit instability): arxiv.org/abs/2309.14322
  • Gemma-2 soft-caps: arxiv.org/abs/2408.00118
  • mHC (Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections, DeepSeek-V4): arxiv.org/abs/2512.24880
  • Aux-loss-free load balancing: arxiv.org/abs/2408.15664; DeepSeek-V3: arxiv.org/abs/2412.19437
  • Practical Efficiency of Muon (large-batch persistence): arxiv.org/abs/2505.02222
  • Spectral Scaling Laws of Muon (final-layer M⁻⁰·⁹⁶): arxiv.org/html/2606.04058
  • Silent Data Corruption in LLM training: arxiv.org/html/2502.12340v1
  • MaxText QK-Clip: maxtext/src/maxtext/utils/qk_clip_utils.py
  • MaxText z-loss (cross_entropy_with_logits): maxtext/src/maxtext/utils/max_utils.py
  • MaxText skip_step_on_spikes: maxtext/src/maxtext/optimizers/optimizers.py
  • OLMo-core skip_step_optimizer (reference impl): OLMo-core/skip_step_optimizer.py

Chapter 12 · Chinchilla is a foil, not a manual

Modern scaling laws: beyond Chinchilla, for MoE, for data quality, and for your own dataset

At a 10T-total / 200B-active scale on 100,000 GB200 GPUs, the loss surface is no longer L(N, D) — it is a six-dimensional object in total params, tokens, active params, granularity, sparsity, and precision, and every exponent in it is a function of your corpus. A 1% loss-prediction error here is tens of millions of dollars of mis-allocated compute.

~28 min read6 figuresgrounded in MaxText MoE configs + the published scaling literature

Chinchilla is not your operating manual. It is a foil. Hoffmann 2022 corrected Kaplan's allocation artifact — Kaplan's untuned warmup, mismatched LR decay, and loss read at non-converged points had inflated parameter scaling to the famous ~0.73/0.27 split — and settled the dense world on roughly equal exponents, a≈b≈0.5, ~20 tokens/parameter (Hoffmann 2022 via DeepSeek-LLM). For a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200 GPUs, that constant is worse than useless: it will mis-size your model by a factor of several and mis-allocate tens of millions of dollars of compute. Three structural facts break it.

First, the loss surface is no longer L(N, D). It is at minimum L(N, D, Nactive, G, S, P) — a six-dimensional object in total params, tokens, active params, expert granularity, sparsity, and numerical precision — and the cross-terms are not separable. Second, the objective is not compute-optimal loss at fixed training FLOPs. With a billion-plus inference requests amortized against one training run, the correct objective is lifetime cost, which pushes you far into the over-trained regime, to 50–100+ tokens per active parameter, well past the Chinchilla ratio. Third — and this is the fact that makes the other two operational rather than academic — every exponent in the law is a function of your data quality. DeepSeek demonstrated this directly: refitting one functional form on "early in-house," "current in-house," and OpenWebText2 moved the optimal-allocation exponents from a=0.450/b=0.550 to a=0.524/b=0.476 to a=0.578/b=0.422 (DeepSeek-LLM). Better data shifts budget toward parameters. There is no transferable Chinchilla constant; there is only the law you fit on your own corpus.

Published exponents tell you the form; only your own sweep tells you the coefficients — and at 10²⁶ FLOPs the coefficients are where the money is.
6axes
L(N, D, N_a, G, S, P) — not L(N,D)
~7G
optimal granularity, not the 2024 law's 64
10ktok/p
no loss flat-lining (50× Chinchilla)
~192×
MiniCPM measured data:model, vs ~20× Chinchilla

This chapter assembles the modern multi-axis law piece by piece — granularity, sparsity, the comprehensive coupled form, data-constrained repetition, inference-aware over-training, precision, hyperparameters, and distillation — gives the fitted formulas you would actually regress, and closes with the concrete shape of a scaling-law campaign for the 10T/200B target. The recurring instruction is the same throughout: published exponents tell you the form; only your own sweep tells you the coefficients, and at 10²⁶ FLOPs the coefficients are where the money is.

01The MoE multiplicative axes: granularity and sparsity

Granularity and sparsity are not knobs that perturb a dense law. They are independent multiplicative axes, and the literature converges on one through-line: the optimal value of each rises monotonically with training compute. A bigger budget wants finer experts and more total params per active param.

Granularity

Define G = dff / dexpert: the ratio of the dense FFN width to a single expert's width. G=1 is a "standard" MoE where each expert is a full dense FFN; G=8 slices that FFN into eight narrower experts and routes to more of them to keep active params fixed. The Krajewski/DeepSeek line (Krajewski 2024) fits the form below.

moe L(N, D, G) = c + (g / Gγ + a) · N−α + b · D−β For E=64 experts: a=18.1, α=0.115, b=30.8, β=0.147, g=2.1, γ=0.58, c=0.47. Granularity enters only the parameter term as the multiplicative discount g/Gγ.

Because γ=0.58 < 1, returns to granularity are sublinear but the fit is unbounded — there is no internal optimum, so compute-optimal G simply climbs with budget: G=8 at 64×100M, G=16 at 1–3B active, G=32 at 7B, G=64 by 300B/2.96T. The headline that paper sells is dramatic and partly real: at 10²⁵ FLOPs an optimized fine-grained MoE needs ~40× fewer FLOPs than a dense model for equal loss, and G=1 (expert = full FFN) is suboptimal at nearly every budget.

Do not build to G=64

The G=64 recommendation does not survive contact with a more complete law. The 2024 fit omits the costs that finer experts actually incur — the activated-parameter term, the shared-expert contribution, and the routing plus all-to-all communication that fine granularity multiplies. Add those and the optimum collapses, as §02 shows.

Sparsity as its own axis

Frantar's pre-MoE-hype result (Frantar 2023) is the under-cited origin of the key fact, and it is architecture-general (fit on T5/C4 and ViT/JFT, not just MoE):

data L(S, N, D) = (aS · (1−S)bS + cS) · N−bN + (aD / D)bD + c aS=16.8, bS=0.722, cS=45.0, bN=0.245, bD=0.203, c=0.651. S is the sparsity fraction.

The load-bearing conclusion: optimal sparsity increases with training data — roughly 50% sparsity is optimal at 2–3× Chinchilla training length, and it keeps climbing past that. Apple's "Parameters vs FLOPs" (Yun 2025) restates this for MoE with S=(E−K)/E:

moe L = a · N−α + b · D−β + c · (1−S)−λ + d · (1−S)−δ · N−γ + e At fixed training compute, raising S while proportionally growing total params always lowers loss.

This pushes the result to its aggressive limit: optimal sparsity approaches ~0.9 and trends toward 1.0 for large models; as S rises, optimal total N rises and optimal active N falls. The practical implication for the build is direct. DeepSeek-V3 sits at 37B/671B ≈ 0.945 sparsity. A 200B-active / 10T-total model is S ≈ 0.98 — meaningfully sparser than V3, and exactly consistent with "sparser wins as you scale." If anything the law argues you are under-sparse: at this compute scale, the optimum may want even more total params behind your 200B active.

The two axes are coupled through compute. More budget → finer G and higher S and more tokens, simultaneously. Treating them independently (sweeping G at fixed S, or vice versa) will give you a saddle, not the optimum. You need the coupled form.

One more subtlety the separable laws hide: the granularity and sparsity optima interact through the routing cost. Finer experts (higher G) and higher sparsity both increase the number of experts E, which increases all-to-all token-routing traffic and load-balancing variance. The Frantar and Apple laws price the statistical benefit of sparsity but not the systems cost of the routing it implies; the comprehensive law's collapse of G_opt from 64 to ~7 is largely the systems cost reasserting itself. This is why the right place to fit is the coupled form with real wall-clock-equivalent FLOPs, not an idealized FLOP count that pretends routing is free.

02The comprehensive coupled law — fit against this one

The single most useful template for the 10T target is the first law to couple all five MoE axes in one fitted form (Cao 2025):

data L(N, D, Na, G, S) = (eG + f/G + mS² + nS) · (1/Nα + k/Naα + h·Na/N) + a/Nα + b/Dβ + c/Naα + ε e=0.1577, f=7.2446, m=5.1395, n=−3.2363, k=0.0013, h=0.0450, a=38.05, α=0.2383, b=27129, β=0.4694, c=31.10, ε=1.8182. N total, Na active, D tokens, G granularity, S shared-expert ratio.

Two facts from this fit should reset your priors.

MoE exponents are not dense exponents with a substitution

This law gives β ≈ 0.47 and α ≈ 0.24, versus dense Chinchilla's roughly 0.67/0.35. The data exponent is markedly steeper and the parameter exponent shallower. Concretely: MoE rewards tokens more and raw parameters less than a dense intuition predicts — another vector pointing at over-training (§03) and at not over-spending on total param count beyond what sparsity buys.

Optimal granularity is ~7, not ~64. Minimizing this coupled law yields Gopt ≈ 6.78 (build to G ∈ [7,9], allow up to 12) and a shared-expert ratio around 0.31. That is not a lone theoretical result — it is the independently-discovered production frontier. DeepSeek-V3.1 uses G=9, Kimi-K2 uses G=9, Qwen3-235B-A22B uses G=8. Three frontier labs converged on G=8–9 without coordinating, because once you charge for activated params, shared experts, and all-to-all routing, the G=64 of the 2024 law is a mirage. The shared experts matter: at ~0.31 shared ratio you keep one or two always-on experts that capture the common-token computation, letting the routed experts specialize and reducing routing-induced variance.

Build recommendation: G ∈ [8,12], 1–2 shared experts

Do not build G=32–64. Charge for activated params, shared experts, and all-to-all routing, and the coupled optimum sits at G ≈ 7 with a shared-expert ratio near 0.31 — i.e. one or two always-on experts.

A complementary lens is "efficiency leverage" (Ling team 2025), which reframes the whole question as EL = Cdense / Cmoe, the dense-to-MoE compute ratio for equal loss:

moe EL(A, G, C) = Â(α + γ·(log G)² + β·log G), α = a + d·log C a=1.23, d=−0.0761, γ=0.0167, β=−0.117, where A is the activation ratio. The d·log C dependence means MoE's efficiency advantage amplifies with compute.

The crucial term is α = a + d·log C: the d·log C dependence means MoE's efficiency advantage amplifies with compute. The bigger your budget, the more the sparse architecture pays off relative to dense — which is the entire economic thesis of a 100k-GB200 run. Their validated pilot, Ling-mini-beta (0.85B active / 17.5B total, 3.4% activation, G=12, 1 shared expert), hit >7× efficiency versus a 6.1B dense baseline at equal loss on 1T tokens, with optimal G ≈ 12 and the finding that lower activation is always better, down to 0.8%. For the 200B-active build at ~2% activation, this is corroborating evidence that you are in the right sparsity neighborhood and could defensibly go sparser.

AxisWhat it isDirection with compute10T/200B target
Granularity Gd_ff / d_expertrises, but capped ~7–12 by routing/comms costG = 8–12
Sparsity S=(E−K)/Einactive expert fractionrises monotonically, →~0.9–0.98S ≈ 0.98 (defensibly higher)
Shared expertsalways-on experts~0.31 of expert budget1–2 shared
Total vs active N/N_adilution factortotal rises, active falls at fixed compute~50× (10T/200B)
Activation ratioN_a / Nlower is better, down to ~0.8%~2%, could trend lower
 The five MoE axes and their compute-direction, with the 10T/200B build targets.

03Inference-aware over-training: train smaller and longer

Compute-optimality minimizes loss at fixed training FLOPs. That is the wrong objective for a model you will serve a billion times. Sardana & Frankle (2024) replace it with lifetime cost:

data minimize 6·N·Dtr + 2·N·Dinf subject to L(N, Dtr) = ℓ Training FLOPs (6N per token) plus lifetime inference FLOPs (2N per token), minimized jointly under a target loss ℓ.

And a real-cost version that weights each term by hardware utilization:

compute 6·N·Dtr·Ctr/MFUtr + 2·N·Cinf·(Dinp/MFUinp + Dout/MFUout) Each term scaled by per-phase cost and utilization — prefill and decode have different MFU, so they enter separately.

The conclusion is sharp and one-directional: with large inference volume you train a smaller model on more tokens. Across 47 models from 150M to 6B they pushed to 10,000 tokens per parameter with no loss flat-lining — fifty times the Chinchilla ratio, and the curve still had not bent. The penalty for ignoring this is large and counterintuitive: their 70B example "costs 36% more than cost-optimal" despite using only 1.3% more training FLOPs, because compute-optimality picked too large a model that is then expensive to serve forever.

For the 10T/200B build this settles the token budget. A 200B-active model is in the cost-optimal zone at 50–100+ tokens per active parameter — i.e. tens of trillions of tokens, not the ~4T that naive Chinchilla (≈20 tok/active-param) would prescribe. This is the reason your scaling-law campaign must not optimize min-loss-at-fixed-FLOPs. You are fitting L(N, D) to then plug into a lifetime-cost objective, and the optimum lives far to the right of the IsoFLOP frontier's tangent point. Practically: pin total/active params from the MoE sparsity argument, then ride D out to wherever the marginal training FLOP stops beating the marginal inference saving — which, at frontier inference volumes, is very far indeed.

The MoE accounting subtlety

The 2·N·Dinf inference term should use active params for the dense-equivalent forward FLOPs (you only pay for routed experts per token), while the 6·N·Dtr training term also runs on active params per token but must carry the total-param memory and all-to-all communication footprint that determines your achievable MFU. The decoupling of total from active is precisely what makes MoE attractive here: you get the representational capacity of 10T params at the inference FLOP cost of 200B, so the inference term is ~50× smaller than a dense model of equal capacity. This is the quantitative core of why a 100k-GB200 owner builds sparse and over-trains hard — the sparsity multiplies the inference-amortization argument, not just the training one.

There is a tension here you must hold in mind, surfaced in §06: over-training is exactly what makes a checkpoint fragile under cheap post-training quantization. You cannot independently maximize over-training and minimize inference precision.

04Data-constrained scaling: the binding constraint at 10T

Here is the collision. The sparsity argument wants more total params. The inference-aware argument wants tens of T of tokens. At 200B active, Chinchilla-optimal is ~4T unique tokens; the over-trained, inference-aware target wants far more. But only ~15–20T high-quality unique tokens realistically exist. Data, not compute, is the binding constraint of this build, and Muennighoff's data-constrained law (2023) becomes the operative object.

Repeated tokens are worth less than fresh ones, and the law quantifies the decay. Effective data from R_D repetitions of U_D unique tokens:

data D′ = UD + UD·RD* · (1 − e−RD/RD*), RD* ≈ 15.4 Effective tokens from RD repetitions saturates: the (1 − e−·) term caps how much a repeated token can ever be worth.

And an analogous diminishing return on "excess" parameters:

data N′ = UN + UN·RN* · (1 − e−RN/RN*), RN* ≈ 5.3 The same saturating form for "excess" parameters. Because RN* < RD*, params saturate faster than data.

The operating rules fall straight out of the constants:

  • ≤4 epochs of repetition are nearly free — repeated-token loss sits within noise of unique-token loss.
  • Returns decay sharply past R_D* ≈ 15 and are effectively nil by ~40 epochs.
  • Because R_N* < R_D* (5.3 < 15.4), in the data-constrained regime you should scale epochs faster than parameters — the inverse of the Chinchilla allocation. Repeating data buys more than adding params once you are data-bound.

For the build: plan ~5T unique high-quality tokens at ≤4 epochs ≈ ~20T effective tokens before repetition decay bites hard. Beyond that, marginal compute is better spent on data acquisition, cleaning, and synthetic generation than on naively growing parameters — data sourcing is a compute-equivalent lever and should be budgeted as one. A useful nuance: larger datasets tolerate more repetition before decay (2025 follow-up), so the 4-epoch rule is a floor for a clean corpus, not a hard ceiling for a large one. The campaign should fit R_D* and R_N* on your corpus rather than inheriting 15.4/5.3 — these too are quality-dependent.

Treat data sourcing as a compute-equivalent lever

Once you are data-bound, every clean trillion tokens you source is worth more than the parameters that trillion of FLOPs would otherwise buy. Budget data acquisition, cleaning, and synthetic generation as a first-class line item in the compute plan, not an afterthought.

05Precision scaling and the FP4/GB200 angle

Precision is now an axis of the loss law, and on GB200 it is the axis with the most money attached. Kumar et al. (2024), across 465+ runs, model an effective parameter count that degrades as precision drops:

prec Neff(P) = N · (1 − e−P/γ)³ · L = A·Neff−α + B·D−β + E + δPTQ When weight / activation / KV precisions match. Effective params degrade with bit-width P; δPTQ is the post-training-quantization penalty.

Two results bind the precision plan:

  1. Compute-optimal training precision is ~7–8 bits when N, D, and P are jointly optimized. FP8 is near-optimal; BF16 is over-precise at scale — you are paying for bits that buy no loss. This is the theoretical license for low-precision pretraining.
  2. PTQ degradation δ_PTQ grows with D/N. The more you over-train — which §03 mandates — the worse the model tolerates cheap post-training quantization at inference. Over-training and aggressive low-precision inference are in direct tension; you cannot have both for free.

For GB200 specifically, the relevant evidence is NVIDIA's NVFP4 pretraining recipe (2025) — pretraining, not just inference quant. NVFP4 uses E2M1 elements with FP8 (E4M3) scales over 16-element micro-blocks and an FP32 tensor-global scale. Stable FP4 pretraining required a specific stack of tricks:

Random Hadamard
16×16 on Wgrad
Spreads outliers across the block before quantization.
Stochastic rounding
gradients only
Deterministic forward, stochastic backward — keeps gradient estimates unbiased.
2D 16×16 scaling
weights
Consistency between forward and backward passes.
~16% in BF16
first 2 + last 8 blocks
The layers carrying disproportionate sensitivity.
BF16 last ~18% tokens
decay phase
Without it the gap widens from <1% to ~1.5% during WSD decay.

That last trick is the subtle one: BF16 for the last ~18% of training tokens — without this the loss gap widens from <1% to ~1.5% during the decay phase; the FP4 numerics that are fine in the stable phase hurt precisely where the WSD decay is recovering loss. The result: a 12B model on 10T tokens hit MMLU-pro 62.58% versus 62.62% for FP8 — a tie. Nemotron 3 (2025) pushed NVFP4 to 25T tokens on a hybrid Mamba-MoE, evidence the recipe scales to the token horizon this build needs.

nvfp4_quant.pyNVFP4 pretraining stackillustrative
def random_hadamard(x, H):                    # [1] 16x16 Hadamard on Wgrad inputs
    return x.reshape(*x.shape[:-1], -1, 16) @ H

def quantize_nvfp4(x, *, stochastic=False):   # [2]
    # E2M1 elements, FP8(E4M3) scale per 16-elem micro-block, FP32 tensor scale
    blocks = x.reshape(*x.shape[:-1], -1, 16)
    blk_scale = jnp.max(jnp.abs(blocks), axis=-1, keepdims=True) / 6.0   # E2M1 max=6
    q = blocks / blk_scale
    if stochastic:                            # [3] gradients only -> unbiased
        q = jnp.floor(q + jax.random.uniform(jax.random.PRNGKey(0), q.shape))
    else:
        q = jnp.round(q)                      # deterministic on the forward pass
    return (q * blk_scale).reshape(x.shape)

# ~16% of linears stay BF16 (first 2 + last 8 blocks); decay tokens run BF16.
keep_bf16 = lambda i, n: (i < 2) or (i >= n - 8)    # [4]

1Hadamard before quantize. A 16×16 random Hadamard on the Wgrad inputs spreads outliers across the micro-block so no single element saturates the E2M1 range.

2Two-level scale. E2M1 elements share an FP8 (E4M3) scale over a 16-element micro-block, under an FP32 tensor-global scale — the NVFP4 format GB200 supports natively.

3Stochastic backward only. Deterministic rounding on the forward pass, stochastic on gradients — keeps the gradient estimate unbiased without adding forward-pass noise.

4~16% stays BF16. The first 2 and last 8 blocks carry disproportionate sensitivity; pair this with BF16 for the final ~18% of tokens or the decay-phase gap widens to ~1.5%.

The FP4 caveat you must not gloss over stated in the recipe

FP4 bulk GEMMs are defensible on GB200 — that is the whole reason to buy Blackwell. But budget BF16 for the head and tail layers (~16%) and for the final ~15–20% of tokens, and treat the precision schedule as part of the WSD decay plan. And crucially: because δPTQ grows with D/N and you are over-training hard, do not plan aggressive INT4 PTQ on the over-trained checkpoint — the very over-training that makes the model cheap to serve also makes it the worst candidate for the cheapest inference quantization. The capacity to be quantized at inference is something you spend by over-training.

Reference every FLOP against the dense peak

NVIDIA's headline 720 PFLOP/s FP8 / 360 PFLOP/s BF16 per GB200 NVL72 rack are with-sparsity (2:4) figures — the page footnotes "Dense is one-half sparse spec shown." Dense peak is 360 PFLOP/s FP8 and 180 PFLOP/s BF16 per rack (~4.5 / ~2.25 PFLOP/s per B200 GPU), so a 100,000-GPU cluster is ~0.45 EFLOP/s dense FP8 peak, not ~1.0 EFLOP/s (the EFLOP figure only holds counting sparsity, which is not meaningful for dense training FLOPs). Realistic large-MoE FP8 MFU is ~35–40% for 2025–2026, lower for heavy-routing configs (NVIDIA GB200 NVL72).

06Hyperparameter scaling laws: LR, batch size, weight decay

You cannot tune learning rate and batch size by hand at 10T tokens — a single bad LR wastes a run worth more than a small company. The HP scaling laws let you fit η(N,D) and B(D) from cheap small-scale proxies and extrapolate. Three converging fits matter.

DeepSeek-LLM (2024) fits against compute C directly:

optim ηopt = 0.3118·C−0.125, Bopt = 0.2920·C0.3271 Uses non-embedding FLOPs/token M = 72·nlayer·dmodel² + 12·nlayer·dmodel·lseq as the scale variable, because 6N bookkeeping distorts at large model size.

Importantly at 200B scale, DeepSeek-LLM uses non-embedding FLOPs/token M as the scale variable instead of N, because the 6N bookkeeping distorts at large model size where embeddings and attention shift the real per-token compute.

StepLaw / Predictable Scale (2025) is the empirical backbone — 3,700 LLMs, ~1M H800-hours, 100T tokens of sweeping:

optim η(N, D) = 1.79·N−0.713·D0.307, B(D) = 0.58·D0.571 Convex plateau (off-optimal costs little, coarse grids suffice); prediction error <0.5% across sparsity levels, so it transfers to MoE.

Two properties make this directly usable. The optimum is a convex plateau, not a sharp peak — being somewhat off-optimal costs little, so coarse grids suffice. And the prediction error is <0.5% across sparsity levels, meaning the law transfers to MoE. This is the law to extrapolate your LR and batch from.

MoE-specific HP fit (Ling team 2025):

optim ηopt = 1.1576·C−0.1529, Bopt = 0.0694·C0.3644 MoE correction vs dense at equal compute: larger batch, slightly lower LR.

The MoE correction relative to dense at equal compute is larger batch, slightly lower LR. The mechanism: each expert is updated only on the routed subset of a batch, so the per-expert effective batch is smaller than the global batch — you compensate by enlarging the global batch and softening the LR to keep per-expert update noise in the same regime. At 200B active with top-k routing over G=8–12, this correction is not negligible; do not inherit a dense LR/batch schedule.

Do not inherit a dense LR/batch schedule

Each expert sees only the routed subset of a batch, so its per-expert effective batch is smaller than the global batch. Compensate with a larger global batch and a softer LR. At 200B active over G=8–12 top-k routing this correction is material — inheriting a dense schedule under-batches every expert.

Weight decay and its batch coupling are the usually-ignored axis. "Power Lines" (2025) extends HP scaling to weight decay and its coupling with batch size, and at 10T tokens the effective-LR / WD interaction materially shapes the late-training loss that the WSD decay phase is trying to recover. The Power Scheduler line (2024) gives batch- and token-agnostic LR schedules that survive the extrapolation.

WSD makes all of this affordable at 10T scale. MiniCPM's Warmup-Stable-Decay scheduler (2024) decouples the stable phase's LR from a pre-committed token count: you train at constant LR through the stable phase, then decay to recover most of the loss, and crucially you can fork annealing from a single stable checkpoint to run cheap proxy sweeps without retraining the stable trunk. This is what let MiniCPM measure its own data-to-model optimum at ~192× (versus Chinchilla's ~20×) rather than assume it — a tenfold disagreement with Chinchilla, discovered only because WSD made the measurement cheap. For the 10T campaign, WSD forks are how you run the dozens of IsoFLOP configs in §08 without paying full freight for each.

07Distillation scaling laws — for the serving fleet, not the flagship

Distillation has its own law, and it tells you not to distill the flagship. Busbridge et al. (Apple 2025) find that student loss is governed by the teacher's cross-entropy L_T, not the teacher's size, through a broken power law that produces a capacity gap: past an optimum, a stronger teacher actively hurts the student, because the teacher's KL target grows faster than the student can fit it. The optimal teacher is only "slightly larger than the student," then the benefit plateaus.

Supervised learning always wins given enough student compute

Distillation pays only when the teacher already exists or is amortized across many students (where it fits at ~1% error). For the 200B flagship this means distillation is a tool for the downstream serving fleet — the smaller models you deploy in cost-sensitive contexts — sized with a ~1×-student teacher, not a scheme for compressing the full 200B into a tiny student. Do not budget the flagship's quality on a distillation shortcut; budget it on supervised pretraining tokens, and reserve distillation for spinning out the small-serving variants once the flagship exists and its inference cost is already sunk.

08Running the scaling-law campaign for the 10T/200B target

Everything above is prologue to one decision: how do you actually fit the law that sizes a 10²⁶-FLOP run? Labs keep these laws private not as theater but because a 1% loss-prediction error at this scale is tens of millions of dollars of mis-allocated compute, and the exponents are corpus-specific. Here is the campaign.

(1) Fit your own law on your own data. This is non-negotiable — DeepSeek's quality-exponent result means published a/b/α/β are wrong for your corpus. Run an IsoFLOP/IsoLoss sweep at 1e18–3e20 FLOPs over ~30–50 MoE configs, varying active params N_a, granularity G ∈ {4…16}, sparsity S, and shared-expert count. Fit the coupled form L(N, D, N_a, G, S) from §02 (Cao 2025), or your own variant, with Huber loss in log-space (Huber for robustness to the noisy small-scale runs, log-space because the law is multiplicative). Use IsoFLOP profiles — train each config to a fixed FLOP budget, read the loss minimum across the param/token tradeoff — and stack several FLOP levels to pin the exponents.

python — fit the coupled MoE law (Huber in log-space, the §08 recipe)
import jax, jax.numpy as jnp, optax

# runs: arrays over the IsoFLOP sweep (N total, N_a active, D tokens, G, S, observed loss)
def predicted_loss(theta, N, Na, D, G, S):
    e, f, m, n, k, h, a, alpha, b, beta, c, eps = theta
    coup = (e*G + f/G + m*S**2 + n*S) * (1/N**alpha + k/Na**alpha + h*Na/N)
    return coup + a/N**alpha + b/D**beta + c/Na**alpha + eps

def objective(theta):                                   # Huber in log-space
    pred = predicted_loss(theta, N, Na, D, G, S)
    resid = jnp.log(pred) - jnp.log(loss_obs)           # multiplicative law -> log
    return optax.huber_loss(resid, delta=1e-3).mean()   # robust to noisy small runs

theta = jnp.array([0.1577, 7.2446, 5.1395, -3.2363, 0.0013, 0.0450,
                   38.05, 0.2383, 27129.0, 0.4694, 31.10, 1.8182])  # Cao'25 init
opt = optax.adam(1e-2); state = opt.init(theta)
for _ in range(20000):
    g = jax.grad(objective)(theta)
    upd, state = opt.update(g, state); theta = optax.apply_updates(theta, upd)

(2) Tune HPs with StepLaw/WSD proxies. Extrapolate η(N,D) and B(D) from small-scale fits (StepLaw), exploiting the convex plateau so a coarse grid suffices. Apply the MoE correction (larger batch, lower LR). Use WSD annealing forks from a shared stable checkpoint to make the per-config cost cheap.

(3) Apply the data-constrained correction. Set the unique-token budget (~5T high-quality), cap epochs at ≤4 (Muennighoff), fit your own R_D*/R_N*, and treat data acquisition as a compute-equivalent lever in the budget — every clean trillion tokens you source is worth more than the parameters that trillion of FLOPs would otherwise buy.

(4) Re-optimize allocation under the inference-aware objective. Do not stop at compute-optimal. Plug the fitted L(N, D) into the lifetime-cost objective 6N·D_tr + 2N·D_inf (Sardana & Frankle) with your real inference volume, and read off fewer active params / more tokens than naive compute-optimal — the optimum sits far to the right of the IsoFLOP tangent.

(5) Choose precision per the precision law. FP8/NVFP4 for bulk GEMMs, BF16 for head/tail layers and the final ~15–20% decay (NVFP4), and avoid INT4 PTQ on the over-trained checkpoint because δ_PTQ grows with your D/N (Kumar).

The deliverable is a surface, not a number

The output of this campaign is not a single number. It is a fitted five-dimensional surface plus a precision schedule plus an HP extrapolation, all conditioned on your corpus. Build to G=8–12, 1–2 shared experts, S≈0.98, tens of T tokens at ≤4 epochs of a ~5T-token clean core, FP4 bulk with BF16 head/tail/decay, and LR/batch from a StepLaw fit with the MoE correction. Anyone handing you a Chinchilla constant for this run is handing you a foil.

Sources

  • Hoffmann / Chinchilla (compute-optimal dense law, ~20 tok/param): arxiv.org/abs/2203.15556
  • Krajewski/DeepSeek granularity law (G-discount, ~40× FLOP win): arxiv.org/abs/2402.07871
  • Comprehensive coupled MoE scaling law (G_opt≈7, β≈0.47): arxiv.org/abs/2509.23678
  • Efficiency Leverage, Ling team (EL amplifies with compute, MoE HP fit): arxiv.org/abs/2507.17702
  • Apple optimal-sparsity law (S→0.9–1.0): arxiv.org/abs/2501.12370
  • Frantar sparsity law (architecture-general, optimal S rises with D): arxiv.org/abs/2309.08520
  • Data-constrained scaling, Muennighoff (R_D*≈15.4, R_N*≈5.3, ≤4 epochs free): arxiv.org/abs/2305.16264
  • Data-constrained follow-up (larger datasets tolerate more repetition): arxiv.org/abs/2511.13421
  • Inference-aware scaling, Sardana & Frankle (lifetime cost, 10k tok/param): arxiv.org/abs/2401.00448
  • Precision scaling law, Kumar et al. (N_eff(P), δ_PTQ grows with D/N): arxiv.org/abs/2411.04330
  • NVFP4 pretraining, NVIDIA (Hadamard, stochastic rounding, BF16 tail): arxiv.org/abs/2509.25149
  • Nemotron 3 NVFP4 at 25T tokens (hybrid Mamba-MoE): arxiv.org/abs/2512.20856
  • StepLaw / Predictable Scale (η(N,D), B(D), <0.5% error, MoE transfer): arxiv.org/abs/2503.04715
  • DeepSeek-LLM (HP-vs-compute fit, data-quality exponents, M scale var): arxiv.org/abs/2401.02954
  • Distillation scaling law, Apple (capacity gap, teacher CE not size): arxiv.org/abs/2502.08606
  • MiniCPM / WSD scheduler (forkable anneal, measured ~192× data:model): arxiv.org/abs/2404.06395
  • Power Lines: weight decay & batch-size coupling: arxiv.org/abs/2505.13738
  • Power Scheduler (batch/token-agnostic LR schedule): arxiv.org/abs/2408.13359
  • NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 (dense vs sparse peak FLOPs, MFU band): nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/gb200-nvl72

Chapter 13 · The corpus is the variable the FLOP budget can't buy

Data curation in 2026: from Common Crawl to taxonomied corpora, custom crawlers, and active selection

For a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE on 100k GB200, data is not a preprocessing step you finish before the run — it is the binding constraint and the single highest-leverage variable in the whole campaign. The architecture and the systems plan are largely solved by mid-2026; what separates a frontier model from a mediocre one on the same FLOP budget is the corpus.

~30 min read5 figuresgrounded in the open-corpus lineage + Essential-Web taxonomy

The architecture and the systems plan are, by mid-2026, largely solved problems (G=8–12, S≈0.98, FP4 bulk GEMMs, WSD schedule); what separates a frontier model from a mediocre one trained on the same FLOP budget is the corpus. This chapter is the data-engineering counterpart to the scaling-law campaign in §7 and §8: it argues that the field has undergone a structural phase change — from monolithic filtering, where one classifier makes one keep/discard decision per document and 90% of the web is thrown away forever, to queryable taxonomied corpora plus model-aware active selection, where annotation is decoupled from selection and the discarded tail is recycled rather than deleted. We walk the open-corpus lineage (FineWeb, DCLM, Nemotron-CC, RefinedWeb, RedPajama-v2, TxT360), the Essential-Web taxonomy template, the classifier/dedup/decontam stack, data mixing (DoReMi → RegMix → CLIMB → online bandits), synthetic data (WRAP → BeyondWeb → REWIRE → Nemotron-CC-Math), and the genuine frontier — datamodels, influence functions, learnability, and per-token selection — before specifying a concrete 10T-token stack.

The "discard 90%" orthodoxy was a small-model artifact. For a 10T/200B MoE on 100k GB200, where unique tokens are the binding constraint and acquisition / synthesis / recycling are the only ways past the data wall, the corpus is engineered, not filtered.
24T
Essential-Web labeled tokens (23.6B docs)
~15–20T
unique HQ tokens on the whole open web
2.1×
BETR compute multiplier over DCLM
~10⁵GPU-hr
whole-corpus annotation, one-off

01The structural shift: decouple annotation from selection

The defining move of 2025–2026 is to stop baking a single quality decision into the artifact. Pre-2025 web corpora — FineWeb-Edu, DCLM-baseline, Nemotron-CC — score every document with a classifier, keep the top X%, and the discarded 90% survives only as a URL and a scalar score. That is lossy in a way that compounds: every time you want to change the mix (upsample medical, downsample boilerplate, concentrate math late in training), you must retrain a bespoke high-recall classifier and re-filter trillions of tokens, a weeks-to-months loop. Essential-Web v1.0 (Essential AI, arXiv 2506.14111, 24T tokens, 23.6B documents) inverts this. Every document carries the EAI-Taxonomy: 12 categories in 5 groupings — (1) FDC, Free Decimal Correspondence, a public-domain Dewey analogue with 3 hierarchical levels (e.g. 5→Science, 51→Mathematics, 512→Algebra); (2) Bloom (Cognitive Process × Knowledge Domain); (3) Document Type (V1 broad / V2 fine); (4) Content Quality = Reasoning Depth + Educational Level (the FineWeb-Edu axis) + Technical Correctness; (5) Extraction (HTML artifacts + Missing Content). The corpus contains 14.1M unique primary-label combinations and 1.2B primary/secondary combos, which is the whole point: selection becomes a WHERE clause over columns, written in under fifteen minutes, instead of a classifier you train and validate.

The empirical payoff is that pure SQL-style filters over the taxonomy produce datasets competitive with or beating task-specialized SOTA pipelines: math −8.0% vs SOTA, web code +14.3%, STEM +24.5%, medical +8.6% (the medical figure is +3.2pp / 8.6% over DCLM-baseline, +4.9pp over TheBlueScrubs), all reached without training a single domain classifier (arXiv 2506.14111). For a 100k-GB200 run this is the correct template — but it is a template, not a ceiling.

The one move to remember: annotate once, select by query

Pre-annotate your entire crawl once with a rich, orthogonal taxonomy, then drive every per-stage mix, curriculum decision, and domain upsampling via cheap queries. Do not re-filter 10T tokens each time the mix changes. And do not treat the EAI category set as the final word — extend it with axes your evals care about (license class, language, toxicity tier, code-vs-prose, instruction-density, paywall/provenance flag, recency bucket). The taxonomy is metadata you own; over-annotate it.

The load-bearing insight is the annotator economics. Essential AI chose the teacher by orthogonality, correctness, and expressivity, measured via inter-category normalized mutual information (NMI) on held-out sets, and Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct won over Qwen2.5-72B and DeepSeek-V3 — a larger teacher was not better for labeling. They then distilled it into EAI-Distill-0.5b, a fine-tuned Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct trained on ~104.6M Common Crawl documents labeled by the teacher (two annotation passes to add categories), reaching within 3% annotator agreement, 14% NMI, and 1% domain-recall of the 32B teacher. Full-corpus inference over 23.6B documents cost ~90k AMD MI300x GPU-hours (512 MI300x, roughly one week), vLLM for the Qwen models, SGLang for DeepSeek-V3 (arXiv 2506.14111).

The lesson for 10T-scale: a 0.5–1B distilled annotator makes whole-corpus rich labeling a one-off ~10⁵ GPU-hour cost — negligible against a 100k-GB200 pretrain that will burn 10⁷–10⁸ GPU-hours. There is no excuse not to over-annotate. The Essential-Web appendix even exposes the exact pre-classifier heuristic gates (frac_chars_dupe_Ngrams thresholds, top-2/3-gram fractions, word_count < 50) and the two-pass + NMI teacher-selection methodology (PDF) — a copyable spec for building your own distilled annotator rather than re-deriving it.

selection.sqlEAI-Taxonomy WHERE clausereplaces a bespoke classifier + re-filterillustrative
-- "concentrate clean, technically-correct math late in training"
SELECT doc_id, tokens
FROM corpus
WHERE fdc_level1 = 5            -- Science                       -- [1]
  AND fdc_level2 = 51           -- Mathematics
  AND content_quality.reasoning_depth   >= 3                    -- [2]
  AND content_quality.technical_correct  = TRUE
  AND extraction.html_artifacts          = 'clean'  -- no mangled LaTeX  [3]
  AND license_class IN ('permissive', 'licensed')   -- an axis you added [4]
ORDER BY content_quality.educational_level DESC;

1FDC hierarchy. The 3-level Dewey analogue (5→Science, 51→Mathematics, 512→Algebra) lets one column express topic at any granularity — no per-domain classifier to train.

2Content-quality axes. Reasoning Depth + Technical Correctness are orthogonal EAI columns; this is the FineWeb-Edu signal plus correctness, queried not re-filtered.

3Extraction guard. The Extraction axis flags HTML artifacts — exactly the math/LaTeX-mangling §06 warns about; filter it out at query time.

4An axis you own. license_class is not in the base EAI set — it is a provenance column you add at crawl. Over-annotate: the taxonomy is metadata you control.

02The open-corpus lineage and what each contributes

Before designing a stack, fix the reference points. The open web-corpus lineage is the empirical record of what filtering does and does not buy, and a frontier lab should treat each as a tested recipe rather than a finished dataset to consume.

CorpusScaleCore methodKey result / lesson
RefinedWeb (2023)~5T (600B released)Heuristic + MinHash, CC-only, no LLM classifierWeb-only can match curated mixes; heuristics + strong dedup go far
RedPajama-v2 (2023)30T tokens, 100B docsRaw CC + 40+ precomputed quality signals, no filtering appliedThe original "ship signals, let users filter" idea — proto-taxonomy
FineWeb / FineWeb-Edu (2406.17557)15T / ~1.3TLinear regressor on Snowflake-arctic-embed-m, 460k Llama-3-70B edu scores, threshold ≥3Edu-classifier filtering is the workhorse; F1≈82%; per-snapshot (not global) dedup
DCLM-baseline (2406.11794)3.6T (from 240T pool)fastText on OH-2.5 + ELI5 positives, top-~10%7B@2.6T → 64% MMLU; established the fastText-classifier baseline
Nemotron-CC (2412.02595)6.3T (4.4T real + 1.9T synth)Classifier ensemble + rephrasingRecall fix: HQ subset beats DCLM by +5.6 MMLU @8B/1T with 4× more unique tokens
TxT360 (LLM360)40.21B docs → 15T+Global near-dedup of 99 CC snapshots + 14 curated sources, rich metadataControlled upsampling beats FineWeb-15T; ~17% exact dupes removed
Dolma 3 / Olmo 3 (2512.13961)~9.3T pool → ~5.9T mixGlobal dedup (Duplodocus) + quality-aware upsampling ≤7×Most fully-disclosed end-to-end SOTA stack; rebukes "discard 90%"
 The open lineage is recipes-plus-ablations; the ablations are the asset. Mine each for its decision, not its tokens.

Two structural reads. First, the lineage bifurcates into a filtering school (RefinedWeb → DCLM → FineWeb-Edu: train a classifier, keep the top slice) and a metadata school (RedPajama-v2 → TxT360 → Essential-Web → Dolma 3: ship signals/labels, defer selection). The metadata school has won at the frontier because, as §06 makes precise, the selection decision is scale- and target-dependent and you cannot bake it once. Second, the absolute scales matter for a 10T MoE: DCLM-Pool is 240T raw CC tokens, FineWeb is 15T, RedPajama-v2 is 30T raw, Essential-Web is 24T labeled — but the unique high-quality yield after aggressive filtering and dedup is realistically only ~15–20T tokens across the whole open web. That ceiling, not the raw pool, is what your epoch budget must respect (§05).

A third read, easy to miss, is that these corpora are recipes plus ablations, and the ablations are the asset. DCLM's contribution was less the 3.6T-token artifact than DCLM-Bench — a controlled 1B/7B testbed that let the field A/B filtering choices on a fixed compute budget; FineWeb's was the methodical demonstration that per-snapshot dedup, a simple edu classifier, and good extraction beat elaborate hand-tuned heuristic cascades. A frontier lab should mine each release for its decision (what was kept, what was thrown away, and the measured downstream delta) and reproduce the decision on its own corpus, not ingest the tokens.

The right internal artifact is a query, not a dataset

The tokens are downstream-stale the moment your crawler and taxonomy diverge from theirs; the methodology is durable. This is why the right internal artifact is not "our FineWeb" but a versioned, queryable, fully-annotated pool from which any of these recipes can be re-derived in an afternoon by writing a different query.

03Quality classifiers: the recall problem and the fastText renaissance

The 2024 canon converged on two designs. FineWeb-Edu uses a linear regressor on Snowflake-arctic-embed-m embeddings, trained on ~410k of 460k Llama-3-70B-Instruct educational 0–5 annotations (an additive rubric — score points for distinct desiderata — which beats single-rating prompts), threshold ≥3, F1≈82%, yielding ~1.3T tokens from FineWeb's 15T (arXiv 2406.17557). DCLM-baseline uses a fastText classifier with OpenHermes-2.5 + r/ExplainLikeImFive (ELI5) instruction-formatted positives, top-~10% cut → 3.6T tokens, with a 7B@2.6T reaching 64% 5-shot MMLU (arXiv 2406.11794).

The recognized failure mode — stated explicitly in Essential-Web's related work — is that aggressive monolithic classifiers remove ~90% of the data, which is fatal for long-horizon training (Llama-3.1's 15T, your 10T-active run) because it destroys unique-token yield, and these classifiers lag badly on math and code where "educational prose" signal is a poor proxy. Nemotron-CC (arXiv 2412.02595, 6.3T tokens) is the canonical recall fix: ensemble the FineWeb-Edu and DCLM classifiers plus custom model-labeled classifiers, reducing reliance on any single heuristic → 4.4T unique real tokens + 1.9T synthetic; the high-quality subset beats DCLM by +5.6 MMLU at 8B/1T while keeping 4× more unique tokens. Ultra-FineWeb (arXiv 2505.05427, OpenBMB, 1T en + 120B zh) pushes efficient verification: bootstrap a fastText classifier with a verification step — does adding a candidate set actually lower validation loss in a short MiniCPM run? — making filtering cheap enough to iterate.

The division of labor: fastText infers, LLMs label

fastText is back as the inference-time workhorse (millions of docs/sec/CPU core, near-free over 10T tokens) while LLM-as-judge and large embedders supply the labels. Ensemble multiple classifiers for recall rather than trusting one aggressive cut, and verify a classifier by its downstream-loss effect, not its F1 — the Ultra-FineWeb verification loop and Essential-Web's NMI-based teacher selection are the same instinct: optimize the metric you actually care about (loss/eval), not a proxy. For a 200B-active MoE specifically, filter less aggressively than the 7B-proxy literature implies (§06) and lean on the recall-preserving ensemble + taxonomy rather than a top-10% guillotine.

04Deduplication and decontamination

The standard dedup stack has four tiers, and a frontier corpus uses all of them:

exact substring / suffix-array
verbatim repeats
Cheap, high-precision, the floor.
MinHash-LSH fuzzy
near-duplicates
Dominant method. FineWeb deduped per-snapshot, not globally.
Bloom-filter (BFF)
global, pool-scale
DCLM's approach; memory-efficient at pool scale.
semantic (SemDeDup/D4/SoftDeDup)
paraphrase-level
Embed-cluster-drop; catches redundancy MinHash misses.

The four tiers in detail: exact substring / suffix-array dedup removes verbatim repeats — cheap, high-precision, the floor. MinHash-LSH fuzzy dedup is the dominant near-duplicate method; FineWeb deliberately deduped per-snapshot, not globally, after finding that global dedup over-removes and the survivors skew toward low-quality boilerplate that happens to be unique (arXiv 2406.17557). Bloom-filter dedup (BFF) is DCLM's approach — memory-efficient global dedup at pool scale. Semantic dedup covers SemDeDup (embed, cluster, drop near-duplicate embeddings), D4 (dedup + diversify via SSL-prototype pruning), and SoftDeDup (reweight by n-gram "data commonness" instead of hard removal) — this tier catches paraphrase-level redundancy that MinHash misses.

TxT360 globally near-deduplicated 99 CC snapshots + 14 curated sources to 40.21B docs (~17% exact dupes removed), with rich per-document metadata enabling controlled upsampling into a 15T+ corpus that beats FineWeb-15T (LLM360). The 2026 frontier reference is Olmo 3 / Dolma 3 (arXiv 2512.13961): aggressive global dedup at trillion-token scale via their new "Duplodocus" tool, plus quality-aware upsampling that repeats high-quality docs up to 7× rather than flat top-k filtering — a direct rebuke of the "throw away 90%" school.

Resolving the FineWeb-vs-Dolma global-dedup tension

Global dedup is correct when paired with quality-aware upsampling and rich metadata (so you re-add the high-quality survivors deliberately), and harmful when it is the only knob (you over-remove and keep the unique junk). For a 10T MoE, run global dedup to establish the unique-token inventory, then upsample by taxonomy class — do not let global dedup silently set your mix.

Decontamination remains GPT-3-era 13-gram overlap as the floor, but it is known-leaky to paraphrase. Modern pipelines (Olmo 3 emphasizes "stronger decontamination") add embedding-similarity and LLM-semantic checks against the full eval suite — not just public benchmarks but your private evals, since a leaked private eval is undetectable from the outside and corrupts your own go/no-go decisions. The decontamination ordering matters operationally: it must run after synthesis, not before, because a rephraser pointed at a document that happens to paraphrase a benchmark question will manufacture fresh contamination that no 13-gram filter on the source corpus would have caught.

Order-of-operations trap: decontaminate after synthesis

Re-run the full decontam sweep after every synthetic-generation and translation pass, and decontaminate the synthetic tokens against the eval suite, not just the raw web. A leaked private eval is undetectable from outside and corrupts your own decisions.

For a 200B-active MoE, dedup matters more than for a dense model: Muennighoff data-constrained scaling shows repeats decay in value (≈4 epochs is the practical degradation point for dense; MoE folklore puts it lower, ~2 epochs, because sparse experts overfit their routed subset faster), so unique-token yield is the binding constraint and every duplicate you fail to remove is an epoch you cannot afford (arXiv 2305.16264). Concretely, Muennighoff's effective-data law is:

data D′ = UD + UD · RD* · (1 − e−RD/RD*) RD* ≈ 15.4 for the dense regime: returns on repetition are nearly free up to ~4 epochs, decay sharply past RD*, and are essentially nil by ~40 epochs.

The corollary for the data pipeline is that a duplicate you fail to catch does not merely waste a slot — it inflates your measured unique-token count, so you under-acquire and discover the shortfall only when the loss curve flattens early. Budget dedup recall as conservatively as you budget GPU-hours.

05Why frontier labs run private crawlers

Three converging pressures make a proprietary crawler mandatory by mid-2026, not optional.

(1) Coverage and freshness. Common Crawl is a sparse, lagging sample of any given domain — it captures a thin slice of each site at irregular intervals. Labs want full-site, near-real-time crawls. Anthropic's ClaudeBot at its June-2025 peak was crawling on the order of 70,900 pages per referred visitor, an extraction footprint far beyond CC's, precisely because CC's per-site depth is shallow.

(2) Access collapse. More than 35% of the top-1000 sites now block GPTBot — a 7× rise since August 2023 — and CCBot is the single most-blocked crawler, so Common Crawl's effective coverage is actively declining (Cloudflare). Cloudflare's mid-2025 flip to permission-default (block AI crawlers unless allowed) and the emerging RSL (Really Simple Licensing) standard adopted by Reddit, Yahoo, Medium, and People Inc. push the whole web toward pay-per-crawl. A corpus built on CC is built on a shrinking, increasingly stale base.

(3) Legal and quality provenance. A November 2025 investigation showed Common Crawl ships paywalled news despite claims to the contrary — a liability a frontier lab prefers to own via direct licenses rather than inherit. The economics are now explicit: Reddit↔OpenAI is reportedly ~$70M/yr for licensed access. reported

Crawler in-house, walled gardens licensed, CC as supplement

Run a proprietary full-coverage crawler for freshness, depth, and clean provenance; license the high-value walled gardens directly (Reddit and forums, news, Stack/code, scientific publishers); and use Common Crawl only as a baseline and supplement, never as the spine. The crawler is also where you enforce provenance metadata — license class, robots/RSL status, paywall flag — that becomes a taxonomy axis and a legal audit trail. Pair direct PDF acquisition with olmOCR-class extraction for scientific literature, which Dolma 3 weights at 13.6% of its mix (§07) — a signal of how much premium clean text now comes from PDFs rather than HTML.

06Synthetic data: rephrasing has won over de-novo textbooks

The decisive empirical result of 2025–2026 is that rephrasing existing web text beats generating textbooks from scratch at scale. This reverses the early Phi/Cosmopedia intuition and is now the consensus.

The lineage: WRAP established source-rephrasing with a ~3× training speedup. Cosmopedia (Mixtral-8x7B, 25B tokens, 30M files) is the open textbook-style baseline. BeyondWeb (DatologyAI, arXiv 2508.10975) is the trillion-scale lesson: targeted, diverse-strategy rephrasing — format transformation, QA-pair extraction, pedagogical-tone rewrite, content restructuring — beats RedPajama by +7.1pp and Nemotron-Synth by +2.6pp at 8B/180B tokens, 7.7× faster than RedPajama; a 3B-on-BeyondWeb beats an 8B-on-Cosmopedia. Three findings reframe the whole synthetic-data cost model:

  1. Rephraser quality saturates by ~3B params. Generator quality does not predict synthetic quality; weak ~3B models rephrase fine. You do not need your flagship to generate data.
  2. Strategy diversity is what sustains gains across trillion-token budgets. Single-strategy synthetic plateaus; diverse-strategy does not. The cost lever is prompt/strategy engineering, not generator size.
  3. The "Demystifying Synthetic Data" scaling study (arXiv 2510.01631) confirms rephrased web shows no degradation at high synthetic fraction, whereas textbook-style synthetic degrades — so you can push the synthetic fraction high if and only if it is rephrasing-based and strategy-diverse.

Nemotron-CC-v2 operationalizes this: Qwen3-30B-A3B rephrasing + Diverse-QA (5 prompt templates), translated into 15 languages for +10.0 Global-MMLU over multilingual-CC-only. Nemotron-CC-Math (arXiv 2508.15096, 133.26B tokens @3+, 52.32B @4+) is the must-copy math pipeline, and its key lesson is non-obvious: the bottleneck for web math is not the quality classifier but HTML extraction. Trafilatura and Resiliparse silently corrupt inline LaTeX and flatten code blocks. Nemotron-CC-Math renders HTML through Lynx — a 1992 text browser — to preserve equations, then uses Phi-4 to standardize to canonical LaTeX, delivering +4.8 MATH, +16.2 MBPP+ over FineMath-4+ with 5.5× more tokens. Most teams have not internalized this; for a math/code-heavy frontier MoE it is free quality.

The cheapest win nobody applies: extract math through Lynx

The bottleneck for web math is HTML extraction, not the classifier. Trafilatura/Resiliparse mangle inline LaTeX. Render through Lynx to preserve equations, then Phi-4 to canonicalize — +4.8 MATH, +16.2 MBPP+ over FineMath-4+ with 5.5× more tokens. Free quality for a reasoning-heavy MoE.

REWIRE (Meta, arXiv 2506.04689) is the clearest "beat the data wall" lever: it recycles the filtered-out low-quality tail by rewriting those discarded documents, outperforming both raw-web and synthetic-only baselines across 10B/70B/300B budgets, with an optimal mix of recycled + raw beating either alone. For a unique-token-constrained 10T budget this is orthogonal to and stackable with rephrasing the kept data — instead of discarding 90%, rewrite the discarded tail into usable tokens.

The synthetic recipe for the frontier run

Keep the synthetic fraction high but diversity-driven, never a textbook monoculture; rephrase the kept web with ≥4 distinct strategies; REWIRE-recycle the filtered tail; render math/code through structure-preserving extraction; translate the best synthetic into the target language set.

07The frontier beyond annotation: model-aware and per-token selection

Even a perfect taxonomy plus SQL is heuristic selection — it matches documents to a human notion of "high quality." The genuine research frontier is model-aware selection: choose data by how the learner uses it to perform on target tasks.

Heuristic similarity is provably suboptimal. DsDm (Engstrom et al., ICML 2024, PMLR v235) shows that selecting by similarity to "high-quality" sources can hurt downstream performance; datamodels / influence functions that predict how training on a document changes target-task loss win instead. MATES (Yu et al., NeurIPS 2024) trains a small data-influence model, periodically refreshed to track the evolving pretraining state (oracle = one-step reference-loss reduction), beating static selection on scaling curves — the key being that influence is non-stationary, so a single up-front score is wrong by mid-training.

Apple's BETR ("Language Models Improve When Pretraining Data Matches Target Tasks," arXiv 2507.12466) is the cleanest 2025 result and the most directly actionable: embed benchmark examples, score corpus documents by similarity to them, distill into a classifier — yielding a 2.1× compute multiplier over DCLM-baseline and 4.7× over unfiltered, validated across 500+ models from 10¹⁹ to 10²² FLOP.

The scaling law to burn in: larger models need less filtering BETR, 500+ models

BETR's scaling-law finding is the one to burn in for a 200B-active build: larger models need less aggressive filtering. Selection strategy must be scale-adapted. Tuning your 10T/200B mix on a 7B proxy's optimal filter threshold will silently over-filter and waste frontier compute — the proxy will tell you to cut harder than the real model wants.

On the learnability / online axis: RHO-Loss (select tokens that are learnable-but-not-yet-learned, via a reference model's holdout loss) is the seed; ESLM (arXiv 2505.19893) does risk-averse per-token selective language modeling, pushing selection below the document granularity to the token level. The RHO-Loss criterion is worth stating precisely because it captures what document-level quality scoring misses: a token is worth training on when the training model's loss on it is high (it has not been learned) but a reference model's holdout loss on it is low (it is learnable and not noise) — high training loss alone selects mislabeled and inherently-random tokens, which is why naive hard-example mining fails.

optim keep token t ⇔ ℒtrain(t) high ∧ ℒrefholdout(t) low RHO-Loss: learnable-but-not-yet-learned. High training loss alone selects mislabeled/random tokens — the reference-model gate filters those out.

Per-token selection is the logical endpoint of the recall argument: instead of discarding a document wholesale, keep its high-signal spans and skip its boilerplate, navigation chrome, and license footers. This is still research-grade at trillion-token scale — the reference-model forward pass over 10T tokens is a real cost, and the gains shrink as the base corpus quality rises — but it is where the field is heading, and a cheap approximation (mask obvious boilerplate spans flagged by the Extraction taxonomy axis) is already deployable today.

Per-domain mixing: DoReMi → RegMix → CLIMB → online bandits

Per-domain mixing has matured well past DoReMi (Group-DRO to derive domain weights from a proxy). RegMix (arXiv 2502.10341 lineage) fits a regression over many small proxy models trained on random mixtures, matching or exceeding DoReMi at ~10% of the compute. WebOrganizer (weborganizer.allen.ai, Wettig et al.) organizes the web into 24 topic × 24 format domains and, combined with RegMix, shows that topic-defined domains beat source-defined domains for mixing — the "Topic Over Source" result (arXiv 2502.16802) — which is directly relevant because your taxonomy already gives you topic columns; mix over those, not over URLs. CLIMB / Nemotron-CLIMB (NVIDIA, arXiv 2504.13161) does k-means-cluster → proxy-eval → regression-predictor → iterative bootstrap of the mixture, with a 1B model +2.0% over Llama-3.2-1B at 400B tokens. Online Data Mixing (a multi-armed bandit over domains during training) closes the loop for a run far too expensive to re-sweep — you adjust the mixture from live loss signals rather than committing it up front.

python — RegMix proxy fit → online-bandit mixture adaptation (illustrative)
import numpy as np

# 1) RegMix: fit a regressor over many small proxy runs on random mixtures
#    X = sampled topic-format weights, y = proxy validation loss
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingRegressor
reg = GradientBoostingRegressor().fit(X_mixtures, y_proxy_loss)   # ~10% of DoReMi compute
w0 = minimize_mixture(reg)            # initial 24-topic x 24-format weights

# 2) Online Data Mixing: EXP3 bandit over domains *during* the real run
w = w0.copy()
for step, loss_per_domain in stream_training_losses():           # live signal, no re-sweep
    reward = -np.asarray(loss_per_domain)        # higher loss -> sample that domain more
    w *= np.exp(eta * (reward - reward.mean()))  # multiplicative weights update
    w /= w.sum()                                  # renormalize to a sampling distribution
    set_dataloader_mixture(w)                     # mix over TOPIC columns, not source URLs
RegMix/CLIMB sets the initial mix, a bandit adapts it online

For a single 10T run you cannot afford a full mixture sweep at scale, so the practical recipe is: RegMix/CLIMB to set the initial topic-format mixture from proxies, then an online bandit to adapt it during the run. Mix over topic columns from your taxonomy, not over source URLs.

08A SOTA 10T-token data stack (June 2026)

Putting it together, here is the concrete stack for the 10T-total / 200B-active target.
