The numbers throughout are governed by the binding constraint from §04: realistically ~15–20T unique high-quality tokens exist, MoEs tolerate fewer epochs than dense models, so the unique-token budget — not the raw pool — is what you optimize, and acquisition/synthesis/recycling are compute-equivalent levers for beating the wall.

StageActionTooling / referenceFrontier choice for 10T/200B
1. AcquireProprietary full-coverage crawler + licensed walled gardens + CC baselineClaudeBot-class crawler; RSL-licensed Reddit/news/code/scienceCC is a supplement, not the spine; provenance metadata captured at crawl
2. ExtractStructure-preserving extractionLynx for math/code, olmOCR for PDFsNever let Trafilatura mangle LaTeX; PDFs are a premium source
3. DedupGlobal trillion-token dedup, all four tiersMinHash-LSH + suffix-array + BFF + SemDeDup; Duplodocus-classGlobal dedup to set unique inventory, then upsample by class
4. AnnotateOnce, into a rich orthogonal taxonomyEAI-Taxonomy template + distilled 0.5–1B annotator (~10⁵ GPU-hr)Queryable metadata, not a single score; extend EAI axes
5. SelectSQL filters + model-aware overlaysEAI WHERE clauses + BETR/datamodel influence, scale-adaptedFilter less hard than 7B-proxy implies; influence for target alignment
6. SynthesizeDiverse-strategy rephrasing + tail recycling + Diverse-QABeyondWeb principles, REWIRE, Nemotron-CC-Math, multilingualHigh synthetic fraction, diversity-driven, ~3B rephraser suffices
7. MixTopic-format mixing + quality-aware upsampling + curriculumRegMix/CLIMB → online bandit; Dolma-3 upsampling ≤7×Math/code/reasoning concentrated late, after LR decay
8. Decontaminate13-gram floor + embedding/LLM-semanticAgainst the full eval suite incl. private evalsRe-run after every synthetic-generation pass
 The whole stack is cheap relative to the pretrain — which is exactly why there is no excuse to skimp on it.

Reference proportions. Dolma 3's ~5.9T mix is the most fully-disclosed SOTA baseline: CC 76.1%, olmOCR science PDFs 13.6%, Stack-Edu code 6.89%, FineMath-3+ 2.56%, arXiv 0.86% (arXiv 2512.13961). A frontier 200B-active MoE should shift materially away from that distribution — Dolma is deliberately CC-heavy for reproducibility and license-cleanliness, not because 76% web is optimal. The frontier shift is toward (a) a much larger code and math fraction (these carry the reasoning signal that MoE experts specialize on, and the granularity/sparsity scaling laws reward specialization), (b) a high synthetic fraction of strategy-diverse rephrased + recycled tokens, and (c) license-clean premium sources (licensed forums, news, publishers) replacing the riskiest CC tail.

Curriculum and the late-stage decay. The mixture is not static across training. The strong, repeatable finding is that the LR decay phase wastes your best data if you place it early — high-quality math/code/reasoning and the cleanest synthetic should be concentrated late, after the learning rate has decayed, where the model can actually absorb it (this couples directly to the WSD scheduler from §8: fork the annealing phase and feed it your premium mix). With ~5T unique high-quality tokens repeated at ≤4 epochs you reach ~20T effective tokens before repetition decay bites — and quality-aware upsampling (Dolma-3 style, repeating the best documents up to 7×) is the mechanism for getting there without flat top-k throwing away recall.

Pipeline cost and where it runs. The whole stack is cheap relative to the pretrain, which is the reason there is no excuse to skimp on it. Annotation is the largest single line item and it is ~10⁵ GPU-hours for a distilled 0.5–1B model over tens of billions of documents — at most a few tenths of a percent of a 10⁷–10⁸ GPU-hour pretrain. Dedup, extraction, and fastText classification are overwhelmingly CPU work and run on a commodity cluster off the critical accelerator path; the GPU-resident costs are the annotator, the synthetic rephraser (a ~3B model, embarrassingly parallel, run with vLLM/SGLang at high batch), and the optional reference-model forward pass for influence/learnability scoring.

Run the pipeline as a standing service, not a one-shot job

Treat the data pipeline as a standing service that runs continuously and feeds a versioned pool, not a one-shot job before the run: the crawler is always crawling, the annotator re-labels new documents as they land, and the mixture is re-queried per training stage. The 100k-GB200 fleet should never idle waiting on data, and with the pipeline decoupled and CPU-bound it never will — the binding constraint is unique-token supply, not pipeline throughput.

The through-line: stop deleting data

Every recommendation here points the same direction: stop deleting data. Annotate once and select by query; recall-preserve with ensembles and taxonomy; recycle the filtered tail with REWIRE; upsample quality rather than discarding the rest; adapt selection to your model's scale (filter less at 200B); and treat synthetic as a strategy-diversity problem, not a generator-size problem. The lab that owns its crawler, its taxonomy, and its model-aware selection loop owns the variable that the FLOP budget cannot buy.

Sources

  • Essential-Web v1.0 / EAI-Taxonomy: arxiv.org/abs/2506.14111, PDF
  • FineWeb / FineWeb-Edu: arxiv.org/html/2406.17557v1
  • DCLM: arxiv.org/abs/2406.11794
  • Nemotron-CC: arxiv.org/abs/2412.02595
  • Nemotron-CC-Math (Lynx + Phi-4 extraction): arxiv.org/html/2508.15096v1
  • Ultra-FineWeb (verification-loop fastText): arxiv.org/abs/2505.05427
  • TxT360 (LLM360): llm360.ai/news/txt360-blogpost.html
  • Olmo 3 / Dolma 3 / Duplodocus: arxiv.org/pdf/2512.13961
  • BeyondWeb (DatologyAI): datologyai.com/blog/beyondweb
  • Demystifying Synthetic Data: arxiv.org/abs/2510.01631
  • REWIRE (Recycling the Web): arxiv.org/pdf/2506.04689v1
  • Apple BETR: arxiv.org/abs/2507.12466
  • DsDm (Engstrom et al.): proceedings.mlr.press/v235/engstrom24a.html
  • WebOrganizer / Topic Over Source: weborganizer.allen.ai, arxiv.org/abs/2502.10341, arxiv.org/abs/2502.16802
  • CLIMB / Nemotron-CLIMB: arxiv.org/abs/2504.13161
  • ESLM (per-token selective LM): arxiv.org/abs/2505.19893
  • Data-constrained scaling (Muennighoff et al.): arxiv.org/pdf/2305.16264
  • Cloudflare / crawler-blocking and pay-per-crawl: cloudflare.com/press/.../cloudflare-just-changed-how-ai-crawlers-scrape-the-internet

Chapter 14 · The bottleneck nobody photographs

SOTA dataloading and I/O at 100k GPUs: making the loader invisible

At a 10T-token / 200B-active run spread across ~12,500 hosts, the dataloader stops being a per-host detail and becomes a distributed storage system with a hard real-time SLA. Every microbatch boundary, on every host, the accelerators must already own their tokens — or they idle and you burn $30–60/s in depreciation doing nothing.

~24 min read5 figuresgrounded in MaxText grain_data_processing.py + olmo_data_grain.py

There is a photogenic version of a 100,000-GPU run — the rack rows, the NVLink spine, the loss curve bending. The dataloader appears in none of it, which is exactly why it kills runs. At this scale the loader stops being a per-host detail and becomes a distributed storage system with a hard real-time SLA: every microbatch boundary, on every one of ~12,500 hosts (8 GPU/host across ~1,389 NVL72 racks), the accelerators must already own their tokens or they idle. A 100,000-GB200 cluster at ~0.45 EFLOP/s dense FP8 peak and a realistic 35–40% large-MoE FP8 MFU (NVIDIA GB200 NVL72; sparse-vs-dense footnote) is burning on the order of $30–60/s in depreciation. A 200ms host stall per step, repeated, is a multi-percent tax on the most expensive compute humanity has ever pointed at a corpus. The loader is also the single component most likely to be non-deterministic on restart, and a 10T-token MoE run will restart dozens of times. This chapter is the punch-list for making the I/O layer invisible — which is the only acceptable state for it to be in.

The entire art of dataloading at this scale is converting many small requests into few large sequential ones, spread across the keyspace and across time — and keeping the training loader dumb, deterministic, and stateless so a restart is free.
40TB
10T tokens × 4 B/token (uint32 IDs)
400GB/s
sustained read at 10⁸ tok/s — a non-problem
125kreq/s
12,500 hosts × 10 reads/s — 25× the GCS cliff
~5,000rd/s
flat-namespace GCS cold-start ceiling/bucket

The pipeline you are building turns a global corpus into a hot stream of device-resident tokens. Each stage has a failure mode; the figure below is the map for the chapter — the request-rate wall lives at the host→object-store edge, the determinism and resume guarantees live in the shuffle/sample stages, and the host-stall risk lives in everything between shard decode and the device transfer.

01The five hard requirements

A frontier loader at 100k hosts must satisfy all five of these simultaneously; most loaders satisfy three or four, and the missing one is where the run bleeds.

1 · Deterministic
batches = f(seed, step, topology-inv)
Same checkpoint → bit-identical batches. The only way to debug a loss spike, reproduce a NaN, or trust an ablation.
2 · Checkpointable O(small)
tiny, off-critical-path
Per-host blobs scaling with process_count become 12,500 small-object reads on every restart — the exact pattern that triggers object-store throttling.
3 · Exactly-sharded
every token once/epoch
Disjoint across DP replicas, no overlap, no gaps. Silent over/under-sampling at the shard boundary corrupts the data-constrained scaling budget.
4 · No host stalls
fully overlapped, prefetched
Shard decode, collation, packing, host→device transfer never block. Colocated, multi-threaded, prefetched, with no CPU-heavy stage on the hot path.
5 · Fault-tolerant + elastic
survives a world-size change
Survives preemption, host failure, and the hard one — reshard from N to N′ hosts must resume the same logical stream, not re-shuffle or re-emit.

The MaxText input pipeline (the user's actual codebase, MaxText) routes dataset_type to one of grain | hf | olmo_grain | tfds | c4_mlperf | synthetic in input_pipeline_interface.py, and only the Grain-family paths (grain, olmo_grain) plus, in the broader field, MosaicML StreamingDataset and Megatron-Energon credibly hit all five. The rest fail requirement 1, 3, or 5.

02The bandwidth is trivial; the request rate is lethal

Start with the bytes. Pre-tokenized IDs at a DeepSeek-class >100k vocab need uint32 (4 B/token); uint16 bit-packing (2 B/token) is off the table without sub-vocab remapping. A 10T-token corpus is therefore 40 TB on disk as raw IDs. A 200B-active MoE on 100k GB200 plausibly consumes O(10⁷–10⁸) tokens/s wall-clock. The sustained read bandwidth this implies:

ThroughputBytes/tokenSustained read
2×10⁷ tok/s4 B80 GB/s
1×10⁸ tok/s4 B400 GB/s
 A single GCS or S3 bucket sustains multi-hundred-GB/s aggregate without complaint. Bandwidth is a non-problem.

The wall is request rate. GCS flat-namespace buckets start at ~5,000 object reads/s and ~1,000 writes/s per bucket, auto-scale no faster than doubling every ~20 minutes, and top out near 80k reads/s only with good key distribution; hierarchical-namespace (HNS) buckets give up to 8× higher initial QPS (GCS request-rate guidelines). S3 sustains 5,500 GET/HEAD and 3,500 PUT per partitioned prefix, returning 503 SlowDown while it splits partitions in the background (S3 performance guidelines).

Now do the arithmetic that should keep you up at night. 12,500 hosts issuing even 10 reads/s each = 125,000 req/s — 25× a flat GCS bucket's cold-start ceiling, 23× a single S3 prefix. At job launch and after every preemption restart, 12,500 hosts cold-start the same keyspace simultaneously. That is a textbook thundering herd, and it manifests as a 503/429 storm precisely at the moment you most want the run back up. The bandwidth math says "easy"; the request math says "you will be throttled to a crawl unless the access pattern is engineered." The entire art of dataloading at this scale is converting many small requests into few large sequential ones, and spreading them across the keyspace and across time.

The structural mitigations, in priority order:

  • Large shards. ArrayRecord or WebDataset shards of 256 MB–1 GB so one GET amortizes thousands of samples. This is the single biggest lever on request count.
  • Prefix fan-out. Hash shard names across thousands of prefixes so S3/GCS partition the keyspace and your QPS ceiling multiplies by the prefix count.
  • HNS buckets for both data and checkpoints — the 8× initial-QPS multiplier is free if you provision the bucket type correctly up front.
  • Staggered exponential-backoff ramp so 12,500 hosts don't cold-start the same prefix in the same second. Jittered startup is mandatory, not optional.
  • A read-amplification-reducing loader that holds shards open (mmap or HTTP range-read) rather than re-GETting per sample.

WebDataset's purely sequential tar reads are 3–10× faster than random access on local disk and are object-store-friendly because they fold N small GETs into one streamed GET. ArrayRecord trades pure-sequential for true random access via its Riegeli-backed per-record index (Grain + ArrayRecord) — which is what buys you a real global shuffle. That tradeoff (sequential-tar throughput vs random-access shuffle quality) is the fault line dividing the loader matrix below.

The one failure mode to engineer against

At 12,500 hosts the killer is never aggregate bandwidth — it is the cold-start request storm. Every job launch and every preemption restart fans the same keyspace out to all hosts at once. Large shards + thousands of name-hashed prefixes + a jittered backoff ramp are what keep you under the ~5,000-reads/s cliff.

03The loader matrix

LoaderFormatTrue global shuffleResume stateElastic (world-size change)Object-store postureStack
Grain + ArrayRecordArrayRecord/RiegeliYes (index stream)Tiny JSON per shard, or stateless (OLMo sampler)Yes via ElasticIterator (global scalar)Random-access GETs; needs large shardsJAX (MaxText default)
MosaicML StreamingDataset.mdsWithin-block (py1e)Mid-epoch, no re-downloadYes, iff global batch constant & NCN pinnedShard download + SSD cachePyTorch
Megatron-Energon + WebDatasettar + .nv-metaPer-shard / blendedSavable/restorable loader statePer-rank deterministic, not elasticSequential-tar streaming (best)PyTorch/Megatron
Megatron-LM indexed.bin/.idx mmapYes (offline index).npy memmapsNo — assumes staged local NVMeZero object-store pressure once stagedPyTorch/Megatron
tf.data / TFRecordTFRecordNo (window only)WeakNoSequentialTF/JAX
HF IterableDatasetparquet/arrowWindowShard+resume (improving)NoVariableHF
Ray Data / DaftanyN/A (offline)N/AN/ACloud-native, 32k req/s/VMOffline prep
 Only the Grain-family, StreamingDataset, and Energon rows credibly satisfy all five requirements; the bottom three know their lane (offline prep or non-deterministic).

Grain + ArrayRecord — the MaxText default, and the right default

Grain's IndexSampler is the only widely-deployed training loader that does a true global shuffle over a non-resident dataset: it generates a permuted index stream on the fly and random-accesses ArrayRecord records by key, because Riegeli embeds a per-record position index (Grain + ArrayRecord). Determinism is a pure function of (seed, shard_options, epoch). The MaxText wiring in grain_data_processing.py is the canonical shape: MapDataset.source(ArrayRecordDataSource) → .shuffle(seed) → .repeat(num_epoch) → host-strided sharding → to_iter_dataset(ReadOptions(num_threads, prefetch_buffer_size)), with grain_num_threads and grain_prefetch_buffer_size as the host-stall-avoidance knobs.

python — MaxText grain_data_processing.py pipeline shape (illustrative)
# MapDataset (random-access) -> IterDataset (streamed, prefetched)
ds = grain.MapDataset.source(ArrayRecordDataSource(paths))     # Riegeli per-record index
ds = ds.shuffle(seed=config.data_shuffle_seed)                 # true global shuffle
ds = ds.repeat(num_epoch)
ds = ds[shard_index::num_shards]                               # host-strided exact-shard
it = ds.to_iter_dataset(
    grain.ReadOptions(num_threads=config.grain_num_threads,    # host-stall knobs
                      prefetch_buffer_size=config.grain_prefetch_buffer_size))

# curriculum mixtures: mix at the IterDataset level so each component
# checkpoints its position independently (re-weightable after restart):
it = grain.IterDataset.mix([math_it, code_it, web_it], weights=[0.3, 0.2, 0.5])

Two non-obvious design choices in that file are load-bearing at frontier scale:

  1. Mixtures use grain.IterDataset.mix(weights), not MapDataset.mix. MaxText does this specifically so each mixture component checkpoints independently, which means you can re-weight the curriculum after a restart without corrupting per-component position state (the user's actual codebase, MaxText, grain_data_processing.py). For a long curriculum that shifts toward math/code/reasoning after LR decay, this is the property that lets you change the mix at step 800k without a fresh data pipeline.
  2. The elastic path. grain.experimental.ElasticIterator (enabled via grain_use_elastic_iterator) does sharding and batching internally from a single global_batch_size, and its checkpoint state is a single global scalar. MaxText exploits this in multihost_dataloading.py: under elastic mode only process 0 writes process_0.json, and every process reads that same file on restore — so the iterator survives a change in jax.process_count() (the user's actual codebase, MaxText). This is the cleanest elastic-resize-survival design among the JAX loaders, and it directly kills the requirement-5 failure mode.
The non-elastic Grain trap — N files in, N files out

The non-elastic Grain path writes process_{i}-of-{n}.json per host. That blob is correct, but it is N files written and N files re-read on restart — 12,500 small-object operations against the checkpoint bucket on every resume, exactly the request pattern §02 says to avoid. At 100k hosts you want the elastic path or the stateless sampler below, not per-host JSON.

MaxText's OLMo Grain loader — the stateless-resume blueprint

src/maxtext/input_pipeline/olmo_data_grain.py is the most instructive in-repo artifact for your scale, because it eliminates iterator-state checkpointing entirely. Instead of serializing position, OlmoIndexSampler.__getitem__(i) is a pure function: it maps per-host step i to a record key via a single Fisher-Yates permutation over [0, total_instances) keyed by SHA-256("olmo-shuffle:{seed}:{epoch}") truncated to 64 bits (the user's actual codebase, MaxText — the loader explicitly uses SHA-256 "because it has no fixed points unlike a raw multiply"). The epoch is (i + initial_step) // n_per, the in-epoch position is (i + initial_step) % n_per, and resume needs zero serialized iterator state: you pass initial_step = train_step × per_host_batch at construction and the (seed, shard, absolute_step) tuple fully determines the next record (olmo_data_grain.py).

src/maxtext/input_pipeline/olmo_data_grain.pyOlmoIndexSamplerstateless resumetrimmed
class OlmoIndexSampler:
    def __getitem__(self, i):                                  # [1]
        step   = i + self.initial_step                         # absolute step, no state
        epoch  = step // self.n_per                            # n_per = instances/epoch
        pos    = step %  self.n_per                            # in-epoch position
        # SHA-256 seed "has no fixed points unlike a raw multiply"
        key    = sha256(f"olmo-shuffle:{self.seed}:{epoch}".encode())
        seed64 = int.from_bytes(key.digest()[:8], "big")       # truncate to 64 bits
        perm   = fisher_yates(self.total_instances, seed64)    # [2]
        return perm[pos]                                       # record key -> ArrayRecord

    def __repr__(self):                                        # [3]
        # DELIBERATELY excludes initial_step so Grain's repr-based
        # sampler-equality check accepts a resumed loader as "the same"
        return f"OlmoIndexSampler(seed={self.seed}, shard={self.shard})"

1A pure function of step. No serialized position. initial_step = train_step × per_host_batch is passed at construction; the loader needs zero checkpoint blob on the restart path.

2Per-epoch Fisher-Yates. One permutation over [0, total_instances), re-keyed each epoch via SHA-256 — chosen precisely because it has no fixed points.

3The repr trick. __repr__ excludes initial_step so a resumed run at a different offset still looks like "the same" sampler to Grain's bookkeeping. olmo_data_grain_resume_test.py pins this contract.

The cleverest detail is a one-line compatibility hack with deep consequences: __repr__ deliberately excludes initial_step so that Grain's repr-based sampler-equality check still accepts a resumed loader as "the same" sampler — a resumed run with a different offset must not look like a different pipeline to Grain's bookkeeping (the user's actual codebase, MaxText). This is the pattern you want at 12,500 hosts: zero per-host checkpoint state on the critical restart path, so a resume triggers no small-object read storm at all. The olmo_data_grain_resume_test.py pins this contract explicitly.

The loader also documents its own scaling defect in-code, which is gold. shuffled_global_indices materializes a full numpy rng.permutation(total) — ~5.8 GB at uint64 for the 724M-instance OLMo mix, allocated per epoch per host — and the source comment says production should swap to OLMo-core's on-disk build_and_save_global_indices memmap scheme (olmo_data_grain.py; OLMo-core data loading). At trillions of instances that allocation is fatal twice over: it overflows RAM, and OLMo-core's uint32 index format itself overflows at 4.29B instances, forcing uint64 or a block-structured index. The fix is to compute the permutation once, write global_indices.npy as a memmap, and mmap-share it across all forked Grain workers — never recompute in RAM.

The supporting OlmoNpyDataSource shows the right fork-safety discipline: per-worker LRU-cached np.memmap handles with max_open_files=64 over the 950-file mix (to stay under ulimit -n), and it copies out of the read-only memmap before returning so forked Grain workers can pickle the result without dragging the mmap object across the fork boundary (the user's actual codebase, MaxText). No shared mutable state across fork is the whole game for host-stall avoidance — a shared mmap that must be re-faulted or a lock that serializes workers is how you reintroduce stalls.

Move the permutation to an on-disk memmap

Adopt OLMo-core's build_and_save_global_indices to kill the ~5.8 GB/host/epoch rng.permutation allocation the MaxText loader explicitly warns about. Compute the permutation once, write it as uint64 (you are over the 4.29B uint32 line at ~5B instances), and mmap-share it across forked workers. Never recompute in RAM.

MosaicML StreamingDataset — the other production-grade elastic-deterministic loader

Streaming's elastic determinism is real but conditional: it survives a world-size change iff the global batch size stays constant and is divisible by every GPU count you run on, and num_canonical_nodes (NCN) is pinned across runs (Streaming elastic determinism). NCN partitions the sample space into buckets (default = physical node count of the first run, or 64× under py1s/py2s). The default shuffle is py1e with shuffle_block_size = max(4×10⁶ / NCN, 1<<18) (floor 262,144) and shuffle_seed = 9176 (Streaming shuffling reference). The algorithm zoo and its tradeoff:

AlgoShuffle qualityDownload localityDisk footprint
py1ebest balancegoodmoderate
py1brgooddownload-optimallow
py1s / py2slowergoodminimal
naivetrue globaldestroys localitysingle-node only
 py1e shuffles within a block to preserve object-store read locality; naive truly-global shuffle is unusable at scale.

The design lesson generalizes to any 100k-host loader: py1e deliberately shuffles within a block rather than globally, to preserve object-store read locality. naive global shuffle is unusable at scale precisely because it converts every sample read into a random GET against a random shard — the §02 catastrophe. Mid-epoch resumption replays the deterministic order without re-downloading already-seen shards. The tuning tension at 100k GPUs: NCN must be large enough that bucket count ≥ node count and gives download diversity, but shuffle_block_size shrinks as NCN grows (it is 4e6/NCN), trading shuffle quality for cache footprint. You tune the NCN × block-size product against per-node SSD cache size — and you treat both NCN and shuffle_seed as part of run identity.

Megatron-Energon + WebDataset, and the legacy indexed mmap

If the stack is PyTorch/Megatron-Core rather than JAX, Megatron-Energon is the Grain-equivalent. It layers .nv-meta (dataset.yaml, split.yaml) over WebDataset tar shards and provides savable/restorable loader state, deterministic multi-dataset blending, built-in packing/grouping/joining, and native S3/GCS/Azure streaming (Megatron-Energon features). Its sequential-tar I/O is the most object-store-throughput-friendly format in the field. The catch: it is per-rank deterministic but does not offer Streaming's elastic-determinism across arbitrary world-size changes — so on Energon you pin your topology or accept a re-shuffle on resize.

Megatron-LM's indexed .bin/.idx mmap dataset is the legacy workhorse. IndexedDataset (.bin payload + .idx metadata) and GPTDataset build three cached .npy memmaps — a document index (Do_idx, shuffled), a sample index (Sa_idx, shape [N+1,2] giving sample bounds), and a shuffle index — and blend datasets via a compiled build_blending_indices in helpers.cpp (Megatron-Core datasets). It is deterministic, fast, and exerts zero object-store request pressure once staged — but it assumes the corpus is staged to local NVMe. At 10T tokens × 4 B = 40 TB, that is feasible as a per-rack-local stage but forces an explicit copy step and breaks the streaming/elastic story. It also has a known BlendedDataset IndexError in extreme many-component blends (issue #705) that bites exactly when you have hundreds of curriculum sources. For a 100k-GB200 streaming run, indexed-mmap is the wrong default; it is the right tool only if you can fully stage the corpus rack-local and never reshape.

tf.data / HF / Ray / Daft — know their lane

tf.data (+ tf.data service) pipelines beautifully but has weak fine-grained checkpoint/resume, and TFRecord's sequential format cannot do a true global shuffle — window-shuffle only. MaxText reflects this honestly: for tfrecord/parquet it falls back to a WindowShuffleIterDataset (the user's actual codebase, MaxText). HF IterableDataset shard-and-resume is improving but is not the tool for deterministic 100k-host training. And Daft / Ray Data are offline-prep engines, not training loaders — which is precisely their correct role. The proof point is Essential-Web v1.0: 24T tokens / 23.6B documents from CommonCrawl, processed in a 7-day window with "0 crashes" on a Daft pipeline sustaining 32,000 requests/s per VM (Essential AI / Daft case study). The pattern crystallizes the whole chapter: do the heavy global shuffle, dedup, and offline pre-tokenization in Daft/Ray/Spark, emit large ArrayRecord/WebDataset shards, and keep the training loader dumb, deterministic, and stateless.

04Tokenization: offline, always, at this scale

Online tokenization is malpractice at 100k hosts. It burns the exact host CPU the loader needs for shard decode and collation, and it puts a variable-latency, branchy CPU stage on the hot path — the textbook source of host stalls. HF's Rust tokenizers runs ~1 GB text / 20 s / core ≈ 12.5M tokens/s/core (HF tokenizers). A 10T-token corpus is ~40 TB of text ⇒ ~10⁶ core-seconds — a few thousand cores for a few hours, embarrassingly parallel inside the same Daft/Ray job that does dedup and shuffle. Tokenize once, store uint32 IDs in ArrayRecord/.bin, and the training loader becomes a pure index → fetch → collate function with no tokenizer in sight. The marginal cost of offline pre-tokenization is rounding error against the pretrain; the cost of online tokenization is a permanent MFU tax plus a non-reproducibility risk if the tokenizer version ever drifts.

Tokenize once, offline, in the prep job

~10⁶ core-seconds = a few thousand cores for a few hours — rounding error against the pretrain — done inside the same Daft/Ray job that dedups and shuffles. Store uint32 IDs and the loader becomes a pure index→fetch→collate with no tokenizer on the hot path and no version-drift reproducibility risk.

05Packing, masking, and the global-ordering problem

Packing. MaxText's MoE-grade path is greedy online packing (packing/sequence_packing.py) emitting inputs_segmentation / inputs_position, consumed by the TPU splash-attention kernel (GPU: flash-attention varlen) for intra-document masking with no cross-document attention; the c4_mlperf pipeline asserts packing is on (the user's actual codebase, MaxText, input_pipeline_interface.py). Two engineering notes:

  • Best-fit-decreasing bin-packing (sort documents descending, place each in the tightest-fitting bin) minimizes pad waste versus first-fit. Do it offline so the packing itself is deterministic and checkpointable — online greedy packing is order-dependent and complicates exact resume.
  • The splash-attention divisibility constraint is real and visible in the OLMo loader: q_seq_len must be divisible by 512, so the loader emits the full length L and masks the boundary position rather than producing L-1 (the user's actual codebase, MaxText — olmo_data_grain.py comments this explicitly). Get this wrong and the kernel silently rejects or mis-shapes batches.
The splash-attention 512-divisibility trap

q_seq_len % 512 == 0 is a hard kernel constraint. The OLMo loader emits the full length L and masks the boundary rather than emitting L-1. Violate it and splash-attention silently rejects or mis-shapes batches — a class of bug that surfaces as garbage loss, not a clean crash.

To mask cross-document attention or not. This is a genuine fork, not a default. The controlled study on sequence composition shows intra-document causal masking achieves lower perplexity than naive random packing or semantic grouping (arXiv:2402.13991). Yet DeepSeek-V3 trains on 14.8T tokens using document packing and deliberately does not apply cross-sample attention masking during pretraining — it applies it only during SFT — as an efficiency choice (DeepSeek-V3). The MaxText splash/flash-varlen path makes intra-doc masking cheap enough that for a 200B-active MoE I would take the perplexity win: the kernel cost is small relative to the MoE all-to-all, and the no-cross-doc-attention property removes a class of subtle long-context contamination. But the decision is workload-specific; if your packed sequences are mostly single long documents (high chunk_attn_window_size, long-context curriculum), the masking benefit shrinks and DeepSeek's choice is defensible.

Cross-document masking controlled study + V3

The perplexity win from intra-document masking is measured (arXiv:2402.13991), yet DeepSeek-V3 skips it in pretraining for efficiency. For a 200B-active MoE the kernel cost is small relative to the MoE all-to-all, so take the win — unless your curriculum is mostly single long documents, where the benefit shrinks and V3's choice is defensible.

True global shuffle at 10T tokens. A uniform global shuffle needs either ArrayRecord random-access (Grain's index stream) or an offline permutation materialized as an on-disk index (OLMo-core global_indices.npy). Both Streaming's py1e and Grain's index stream are bucketed approximations — they shuffle within canonical-node/shard buckets to preserve download locality — and that is the correct tradeoff, because a naive truly-global shuffle destroys object-store read locality and is unusable at this scale.

mem uint32 index overflows at 2³² ≈ 4.29B · 10T tok @ ~2k/seq ≈ 5B instances → use uint64 A 10T-token corpus packed to ~2k-token sequences is already past the uint32 line. Plan on uint64 or block-structured indices from day one.

Remember the integer-width trap: a uint32 index overflows at 4.29B instances; a 10T-token corpus packed to ~2k-token sequences is ~5B instances, already over the line, so plan on uint64 or block-structured indices from day one.

06The punch-list: what to fix at 100k hosts, concretely

Ordered by leverage. These map directly onto MaxText/Grain knobs you can set today.

  1. Don't have 12,500 hosts read GCS. Set expansion_factor_real_data > 1. Only jax.process_count() / factor hosts load real data; the rest receive batches over the device mesh via get_process_loading_real_data + PlaceHolderDataIterator in input_pipeline_interface.py (default in base.yml is -1.0 = off; the comment states each loading host then loads per_device_batch_size × factor). This cuts object-store request fanout by the factor directly and is the single highest-leverage knob (MaxText Grain data-input guide; the user's actual codebase, MaxText). At a factor of 8 you go from 12,500 readers to ~1,560 — back inside an HNS bucket's headroom.
  2. Colocate the loader. Set colocated_python_data_input=True (the RemoteIterator path in multihost_dataloading.py) so Grain runs on the accelerator host and shard decode + collation use host CPU/RAM with NVLink/ICI-adjacent transfer instead of PCIe round-trips, eliminating a class of host stalls (the user's actual codebase, MaxText; flagged "experimental, under testing" in base.yml).
  3. Choose a stateless or single-scalar resume path. Either ElasticIterator (single global-scalar state, process_0.json only, survives process_count change) or the OLMo initial_step sampler (zero serialized state). Avoid the per-host process_{i}-of-{N}.json path — it is N writes and N reads on every restart, a small-object storm against the checkpoint bucket at 12,500 hosts.
  4. Move the shuffle permutation to memmap. Adopt OLMo-core's build_and_save_global_indices to kill the ~5.8 GB/host/epoch rng.permutation allocation the MaxText OLMo loader explicitly warns about. Compute once, uint64, mmap-share across forked workers (olmo_data_grain.py).
  5. Provision storage for the request wall: HNS buckets (8× initial QPS) + ≥256 MB shards + thousands of name-hashed prefixes + jittered exponential-backoff ramp so the 12,500-host cold start and every post-preemption restart clears the 5,000-reads/s cliff instead of triggering a 429/503 storm (GCS request-rate; S3 performance).
  6. Pin shuffle identity. Treat data_shuffle_seed (and StreamingDataset's num_canonical_nodes if you use it) as part of run identity, frozen in config, so any elastic resize stays bit-deterministic. A reshard that silently re-seeds the shuffle is an undetectable correctness bug.
  7. Pre-tokenize and pre-pack offline (best-fit-decreasing, deterministic) so the training loader is a pure deterministic index→fetch→collate with no CPU-heavy stage on the critical path — the Essential-Web/Daft pattern of decoupling heavy offline work from a dumb online loader (Essential AI / Daft).
The single highest-leverage knob

expansion_factor_real_data ≈ 8 turns 12,500 GCS readers into ~1,560 by fanning batches out over the device mesh — a direct division of your object-store request rate, default off (-1.0) in base.yml. Set it before anything else.

07Recommendation for the 10T / 200B-A / 100k-GB200 target

Build the offline pipeline in Daft or Ray Data: dedup, global shuffle, pre-tokenize to uint32, best-fit-decreasing pack, and emit 256 MB–1 GB ArrayRecord shards across thousands of HNS-bucket prefixes. On the training side, run Grain with the OLMo-style stateless pure-function sampler backed by an on-disk uint64 global_indices memmap — zero per-host resume state, which is the property that makes restarts free at 12,500 hosts. Drive object-store fanout down with expansion_factor_real_data ≈ 8 and colocated_python_data_input=True. Use grain.IterDataset.mix for the curriculum so you can re-weight toward math/code/reasoning after LR decay without rebuilding the pipeline. Turn on intra-document masking via splash/flash-varlen (take the perplexity win; respect the q_seq_len % 512 == 0 constraint). Freeze data_shuffle_seed and your shard layout as run identity.

yaml — MaxText base.yml overrides for the 100k-host loader
dataset_type: olmo_grain            # stateless pure-function sampler
data_shuffle_seed: 1234             # FROZEN — part of run identity
expansion_factor_real_data: 8       # 12,500 readers -> ~1,560 (default -1.0 = off)
colocated_python_data_input: true   # RemoteIterator: Grain on the accelerator host
grain_num_threads: 16               # host-stall avoidance
grain_prefetch_buffer_size: 128     # overlap shard decode with compute
grain_use_elastic_iterator: true    # single global-scalar resume state
packing: true                       # best-fit-decreasing, offline & deterministic
# q_seq_len must satisfy q_seq_len % 512 == 0 for splash-attention

The success criterion is brutal and simple: the loss curve never knows the loader exists. No step ever waits on a GET; no restart ever triggers a 503 storm; every resume reproduces the exact stream as if the run was never interrupted. The dataloader earns its invisibility — which is the only photograph of it worth taking.

The meta-lesson

Decouple heavy offline work from a dumb online loader. Do the global shuffle, dedup, and pre-tokenization in Daft/Ray; emit large ArrayRecord shards across HNS prefixes; and keep the training loader a pure, stateless, deterministic index→fetch→collate. Every one of the five requirements is satisfied by where you put the work, not by a cleverer online loader.

Sources

  • MaxText OLMo Grain loader (stateless sampler, initial_step, repr trick, 5.8 GB defect): github.com/AI-Hypercomputer/maxtext · olmo_data_grain.py
  • OLMo-core data loading (build_and_save_global_indices memmap, VSL curriculum): olmo-core.readthedocs.io · data_loading
  • MaxText Grain data-input guide (expansion_factor_real_data): maxtext.readthedocs.io · data_input_grain
  • Grain + ArrayRecord / Riegeli global shuffle: developers.googleblog.com · Grain + ArrayRecord
  • MosaicML StreamingDataset shuffling (py1e, shuffle_block_size): docs.mosaicml.com · shuffling
  • MosaicML StreamingDataset elastic determinism: docs.mosaicml.com · elastic determinism
  • GCS request-rate & HNS guidelines: docs.cloud.google.com · storage request-rate
  • Amazon S3 performance / per-prefix limits: docs.aws.amazon.com · S3 optimizing-performance
  • Megatron-Energon (.nv-meta, packing, object-store streaming): docs.nvidia.com · megatron_energon
  • Megatron-Core indexed datasets (Do_idx/Sa_idx/shuffle index, build_blending_indices): docs.nvidia.com · megatron-core datasets
  • Essential AI / Daft (24T tokens, 32k req/s/VM, 0 crashes): eventual.ai · Essential-Web v1.0
  • Sequence composition / intra-document masking perplexity study: arxiv.org/pdf/2402.13991
  • DeepSeek-V3 (document packing, no pretraining cross-sample masking): arxiv.org/pdf/2412.19437
  • HuggingFace Rust tokenizers throughput: github.com/huggingface/tokenizers
  • NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 (dense-vs-sparse FLOP footnote): nvidia.com · GB200 NVL72

Chapter 15 · You cannot raise MTBF — collapse MTTR

Fault tolerance and reliability: keeping 100k GPUs training for months

A 10T-total / 200B-active MoE pre-trained to tens of trillions of tokens will hold 100,000 GB200 GPUs for two-to-four months and absorb thousands of interruptions along the way. The whole discipline reduces to one move: you cannot raise the mean time between failures, you can only collapse the mean time to recover — and convert hard-fails into soft-degrades.

~24 min read5 figuresgrounded in MaxText src/maxtext/common/checkpointing.py + Orbax EmergencyCheckpointManager

At 100,000 GB200 GPUs the question is no longer whether something will break during your run — it is whether each break costs you 30 seconds or 30 minutes, and whether you ever notice the breaks that don't announce themselves. A 10T-total / 200B-active MoE pre-trained to a few tens of trillions of tokens will occupy this fleet for somewhere between two and four months of wall-clock. Over that horizon the cluster will sustain thousands of hardware-or-software interruptions. The entire discipline of reliability engineering at this scale reduces to one ruthless reframing, which you should tattoo onto the run's dashboard: you cannot raise MTBF, you can only collapse MTTR and convert hard-fails into soft-degrades. Every technique in this chapter — multi-tier checkpointing, in-memory replication, elastic meshes, hot spares, NTP-style degraded tensor parallelism, microsecond network failover — is an MTTR-reduction or a fail-to-degrade conversion. None of them buys you a single extra minute of MTBF. Internalize that and the design space collapses into something tractable.

You cannot raise MTBF, you can only collapse MTTR and convert hard-fails into soft-degrades. Every primitive in this chapter is one of those two moves — nothing here prevents a single failure.
20–30min
design MTBF, 100k GB200
466faults
Llama 3 405B, 54-day snapshot, 16k H100
55→97%
goodput swing, naive vs sub-minute MTTR
3.5%
CheckFreq frequent-tier overhead bound

01The MTBF/MTTR arithmetic, and why MTTR is the only lever

The governing identity for a synchronous data-parallel job, once you are past the regime where checkpoint save overhead dominates, is simply goodput ≈ MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).

mem goodput ≈ MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) Once save overhead is amortized, every point of goodput is an MTTR story. At MTBF=30 min you need MTTR ≈ 20 s to clear 99%.

The load-bearing fact is that MTBF scales inversely with the device count. A synchronous collective fails the instant any single participant faults; if one GB200 (counting each Blackwell die as a GPU) has a mean time to interruption of T_node, an N-GPU job fails at rate N / T_node. There is no architecture, no scheduler, no compiler that escapes this — it is a property of the all-reduce barrier.

Calibrate against the best public data point. Meta's Llama 3 405B pre-training logged 466 interruptions over a 54-day snapshot on 16,384 H100s — 47 planned and 419 unexpected, roughly one unexpected fault every ~3 hours (Llama 3 Herd, §3.3.4). Linearly extrapolating the per-GPU interruption rate from 16,384 to 100,000 GPUs scales the fault rate by ~6.1×, which moves the mean inter-fault interval from ~3 hours to roughly 25–35 minutes. And that is the optimistic extrapolation, because it holds the per-GPU rate constant. Blackwell does not. Higher power density per package (a GB200 superchip dissipates ~2.7 kW), the immature copper NVL72 backplane with sub-optimal diagnostics as of 2025–26 (SemiAnalysis), and a larger SRAM/HBM footprint per die (more bits to flip) all push the per-GPU rate up. The honest planning assumption for a 100k GB200 run is a mean time between job-affecting interruptions in the tens-of-minutes regime — call it 20–30 minutes as your design MTBF.

Now feel what that does to goodput as a function of MTTR. A naive restart loop at this MTBF — re-queue the job, re-init the JAX distributed runtime and NCCL/collective state, reload a multi-TB checkpoint from object storage, replay the lost steps since the last save — easily costs 20–40 minutes and lands you at 50–60% goodput, i.e. you pay for 100k GPUs and effectively train on ~55k. Drive MTTR to one minute via in-memory checkpoint restore and a warm runtime and you recover ~97%.

MTBFMTTRRaw goodput MTBF/(MTBF+MTTR)
30 min30 min50%
30 min20 min60%
30 min10 min75%
30 min3 min91%
30 min1 min97%
30 min20 s99%
 At a 30-minute design MTBF, the entire goodput range from 50% to 99% is selected by MTTR alone.
The one delta to remember

55% vs 97% goodput is tens of millions of dollars of compute on a run this size, and it is entirely an MTTR story. This is why the rest of the chapter spends its budget on recovery latency, not failure prevention.

A second-order consequence the Daly/Young checkpoint-interval result makes precise: the optimal checkpoint interval is ≈ sqrt(2 · checkpoint_cost · MTBF). As MTBF shrinks toward 20 minutes, the optimal interval shrinks with its square root, which forces you to checkpoint frequently. You cannot checkpoint a multi-TB MoE state to GCS every few minutes. The only resolution is to decouple the cheap, frequent checkpoint (host RAM / peer RAM, seconds) from the expensive, durable one (object store, minutes, rare) — which is exactly the multi-tier hierarchy in §03.

mem checkpoint_intervalopt ≈ √( 2 · checkpoint_cost · MTBF ) Daly/Young. As MTBF → 20 min the optimal interval shrinks with √MTBF — forcing frequent saves that only a cheap in-memory tier can afford.

02Goodput, not MFU: measure badput or you are flying blind

MFU (fraction of dense peak FLOP/s a running step achieves) and goodput are orthogonal axes, and conflating them is the most common instrumentation failure at scale. A job can sustain a respectable 38% FP8 MFU on every step it runs and still bleed half its wall-clock to restarts, yielding an effective MFU of ~19%.

Quote MFU against dense peak, always

A GB200 NVL72 rack is 360 PFLOP/s dense FP8 / 180 dense BF16, not the 720/360 sparse marketing figures — NVIDIA's own page footnotes "Dense is one-half sparse spec shown." A 100k-GPU fleet is therefore ~0.45 EFLOP/s dense FP8 peak, ~4.5 PFLOP/s/GPU; the ~35–40% large-MoE FP8 MFU band of 2025–26 must be referenced against that denominator, not the sparse one (NVIDIA GB200 NVL72).

The right primary KPI is goodput, decomposed via an explicit badput taxonomy. Adopt Google's ML-Goodput-Measurement library — it is already wired into MaxText — and treat its categories as first-class dashboard rows.

Badput bucketWhat it capturesYour target at 100k
ACCELERATOR_INITIALIZATIONdevice/runtime bring-upamortized, near-zero steady-state
TRAINING_PREPcheckpoint load, optimizer/model creationonly on (re)start
PROGRAM_STARTUPJIT trace/compileavoid re-JIT on restart
DATA_LOADING_SYNCinput pipeline stalls<1%
CHECKPOINT_SAVEblocking save window<1% (async)
CHECKPOINT_RESTOREreload on recoverythe MTTR core
WASTED_PROGRESSsteps lost since last good checkpointbounded by save interval
INFRASTRUCTURE_RECOVERYrestart downtimethe other MTTR core
CUSTOM_BADPUT_EVENTSevals, elastic scale eventsattributed, not hidden
 Google ML-Goodput badput taxonomy, treated as first-class dashboard rows.

The library also reports step_time_deviation — step-time jitter — which is disproportionately important at 100k because the slowest of 100,000 ranks sets the step time. A single straggler on a thermally throttled node drags the synchronous barrier; the tail, not the mean, is your throughput. Meta observed exactly this: diurnal datacenter temperature swings drove 1–2% throughput variation as ambient temperature modulated DVFS across the fleet, plus synchronized power swings of tens of MW as the whole job stalled and resumed on checkpoint boundaries — a substation-level concern on a >150 MW plant that you must address with staggered restart / power smoothing (Llama 3 Herd).

Crucially, MaxText attributes elastic events rather than burying them: src/maxtext/utils/elastic_utils.py calls record_custom_badput_event_start_time / record_custom_badput_event_end_time around scale-down, scale-up, and an explicit elastic_reinitialization event, so a scale-up that costs you 90 seconds shows up as CUSTOM_BADPUT_EVENTS, not as mysterious WASTED_PROGRESS. The reliability bar is concrete: Llama 3 sustained >90% effective training time at 16k with only 3 of 466 events needing significant manual intervention (Llama 3 Herd). Holding >90% goodput at 100k requires WASTED_PROGRESS + INFRASTRUCTURE_RECOVERY to stay in the single-digit-percent range, which is only achievable if recovery is automated and sub-minute.

03Checkpointing: the multi-tier hierarchy is mandatory

A 10T-total MoE has multi-TB optimizer state. With AdamW you carry two moments per parameter; at BF16 master weights plus FP32 moments the optimizer-plus-weights footprint is on the order of 80–120 TB of sharded state across the fleet. Writing that to a remote object store every step is physically impossible, and reading it back on restart is the single largest term in a naive MTTR. The state of practice is a multi-tier scheme that decouples save frequency from durability: cheap copies often, durable copies rarely.

Tier 0 · async dev→host
pinned host RAM, then flush
Short blocking snapshot, background flush. OCDBT + Zarr3, ~2 GB files.
Tier 1 · in-memory peer
redundant replicas in DRAM
Gemini placement proof. Recover from a peer in seconds, near-zero MTTR.
Tier 2 · durable store
GCS/S3, rare & authoritative
Survives correlated multi-rack loss. Minutes to write/read, every N intervals.

Tier 0 — async device→host snapshot. Orbax (and DeepSpeed) snapshot device arrays into pinned host memory under a short synchronous blocking window, then flush to storage on a background thread while training continues. MaxText's save_checkpoint (src/maxtext/common/checkpointing.py) issues an ocp.args.PyTreeSave with OCDBT + Zarr3 and an explicit per-array chunk_byte_size, bounding file sizes for parallel I/O. The relevant config knobs are checkpoint_storage_use_ocdbt, a target_data_file_size of ~2 GB, and a checkpoint_storage_concurrent_gb ceiling (~96 GB) applied symmetrically to save and restore (save_concurrent_gb / restore_concurrent_gb). OCDBT's B-tree manifest is what makes a checkpoint with hundreds of thousands of expert shards openable without listing millions of objects.

Tier 1 — in-memory / peer / redundant. This is the near-zero-MTTR design and the most important tier for you. The canonical primary source is Gemini (SOSP '23, Wang et al.): checkpoint to the CPU DRAM of host machines — orders of magnitude more aggregate bandwidth than network storage — store redundant replicas across machines under a placement strategy that provably maximizes the probability that a surviving peer holds your shard after a failure, and use host-device traffic coordination to interleave the snapshot copy with training communication so it does not steal collective bandwidth. On recovery you pull weights from a peer's RAM in seconds rather than re-reading a multi-TB checkpoint from GCS over minutes. The placement-optimality proof is the non-obvious contribution and the reason Gemini, not a naive "copy to neighbor," is the design to copy.

Tier 2 — durable object store. The rare, authoritative copy in GCS/S3 that survives a correlated multi-rack loss or a datacenter-level event. Minutes to write, minutes to read, taken every N×(local interval).

Two pieces of supporting literature sharpen the tier design:

  • CheckFreq (FAST '21, Mohan et al.): two-phase checkpointing (snapshot to pinned/GPU memory, then persist) pipelined against compute, with adaptive rate tuning that algorithmically bounds runtime overhead to within 3.5% while cutting recovery from hours to seconds, plus a resumable data iterator so each sample is seen exactly once per epoch. The 3.5% overhead bound is the concrete budget your frequent-tier should respect.
  • ByteCheckpoint (ByteDance, arXiv 2407.20143): a parallelism-agnostic checkpoint representation enabling load-time resharding — resume on a different chip count or parallelism config without a separate offline conversion job. This is the missing capability for bringing a 100k job back on fewer GPUs after losing several racks, and for elastic scale events generally. It reports a 54.2× average reduction in checkpoint stall and up to 9.96× / 8.80× save/load speedups vs open-source baselines — a concrete benchmark target. Microsoft's Nebula (DeepSpeed/Azure) and Meta's internal tiered checkpointing are the other production points; all converge on the same three-tier shape: RAM peer copy (seconds) → local SSD/ramdisk (tens of seconds) → durable object store (minutes, rare).

04MaxText/Orbax emergency & multi-tier checkpointing, concretely

The reference implementation you will adapt lives in src/maxtext/common/checkpointing.py. Orbax exposes orbax.checkpoint.experimental.emergency.checkpoint_manager.CheckpointManager (aliased EmergencyCheckpointManager) and an EmergencyReplicatorCheckpointManager, both constructed with a two-tier options object.

src/maxtext/common/checkpointing.pyEmergencyCheckpointManagertwo-tier optionstrimmed
from orbax.checkpoint.experimental.emergency.checkpoint_manager import (
    CheckpointManager as EmergencyCheckpointManager,                 # [1]
)

mgr = EmergencyCheckpointManager(
    options=CheckpointManagerOptions(
        local=LocalCheckpointOptions(                                # [2]
            save_interval_steps=local_save_interval_steps),          # frequent, per-process dir
        persistent=PersistentCheckpointOptions(                      # [3]
            save_interval_steps=persistent_save_interval_steps),     # rare, flush to GCS
    ),
    ...,
)
# restore broadcasts ONE surviving replica across the replica mesh:
restore_args = ocp.type_handlers.SingleReplicaArrayHandler(...)      # [4]

1The emergency manager. Aliased from the experimental Orbax path; EmergencyReplicatorCheckpointManager is the replicator-backed sibling.

2Frequent local tier. LocalCheckpointOptions writes to a per-process local directory — on GPU the distributed process_id is appended so each process owns its slice.

3Rare durable tier. PersistentCheckpointOptions flushes rarely to GCS — the authoritative copy taken every N local intervals.

4Recover from a peer. SingleReplicaArrayHandler broadcasts one healthy replica's data across the replica axis — Gemini's "recover from a peer, not from storage," productionized.

The mechanism: LocalCheckpointOptions writes frequent checkpoints to a per-process local directory — on GPU the distributed process_id is appended to the path so each process owns its slice — while PersistentCheckpointOptions flushes rarely to GCS. The governing config keys in base.yml are enable_multi_tier_checkpointing, local_checkpoint_directory, local_checkpoint_period, and multi_tier_checkpointing_backup_interval_minutes; enable_emergency_checkpoint is the legacy flag now superseded by multi-tier checkpointing.

Restore is where the MTTR savings land. On recovery the EmergencyCheckpointManager reconstructs from the freshest available tier and broadcasts a single surviving replica across the replica mesh via ocp.type_handlers.SingleReplicaArrayHandler (gated by enable_single_replica_ckpt_restoring). A freshly swapped-in node therefore pulls its weights from a healthy replica's local store rather than re-reading GCS — exactly the Gemini "recover from a peer, not from storage" pattern, productionized. Because the array handler broadcasts one replica's data across the replica axis, you also avoid every replica independently hammering the object store.

Preemption handling. When enable_autocheckpoint is set, the save-decision policy composes a preemption policy with the fixed-interval policy:

python — MaxText autocheckpoint save-decision policy (preemption forces a save)
save_decision_policy = AnySavePolicy([
    PreemptionCheckpointingPolicy(),          # inbound preemption forces a save
    FixedIntervalPolicy(save_interval_steps),  # regular cadence otherwise
])
# train loop: drain the async save before exiting on a preemption
if checkpoint_manager.reached_preemption(step):
    checkpoint_manager.wait_until_finished()   # clean, resumable boundary

so an inbound preemption signal forces a save independent of the regular cadence. The training loop calls checkpoint_manager.reached_preemption(step) and, on a positive, drains the async save with wait_until_finished() before exiting — converting a preemption from lost-work into a clean, resumable boundary. Concretely in MaxText's maybe_save_checkpoint, the save fires when force_ckpt_save, or reached_preemption(actual_step), or (enable_emergency_checkpoint and step % local_checkpoint_period == 0), or (enable_autocheckpoint and reached_preemption(step)).

Failure path of the replication service. The in-memory tier itself can fail. replicator_error_handler(config) (called when the manager is an EmergencyCheckpointManager/EmergencyReplicatorCheckpointManager) polls replicator.errors / replicator.failed sentinel files, logs their contents, removes them, and escalates fatal replicator failures — so a sick replication daemon surfaces loudly instead of silently degrading your fastest recovery tier into a no-op.

A silent-degrade trap — watch the replicator

The fastest recovery tier can rot into a no-op without announcing itself: if the replication daemon is sick, your "in-memory restore" silently falls back to GCS and your MTTR quietly balloons. replicator_error_handler is what surfaces that — wire its sentinel polling into your run before you trust the peer tier.

Recommended cadence for the 10T run

Set the local tier every 5–15 steps (seconds of WASTED_PROGRESS, ~3.5%-class overhead per CheckFreq), the peer-replica tier every ~50–100 steps, and the durable GCS tier every ~30–60 minutes of wall-clock (multi_tier_checkpointing_backup_interval_minutes). Keep at least 3–5 generations of durable checkpoints retained, not for storage hygiene but because SDC rollback (§07) may require reverting past a silently corrupted save.

05Elastic training, hot spares, and fast restart

There are two restart philosophies, and a 100k run should run both simultaneously, each for the failure class it suits.

Hot spares (keep the mesh shape). Over-provision a few percent of nodes; on failure, swap a spare into the failed slot, preserving mesh shape, global batch, and the exact optimization trajectory. The cost is idle reserved GPUs — the explicit critique that SPARe-class work levels at the approach. For a 100k target, reserving ~2–3% (≈2,000–3,000 GPUs) is cheap insurance that keeps the common single-node failure entirely transparent: no reshard, no re-JIT, no batch-size change, identical training math. This is your default for the high-frequency, low-blast-radius failures (the ~58.7% GPU-issue bucket below).

Elastic (shrink the world, continue degraded). When you lose more capacity than your spare pool covers — several racks, a network plane, a power domain — shrink the live device set, re-derive the mesh, continue at reduced throughput, and scale back when capacity returns. MaxText implements this over Pathways (src/maxtext/utils/elastic_utils.py, pathwaysutils.elastic.manager.Manager): get_slices / active_slice_indices recompute the live device set (filtering jax.devices() by slice_index in elastic_manager.active_slice_indices), and maybe_elastic_scale_up(config, checkpoint_manager) re-admits recovered slices on checkpoint boundaries so a rejoin never tears a step. Incomplete checkpoints are GC'd after an elastic event so a half-written local checkpoint from a dying slice cannot poison restore.

The subtle correctness requirement under rescale is data-position invariance: if jax.process_count() changes, your input pipeline must not skip or repeat tokens. MaxText solves this with Grain's ElasticIterator, whose state is a single global scalar persisted to process_0.json (rather than per-process process_{i}-of-{n}.json files that would be invalidated by a process-count change). multihost_dataloading.py reads and writes that single global scalar, so the data cursor survives any rescale — the exact property that lets an elastic event resume exactly where it left off on a different world size. ByteCheckpoint-style load-time resharding (§03) is the complementary weight-side capability: together they let you bring the job back on a different chip count with neither a data-replay bug nor a checkpoint-conversion job.

The non-obvious rescale bug — data position

Per-process process_{i}-of-{n}.json iterator state is invalidated the instant process_count() changes, silently skipping or repeating tokens on every elastic event. Grain's ElasticIterator persists a single global scalar instead — that one design choice is what makes "resume exactly where it left off on a different world size" actually true.

Fast restart in either philosophy depends on two things the naive loop gets wrong: keeping the JAX distributed runtime and collective state warm across a recovery so you avoid full re-init and full re-JIT (PROGRAM_STARTUP badput), and restoring from the freshest in-memory tier rather than GCS. Get both right and a single-node failure under hot-spare swap is a sub-30-second soft event; a multi-rack loss under elastic rescale is a 1–3 minute event dominated by the rescale barrier and a peer-RAM reload, not a cold restart.

06The NVL72 blast radius: NTP and degraded tensor parallelism

The GB200 NVL72 reframes the failure-domain problem. A single GPU failure now jeopardizes a whole 72-GPU NVLink scale-up domain, because tensor-parallel groups live inside it. The sharpest quantitative statement is the Nonuniform Tensor Parallelism (NTP) paper (arXiv 2504.06095): for TP=64, as little as 0.1% of GPUs in a failed state drops fleet availability to 94%, because every failed GPU sterilizes its entire scale-up domain for tensor-parallel work — one dead die takes 72 GPUs offline for TP. SemiAnalysis independently flags the NVL72 copper backplane as a reliability weak point with immature diagnostics, reporting meaningful per-failure perf degradation via reconfiguration (SemiAnalysis).

One dead die sterilizes 72 GPUs for TP

At TP=64, just 0.1% of GPUs failed → 94% fleet availability, because each failed GPU takes its whole 72-GPU NVLink domain offline for tensor-parallel work. The NVL72 turns a single-die fault into a domain-scale blast radius — this is why TP sizing and EP-as-fault-boundary are reliability decisions, not just throughput ones.

NTP's mitigation is to run degraded, nonuniform TP groups: redistribute a failed GPU's shard across the survivors in its domain so the domain keeps running at reduced-but-nonzero throughput instead of going dark — graceful degradation rather than binary fail. The design implications for the 10T/200B MoE are direct and should shape your parallelism plan from the start:

  1. Keep TP modest. The bigger the TP group, the larger the blast radius per failed GPU. Prefer TP that fits comfortably inside a fraction of the NVL72 domain.
  2. Exploit expert parallelism as the natural fault boundary. Each expert is an independent computation. A lost expert-parallel rank degrades far fewer collectives than a lost TP rank, because the EP all-to-all touches only the routing of tokens, not a tightly-coupled matmul shard. In the user's actual codebase (MaxText), src/maxtext/layers/moe.py already exposes the levers that make this graceful: RoutedMoE with permute() / unpermute() / local_permute() / get_all_to_all_params() implementing the EP all-to-all, and crucially the dropless vs. memory tradeoff via ragged_buffer_factor and capacity_factor (where -1.0 provisions the dropless worst-case buffer). Under a degraded domain you can raise capacity tolerance to keep routing valid while a domain runs short-handed.
  3. CP-as-EP for context parallelism. MaxText's custom_mesh_and_rule (CP_AS_EP) lets context-parallel work ride the expert-parallel axis, keeping the high-bandwidth all-to-all inside the NVL72 domain while preserving the independent-shard fault property.
  4. Pair NTP degradation with the in-memory checkpoint tier. A domain loss then becomes a seconds-scale soft event — degrade the surviving 71 GPUs via NTP, pull the lost shard's state from a peer's RAM — rather than a minutes-scale cold restart of the whole NVLink domain.
The composite policy — three layers, three blast radii

Hot spares absorb single-GPU/single-node faults transparently; NTP keeps a wounded NVL72 domain producing while a spare is swapped in or a rebuild runs; elastic rescale handles capacity losses larger than the spare pool. Three layers, each tuned to a different blast radius.

07Silent data corruption: the failure that doesn't crash

SDCs are the uniquely dangerous failure class at 100k because they corrupt gradients, activations, or weights without any error signal, then propagate into the checkpoint and poison the run — you discover them days later as a loss curve that quietly diverged or plateaued, by which time every recent checkpoint is contaminated. Meta's foundational work — "Silent Data Corruptions at Scale" (Dixit et al., arXiv 2102.11245) and the SOSP '23 follow-up on a large production CPU population — quantifies device-level SDC prevalence (a non-trivial fraction of cores in a fleet exhibit data-dependent miscompute) and introduces the two-pronged detection strategy you should adopt: FleetScanner (out-of-production targeted testing — periodically pull suspect nodes and hammer them with deterministic test patterns) and Ripple (in-production opportunistic testing — interleave lightweight verification with real work). NVIDIA's OCP SDC-in-AI whitepaper covers the Blackwell-era hardware-detection direction.

Practical mitigations for the run, layered cheapest-first:

  • Cheap, always-on numerical guards. NaN/Inf detection plus gradient-norm spike guards. The user's actual codebase (MaxText) ships exactly this as skip_step_on_spikes() in src/maxtext/optimizers/optimizers.py: it maintains a rolling mean and Bessel-corrected (N−1) unbiased std over the last interval steps for both loss and gradient-norm, and zeroes the update when (loss − mean) > scaling_factor·std (and symmetrically for grad-norm), while preserving optimizer state and appending the spike to the buffer so a new regime can re-baseline. This is your first line against an SDC that manifests as a one-step numerical excursion — it neutralizes the bad step without rolling back. The companion z_loss (via z_loss_multiplier) regularizes logit magnitude and further suppresses the spiky regime SDCs love to create.
  • Periodic deterministic recompute / dual-run hashing. Sample a subset of collectives or a subset of layers and recompute them on a second device, comparing hashes. A mismatch with no hardware error is an SDC. Budget this at a small duty cycle (Ripple-style) so it costs <1% throughput.
  • Rollback depth. Keep enough durable checkpoint history (the 3–5 generations from §04) that when a divergence is finally diagnosed you can revert past the first contaminated save. This is the single reason not to aggressively GC old durable checkpoints.
python — MaxText skip_step_on_spikes (rolling-std gate, illustrative)
# rolling stats over the last `interval` steps, for loss AND grad-norm
mean = buf.mean()
std  = buf.std(ddof=1)                       # Bessel-corrected (N-1) unbiased std
is_spike = (value - mean) > scaling_factor * std
updates = jax.tree.map(lambda u: jnp.where(is_spike, 0.0, u), updates)  # zero the step
buf = roll_append(buf, value)                # keep optimizer state; re-baseline a new regime
The asymmetry to remember

Every other failure in this chapter is loud and self-announcing; SDC is silent and self-replicating. It deserves disproportionate instrumentation relative to its raw frequency.

08Straggler detection, hang detection, and flight recorders

At 100k the slowest rank sets the pace and a hung rank deadlocks the entire collective — a single stuck all-reduce can freeze the whole job until a watchdog fires. The production tool for diagnosing this is the PyTorch/NCCL Flight Recorder (c10d): a circular in-memory buffer (TORCH_NCCL_TRACE_BUFFER_SIZE ≈ 2000) logging the enqueue/start/end of every collective and p2p op with its process group, ranks, tensor sizes, and stack traces (PyTorch blog). On a watchdog timeout (TORCH_NCCL_DUMP_ON_TIMEOUT), every rank dumps its buffer over a side TCPStore channel — the clever bit being that even the hung rank dumps, because the dump path doesn't depend on the stuck collective — and an analyzer applies heuristics to localize the stuck collective and name the laggard rank. This turns a multi-hour "which of 100,000 ranks is wedged" investigation into minutes.

In the JAX/MaxText world you do not have the PyTorch c10d recorder, but you assemble the equivalent from three pieces:

  1. Coordination-service heartbeats. The JAX distributed runtime's coordination service detects a dead/unresponsive process and surfaces it; this is your hang detector.
  2. Step-time-deviation monitoring. The goodput library's step_time_deviation jitter metric is your straggler detector — a rank or slice that is consistently in the slow tail (e.g. from thermal throttling, a degraded NVLink, or a partial NIC failure) shows up as a persistent step-time outlier well before it fully fails.
  3. Orchestrator excision. On detection, the orchestrator kills and restarts the bad node (hot-spare swap) or elastically excises the bad slice (active_slice_indices), then restores from the freshest in-memory tier.
The detection→recovery loop must close in under a minute

The design pattern is uniform across stacks: watchdog detects the hang or straggler in seconds → recorder/jitter-metric localizes it → orchestrator excises and restarts → in-memory tier restores. The whole loop must close in well under a minute to keep INFRASTRUCTURE_RECOVERY in budget.

09Microsecond network failover: MRC turns link flaps into non-events

The network is a first-class failure source — in Llama 3's spectrum, network switch/cable faults were a named bucket, and at 100k the scale-out RDMA plane is enormous. The default failure mode of vanilla RoCEv2/Ethernet is brutal: a transient link flap that takes seconds of routing-protocol reconvergence will time out NCCL, which faults the collective, which restarts the job — minutes of MTTR for a flap that lasted milliseconds.

MRC (Multipath Reliable Connection), open-sourced May 2026 by OpenAI with AMD/Broadcom/Intel/Microsoft/NVIDIA via OCP and detailed in arXiv 2605.04333, is built precisely for this regime. It extends RoCEv2 Reliable Connection semantics but restricts the connection to one-sided write / write-with-immediate, so every data packet carries its RDMA virtual address and rkey and the receiving NIC can DMA each packet to its final location in any arrival order — eliminating the reorder buffer and enabling true packet spraying. Each QP rotates through 128–256 Entropy Values, each mapping via static SRv6 source routing to one physical path. On a path failure the NIC simply stops using the affected EV(s) in microseconds — one dead EV out of 256 costs ~0.4% of paths and is dropped without the collective ever observing a fault. PFC is disabled; loss is handled with SACK fast retransmit, ECN for load-balance signaling, and packet trimming.

256EV
entropy values / QP → SRv6 paths
0.4%
paths lost per dead EV — dropped in µs
770Gb/s
point-to-point, ConnectX-8
92GB/s
NCCL sustained at 42K GPUs

The relevance to this chapter is not the throughput (though that is excellent — ~770 Gb/s point-to-point on ConnectX-8, 92 GB/s NCCL sustained at 42K GPUs); it is tail-latency suppression. At 100k GPUs a step waits on the slowest all-to-all, so the p99.9 tail of fabric transfers — which on vanilla RoCE spikes to seconds on a single link flap — is your goodput. MRC converts that class of transient flap from an NCCL-timeout-triggered job restart into a sub-millisecond, training-transparent reroute. In MTBF/MTTR terms it does not raise MTBF, but it removes an entire category of fault from the synchronous collective's view, which is functionally a fail-to-non-event conversion — the highest-value move in the playbook. For the 100k GB200 buildout, run scale-out as an 800G spray fabric (MRC over Spectrum-X / Tomahawk-5) on disjoint, Acceleron-style network planes rather than a single-plane fabric, so that even a whole-plane failure reroutes instantly instead of fate-sharing the job.

Fail-to-non-event is the highest-value move

MRC doesn't add a minute of MTBF — it removes link flaps from the synchronous collective's field of view entirely. Removing a whole fault class is strictly better than recovering from it faster, which is why a spray fabric on disjoint planes is the network design for a run this size.

10Putting it together: the reliability stack for the 10T/200B run

Layer the defenses by blast radius and bind each to its recovery latency.

Failure classFrequency at 100kDefenseTarget MTTR
Transient link flapcontinuousMRC EV-blacklist, microsecond reroute~0 (non-event)
Single GPU / node fault (~59% are GPU issues)every ~20–30 minhot-spare swap, warm runtime, peer-RAM restore<30 s
NVL72 domain woundedper-domainNTP degraded TP + spare swapseconds (degraded)
Multi-rack / plane lossrareelastic rescale (Pathways) + ByteCheckpoint resharding + ElasticIterator1–3 min
Silent data corruptionrare, dangerousspike-skip guards, Ripple/FleetScanner sampling, rollback past contaminated checkpointsbounded by checkpoint history
Correlated / datacenter eventvery raredurable GCS tier, 3–5 generations retainedminutes
 The reliability stack, layered by blast radius. Each row is an MTTR-reduction or a fail-to-degrade conversion — none raise MTBF.

The arithmetic closes the chapter the way it opened it. With a design MTBF of ~25 minutes, you need a blended MTTR under ~90 seconds to hold goodput above 90% — and the only way to a 90-second blended MTTR is the hierarchy above: MRC removing flaps entirely, hot spares making the common GPU fault a sub-30-second event, NTP keeping wounded NVL72 domains producing, the Orbax EmergencyCheckpointManager restoring from peer RAM rather than GCS, and the spike guards plus checkpoint-history depth catching the silent failures that would otherwise poison the whole run. Llama 3 proved >90% goodput is achievable at 16k because automation absorbed all but 3 of 466 interruptions. At 100k GB200 the fault rate is ~6× higher and the NVL72 blast radius is larger, so the automation bar is correspondingly higher — but every primitive needed to clear it now exists in the open, and most of it is already wired into the user's actual codebase (MaxText).

The meta-lesson

Build the run around MTTR, instrument it with the badput taxonomy, and treat every fault as a soft-degrade. There is no other path to keeping 100,000 GPUs training for months.

Sources

  • Llama 3 Herd of Models (reliability §3.3.4: 466 interruptions, >90% goodput, failure spectrum, thermal/power effects): arxiv.org/abs/2407.21783
  • Llama 3 failure breakdown coverage (GPU ~58.7%, HBM3 ~17.2%): tomshardware.com — Llama 3 failure breakdown
  • Gemini: Fast Failure Recovery with In-Memory Checkpoints (SOSP '23): cs.rice.edu/~eugeneng/papers/SOSP23.pdf
  • CheckFreq (FAST '21, 3.5% overhead bound, adaptive rate): usenix.org/conference/fast21/presentation/mohan
  • ByteCheckpoint (parallelism-agnostic, load-time resharding, 54.2× stall reduction): arxiv.org/abs/2407.20143
  • Google ML-Goodput-Measurement (badput taxonomy, step_time_deviation): docs.cloud.google.com/tpu/docs/goodput
  • MaxText checkpointing reference (EmergencyCheckpointManager, multi-tier, autocheckpoint, replicator_error_handler): maxtext/src/maxtext/common/checkpointing.py
  • Nonuniform Tensor Parallelism (TP=64, 0.1% failed → 94% availability, degraded TP groups): arxiv.org/pdf/2504.06095
  • PyTorch/NCCL Flight Recorder (circular buffer, side-TCPStore dump, analyzer): pytorch.org/blog/flight-recorder
  • Meta "Silent Data Corruptions at Scale" + FleetScanner/Ripple: arxiv.org/abs/2102.11245
  • MRC (Multipath Reliable Connection), arXiv 2605.04333: arxiv.org/abs/2605.04333
  • OCP MRC 1.0 spec: opencompute.org/documents/ocp-mrc-1-0-pdf
  • SemiAnalysis GB200 NVL72 reliability (copper backplane, per-failure degradation, MTBI): newsletter.semianalysis.com — H100 vs GB200 NVL72
  • NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 (dense vs sparse FLOP/s, NVLink5): nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/gb200-nvl72

Chapter 16 · The stage that quietly makes the model

Midtraining, annealing, and long-context: where the checkpoint becomes your model

The bulk pre-train spends 80–90% of your FLOPs; midtraining decides what the checkpoint is actually good at. For a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200 GPUs, the 1–2T-token decay tail is where loss drops non-linearly and benchmark capability gets locked in — and the long-context extension is a systems problem that, done naively, wastes hundreds of billions of tokens and leaves 65% of your context-parallel ranks idle.

~24 min read5 figuresgrounded in WSD · OLMo 2 Dolmino · DeepSeek-V3 YaRN

The bulk pre-train is where you spend 80–90% of your FLOPs; midtraining is where you decide what the checkpoint is actually good at. This is the stage that converts a high-entropy next-token predictor over web sludge into a base model that does arithmetic, reads code, follows instruction-shaped documents, and attends across 128K tokens — and it does all of this before any chat template, reward model, or RL touches the weights. It is also the stage labs disclose least and confuse most: "midtraining," "annealing," and "continued pre-training" are used interchangeably and contradictorily across reports. For a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200 GPUs, getting this stage wrong is not a rounding error. The decay tail alone is 1–2T tokens — a low-LR phase where loss drops non-linearly and benchmark capability gets locked in — and the long-context extension is a systems problem that, done naively, wastes hundreds of billions of tokens and leaves 65% of your context-parallel ranks idle. This chapter treats midtraining, annealing, and long-context as one continuous design surface and gives the concrete recipe.

Midtraining is where a generic 200B-active predictor becomes your model — its math, code, reasoning, long-context, and agentic competence are all decided in the ~1–2T decay tokens plus the few-thousand-step extension, a single-digit percentage of total FLOPs.
5–10%
of FLOPs in mid-training (OLMo 2)
1–2T
decay-tail tokens (10–20%)
10×
fewer tokens — YaRN vs PI extension
65.75%
CP latency lost to slowest rank

01Definitions: where midtraining sits, and why the vocabulary is a minefield

The field has converged on a three-phase taxonomy, even where it disagrees on names (Mid-Training survey, 2510.06826).

PhaseLR regimeDataTokens (frontier scale)Output format touched?
Pre-trainingwarmup → stable peak (or cosine bulk)near-stationary, web-heavyDeepSeek-V3 14.8T; Qwen3 S1 ~30T; SmolLM2 ~11T; Olmo 3 up to 5.9Tno
Mid-trainingdecaying (WSD cooldown), lowshift to HQ/code/math/reasoning/instruction; extend context; inject skillsOLMo 2 explicitly "5–10% of training FLOPs"no chat tokens, no RL
Post-trainingSFT/DPO/GRPO/RLVRrole/turn structure + rewardsmallyes — turns, special tokens, reward
 The three-phase consensus. The boundary that matters: mid-training still uses LM loss over documents and still moves the base checkpoint.

The operational boundary that matters: mid-training still uses LM loss over documents and still moves the base checkpoint; post-training introduces role/turn structure and reward signals (Mid-Training survey). OLMo 2 is the cleanest operationalization — it explicitly names a "mid-training" second stage consuming 5–10% of training FLOPs, distinct from both pre- and post-training (OLMo 2, 2501.00656). The survey frames the phase as the bridge that shifts the model "from memorization toward abstraction."

Beware the vocabulary collisions, because they will trip you when reading recipes (Vintage Data, "What's the deal with mid-training"):

  • Llama 3 and MiniCPM fold the data-mix shift into the pre-training LR annealing tail and call it "annealing," not "mid-training" (Llama 3, 2407.21783; MiniCPM, 2404.06395).
  • OLMo 2 calls essentially the same data-quality anneal "mid-training" (OLMo 2).
