  • SmolLM3 uses "mid-training" to mean a 140B-token reasoning injection that is arguably early post-training (SmolLM3 blog).
Collapse the vocabulary into two axes

For the 10T/200B build, collapse "annealing," "mid-training," and "continued pre-training" into one engineering surface: a WSD-style decay tail whose data mixture and sequence length you control as two independent axes. Everything below is how to set those two axes.

02WSD and the decay phase: anneal on good data, because the cooldown cashes in

The mechanism originates with MiniCPM's Warmup-Stable-Decay (WSD) schedule, which replaces cosine with warmup → long stable plateau at peak LR → short decay to ≈0 (MiniCPM, 2404.06395). Two empirical findings from MiniCPM drive the entire modern practice:

  1. Loss drops sharply and non-linearly during the decay phase. The stable-phase loss is a poor estimate of final loss; the cooldown "cashes in" a large fraction of the model's final capability in the last ~10% of steps. This is why you cannot judge a run from its plateau.
  2. WSD decouples the decay from total length. Because the plateau is flat, you can branch a single stable checkpoint into multiple short decays with different data mixtures and sequence lengths. This is what makes "anneal-as-evaluation" cheap — and it is the structural reason midtraining is a separable stage rather than a tuning detail.

MiniCPM uses exponential annealing with T≈5000 steps (~20B tokens), decay ≈10% of training, and — the load-bearing move — changes the data mixture during decay to upsample high-quality + SFT-format data, mixing new data strictly during the decay phase (the knobs that govern this in a MaxText-shaped run are decay_fraction and decay_shape) (MiniCPM). The survey corpus reports decay length is typically 8–20% of steps, and that √-decay or "lowered-linear" (α≈0.7) decay shapes beat pure linear (Mid-Training survey).

Why "anneal on good data" beats spreading good data uniformly

The intuition: during the high-LR plateau the model is still reorganizing representations and partially forgets specifics; concentrating your scarce high-quality math/code/reasoning tokens into the low-LR tail means the model commits them to weights with minimal subsequent overwriting. This connects to the scaling-law finding that LR decay "wastes" the best data if it is fed too early — you want your premium tokens arriving precisely as the LR collapses. It is the single highest-leverage curriculum decision in the whole pipeline.

The canonical recipes, side by side

Lab / corpusDecay budgetHQ mixture during decaySouping?Key ablation
MiniCPM~10%, T≈5000 steps (~20B tok)upsample HQ + SFT-format—loss drops sharply in decay
Llama 3 405Bfinal ~40M tok, linear→0 at 128Kupsample very-high-quality sources—anneal-as-probe: +24.0% GSM8K, +6.4% MATH on 8B; negligible on 405B
OLMo 2 (Dolmino)50B/100B/300B stages~50% DCLM / ~50% other (math + StackExchange QA)yes (weight-avg)removing HQ data in anneal "significantly drops" benchmarks
Olmo 3100B integration annealDolma 3 Dolmino, microannealing-selectedyesparallel microanneals + 5 integration rounds
SmolLM2math 60B+40B; code 200Bmanual per-stage mixing-rate refinement—per-stage rate tuning beats fixed
Qwen3Reasoning Stage S2 ~5T @ 4096up-weight STEM/code/reasoning/synthetic—treated as mid-training-like up-weighting
 The decay budget clusters at 8–20% of steps; the data-mix shift to HQ math/code/reasoning is universal. Souping (weight-averaging survivors) is now standard at OLMo scale.

OLMo 2 / Dolmino is the most reproducible blueprint. Dolmino Mix 1124 draws from an 832.6B-token high-quality pool — DCLM-Baseline ~752B, FLAN (decontaminated) ~17B — plus a math mix (TuluMath ~230M, Dolmino SynthMath ~28.7M, GSM8K, MathCoder2, MetaMath) and StackExchange QA. Stage-2 mixtures of 50B / 100B / 300B tokens are sampled at roughly 50% DCLM / 50% everything-else (Dolmino Mix 1124). The 7B mid-trains 3×50B runs with different data orders; the 13B does 3×100B + 1×300B; the resulting checkpoints are model-souped (weight-averaged) (OLMo 2, 2501.00656). The load-bearing ablation: removing high-quality data during annealing significantly drops benchmark performance — the anneal is not optional polish, it is where the capability lives.

Olmo 3 refines this into a feedback loop worth copying wholesale. It uses microannealing: many candidate sources are ablated in parallel via lightweight short anneals, the winners are combined into a 100B-token integration anneal (Dolma 3 Dolmino Mix), and the whole thing runs over five rounds of integration tests (Olmo 3, 2512.13961). This is the clearest published operationalization of "anneal-as-evaluation" as a distributed data-selection harness, not a one-shot.

The scale caveat you must not ignore

Llama 3 repurposes annealing as a data-quality probe: anneal a 50%-trained 8B from peak LR to 0 over 40B tokens with 30% weight on a candidate dataset / 70% default mix, and read the benchmark delta — Meta calls this "more efficient than scaling-law experiments for every small dataset." On the 8B, annealing on small high-quality math sets delivered +24.0% GSM8K and +6.4% MATH. But the gains were negligible on the 405B, which already had the in-context capability (Llama 3, 2407.21783).

Anneal-as-probe sensitivity shrinks with scale — the #1 caveat for a 200B-active target

This is the single most important caveat for a 200B-active target. A 7B or 8B proxy used to rank candidate decay datasets will over-state the gains your 200B-active model will see — the larger model has already absorbed much of that capability during bulk pre-training. Use the proxy to rank candidate sources (the ordering largely transfers), but discount the magnitude of predicted gains heavily, and confirm the top few survivors with at least one anneal at a meaningfully larger proxy (e.g. a 30–70B-active config) before committing the full decay. This is corroborated independently by Apple's BETR finding that larger models require less aggressive filtering (BETR, 2507.12466) — the whole selection apparatus must be scale-adapted.

Decay recipe for 10T/200B

  • Budget the decay tail at ~10–20% of tokens (≈1–2T). Toward the lower end given that anneal value softens at this scale; reserve the upper end if long-context extension is folded in.
  • Use √-decay or lowered-linear (α≈0.7), not pure linear; decay to ~0 (or to the floor you will hold during RoPE extension — see §04).
  • Upsample math / code / reasoning / instruction-formatted data, with FIM injected here (§06). Decontaminate against the full eval suite (13-gram floor + embedding/semantic checks).
  • Run several short anneals from one stable checkpoint with different data orders/mixtures, then soup the survivors (weight-average). Souping buys robustness for free and is now standard at OLMo/Olmo scale.
  • A non-obvious alternative worth piloting: WSM argues the decay phase is equivalent to a form of checkpoint merging and proposes eliminating the explicit decay by merging stable-phase checkpoints instead (WSM, 2507.17634). At 10T scale, where every checkpoint is expensive, the merge-instead-of-decay reframing interacts directly with souping and with continued-pretraining branching; treat it as a high-value ablation, not yet a default unproven at 10T.

In a MaxText-shaped config the WSD tail is just a learning-rate schedule plus a mixture swap on the dataloader — the two independent axes from §01. The decay floor you stop at is the same floor you will hold through the YaRN extension (§04), so it is worth wiring as a named constant rather than letting cosine drive it to zero.

yaml — MaxText WSD decay tail (illustrative; learning_rate_schedule + decay mixture)
# --- learning-rate schedule: warmup -> stable plateau -> sqrt decay to floor ---
learning_rate: 3.0e-4                 # stable-plateau peak LR
warmup_steps_fraction: 0.03
learning_rate_schedule_steps: -1      # = total steps; -1 uses config total
cosine_learning_rate_final_fraction: 0.0
# WSD: hold peak across the plateau, then decay only the tail
decay_fraction: 0.15                  # ~10-20% of steps in the cooldown
decay_shape: "sqrt"                   # sqrt or lowered-linear (alpha~0.7), not pure linear
decay_end_lr: 7.3e-6                  # FLOOR: held through RoPE/YaRN extension, do NOT hit 0

# --- decay-phase data mixture (mix new HQ data strictly during decay) ---
data_mixture:
  decay_phase:
    dclm_web_hq:        0.50          # ~50% DCLM / 50% everything-else (Dolmino-style)
    math_synth:         0.18          # TuluMath / SynthMath / MetaMath / MathCoder2
    code_fim:           0.16          # FIM injected at document level, PSM format
    reasoning_cot:      0.10
    instruction_agentic:0.06          # function-call / ReAct traces, NO chat special tokens

03Long-context extension via RoPE rescaling: the method lineage

Every current recipe extends a short-context base via RoPE frequency rescaling rather than training long from scratch — because attention is quadratic and long documents are scarce and low-quality. You train the bulk and the decay at 4K–8K (DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3) and then extend. The method lineage:

MethodCore ideaLimitation it fixes
Position Interpolation (PI)linearly compress positions; scale θ by 1/sextrapolation explodes; PI is stable but blurs all frequencies
NTK-awarescale the RoPE base, spreading interpolation pressure across dimsPI crushes high-frequency (local) channels
NTK-by-partsinterpolate low-freq dims, extrapolate high-freq dimsuniform scaling over- or under-corrects per dimension
YaRNNTK-by-parts + attention temperature √t = 0.1·ln(s)+1 on logitsrecovers attention-distribution entropy at long ctx
LongRoPEevolutionary search over non-uniform per-dim rescale factors>2M-token extension where closed-form scaling fails
 The RoPE-rescaling lineage. Each row fixes the failure mode of the row above.

YaRN is the workhorse (YaRN, 2309.00071). Its two contributions: (1) NTK-by-parts interpolation that interpolates low-frequency dimensions and extrapolates high-frequency ones, and (2) a temperature/attention-scaling factor √t = 0.1·ln(s)+1 applied to the attention logits (the attention_factor in a YaRN rope_scaling block), which recovers the entropy of the attention distribution at long context. The payoff is efficiency: YaRN needs ~10× fewer tokens and 2.5× fewer steps than PI-style fine-tuning, and it was published at ICLR 2024. For a 100k-GPU run, that token efficiency is the difference between a 40B-token extension and an 800B-token one.

attn √t = 0.1 · ln(s) + 1  ·  θi rescaled by NTK-by-parts (interp low-freq, extrap high-freq) s = extension factor (e.g. 40). The attention-temperature term recovers the entropy of the attention distribution at long context — the difference YaRN adds over plain NTK-by-parts.

04The reference MoE long-context recipe: DeepSeek-V3, and the alternatives

DeepSeek-V3 is the safest template for a sparse model, because it is the only fully-disclosed MoE long-context recipe and it is conspicuously conservative. Two YaRN stages of 1000 steps each, 4K → 32K → 128K, with YaRN s=40, α=1, β=32 and the √t=0.1·ln(s)+1 attention scale (DeepSeek-V3, 2412.19437):

StageSeq lenBatchStepsLR
132K192010007.3×10⁻⁶
2128K48010007.3×10⁻⁶
 DeepSeek-V3's two-stage YaRN extension. The LR is held at 7.3×10⁻⁶ — exactly the final pre-training LR — for both stages. You do not re-warm.

The two details that make this the recommended MoE template: the LR is held at 7.3×10⁻⁶ for both stages — exactly the final pre-training LR — and each stage is only 1000 steps. You do not re-warm. Holding the LR at the decay floor avoids the loss-spike and router-destabilization risk of re-warming a fully-annealed sparse model, where re-warming can scramble expert-routing balance that took trillions of tokens to settle. For a 200B-active MoE, router stability through extension is a first-order concern that dense recipes simply do not have, and DeepSeek's constant-low-LR two-stage approach is the de-risked default.

Do not re-warm a fully-annealed MoE

Hold the LR at the decay floor (~7×10⁻⁶) through both YaRN stages. Re-warming a collapsed-LR sparse model risks a loss spike and scrambles expert-routing balance that took trillions of tokens to settle — a failure mode dense models never see. Constant-low-LR, two stages, ~1000 steps each.

The alternatives, and when to reach for them:

  • Qwen2.5 / Qwen3 combine RoPE ABF (base 10,000 → 1,000,000), YaRN, and Dual Chunk Attention (DCA). DCA remaps relative positions into intra-/inter-/successive-chunk patterns so no pairwise distance exceeds the pre-train length, enabling 4× extrapolation at inference; Qwen2.5-Turbo hits 100% 1M-token passkey with RoPE base 10,000,000 + YaRN + DCA (Qwen3, 2505.09388; Qwen2.5-Turbo, 2501.15383). Reach for ABF+DCA if your inference-time target length materially exceeds your training length and you want to amortize extension cost.
  • SmolLM3 removes RoPE from every 4th layer (NoPE), trains the decay mixture at 64K with raised RoPE θ, then YaRN-extrapolates to 128K (SmolLM3 blog). NoPE is an architectural bet to make at design time, not retrofittable.
  • LongRoPE for true >2M-token via evolutionary per-dimension search — relevant only if you are chasing million-token contexts (§06).

05Progressive sequence schedules, length mixing, and context parallelism

Extension is staged, not one-shot, for two reasons: attention cost is quadratic, and short-context skills regress if you jump too far too fast. The consistent guard across every disclosed recipe is length-distribution mixing — always retain a large fraction of short documents at each stage.

LabScheduleLength mix per stageGate to advance
Llama 3 405B8K → 128K in 6 stages over ~800B tok—short-ctx evals fully recover and NIAH solved
Qwen2.5-Turbo32K → 64K → 128K → 256K40% at current max, 60% shorterpasskey at length
Qwen3 long-ctx32K seq len75% in 16,384–32,768 tok, 25% in 4,096–16,384—
Olmo 3extend to ~65K, 600B-tok "Longmino" pool34% long / 66% shortYaRN on full-attention layers only
 The numbers cluster tightly: keep 40–66% short data at each long stage. NIAH and passkey are the universal acceptance tests.

The numbers cluster tightly: keep 40–66% short data at each long stage. Llama 3's discipline is the model to emulate — advance to the next length only when (a) short-context evals fully recover and (b) needle-in-a-haystack (NIAH) is solved at the current length (Llama 3, 2407.21783). NIAH and passkey are the universal acceptance tests; they gate each stage. Olmo 3 adds two refinements worth adopting: apply YaRN only to full-attention layers (leave sliding-window layers untouched), and GZIP-compressibility filtering on the long pool, dropping the top and bottom 20% to remove both boilerplate-repetitive and near-random documents (Olmo 3, 2512.13961).

Context parallelism is the systems enabler — and its pathology will bite at 100k GPUs

At 128K tokens, the sequence does not fit in one device's activation budget; you shard it across a context-parallel (CP) group. The non-obvious lesson from the Llama 3 systems paper (distinct from the model card, and rarely cited alongside it) is the choice of CP algorithm and the load-balancing fix (Scaling Llama 3 Training, ISCA'25):

  • Llama 3 uses an all-gather-based pass-KV scheme — all-gather the K/V tensors, then compute local-query attention — rather than ring attention. The reason is decisive for any real pretraining corpus: pass-KV tolerates irregular document masks (intra-document attention only). Ring attention's pipelined structure fights document-boundary masking.
  • The cost: with document masks, 65.75% of CP exposed latency is spent waiting on the slowest rank, because causal + document-mask work is wildly unbalanced across the sequence (early positions attend to little; late positions attend to much). Separately, CP collective exposure is ~7.64% of step time. The slowest-rank wait is the killer.
  • The mitigation, and you must implement it: load-balanced sharding. Split the sequence into 2×CP chunks and give rank i both chunk i and chunk (2·CP − 1 − i). Pairing an early (cheap) chunk with a late (expensive) chunk equalizes the per-rank attention work, collapsing the slowest-rank tax.

Two systems mandates fall out of this for the 10T/200B plan. First, document packing with proper block-diagonal / intra-document masking is mandatory so packed sequences do not leak attention across document boundaries — and this masking interacts directly with CP load balance, because the mask determines the per-position cost the 2×CP sharding must equalize. Second, on a 100k-GB200 fabric the CP group should be sized to fit within high-bandwidth NVLink domains (the NVL72 rack) where possible, since pass-KV all-gathers K/V every step; pushing CP across the slower scale-out network turns the ~7.64% collective exposure into something far worse. Reserve CP for the long-context stages only — there is no reason to pay it during the 4K–8K bulk.

CP discipline: pass-KV, 2×CP balance, NVLink-domain sizing

Use all-gather pass-KV (not ring) so CP tolerates intra-document masks. Implement 2×CP load-balanced sharding (rank i ← chunks i and 2·CP−1−i). Keep the CP group inside one NVL72 NVLink domain since pass-KV all-gathers K/V every step. And turn CP on only for the long stages — never pay it during the 4K–8K bulk.

06Synthetic long-context data and capability injection

Synthetic long data, because real long documents are scarce and bad

Real documents at 128K+ are rare and low-quality, so synthesis dominates the long stages. Llama 3 found naive long-SFT regressed short capabilities and built synthetic data around three use-cases (Llama 3, 2407.21783):

  1. (Multi-turn) QA over long contexts.
  2. Hierarchical long-document summarization — summarize 8K chunks with the 8K model, then summarize the summaries, and train on the full document. This bootstraps long-doc competence from a short-context model.
  3. Reasoning over code repositories — pack and reason across repo-scale code.

The contrarian budgeting result is ProLong: data engineering beats token budget. Continuing Llama-3-8B on 20B@64K then 20B@512K beats Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on most long-context tasks using ~5% of the long-context budget (40B vs 800B tokens), while emphasizing two things — retain short data and pack code repositories (ProLong, ACL 2025; arXiv 2410.02660). For the 10T plan this means: do not brute-force the extension with a giant long-context budget. A well-engineered 40–80B-token long mixture, gated by NIAH/passkey, will outperform a poorly-curated 800B one. Spend the saved tokens on the decay-phase capability mix instead.

ProLong: ~40–80B well-engineered beats 800B brute-forced

Data engineering beats token budget at long context. A curated 40–80B-token long mixture — retaining short data, packing code repos, gated on NIAH/passkey — outperforms a poorly-curated 800B one. Spend the ~760B saved tokens on the decay-phase capability mix instead.

For true million-token recipes, the pattern is stepwise RoPE scaling with document concatenation — e.g. 180K → 350K → 650K → 1M, concatenating 4/8/12 docs, with per-length sample counts shrinking from 2000 to 200 as length grows (Hierarchical synthetic data to 1M, 2504.12637). Sample counts shrink because each long example is enormously expensive and the marginal capability per example flattens.

Capability injection: midtraining is the cheapest place to add discrete skills

Because the decay phase commits data to weights with minimal overwriting, it is the optimal point to inject targeted capabilities — without introducing chat special tokens, preserving the base format for downstream post-training.

  • Code fill-in-the-middle (FIM). DeepSeek-Coder applies FIM at rate 0.5 in PSM (Prefix-Suffix-Middle) format with sentinel tokens; FIM rates of 50–90% show no left-to-right degradation while enabling infilling (DeepSeek-Coder, 2401.14196). Inject FIM during the decay/code anneal, at the document level after packing, with sentinels reserved in the vocab.
  • Math / reasoning. Up-weight synthetic chain-of-thought (MetaMath, MathCoder2, TuluMath) as Qwen3 S2 and OLMo 2 Dolmino do. This is where the GSM8K/MATH gains in §02 come from.
  • Multilingual. SmolLM3 injects 6 native + 3 additional languages during the staged curriculum (SmolLM3 blog).
  • Tool-use / agentic traces. Increasingly seeded in midtraining as instruction-like documents (function-call traces, ReAct-style transcripts) before formal post-training. Olmo 3 explicitly includes "instruction and thinking data" in midtraining while avoiding special tokens to preserve the base format (Olmo 3, 2512.13961). This is a meaningful 2026 shift emerging: agentic capability is being moved earlier, into the base, rather than left entirely to post-training.

The FIM transform is a document-level rewrite applied after packing, with three sentinels reserved in the vocabulary. The annotated card below is the canonical PSM construction — the one knob that matters is the rate (0.5), and the one rule that matters is "apply at the document level, not the token level."

data/transforms/fim.pyDeepSeek-Coder PSMrate 0.5illustrative
FIM_PRE, FIM_SUF, FIM_MID = "<|fim_begin|>", "<|fim_hole|>", "<|fim_end|>"   # [1]

def fim_transform(doc_tokens, rng, rate=0.5):                  # [2]
    if rng.random() >= rate:
        return doc_tokens                                     # left-to-right, unchanged
    n = len(doc_tokens)
    i, j = sorted(rng.integers(0, n, size=2))                 # two random cut points
    prefix, middle, suffix = doc_tokens[:i], doc_tokens[i:j], doc_tokens[j:]
    # PSM: Prefix-Suffix-Middle ordering, sentinels reserved in vocab   # [3]
    return [FIM_PRE] + prefix + [FIM_SUF] + suffix + [FIM_MID] + middle  # [4]

1Sentinels reserved in vocab. The three FIM tokens are added to the tokenizer up front — they are not chat special tokens, so the base format stays clean for post-training.

2Rate 0.5. DeepSeek-Coder applies FIM at 0.5; rates of 50–90% show no left-to-right degradation while enabling infilling.

3Document level, after packing. Apply the transform per document inside the packed sequence — not per token — so infilling spans coherent code.

4PSM ordering. Prefix → Suffix → Middle: the model reads both ends, then predicts the hole. This is the format that enables editor-style infilling at inference.

Continued pre-training rides the same machinery

Domain adaptation and continued pre-training are not a separate pipeline — they are the WSD branch pattern reused. Branch from a stable-phase checkpoint and run a short decay over domain data, exactly the MiniCPM "mix new data strictly during decay" pattern (MiniCPM, 2404.06395). The reason to branch from the stable checkpoint rather than re-warming a fully-annealed one is the loss-spike and (for MoE) router-rebalancing risk of re-warming a collapsed-LR model. Keep your last stable-plateau checkpoint as the canonical fork point for every downstream domain variant; this is also why the plateau checkpoint, not the final souped model, is the asset to preserve.

The stable-plateau checkpoint is the asset to preserve

Because WSD decouples the decay from total length, the stable-plateau checkpoint — not the final souped model — is the canonical fork point for every downstream decay, domain variant, and continued-pretraining branch. Re-warming a fully-annealed model risks a loss spike and (for MoE) scrambled router balance. Preserve the plateau.

07The integrated midtraining pipeline for 10T/200B on 100k GB200

Pulling the stage together into one executable plan. Bulk pre-train consumes ~8–8.5T tokens at stable peak LR, 4K–8K context. Then:

Step 1 — Decay tail (≈1–2T tokens, 10–20%). √-decay or lowered-linear (α≈0.7) from peak to the floor. Upsample math/code/reasoning/instruction; inject FIM at rate 0.5 PSM; seed agentic/tool traces as instruction-like docs without special tokens. Decontaminate against the full eval suite. Run several short anneals from one stable checkpoint via a microannealing harness (Olmo 3 style), validate with anneal-as-probe deltas, and soup the survivors. Discount the proxy: rank candidate datasets on a 7–8B proxy but verify the top survivors at a 30–70B-active config, because the 405B-scale insensitivity means your small proxy over-states gains for the 200B target.

Step 2 — Staged RoPE/YaRN extension at constant low LR. Use the DeepSeek-V3 MoE template: hold LR at the decay floor (~7×10⁻⁶), two YaRN stages of ~1000 steps each, s=40, α=1, β=32, √t attention scale, 32K then 128K. Do not re-warm — protect router balance. Retain 40–66% short data per stage. Gate each stage on full short-context recovery and NIAH/passkey. Apply YaRN only to full-attention layers if you use a sliding-window/full hybrid.

Step 3 — Long-context systems. CP with all-gather pass-KV (not ring) to tolerate document masks; 2×CP load-balanced sharding (rank i gets chunks i and 2·CP−1−i) to kill the 65.75% slowest-rank tax; block-diagonal intra-document masking on packed sequences; keep CP groups inside NVLink domains.

Step 4 — Long synthetic data. Hierarchical-summary, multi-doc QA, and repo-reasoning data (Llama 3 use-cases). Keep the long budget small and well-engineered (ProLong: ~40–80B beats 800B). If chasing >256K, switch to ABF+DCA (Qwen) or LongRoPE with stepwise concatenation and shrinking per-length sample counts.

The YaRN extension config itself is small — the two things that matter are that the LR floor is held (no re-warm) and that the scaling parameters match DeepSeek-V3's de-risked defaults. In a MaxText-shaped hybrid you set rope_type: yarn with the rescale applied only on the full-attention layers, and learning_rate_warmup_steps: 0 to guarantee no re-warm:

yaml — staged YaRN extension at constant floor LR (DeepSeek-V3 template)
rope_type: "yarn"
rope_scaling:
  factor: 40                 # s = 40  (4K base -> 128K target across two stages)
  alpha: 1                   # beta_fast analog
  beta: 32                   # beta_slow analog
  attention_factor: null     # null => use sqrt(t) = 0.1*ln(s)+1 (YaRN default)
  apply_to: "full_attention_layers_only"   # leave sliding-window layers untouched

extension_stages:
  - {seq_len: 32768,  global_batch: 1920, steps: 1000, learning_rate: 7.3e-6}
  - {seq_len: 131072, global_batch: 480,  steps: 1000, learning_rate: 7.3e-6}

learning_rate_warmup_steps: 0   # CRITICAL: do NOT re-warm a fully-annealed MoE
short_data_fraction: 0.50       # retain 40-66% short docs per stage (anti-regression)
advance_gate: ["short_ctx_recovered", "niah_solved", "passkey_at_length"]

The strategic point. Midtraining is where a generic 200B-active predictor becomes your model — its math, code, reasoning, long-context, and agentic competence are all decided in the ~1–2T decay tokens plus the few-thousand-step extension, a single-digit percentage of total FLOPs. The asymmetry is the whole reason to take it seriously: the bulk pre-train is a commodity you can mostly buy with compute; the midtraining recipe is where the differentiation, and the failure modes, actually live. Preserve the stable-plateau checkpoint, run the microannealing harness, soup, extend conservatively, and gate ruthlessly on NIAH.

The executable summary

Preserve the stable-plateau checkpoint. Run the microannealing harness and soup the survivors. Anneal scarce HQ math/code/reasoning/instruction into a √-decay tail of ≈1–2T tokens, discounting small-proxy gains. Extend conservatively with two constant-low-LR YaRN stages (no re-warm). Run pass-KV CP with 2×CP load balance inside NVLink domains. Gate every stage ruthlessly on NIAH and passkey.

Sources

  • Mid-Training of LLMs: A Survey (2510.06826)
  • OLMo 2 (2501.00656)
  • Dolmino Mix 1124 dataset card
  • Olmo 3 / Dolma 3 technical report (2512.13961)
  • MiniCPM / WSD (2404.06395)
  • Llama 3 (2407.21783)
  • Scaling Llama 3 Training, ISCA'25 systems paper
  • DeepSeek-V3 (2412.19437)
  • YaRN (2309.00071)
  • Qwen3 (2505.09388)
  • Qwen2.5-Turbo (2501.15383)
  • SmolLM2 (2502.02737)
  • SmolLM3 blog
  • ProLong (2410.02660 / ACL 2025)
  • DeepSeek-Coder FIM (2401.14196)
  • Hierarchical synthetic data to 1M tokens (2504.12637)
  • WSM: decay-free LR via checkpoint merging (2507.17634)
  • Apple BETR (2507.12466)
  • What's the deal with mid-training? (Vintage Data)

Chapter 17 · The most expensive free telemetry you'll ever get

The Chinese frontier: DeepSeek, Qwen, Kimi, MiniMax, GLM, Step, and what open weights reveal about systems

For a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200s, the Chinese open-weight labs are not a competitive curiosity — they are your only public ground truth for trillion-scale MoE systems engineering. Western closed labs treat the load-bearing details as moat; DeepSeek, Moonshot, Alibaba, MiniMax, StepFun, Zhipu, Baidu, and Tencent publish them. This chapter reads that telemetry as your pre-run ablation set.

~28 min read6 figuresgrounded in the open V3/V4, Kimi K2, Qwen3-Next, MiniMax, Step-3, CloudMatrix reports

For anyone planning a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200s, the Chinese open-weight labs are not a competitive curiosity — they are your only public ground truth for trillion-scale MoE systems engineering. Western closed labs treat the operationally load-bearing details — optimizer choice and the exact interventions that keep it stable, the FP8/FP4 block-scaling recipe, the pipeline-and-expert-parallel layout, the per-token KV-cache budget, the decode economics in dollars-per-million-tokens — as moat. DeepSeek, Moonshot (Kimi), Alibaba (Qwen), MiniMax, StepFun, Zhipu (GLM), Baidu (Ernie), and Tencent (Hunyuan) publish them. The result is a continuous stream of high-resolution telemetry about how a frontier-class sparse model actually trains and serves.

The single most important meta-lesson, and the one to internalize before reading any of these reports as a recipe: every one of these artifacts is export-control-shaped. Their architecture and systems choices are downstream of two binding constraints — throttled interconnect (the H800's NVLink and cross-node IB are cut relative to the H100; the H20 is FLOP-starved) and constrained compute (H20, Ascend 910C). A large fraction of their hardest-won engineering — DualPipe's bidirectional pipeline overlap, the obsessive communication–computation interleaving, the deliberately narrow expert-parallel degrees — exists to route around an interconnect deficit you will not have. On GB200 NVL72, 72 GPUs share a single flat NVLink5 domain at ~130 TB/s aggregate; the all-to-all that DeepSeek and Kimi contort their schedules to hide is, inside a rack, nearly free. So discount every "we trained on N H800s" number as a constrained-cluster config. What transfers is not their plumbing but the direction of their bets: sparser MoE, cheaper attention, lower precision, and Muon-family optimizers. Those bets are the leading indicator of where the frontier is going, and they are validated at scales — 1T, 1.6T total parameters — that no Western lab has documented publicly.

The Chinese frontier has, in effect, run your ablations in public. Discount their plumbing as export-control artifacts; bank the direction of their bets — sparser MoE, cheaper attention, FP4-in-the-loop, and Muon-family optimizers — as the most expensive free telemetry you will ever get.
1.6T
DeepSeek-V4-Pro total params, 49B active
0spikes
Kimi K2: 15.5T tokens, MuonClip, zero loss spikes
10%
V4-Pro KV cache vs V3.2 at 1M context
$0.055/Mtok
Step-3 decode at 8K vs V3's $0.068

01DeepSeek V3 → V3.2 → V4: the precision-and-attention ratchet

DeepSeek's three-generation trajectory is the cleanest signal in the entire literature, because it isolates one variable per generation against a fixed, well-documented baseline.

V3 (671B total / 37B active, 256 routed + 1 shared expert, MLA, 2048 H800, 14.8T tokens) set the template that the whole field now copies (arXiv:2412.19437). The three durable contributions: (1) FP8 training in the loop with fine-grained block scaling — 1×128 tile scaling on activations, 128×128 block scaling on weights, with periodic high-precision accumulation to dodge the H800 tensor core's ~14-bit accumulation truncation. (2) Aux-loss-free load balancing: instead of an auxiliary balance loss that fights the language-modeling objective, a per-expert bias term is added to the routing logits and adjusted by a simple controller (nudge the bias down for overloaded experts, up for starved ones) so that balance is enforced without injecting gradient noise into the experts themselves. (3) DualPipe, a bidirectional pipeline schedule that runs forward and backward micro-batches from both ends simultaneously to overlap the expert-parallel all-to-all behind compute — pure interconnect-deficit engineering. For your GB200 build, item (2) is the keeper and transfers directly; item (1) is superseded by Blackwell's native microscaling (below); item (3) is largely an H800 artifact.

The one V3 contribution that transfers unchanged

Aux-loss-free balancing — a per-expert bias nudged by a controller, applied to routing-logit selection only — is the keeper. FP8 block-scaling is superseded by Blackwell microscaling; DualPipe is an H800 interconnect artifact. Carry the balancer forward; treat the rest as historical context.

The fourth V3 contribution, often underweighted, is MLA (Multi-head Latent Attention) itself — the KV-cache compression that every later DeepSeek and Kimi model inherits. MLA projects keys and values down to a single shared low-rank latent (per-token cache on the order of a few hundred values rather than per-head K and V), then up-projects per head at attention time, with a decoupled RoPE component carried separately so positional information survives the compression. The practical effect is an order-of-magnitude smaller KV cache than full multi-head attention at near-parity quality, which is what makes a 37B-active model serviceable at long context on memory-constrained hardware. MLA is also the reason QK-Clip (§02) needs its careful RoPE/non-RoPE decomposition: the shared decoupled RoPE key must be left untouched during clipping or it contaminates every head. For your build, MLA is the baseline attention you sparsify on top of — the V3→V3.2→V4 arc is MLA first, then a cheap-selection or compression layer bolted onto it. Do not treat MLA and the cheap-attention mechanism as competing choices; they compose.

MLA and cheap-attention compose — they do not compete

The V3→V3.2→V4 arc is MLA first, then a sparse-selection or compression layer bolted on. Treat MLA as the baseline you sparsify on top of, not an either/or against DSA, CSA/HCA, or a linear hybrid.

V3.2-Exp: the under-read pivot that makes V4 legible

V3.2-Exp (Dec 2025) is the under-read pivot that makes V4 legible (arXiv:2512.02556). It introduces DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA): dense attention is replaced by a lightning indexer — a lightweight 64-head, ReLU-gated dot-product scorer running in FP8 — that scores all KV positions and selects the top-k = 2048 for each query, so core attention drops from O(L²) to O(Lk). The operationally brilliant part is the bootstrap. Rather than training the indexer from scratch (it has no supervision signal of its own), DeepSeek freezes the existing dense V3.1 model and trains the indexer for only 1000 steps / 2.1B tokens to imitate the frozen model's own attention distribution — KL-matching the indexer's selection against where dense attention already attends. Then the full model is adapted with the sparse indexer live. The payoff is a roughly 3–6× long-context cost reduction at 128K with negligible quality loss. This 1000-step distillation is the cheapest known way to retrofit sparse attention onto any existing dense checkpoint, and it is the direct methodological ancestor of everything in V4. If you train a dense-attention proxy first and want to sparsify before the main run, this is the template.

The cheapest known retrofit of sparse attention

Freeze your dense checkpoint, KL-match a lightweight indexer against its own attention distribution for ~1000 steps / 2.1B tokens, then adapt the full model with the sparse selector live. ~3–6× long-context cost cut at 128K, negligible quality loss. If you train a dense proxy first, this is the sparsification path.

V4: the attention bet compounded, plus two systems firsts

V4 (report ~April 2026) compounds the attention bet aggressively and adds two systems firsts that bear directly on a GB200 build (arXiv:2606.19348). The lineup: V4-Pro at 1.6T total / 49B active and V4-Flash at 284B / 13B active, both with 1M-token context, trained on 32–33T tokens. Attention is now a hybrid interleave of two compression schemes:

SchemeMechanismSparse selection?Role
CSA (Compressed Sparse Attention)Compress every m KV tokens into one learned entry, then top-k select among compressed entriesYesRetrieval-preserving layers
HCA (Heavily Compressed Attention)Compression factor m′ ≫ m, no selection — read the whole compressed sequenceNoCheap global-context layers
 Interleaving CSA and HCA spends the KV budget where retrieval matters and starves it where it does not. Source: DeepSeek-V4.

Interleaving CSA and HCA lets the model spend its KV budget where retrieval matters and starve it where it does not. The headline numbers are the ones to quote in any 1M-context serving plan: at 1M context, V4-Pro needs 27% of the single-token inference FLOPs and 10% of the KV cache of V3.2; V4-Flash hits 10% FLOPs and 7% cache (arXiv:2606.19348). That is the difference between a 1M-context 200B-active model being economically serviceable and not.

Three V4 systems details matter most for the 10T run:

  1. FP4 QAT in the loop, at 1.6T scale. V4 applies quantization-aware training in FP4 to the routed-expert weights — running FP4×FP8 matmuls — and pushes FP4 onto the indexer's QK path as well (HF V4-Pro discussion). This is the first public existence proof that a trillion-scale model can be trained with FP4 in the optimization loop rather than quantized post-hoc. It is the single most important data point for your GB200 plan, because Blackwell's native NVFP4 microscaling (E2M1 elements with E4M3 per-block scale factors) is exactly the format this validates. The lesson: NVFP4 on the experts is a trainable recipe at 1.6T, not merely an inference trick — but note it is applied to the routed experts and the indexer, not indiscriminately, and QAT (not naive cast) is doing the work.
  2. Muon at 1.6T. V4 deploys the Muon optimizer at the largest scale ever publicly confirmed, with a hybrid Newton–Schulz iteration (8 convergence + 2 stabilization steps) for the orthogonalization and a custom hybrid-ZeRO bucketing strategy: dense parameters are partitioned via a knapsack assignment across ranks, while the MoE parameters are optimized without ZeRO partition constraints (the experts are already sharded by expert parallelism, so re-sharding their optimizer state is counterproductive) (HF V4-Pro discussion). This is the first confirmation that Muon survives past the trillion-parameter regime — the open question Kimi K2 left at 1.04T is now answered at 1.6T.
  3. mHC — manifold-constrained hyper-connections. The residual stream is widened with parallel mixing paths (hyper-connections), but the per-layer mixing matrices are constrained to be doubly stochastic via Sinkhorn–Knopp projection (t_max = 20 iterations), guaranteeing spectral norm ≤ 1 so each composite layer is non-expansive and the residual neither explodes nor collapses with depth. The overhead is held to ~6.7% of the 1F1B pipeline-stage time (arXiv:2606.19348). Two further quieter changes: MoE gating moves from Sigmoid to Sqrt(Softplus), and the earliest transformer blocks use hash routing (deterministic token→expert assignment) rather than learned routing — a cheap way to stabilize the early layers where learned routing is noisiest.

The strategic tell is in the kernels. DeepSeek explicitly validated its fine-grained EP / MegaMoE fused kernel — reporting a 1.50–1.73× inference speedup (up to 1.96× on latency) — on both NVIDIA GPUs and Huawei Ascend NPUs (arXiv:2606.19348). Whether V4 was trained substantially on Ascend is unconfirmed rumor and should be treated as such (The Register); what is stated is that the serving kernel is dual-stack. That is the strongest public signal yet that a frontier lab is deliberately engineering for a future where it is not NVIDIA-locked — a hedge you should read as a forecast.

Keep the epistemic tiers straight on V4 kernel dual-stack: stated Ascend training: rumor

What the V4 report states: the MegaMoE serving kernel is validated on NVIDIA and Ascend. What is rumored and must be flagged as such: that V4 was substantially trained on Ascend (The Register). Quote the dual-stack serving signal; do not repeat the training rumor as fact.

Other V4 systems facts relevant to your kernel choices:

  • Sqrt(Softplus(·)) affinity replaces V3's sigmoid gating — a small change with quality motivation, trivial to implement.
  • Hash routing for the first layers (deterministic, no aux loss), which MaxText already gates correctly (is_hash_routing turns off lb-loss).
  • FP4 QAT on expert weights plus FP4 on the indexer QK path — the first trillion-scale model trained with FP4 in the loop, directly relevant to GB200's native NVFP4 microscaling.
  • TileLang DSL for the kernels (sub-µs host launchers, Z3-checked indexing, bitwise-reproducible IEEE-754 intrinsics).
  • A pull-based all-to-all (each GPU reads remote activations rather than pushing) with FP32 local-sum reduce-scatter for gradients.

02Kimi K2 / K2-Thinking: optimizer stability is the real disclosure

Kimi K2 (Moonshot) is, for systems purposes, the single most valuable optimizer report in the literature (arXiv:2507.20534). The model: 1.04T total / 32.6B active, 384 experts (top-8 + 1 shared), 61 layers, MLA with only 64 attention heads (versus V3's 128 — a deliberate halving to cut attention cost), trained on 15.5T tokens. The disclosure that matters is how they ran Muon to completion without a single loss spike.

MuonClip = Muon + QK-Clip. The canonical late-emergent failure of Muon at scale is runaway attention logits: Muon's Newton–Schulz orthogonalization equalizes the update RMS across all singular directions, which is precisely the dynamic that drives the product of the Wq and Wk singular values upward until the pre-softmax logit blows past 1000 and the run diverges. QK-Clip attacks this at the weight level, post-update. The exact mechanism, worth replicating verbatim:

  • Per head h, track the max attention logit over the batch: Smaxh = (1/√d) · max(Q·Kᵀ).
  • When Smaxh exceeds threshold τ = 100, compute γh = min(1, τ / Smaxh) and rescale the query/key projection weights (not the activations) by √γh, applying the scaling to the RoPE components appropriately.
  • Only heads that exceed τ are touched; it is per-head and conditional.

This bounded logit growth let Moonshot pretrain 15.5T tokens with zero loss spikes (arXiv:2507.20534). Their Figure 2 is the money chart: logits saturate at 100, hold there, and then decay after roughly 30% of training, after which QK-Clip is essentially inactive — meaning it is a transient stabilizer for the dangerous early-to-mid regime, not a permanent tax.

muonclip.pyQK-Clip, per-head post-updateτ = 100illustrative
def qk_clip(Wq, Wk, q, k, tau=100.0, d_head=128):           # [1]
    # logits per head over the batch, scaled by 1/sqrt(d)
    s_max = (q @ k.transpose(-1, -2)).amax(dim=(0, 1, -2, -1))  # [H]
    s_max = s_max / (d_head ** 0.5)                          # [2]
    gamma = torch.clamp(tau / s_max, max=1.0)               # min(1, tau/s_max)
    scale = gamma.sqrt()                                    # [3]
    touched = s_max > tau                                   # only hot heads
    Wq[touched] *= scale[touched, None, None]               # rescale WEIGHTS
    Wk[touched] *= scale[touched, None, None]               # not activations  [4]
    return Wq, Wk

1Runs post-update. QK-Clip fires after the Muon step, not inside the gradient — it is a weight-level correction, not a loss term.

2The 1/√d scaling. Smax is the pre-softmax logit magnitude; τ=100 is the ceiling Kimi's Figure 2 shows logits saturating against, then decaying past ~30% of training.

3√γ on both projections. Splitting the rescale across Wq and Wk halves the logit product back under τ; apply it to the RoPE components carefully (MLA's decoupled RoPE key must be handled separately).

4Per-head, conditional. Only heads exceeding τ are touched, so unaffected heads carry zero overhead — the transient-stabilizer property.

The full recipe is disclosed and reproducible:

HyperparameterValue
ScheduleWSD (warmup-stable-decay)
Warmup500 steps
Stable LR2e-4 constant for first 10T tokens
Decaycosine to 2e-5 over next 5.5T tokens
Final annealto 7e-6
Weight decay0.1
Global batch67M tokens
ParallelismPP16 + EP16 + ZeRO-1
Schedule overlapinterleaved 1F1B with EP all-to-all overlap
Memoryselective recompute (LayerNorm, SwiGLU, MLA up-proj, MoE down-proj) + CPU activation offload
 The fully-disclosed Kimi K2 pretraining recipe (arXiv:2507.20534). WSD with a 10T-token stable plateau.

The deliberately narrow EP16 is a transferable judgment call, not just an H800 artifact: Moonshot chose a narrower expert-parallel degree than DeepSeek specifically to cut inference-time all-to-all overhead, trading a little training-time balance flexibility for cheaper serving. On a flat NVL72 domain you can afford wider EP at train time, but the principle — pick EP for the serving regime, not just training — stands.

Pick EP for the serving regime, not just training

Kimi's EP16 is narrower than DeepSeek's by design — to cut inference-time all-to-all, trading some train-time balance flexibility for cheaper serving. On a flat NVL72 you can run wider EP at train time, but the judgment call — let the serving all-to-all set your EP degree — transfers.

K2-Thinking: INT4 honesty and cheap data wins

K2-Thinking (Nov 2025) is the post-training and inference-economics complement (HF Kimi-K2-Thinking). It applies native INT4 weight-only QAT to the MoE in post-training, dropping the served model from >1TB to ~594GB, roughly 2× decode speed, and — the part that matters for honesty — all benchmarks are reported at INT4, not at full precision with an INT4 footnote. It sustains 200–300 sequential tool calls in agentic loops at that precision. The widely-cited ~$4.6M training cost is a secondary-source estimate whose derivation is not in the primary report and should be treated as a rumor, not a number to plan against (RecodeChina).

Two numbers, two epistemic tiers INT4 benchmarks: stated $4.6M cost: secondary

K2-Thinking benchmarks are reported at INT4 — set your honesty bar there and report served-precision numbers, not a full-precision flex. The ~$4.6M training-cost figure is a secondary-source estimate with no primary derivation (RecodeChina); do not plan against it.

Finally, Kimi's data-rephrasing ablation is a scale-independent data-efficiency lesson worth banking: on SimpleQA, raw re-epoching gave 23.76% (10 epochs of the same text), one rephrasing run 10× gave 27.39%, and 10 distinct rephrasings × 1 epoch gave 28.94% (arXiv:2507.20534). Diverse rephrasing beats raw re-epoching by ~5 points absolute. For a 10T-token-class data budget where you will inevitably re-use high-value sources, the implication is direct: spend compute generating diverse paraphrases of your highest-value corpus rather than naively repeating it.

03Qwen3 / Qwen3-Next: hybrid linear attention goes mainstream

Qwen3 (Alibaba) matters first for its unified thinking/non-thinking budget mechanism — a single model that toggles chain-of-thought depth on a controllable token budget — and its strong-to-weak distillation pipeline that produces the smaller checkpoints (arXiv:2505.09388). But the architecturally radical member is Qwen3-Next (80B-A3B, ~27:1 sparsity), and it is the clearest public statement that hybrid linear attention is now production-grade.

Qwen3-Next is a 3:1 hybrid: 75% of the layers are Gated DeltaNet — a linear-attention variant combining the delta rule (an error-correcting update to the recurrent state) with exponential gating, a causal Conv1D, and L2-normalized Q/K — and the remaining 25% are full GQA + RoPE softmax attention. The 25% softmax fraction is not decoration: it is what preserves needle-in-a-haystack retrieval and long chain-of-thought recall that pure linear attention loses (qwen.ai blog). The results are the kind of efficiency claim that reorders a roadmap: trained at <10% of the GPU-hours of dense Qwen3-32B, with >10× decode throughput beyond 32K context, plus native MTP (multi-token prediction) for built-in speculative decoding. Qwen3-Coder pushes the same family to 480B-A35B.

The load-bearing detail that secondary coverage omits: in Qwen's own in-context-learning ablations, Gated DeltaNet beat both sliding-window attention and Mamba2 (01.me analysis). And the transferable claim is sharper still — it is the ratio (75/25), not the specific linear mechanism, that is the load-bearing design choice. Whatever cheap-attention primitive you pick, keeping a ~25% softmax backbone is what buys back the retrieval the linear layers cost you.

It is the ratio, not the mechanism

Across Qwen3-Next (3:1 Gated-DeltaNet/softmax) and MiniMax-01 (7:1 lightning/softmax), the load-bearing choice is the fraction of softmax layers, not the specific linear primitive. Keep a ~25%–12.5% softmax backbone and it buys back the needle-in-a-haystack retrieval the linear layers cost you.

04MiniMax: lightning attention at production scale and cheap RL

MiniMax-01 (456B / 45.9B active) is the largest production deployment of linear attention, built on a strict 7:1 lightning-attention : softmax block ratio, trained at 1M-token context and serving up to 4M (arXiv:2506.13585). Lightning attention is a chunked, IO-aware linear-attention kernel; the 7:1 ratio is MiniMax's answer to the same retrieval-vs-cost tradeoff Qwen3-Next solves at 3:1 — MiniMax bets harder on the linear side.

MiniMax-M1 is the more instructive report for anyone budgeting reasoning RL. It scaled M1 to RL on long-horizon reasoning using only 512 H800 GPUs for 3 weeks — roughly $534,700 — and introduced CISPO (Clipped Importance-Sampling Policy Optimization), which clips the importance-sampling weights rather than performing token-level ratio updates as GRPO/DAPO do (arXiv:2506.13585). On long generations, CISPO is cleaner because the lightning-attention FLOP advantage compounds over the long rollouts that dominate reasoning RL — the longer the trajectory, the more the linear-attention savings pay off, which is exactly where token-level methods get expensive. As a concrete, cheap reproduction target for long-horizon reasoning RL on a sparse model, ~$535K on 512 H800 is a number worth holding onto.

A concrete cheap-RL reproduction target

MiniMax-M1's long-horizon reasoning RL ran on 512 H800 × 3 weeks ≈ $534,700 with CISPO (clip the importance-sampling weights, not token-level ratios). Lightning attention's FLOP edge compounds over the long rollouts that dominate reasoning RL — hold this number as your budget anchor for sparse-model reasoning RL.

05Step-3: the inference-economics report

StepFun's Step-3 (321B) is the most important inference-economics report in the corpus, and the only one that frames attention design as an explicit arithmetic-intensity matching problem (arXiv:2507.19427). Two ideas drive it.

MFA (Multi-Matrix Factorization Attention). Step-3 uses 64 query heads × 256-dim with a single shared KV head and a low-rank query projection (7168 → 2048 → 64×256). The design target is an arithmetic intensity of 128 FLOPs/byte, deliberately placed between MLA's ~512 (compute-heavy, and mismatched to the memory-bandwidth-bound decode regime of most hardware) and GQA's ~32 (memory-bound). The insight is that the right attention design is the one whose arithmetic intensity matches the roofline of the hardware you decode on — too high and you waste bandwidth, too low and you waste FLOPs.

Concretely: decode is dominated by the single-token attention step, which reads the entire KV cache once per token. If your attention's arithmetic intensity sits above the hardware's FLOP-to-bandwidth ratio (the roofline ridge), you are compute-bound and leaving memory bandwidth idle; below it, you are bandwidth-bound and leaving tensor cores idle. MLA's ~512 is compute-heavy precisely because its up-projection does a lot of FLOPs per cached byte — fine on a FLOP-rich/bandwidth-poor part, wasteful on a bandwidth-rich one like the H20. GQA's ~32 is the opposite. Step-3's MFA at 128 is a deliberate compromise that decodes efficiently across a range of rooflines, which is exactly what AFD then exploits by placing attention on whichever tier its 128 intensity best fits.

For a GB200 decode plan, compute your own target: B200 dense FP8 is ~4.5 PFLOP/s against HBM3e bandwidth in the multi-TB/s range, and you want your attention's intensity to land near that ridge — not borrow MLA's 512 uncritically.

Compute your own arithmetic-intensity target — don't borrow MLA's 512

The right attention design is the one whose arithmetic intensity matches the roofline of the hardware you decode on. For GB200, take B200 dense FP8 ≈ 4.5 PFLOP/s against multi-TB/s HBM3e and land your attention's intensity near that ridge. MLA's ~512 is compute-heavy and wasteful on a bandwidth-rich part.

AFD (Attention-FFN Disaggregation). Step-3 runs the attention and FFN stages on different hardware tiers entirely: attention on whatever serves it cheaply (H800, H20, A800, 910B, L20), FFN where MoE sparsity economics work out. The sparsity thresholds they publish are available nowhere else and are directly actionable: H800 needs ≥5.8% MoE sparsity to hit high FFN throughput, while the bandwidth-rich-but-FLOP-poor H20 tolerates 0.7% (arXiv:2507.19427). The token transport between tiers is handled by StepMesh, a zero-SM, GPU-Direct-RDMA library operating under a 272µs/layer budget across 61 layers — zero-SM meaning it steals no streaming multiprocessors from compute.

The headline economics: $0.055 per million tokens at 8K context, versus DeepSeek-V3's $0.068, at 4,039 tokens/GPU/s versus 2,324, with the advantage widening to 39% cheaper at 32K (arXiv:2507.19427). For a 200B-active GB200 decode deployment, AFD plus arithmetic-intensity-matched attention is the template: NVL72 makes the inter-tier RDMA Step-3 needs nearly free inside a rack, so the disaggregation that StepFun does across heterogeneous boxes you can do across GPU roles inside one flat domain.

$0.055/Mtok
Step-3 at 8K vs V3's $0.068
4039tok/GPU/s
vs DeepSeek-V3's 2324
272µs/layer
StepMesh zero-SM transport budget
128F/B
MFA arithmetic-intensity target

06GLM, Ernie, Hunyuan, and the rest of the design space

Zhipu GLM-4.5 / 4.6 (355B / 32B active; GLM-4.5-Air at 106B / 12B) uses loss-free sigmoid-gate balance routing, a 200K context, and is agentic-RL-native — trained from the start for tool-use and multi-step agent loops rather than retrofitted (arXiv:2508.06471). It is the cleanest mid-size open data point for the "agentic from scratch" post-training posture that your reasoning/tool-use targets will require.

Baidu Ernie 4.5 (424B / 47B active) is the most instructive multimodal-MoE systems report (Ernie technical report). It uses heterogeneous multimodal MoE with modality-isolated routing — text and vision tokens route to disjoint expert pools — stabilized by a router-orthogonalization loss plus a multimodal token-balanced loss. The systems result: 47% MFU via intra-node expert parallelism, FP8 mixed precision, and fine-grained recompute. If your 10T run is multimodal, modality-isolated routing is the cheap structural win that prevents one modality's tokens from starving the other's experts; the orthogonality loss is the regularizer that keeps the pools genuinely disjoint.

Tencent Hunyuan-Large (389B / 52B active) and Hunyuan-A13B (80B / 13B, 64 + 1 experts) round out the fine-grained-MoE design space, confirming the field-wide convergence on many small experts plus a shared expert (arXiv:2411.02265). The shared-expert pattern — one always-on expert capturing common computation so the routed experts specialize — now appears in essentially every frontier MoE here (DeepSeek, Kimi, Hunyuan, Qwen), and you should treat it as settled default rather than an open choice.

The convergent, no-longer-contested defaults

Across all of them: fine-grained experts (256–384), a shared expert, aux-loss-free or loss-free balance routing, MLA or a factorized-attention variant for KV economy, and an increasingly aggressive cheap-attention layer. None of these is contested anymore. The contested frontier is precision (FP4 vs FP8), optimizer (Muon vs AdamW), and the cheap-attention mechanism (sparse-indexer vs compression vs linear-hybrid).

07Hardware under export controls: the Ascend / CloudMatrix telemetry

Even as an NVIDIA-only shop, the Huawei CloudMatrix384 papers are essential reading, because CloudMatrix384's interconnect is the structural twin of the GB200 NVL72 you are buying (arXiv:2506.12708, arXiv:2508.02520). The machine: 384 Ascend 910C accelerators + 192 Kunpeng CPUs, joined by a Unified Bus (UB) delivering >390 GB/s per NPU unidirectional, with <3% inter-node bandwidth degradation and <1µs added latency — i.e., the whole 384-NPU supernode behaves as a single flat all-to-all domain, exactly as NVL72's 72 GPUs do across NVLink5.

This is why the serving answer converges across both stacks. CloudMatrix-Infer does peer-to-peer disaggregated prefill / decode / cache with EP320 (320-way expert parallelism), hitting 6,688 tokens/s/NPU prefill and 1,943 tokens/s/NPU decode at <50ms TPOT, holding 538 tokens/s/NPU under a strict 15ms SLA, with INT8 maintaining accuracy (arXiv:2506.12708). The strategic signal: a flat UB-supernode flattens the EP all-to-all problem the same way NVL72 does, which is precisely why large-EP MoE serving — DeepSeek's EP144, CloudMatrix's EP320 — is the convergent answer on both hardware lineages. When the interconnect is flat, the cost of wide expert parallelism collapses, and wide EP is what makes a 200B-active model cheap to decode. Your NVL72 rack is your CloudMatrix384; plan for EP that fills the domain.

Your NVL72 rack is your CloudMatrix384

A flat interconnect — UB supernode or NVLink5 domain — collapses the cost of wide expert parallelism. Wide EP (DeepSeek's EP144, CloudMatrix's EP320) is what makes a 200B-active model cheap to decode. Plan for EP that fills the NVLink domain.

The dual-stack convergence also reframes a risk. DeepSeek shipping a serving kernel validated on both NVIDIA and Ascend, and Huawei publishing a worked DeepSeek-class serving stack, together mean the software moat around NVIDIA serving is thinner than the hardware moat. For a 100k-GB200 buyer that is reassuring on supply (the architectural patterns are portable) and sobering on differentiation (so are your competitors').

08Synthesis: what the open reports reveal that closed labs hide, and what transfers

Before the directives, the corpus at a glance — the configs that constitute your public ablation set:

ModelTotal / ActiveExpertsAttentionOptimizerPrecision (train)Sparsity
DeepSeek-V3671B / 37B256 + 1MLA (dense)AdamWFP8 block-scaled~18:1
DeepSeek-V3.2671B / 37B256 + 1MLA + DSA indexerAdamWFP8~18:1
DeepSeek-V4-Pro1.6T / 49Bfine-grainedMLA + CSA/HCAMuon + mHCFP4 QAT (experts)~33:1
DeepSeek-V4-Flash284B / 13Bfine-grainedMLA + CSA/HCAMuon + mHCFP4 QAT~22:1
Kimi K21.04T / 32.6B384 + 1MLA (64 heads)MuonClip— (INT4 post-train)~32:1
Qwen3-Next80B / 3Bfine-grained3:1 GatedDeltaNet/softmaxAdamW—~27:1
MiniMax-01456B / 45.9Bfine-grained7:1 lightning/softmaxAdamW—~10:1
Step-3321B / —fine-grainedMFA (AI=128)AdamW—tier-tuned
GLM-4.5355B / 32Bfine-grainedloss-free sigmoid routeAdamW—~11:1
Ernie 4.5424B / 47Bmultimodal-isolated—AdamWFP8~9:1
Your target10T / 200Bfine-grained + sharedMLA + CSA/HCA or MFAMuon + QK-Clip + mHCNVFP4 QAT50:1
 The open corpus as your pre-run ablation set — and where the 10T/200B/100k-GB200 target lands relative to it.

Pulling the telemetry together into engineering directives for the 10T-total / 200B-active / 100k-GB200 target:

1. Optimizer stability is the gating risk, not loss-landscape exotica. The closed labs let you believe the hard part of trillion-scale training is some mysterious loss-landscape problem. The open reports show the actual failure is mundane and specific: under Muon, attention logits run away. MuonClip (τ=100 QK-Clip) and DeepSeek-V4's Muon + mHC are the named, exact interventions that make trillion-scale Muon safe — logit clipping at the weight level, and doubly-stochastic residual mixing for depth stability. Budget for these as line items from step zero; do not default to AdamW out of caution, because Muon's data-efficiency advantage at large batch is now validated to 1.6T and the stabilizers are known.

2. Precision is heading to FP4-in-the-loop, and it is trainable. DeepSeek V4's FP4 QAT on routed experts and the indexer is the existence proof that GB200 NVFP4 microscaling (E2M1 + E4M3 block scales) is a trainable recipe at 1.6T, not just an inference cast. Plan to train experts in NVFP4 with QAT; keep the precision-sensitive paths (norms, router, attention accumulation, embeddings) higher. The honesty bar is also set: Kimi reports K2-Thinking benchmarks at INT4. Report your served-precision numbers, not your full-precision flex.

3. Attention must be cheap and arithmetic-intensity-matched, without killing retrieval. DSA's sparse indexer, V4's CSA/HCA compression, Step-3's MFA, and Qwen3-Next's Gated-DeltaNet hybrid all converge on the same target: kill the O(L²) and the KV cache while keeping a 25%–12.5% softmax/dense backbone for retrieval. For 1M-context serving of a 200B-active model, a V4-style CSA+HCA interleave or a Step-3-style MFA is mandatory — a dense-attention 200B-active model at 1M context is not economically serviceable, full stop. The V4 numbers (27% FLOPs / 10% cache vs dense at 1M) are your target envelope.

4. Decode is a disaggregation problem. AFD (Step-3) + EP-wide serving on a flat interconnect (CloudMatrix-Infer's EP320) is the cost frontier. Your NVL72 is your CloudMatrix384. Disaggregate prefill / decode / KV-cache and run wide expert parallelism filling the NVLink domain; disaggregate attention from FFN across GPU roles within the rack where the inter-role transport is nearly free. The economics gap Step-3 demonstrates (39% cheaper at 32K) is what disaggregation buys.

5. Sparsity ratios are still climbing, and 50:1 is conservative. Kimi's ~32:1, MiniMax's ~10:1, Qwen3-Next's 27:1, and the DeepSeek-V4-Flash ratio all sit comfortably above where a year of conventional wisdom expected the quality cliff. Your 200B-active / 10T-total is a 50:1 sparsity ratio — which the sparsity scaling laws and these production points put well inside the validated regime, not at the aggressive edge. If anything, the open data suggests you could go sparser; do not let a stale "experts must be ~10× the active params" heuristic cap you.

6. The cheap data wins are real and scale-independent. Kimi's data-rephrasing result (diverse paraphrase × 1 epoch beats raw × N epochs by ~5 points) and Ernie's modality-isolated routing are both low-cost, high-return, and require no scale to validate. Generate diverse rephrasings of your high-value corpus; isolate modality routing if multimodal.

The export-control caveat, restated as a discipline

Discount every "we trained on N H800s" figure as a constrained-cluster artifact. The DualPipe acrobatics, the deliberately narrow EP, the obsessive communication-overlap scheduling — these are deficit-driven and matter far less on a flat NVLink domain where the all-to-all is cheap. What transfers cleanly and should anchor your design: the architectural bets (fine-grained MoE + shared expert, MLA/factorized attention, cheap-attention hybrid with a softmax backbone), the precision direction (FP4-in-the-loop with QAT), the optimizer + stabilizers (Muon + QK-Clip + mHC), and the serving topology (disaggregated, wide-EP on a flat domain). The Chinese frontier has, in effect, run your ablations in public. Read them as the most expensive free telemetry you will ever get.

Sources

  • DeepSeek-V4 technical report (CSA/HCA, FP4 QAT, Muon, mHC, MegaMoE) — arxiv.org/html/2606.19348v1
  • DeepSeek-V4-Pro config discussion (FP4-on-experts, hybrid-ZeRO, gating, hash routing) — huggingface.co/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Pro/discussions/129
  • DeepSeek V4 / Ascend training rumor — theregister.com/2026/04/24/deepseek_v4

  • Kimi K2-Thinking (INT4 QAT, 594GB, agentic tool calls) — huggingface.co/moonshotai/Kimi-K2-Thinking
  • Kimi K2-Thinking $4.6M cost estimate (secondary) — recodechinaai.substack.com
