Chapter 19 · Reading the leaderboard of sprints

Public throughput signals: MLPerf Training, MFU leaderboards, and reading the tea leaves

The public throughput record is a hall of mirrors: "10 minutes on 5,120 Blackwell GPUs" is a real number that answers the wrong question. This chapter extracts what MLPerf legitimately tells a planner sizing a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200 GPUs — a generation multiplier and a per-GPU ceiling — names what it structurally conceals, and re-anchors the planning number on the one honest signal: DeepSeek-V3's ~21% FP8 MFU over a full 14.8T-token MoE run.

~22 min read5 figuresgrounded in MLPerf v5.0/v5.1 + SemiAnalysis tracking

MLPerf Training headlines — "10 minutes on 5,120 Blackwell GPUs" — are engineered to be read as performance leadership, and they are real numbers produced by real silicon. But every one of them is a sub-convergence sprint on a dense model, tuned for the test, with the precision story laundered into a single time-to-train figure. For a planner sizing a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200 GPUs, the danger is not that these numbers are wrong; it is that they are right about the wrong question. They answer "how fast can clean silicon hit a fixed loss target on a 405B dense model over ~450 steps?" Your question is "what sustained MFU will a heavy-routing MoE hold over 10T tokens across a multi-week run on a 1,389-rack fault domain?" The gap between those two questions is the entire content of this chapter. We will extract what MLPerf legitimately tells you (a per-GPU peak-efficiency ceiling and a clean generation multiplier), name what it structurally conceals (MoE all-to-all, failure blast radius, long-horizon convergence, precision management at scale), and then re-anchor your planning numbers on the more honest signals: SemiAnalysis's sustained-MFU tracking, framework benchmark tables, and the single most load-bearing public datapoint of all — DeepSeek-V3's ~21% FP8 MFU over a full 14.8T-token MoE run.

The public throughput record is a leaderboard of sprints, run by capacity vendors, on dense models, at one-twentieth your scale, with precision gains folded invisibly into the clock. For what your run will actually sustain, ignore the 10-minute headline and write down 21%.
The structural caveat that frames the whole chapter

Every large MLPerf run is a sub-30-minute, ~450-step, dense-model sprint on a clean cluster — two to three orders of magnitude shorter than a 10T-token production run, with no MoE benchmark anywhere in the suite. These are short non-frontier runs by construction. Treat every efficiency number backed out of them as a ceiling, never a budget.

10min
v5.1 record, 5,120 Blackwell GPUs, 405B
~450steps
sub-convergence sprint length
20×smaller
public record vs your 100k-GPU target
21%
DeepSeek-V3 FP8 MFU — the real anchor

01What MLPerf Training v5.0/v5.1 actually measure

MLPerf Training is a closed-loop, fixed-quality benchmark: every submission trains to a predetermined eval-loss or accuracy target and reports only time-to-train. It deliberately publishes no MFU, no throughput, no TFLOPS. This is a defensible methodological choice — it makes submissions comparable across wildly different hardware — but it means every efficiency number you want must be backed out, and the back-out is fragile for reasons we will make precise.

The suite has been actively reshaped to track the frontier. v5.0 (results 2025-06-04) executed the pivotal substitution: it retired the long-standing GPT-3 175B pretraining benchmark and introduced Llama 3.1 405B pretraining as "the largest model in the suite," drawing 201 submissions from 20 organizations and debuting Blackwell silicon (GB200, B200-SXM-180GB) alongside AMD MI300X/MI325X and TPU Trillium (MLCommons v5.0). v5.1 (results 2025-11-12) retired BERT in favor of Llama 3.1 8B pretraining and replaced Stable Diffusion v2 with Flux.1, an 11.9B-param transformer diffusion model; it logged 65 unique systems, 12 accelerators, 20 orgs, with roughly half of submissions multi-node — an 86% jump in multi-node share over v4.1 (MLCommons v5.1). The LLM triad you should anchor on:

BenchmarkRole as a signalScale band
Llama 3.1 405B pretrainingFrontier-scale signal; the only large-cluster datapoint512 → 5,120 GPUs
Llama 2 70B LoRA fine-tuningSingle-rack / single-node signal8 → 72 GPUs
Llama 3.1 8B pretrainingSingle-node accessibility signal8 → 64 GPUs
 The entire MLPerf LLM track is dense. Note what is not here.

Note what is not here: there is no MoE pretraining benchmark at any scale. The entire MLPerf LLM track is dense. For a reader whose binding constraints are expert-parallel all-to-all and routing imbalance, the suite measures a different workload class than the one you will run. That is the first and largest caveat, and it is structural, not incidental.

The dense/MoE mismatch is structural

MLPerf prices the pipeline bubble and data-parallel comms of a dense transformer, but it has no mechanism to price MoE token dispatch/combine, routing imbalance, or token drop — because there is no MoE benchmark. Your binding constraint is invisible to the suite by design.

02The headline time-to-train record

The public record reads as a clean two-generation story, and on its own terms it is impressive.

RoundSubmitterGPUsBenchmarkTime-to-train
v5.0NVIDIA reference512 GB200Llama 3.1 405B~20.8 min
v5.0CoreWeave + NVIDIA + IBM2,496 GB200Llama 3.1 405B27.3 min
v5.1NVIDIA2,560 BlackwellLlama 3.1 405B18.79 min
v5.1NVIDIA "Tyche"5,120 BlackwellLlama 3.1 405B10.0 min
 The cleanest apples-to-apples scaling point across rounds is the 2,560-GPU → 18.79-min intermediate, ~45% faster than v5.0's 2,496-GPU run at near-identical scale.

The v5.0 largest submission — CoreWeave + NVIDIA + IBM at 2,496 GB200 GPUs completing Llama 3.1 405B in 27.3 minutes — was billed as the largest GB200 NVL72 cluster ever benchmarked under MLPerf, "34× larger than the only other cloud-provider submission" (CoreWeave). NVIDIA reported a 2.2× per-GPU Blackwell-over-Hopper speedup on this benchmark (121.09 vs 269.12 minutes at 512 GPUs). In v5.1, NVIDIA set the record at 10 minutes on 5,120 Blackwell GPUs, a 2.7× improvement over its best v5.0 Blackwell result, and claimed 85% scaling efficiency from 512 → 5,120 GPUs (a 10× GPU-count increase) (NVIDIA v5.1). The cleanest apples-to-apples scaling datapoint across rounds is the 2,560-GPU → 18.79-min intermediate point, ~45% faster than v5.0's 2,496-GPU run at near-identical scale.

The single biggest lever between rounds was not a hardware change — it was precision. v5.1 was the first MLPerf Training round to use NVFP4 (4-bit) for the bulk of the math, holding the final training iterations in FP8 ("healing") to land the loss target (NVIDIA v5.1). Blackwell does FP4 at 2× its FP8 rate; Blackwell Ultra (GB300) at 3×. This single fact should govern how you read the 2.7× round-over-round jump: a large fraction of it is a 2× FP4-over-FP8 throughput artifact that only holds because the run is short enough that the FP8 healing tail stays cheap. More on why that matters in §04.

prec FP4 throughput = 2 × FP8 (Blackwell) · 3 × FP8 (GB300) The v5.1 round's 2.7× jump is part FP4 hardware, part numerical: most of training ran in 4-bit, with an FP8 "healing" tail short enough to be free over ~450 steps. Over 10T tokens the tail is not free.

At the single-rack/node end, the LoRA and 8B signals fill in the lower scale band and — importantly — provide the only credible non-NVIDIA primary data. v5.0 NVIDIA single-node 8×GB200 hit Llama 2 70B LoRA in ~0.56 min against a widely-cited FP8 reference of 11.145 min at 8 GPUs. In v5.1, GB300 delivered ~5× over Hopper at equal GPU count; Lambda's GB300 NVL72 converged 1.27× faster than the best GB200 NVL72 and 1.6× faster than the top 64×B200. Independent Nebius numbers — 8×B300 LoRA = 8.48 min, 32×B200 = 3.10 min (~3.1× scaling 8→32 GPUs) — show Blackwell Ultra averaging ~12.6% training-time reduction over Blackwell (Nebius). And the genuine cross-vendor hedge: AMD MI355X completed Llama 2 70B LoRA in 10.18 min (8 GPUs) in v5.1, within 3% of B200 FP8 and ~10% faster than NVIDIA's 11.145-min v5.0 FP8 result, on ROCm 7.0 / PyTorch 2.8 / TE v2.2 (AMD ROCm). MI355X is the only non-NVIDIA primary LLM-training signal at this generation, and it lands close enough to matter as a second source against single-vendor framing.

03Who submits at frontier scale — and who conspicuously does not

The composition of the submitter list is itself a signal, and it cuts against the grain of how the market is usually described. The frontier-scale submitters are now neoclouds, not hyperscalers: CoreWeave (2,496 GB200 in v5.0), Nebius, Lambda (GB300 NVL72), plus NVIDIA's own internal clusters ("Tyche," the source of the 5,120-GPU record). No hyperscaler submitted a frontier-scale GB200 pretraining run. Google submitted only TPU Trillium results, not a 405B-at-scale entry.

Two reads follow. First, the entities actually willing to expose large GB200 clusters to a public, audited benchmark are the ones selling capacity, for whom a leaderboard placement is marketing — not the ones running their own frontier models, for whom benchmark exposure is pure downside. Second, and more consequential for you: the public MLPerf record tops out around 5,120 GPUs on a single benchmark — roughly 20× smaller than your 100,000-GB200 target — and every large run is a sub-30-minute sprint. There is no public, audited datapoint within a factor of 20 of your cluster size, and none of any duration resembling a real run.

When NVIDIA reports 85% scaling efficiency from 512 → 5,120 GPUs, you are watching the curve over one decade of GPU count; you need it over nearly three decades, into a regime where the inter-rack network — not NVLink — carries pipeline and data-parallel traffic, and where the 1,389-rack failure surface is live the entire time. The 85% figure does not extrapolate, and treating it as if it does is the most common way a planner gets burned by this data.

Do not extrapolate the 85% scaling figure

85% efficiency over 512 → 5,120 GPUs says nothing about 5,120 → 100,000, where DCN replaces NVLink for PP/DP traffic and the 1,389-rack fault domain is continuously live. Extrapolating it is the single most common planner error against this dataset.

04Backing out MFU — and the three reasons the number is a trap

You can recover an effective efficiency figure from time-to-train: take FLOPs-per-token × tokens ÷ time ÷ GPUs ÷ peak. NVIDIA's own v5.0 405B reference quotes ~1,960 effective TFLOPS per GPU (FP8) — roughly 40–50% of B200's dense-FP8 peak — and the reproduction guide publishes the exact run string needed to compute it independently: TP4-CP2-PP8-DP{n}, GBS=896 (GA112), micro-batch 1, FP8_PARAM_GATHER, TP_COMM_OVERLAP, MAX_STEPS≈450, eval-loss target 5.6, NeMo on NGC 25.04 (NVIDIA reproduce). That ~40–50% looks like a usable planning ceiling. It is not, for three structural reasons, each of which independently disqualifies it as a number you'd budget against.

yaml — the v5.0 405B reference recipe (the exact knobs you'd back MFU out of)
# MLPerf v5.0 Llama-3.1-405B reference — short-run-optimal, NOT a real run
model: llama3.1-405b
parallelism:
  tensor_parallel:   4      # TP4
  context_parallel:  2      # CP2
  pipeline_parallel: 8      # PP8
  data_parallel:     auto   # DP{n} fills the cluster
global_batch_size:   896    # GBS, grad-accum GA112
micro_batch_size:    1
precision:           fp8
flags: [FP8_PARAM_GATHER, TP_COMM_OVERLAP]
max_steps:           450    # sub-convergence: ~2 days of real training, extrapolated
eval_loss_target:    5.6    # train only until "further training would converge"
# absent by design: checkpoint-restart, fault recovery, SDC detection, elastic reconfig
python — backing effective FP8 efficiency out of a time-to-train headline
# MLPerf reports ONLY time-to-train; recover effective efficiency by hand.
flops_per_token = 6 * 405e9            # 405B dense, 6ND approximation
tokens         = 896 * 8192 * 450     # GBS=896 * seq * ~450 sub-convergence steps
time_s         = 27.3 * 60            # v5.0 2,496-GPU run
gpus, peak_fp8 = 2496, 1979e12        # B200 dense-FP8 peak (TFLOPS)

eff_tflops = flops_per_token * tokens / time_s / gpus / 1e12   # ~1,960 TFLOPS (NVIDIA)
mfu_fp8    = (eff_tflops * 1e12) / peak_fp8                     # ~0.40-0.50 — a CEILING
# ...but this is dense, ~450 steps, clean, and FP4-inflated in v5.1. Do NOT budget on it.

(1) Sub-convergence sprints

MLPerf LLM benchmarks train a fraction of the data to a point "indicating further training would converge," not to convergence. The v5.0 405B run is ~450 steps at GBS=896. For the retired GPT-3 175B benchmark, MosaicML's Abhi Venigalla estimated the 11-minute record extrapolated to ~2 days of real full-scale training (IEEE Spectrum) — i.e., the benchmark is two to three orders of magnitude shorter than a production run. Everything that dominates a 10T-token run — long-horizon optimizer-state dynamics, late-training loss-spike recovery, learning-rate-decay-phase behavior — happens entirely outside the measured window. A 450-step efficiency figure tells you about steady-state GEMM throughput on a warm cluster and nothing about the integral over a real run.

(2) Tuned-for-the-test parallelism

The reference configs are short-run-optimal and omit every long-run mechanism. TP4-CP2-PP8 with micro-batch 1 is chosen to minimize wall-clock over 450 steps on a clean cluster; the LoRA reference runs CP8 on a single 72-GPU rack. There is no checkpoint-restart, no fault recovery, no silent-data-corruption detection, no elastic reconfiguration in any of these runs — because over 10–30 minutes you will not hit a failure, so you do not pay for resilience. Over a multi-week run on 1,389 racks where the whole 72-GPU NVLink domain is a single failure domain, you pay for all of it continuously, and it is a real MFU tax that MLPerf structurally cannot show.

(3) Precision laundering

This is the subtlest and, in v5.1, the most important. The "10 min on 5,120 GPUs" headline conflates a hardware gain (Blackwell's FP4 tensor cores) with a numerical gain (running the bulk of training in 4-bit). The 2× FP4-over-FP8 throughput only fully cashes out because the run is short enough that the FP8 "healing" tail is a negligible fraction of total steps. At 10T-token scale, the precision-management overhead is not negligible: the NVFP4 pretraining recipe requires a BF16/FP8 decay tail over roughly the last ~18% of tokens to keep loss error in check (§07), plus Random Hadamard transforms and 2D weight scaling that erode the nominal FLOP advantage. MLPerf proves NVFP4 survives ~450 steps plus a short heal; it proves nothing about FP4 numerical stability over 10T tokens.

The one mistake to refuse

Do not credit the FP4 throughput multiplier to your steady-state MFU as if the healing tail were free. The 2× FP4 artifact in the v5.1 headline holds only because ~450 steps make the FP8 heal tail negligible. Over 10T tokens, the ~18% precision-decay tail is a first-order cost.

A methodological landmine compounds all three: MFU is ill-defined under low precision at all. TorchTitan explicitly refuses to report MFU when Float8 is enabled because the metric becomes "not well-defined" (TorchTitan). The peak-FLOPS denominator differs by 2× between FP8 and FP4, so an "effective TFLOPS" computed against one peak is not comparable to a BF16 framework MFU computed against another. Comparing v5.1's FP4 "effective TFLOPS" to a BF16 MFU table is a category error — exactly the trap a GB200 planner falls into when stacking MLPerf headlines against framework numbers.

compute MFU = FLOPs/token · tokens ÷ time ÷ GPUs ÷ peakHW peakHW differs 2× between FP8 and FP4. An "effective TFLOPS vs FP4 peak" is not comparable to a "BF16 MFU vs BF16 peak" — TorchTitan refuses to report MFU under Float8 for exactly this reason.

05The more honest framework signal

Framework-reported MFU, measured over realistic step counts, is more load-bearing than MLPerf for sizing a real run — though still dense, and still mostly Hopper.

  • Megatron-Core / NeMo (H100, BF16/FP8): up to ~47% MFU, but the shape matters more than the peak. MFU rises with model size (41% → 47–48%) then falls with scale as DP-exposed comms grow. The Llama-3 team's own 405B run used three distinct 4D recipes, and doubling cluster size dropped MFU 43% → 41% once intra-node TP saturated and marginal GPUs spilled into slower data-parallel comms (Megatron-LM). The gap-to-100% decomposes as roughly ~15% pipeline bubble, ~10–15% comms, ~5–10% recompute, plus non-GEMM and host overhead. That decomposition is your mental model for where MFU goes; note that none of those four buckets includes MoE all-to-all, which is additive on top for your workload.
  • TorchTitan (H100 95GiB): reports BF16-equivalent 33–42% MFU for Llama-3.1-8B FSDP, and declines to report MFU at all under Float8. Its 405B 3D config (FSDP4-TP8-PP16, 512 GPUs) yields ~130 tokens/s/GPU — useful strictly as a tokens/GPU anchor, not an MFU one (TorchTitan).
  • MaxText (JAX/TPU): defines MFU = train-step-FLOPs ÷ step-time ÷ peak-HW-FLOPS and tokens/s = global-tokens ÷ step-time ÷ devices. It recently corrected its TFLOPS formula (PR #1988) to account for causal attention — a pointed reminder that published MFUs are sensitive to the attention-FLOP convention (causal halving) and the 6ND-vs-exact-profiling debate. Two papers reporting "MFU" may differ by several points purely on FLOP-counting convention, before any hardware difference.

The codecard below makes the convention sensitivity concrete: this is the shape of MaxText's per-step MFU accounting, where the causal-attention halving (the PR #1988 fix) is the line that moves a reported MFU by several points before any hardware enters the comparison.

MaxText — train-step MFU accountingPR #1988 (causal halving)illustrative
# MFU = train-step FLOPs / step-time / peak-HW-FLOPS  (MaxText definition)
def per_token_flops(n_params, d_model, n_layers, seq_len):
    dense = 6 * n_params                                   # [1] 6ND approximation
    # exact attention term, halved for causality (PR #1988):
    attn  = 12 * n_layers * d_model * seq_len * 0.5        # [2] causal halving
    return dense + attn

def mfu(step_flops, step_time_s, devices, peak_flops):    # [3]
    return step_flops / step_time_s / devices / peak_flops  # peak differs 2x FP8 vs FP4

tokens_per_s = global_tokens / step_time_s / devices      # [4] the MFU-free anchor

16ND is an approximation. The flat 6·N·D rule omits the attention term entirely; "MFU" reported on it differs from exact profiling by the 6ND-vs-profiling debate the source flags.

2The causal halving (PR #1988). Attention is half-masked, so its FLOPs halve — the correction that shifts a published MFU by several points before any hardware difference enters.

3The 2× denominator trap. peak_flops is 2× larger for FP4 than FP8; an "effective TFLOPS vs FP4 peak" is not comparable to a BF16-peak MFU — the §04 category error in code.

4tokens/s is convention-free. Unlike MFU, tokens/s/GPU needs no peak or FLOP convention — which is why TorchTitan's ~130 tok/s/GPU survives as an anchor where its Float8 MFU does not.

The through-line: well-tuned dense framework MFU on the prior generation lives in the high-30s to high-40s, degrades with scale, and is reported against conventions that vary by several points. None of these tables includes a 200B-active MoE, and none is at GB200 maturity.

06SemiAnalysis: the sustained-MFU cross-check

If MLPerf is a sprint stopwatch, SemiAnalysis's "H100 vs GB200 NVL72 Training Benchmarks" is the only public artifact that measures sustained MFU and exposes the software-maturity curve MLPerf hides. It is the indispensable cross-check, for four reasons.

1 · Fresh-architecture MFU is a moving target — and the move is enormous

On identical H100 silicon, GPT-3 175B BF16 MFU rose 34% → 54% from Jan → Dec 2024 (and 29.5% → 39.5% in FP8) — pure software, same chips (SemiAnalysis). A 20-point MFU swing in eleven months from nothing but kernel, compiler, and framework maturation. GB200 in mid-2025 sat early on the equivalent curve. This is the most important single fact in the chapter for your timeline: whatever MFU GB200 shows at your cluster's first light, expect it to be 10–20 points below where it lands a year later, and budget a multi-month software-maturity ramp accordingly.

2 · Sustained dense MFU anchors

Llama-3 405B sustained MFU ≈ 41–43% across cluster sizes; Llama-3 70B ≈ 53–54% BF16 / ~35–38% FP8 (SemiAnalysis). These are real, multi-hour-or-longer figures, not 450-step sprints — and they are below the MLPerf-implied 40–50%+ once you account for the full run.

3 · Cost/energy grounding

405B BF16 ≈ $1.95/M tokens, 8.8 J/token on a 2,304-GPU H100 cluster — concrete $/token and J/token anchors you can re-base for GB200.

4 · The killer caveat for your project

As of that analysis, "there are no large-scale production training runs yet on GB200 NVL72." Software immaturity and NVLink-backplane reliability — the entire 72-GPU rack is one failure domain, and one bad component drains the whole rack — were actively blocking frontier deployments. And the economic bar: GB200 NVL72 TCO is ~1.6× H100's, so GB200 must be ≥1.6× faster than H100 per-GPU just to break even on TCO (SemiAnalysis). The MLPerf 2.2× per-GPU sprint speedup clears that bar; the question is whether sustained GB200 MFU does, early on the maturity curve, with MoE comms layered on. SemiAnalysis's ClusterMAX 2.0 scores 80+ providers across the accelerator landscape, with CoreWeave the lone Platinum cloud — a useful read on which neoclouds can actually deliver the uptime that a multi-week run on 1,389 racks demands.

The real economic bar is 1.6×, not 2.2× SemiAnalysis

GB200 NVL72 TCO ≈ 1.6× H100, so GB200 must sustain ≥1.6× H100 per-GPU just to break even. The MLPerf 2.2× sprint multiplier clears it on clean silicon — but no public datapoint yet confirms that sustained GB200 MoE throughput clears 1.6× early on the maturity curve.

07The DeepSeek-V3 reality check — weight this above everything

The most honest MoE-at-scale public number is not in MLPerf and not in any framework table. It is DeepSeek-V3: 14.8T tokens, 2,048 H800, 2.788M GPU-hours, FP8. Reconstructing effective MFU from the published GPU-hours via FLOP profiling (not the 6ND approximation) lands at ~21.4% FP8 MFU (≈42.9% BF16-equivalent) — about 424 effective TFLOP/s/GPU against a 1,979-TFLOPS FP8 peak (dlrover). This is sub-25% FP8 MFU on a 671B-total / 37B-active MoE over a full 14.8T-token run — the closest public analog to your workload class that exists, and it should be weighted far above any MLPerf 405B-dense sprint.

The one number to write down

DeepSeek-V3: ~21% FP8 MFU over a full 14.8T-token MoE run. Not a 450-step sprint, not a dense model, not a clean cluster — a real MoE over a real horizon on real silicon. This is your anchor; everything in MLPerf is a ceiling above it.

Why is it so much lower than the 41–47% dense framework ceiling? Because everything that makes an MoE an MoE shows up as MFU loss: expert-parallel all-to-all (token dispatch and combine, twice per layer, on the critical path), routing imbalance and token drop (under-utilized experts and dropped tokens are wasted FLOPs), and the load-balancing machinery itself. The dense framework tables price the pipeline bubble and DP comms but not the all-to-all; MLPerf prices none of it because it has no MoE benchmark. DeepSeek-V3 is the number that prices all of it, over a real horizon, on real silicon. For a 200B-active model at 10T tokens — roughly 5.4× the active params and ~0.68× the tokens of V3 — the routing-comms and imbalance penalties scale unfavorably with active param count and expert count, not favorably. There is no reason to expect your sustained MFU to land above V3's; the honest planning assumption is at or below it on the FP8 axis, before GB200's NVLink5 EP-locality advantage claws some back.

That NVLink5 clawback is real and is the one structural reason GB200 should beat a Hopper MoE by more than the raw FLOP ratio: with the entire expert-parallel group able to live inside the 72-GPU, 1.8-TB/s NVLink domain (EP ≤ 72), the all-to-all stays on NVLink instead of spilling to InfiniBand at ~1/4 the bandwidth, as it does on Hopper above EP=8. So the V3 ~21% floor is a Hopper MoE floor; GB200 EP-locality is the lever that lifts it. But it lifts it from that floor, not from the 40–50% MLPerf headline.

Calibrate to a 20–35% sustained-FP8 band

DeepSeek-V3's ~21% is the floor; the well-tuned dense ~47% is a ceiling you will not reach with heavy routing; GB200 NVLink5 EP-locality is the lever pushing you toward the upper end of that band, not above it. A 200B-active MoE at 10T tokens scales the routing/imbalance penalty unfavorably vs V3 — anchor low.

08The honest read for the 10T/200B run on 100k GB200

Pulling the threads together into planning numbers.

MLPerf gives you a generation-multiplier and a per-GPU peak-efficiency ceiling — not a planning number. The 2.2× (v5.0) / 2.7× (v5.1) Blackwell-over-Hopper figures are legitimate relative signals; the absolute 40–50% effective FP8 efficiency is a clean-cluster, 450-step, dense-405B, FP4-inflated ceiling that will not survive contact with your run.

Your binding constraints are absent from MLPerf entirely. MoE all-to-all / EP comms, GB200 NVLink-domain failure blast radius (one bad component drains a 72-GPU rack), checkpoint/restart and silent-data-corruption recovery at 100k scale, long-horizon optimizer-state and convergence dynamics, and FP4/FP8 numerical stability over 10T tokens — none of these appear in a benchmark that proves a ~450-step sprint plus an FP8 healing tail.

A planning table, with the right anchors:

SignalReported MFUWhat it actually representsWeight for your run
MLPerf v5.1 405B "10 min"~40–50% eff. FP8 (backed out)Dense, ~450 steps, clean cluster, FP4 + healCeiling only; do not budget against
Megatron/NeMo dense ceiling~47% (→ ~41% at scale)Dense, realistic steps, HopperUpper bound on a dense run
SemiAnalysis 405B sustained~41–43% BF16Dense, sustained, mature Hopper softwareMature-software dense reference
DeepSeek-V3 (the anchor)~21% FP8 / ~43% BF16-eq671B/37B MoE, full 14.8T tokens, FP8Weight highest
 Stacking MLPerf headlines against framework BF16 tables is a category error — the peak denominators differ 2×. Weight the bottom row above all.

Calibrate to a ~20–35% sustained-FP8 band for a 200B-active MoE — DeepSeek-V3's ~21% as the floor, the well-tuned dense framework ~47% as a ceiling you will not reach with heavy routing, and GB200 NVLink5 EP-locality as the lever that pushes you toward the upper end of that band rather than the lower. This is materially below the 40–50%+ that stacking MLPerf headlines would imply, and the gap is the cost of being an MoE at scale.

Budget a multi-month software-maturity ramp. Mirror the H100 34% → 54% BF16 curve: expect GB200 to come online 10–20 MFU-points below its eventual plateau, and plan the first quarter or two of the run as a ramp, not steady state. The single most valuable thing this whole body of public data tells you is that fresh-architecture MFU is a moving target that moves ~20 points in a year — so the worst time to lock your throughput model is at first light.

Treat the 1.6× TCO break-even as the real bar, not the 2.2× sprint. GB200 wins on perf/TCO only if sustained per-GPU throughput on your actual MoE clears 1.6× H100's — a much harder test than the MLPerf sprint multiplier, and one no public datapoint yet confirms at MoE-at-scale. The competitive hedge to keep live: TPU v7 Ironwood's 3D-torus-plus-OCS topology is arguably better for very large EP than NVL72's 72-GPU island and carries a SemiAnalysis-estimated ~30–44% lower TCO — the one alternative whose public efficiency story, if it materializes at scale, could make the GB200 break-even bar bind against you.

The competitive hedge to keep live estimated

TPU v7 Ironwood's 3D-torus + OCS topology may suit very-large EP better than NVL72's 72-GPU island, at a SemiAnalysis-estimated ~30–44% lower TCO. If that efficiency story materializes at scale, it raises the bar GB200 must clear to justify itself for your run.

The final tea-leaf reading: the public throughput record is a leaderboard of sprints, run by capacity vendors, on dense models, at one-twentieth your scale, with precision gains folded invisibly into the clock. It is genuinely useful for exactly two things — ranking silicon generations and bounding per-GPU peak efficiency — and genuinely misleading for the one thing you most want, which is what your run will actually sustain. For that, ignore the 10-minute headline and write down 21%.

Sources

  • MLCommons — MLPerf Training v5.0 results: mlcommons.org/2025/06/mlperf-training-v5-0-results
  • MLCommons — MLPerf Training v5.1 results: mlcommons.org/2025/11/training-v5-1-results
  • NVIDIA — Blackwell sweeps MLPerf Training v5.1 (10 min / 5,120 GPUs; NVFP4; 85% scaling): developer.nvidia.com/blog/…v5-1-benchmarks
  • NVIDIA — Reproducing MLPerf v5.0 Training Scores (exact configs; ~1,960 TFLOPS): developer.nvidia.com/blog/reproducing-…v5-0
  • CoreWeave — Largest-ever MLPerf submission on GB200 (2,496 GPUs / 27.3 min): investors.coreweave.com — largest-ever MLPerf GB200
  • IEEE Spectrum — GPT-3 time trials; MosaicML ~2-day extrapolation: spectrum.ieee.org/large-language-models-training-benchmark
  • SemiAnalysis — H100 vs GB200 NVL72 Training Benchmarks (sustained MFU; 1.6× TCO; NVLink reliability): newsletter.semianalysis.com/p/h100-vs-gb200-nvl72-training-benchmarks
  • dlrover — MFU for DeepSeek-V3 training (~21.4% FP8 / ~42.9% BF16-equiv): medium.com/@dlrover — MFU for DeepSeek-V3
  • TorchTitan paper (MFU undefined under Float8; 33–42% BF16; 405B tokens/GPU): arxiv.org/html/2410.06511v1
  • NVIDIA Megatron-LM (dense MFU shape; 43% → 41% on cluster doubling): github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM
  • AMD ROCm — MLPerf Training v5.1 (MI355X LoRA 10.18 min): rocm.blogs.amd.com — MLPerf Training v5.1
  • Nebius — MLPerf Training v5.1 results (B200/B300 LoRA scaling): nebius.com/blog/posts/mlperf-training-v5-1-results

Chapter 20 · The payoff — what to actually change in your tree

The punch-list: fixing your codebase, data, and dataloader for a 10T/200B/100k-GB200 run

Every chapter before this argued the physics. This one is the field order of operations: in build order, exactly what to change in your tree — MaxText, JAX/Flax/XLA, HEAD 2026-06 — to take it from a TPU-first research framework to a thing that survives a 10T-total / 200B-active MoE on 100,000 NVIDIA GB200 GPUs.

~30 min read5 figuresgrounded in MaxText base.yml, moe.py, optimizers.py

This is the payoff chapter. The framing assumption is uncomfortable but load-bearing: MaxText was built for TPU pods, and almost every item below is a place where the GPU/NVLink/InfiniBand reality of an NVL72 fleet diverges from the ICI-mesh abstractions baked into base.yml. The punch-list is ordered so that each item de-risks the next; do not reorder it, because the framework decision (item 0) gates everything, and the fault-tolerance and dataloader items (8, 6) are the ones that quietly determine whether you ever finish.

MaxText-on-GPU is a real option, not a TPU toy — but the bet is whether XLA:GPU plus the JAX MoE kernel zoo can hit the ~1,000+ TFLOPS/GPU that Megatron-Core demonstrably does on GB200, on your schedule. Gate the whole run on a 1,000-GPU bake-off.

Throughout, I quote dense hardware numbers, not the marketing sparse ones. A GB200 NVL72 rack is 360 PFLOP/s dense FP8 / 180 PFLOP/s dense BF16 (the 720/360 on NVIDIA's page are 2:4-sparse figures; the page's own footnote says "Dense is one-half sparse spec shown"), i.e. ~4.5 dense PFLOP/s FP8 per B200 GPU, so 100k GPUs is ~0.45 EFLOP/s dense FP8 peak, not 1.0. At a defensible 35–40% large-MoE FP8 MFU that is ~160–180 dense PFLOP/s of useful throughput — every percentage point of MFU you leave on the floor in the items below is ~4 PFLOP/s, and every minute of badput is ~10 EFLOP wasted.

0.45EF/s
dense FP8 peak, 100k GB200
120TB
resident state, Muon (vs 160 Adam)
~30min
extrapolated MTBF at 100k GB200
1.96×
MegaMoE fused-kernel speedup (latency)

00The framework decision: MaxText-on-GPU vs Megatron-Core / TorchTitan

Decide this first; it is the only irreversible item. The honest position: the most-proven 10T-MoE-on-GB200 stack today is Megatron-Core, which reports 1,233 TFLOPS/GPU on GB200 for DeepSeek-V3-685B (vs 1,048 on H100) and 974 (GB200)/919 (H100) for Qwen3-235B — real, published, GB200 MFU envelopes you can design against (Megatron-Core MoE, arXiv 2603.07685). It ships Parallel Folding (separate attention vs MoE meshes), the Flex dispatcher wired to DeepEP/HybridEP, TEGroupedMLP grouped-GEMM, and — critically — it is what NVIDIA optimizes the GB200 software stack for. The DeepSeek lineage (DualPipe, DeepEP, DeepGEMM MegaMoE) is all CUDA/PTX and drops into the PyTorch world with zero impedance mismatch.

MaxText's countervailing strengths are real and the reason you might stay: it already contains a Muon optimizer wired through optax.contrib._muon (optimizers.py, opt_type: "muon"), DeepSeek-V3/V4 routing variants in moe.py, MLA, Orbax emergency/multi-tier checkpointing, Grain elastic dataloading, and a TransformerEngine-JAX bridge that already exposes te_nvfp4/te_mxfp8 recipes (quantizations.py). NVIDIA actively co-develops NVFP4-on-Blackwell support for JAX/MaxText. So the framework decision is not "MaxText is a TPU toy" — it is "can XLA:GPU + the JAX MoE kernel zoo hit the same ~1,000+ TFLOPS/GPU that Megatron-Core demonstrably does on GB200, on your schedule?"

The concrete risks of staying on MaxText/JAX-on-GPU, in priority order:

1 · FP8 accumulation fix
inexpressible in stock XLA
V3 promotes WGMMA partials to FP32 every NC=128 — JAX can't natively express it; you depend on TE-JAX or Pallas:Mosaic-GPU and must verify it.
2 · No fused MegaMoE
unfused dispatch/GEMM/combine
MaxText is ragged_all_to_all then separate ragged_dot/gmm. You leave the 1.50–1.96× fusion win on the table without a multi-month Pallas kernel.
3 · XLA:GPU overlap
collective maturity
DualPipe-style comm-compute overlap (~20 SMs of warp-specialized a2a) is hand-tuned PTX; getting XLA to schedule EP a2a inside the GEMM window is not a flag.

The first risk is the most dangerous. DeepSeek-V3's recipe promotes WGMMA partial sums to FP32 CUDA-core registers every N_C=128 elements (≈4 WGMMAs) to dodge Hopper/Blackwell tensor cores' ~14-bit accumulation and its up-to-2% GEMM error (DeepSeek-V3, arXiv 2412.19437). JAX cannot natively express this two-level accumulation; you depend on TransformerEngine-JAX or a Pallas:Mosaic-GPU kernel to do it for you, and you must verify it does — this is the single most likely correctness/quality landmine in a JAX FP8 port. The second: DeepSeek-V4's MegaMoE fuses EP-dispatch → FP8×FP4 GEMM → SwiGLU+requant → FP8×FP4 GEMM → EP-combine into one SymmBuffer kernel for 1.50–1.73× general / up to 1.96× latency speedup (DeepSeek-V4, arXiv 2606.19348). MaxText's path is unfused: jax.lax.ragged_all_to_all for dispatch/combine, then a separate tokamax.ragged_dot/megablox gmm for the expert GEMM.

The recommendation

If your team's center of mass is JAX and you already have MaxText running at scale on TPU, commit to MaxText-on-GPU but with a hard dependency on TransformerEngine-JAX for the FP8/NVFP4 GEMMs and DeepEP (via the NVIDIA hybrid-ep branch) for the all-to-all — do not try to ride pure XLA collectives for EP. Budget one senior kernel engineer for two quarters to land the FP8 accumulation path and the DeepEP binding, and gate the full-scale run on a 1,000-GPU bake-off against the Megatron-Core MFU numbers above. If that bake-off comes in below ~30% MFU, switch to Megatron-Core; the published GB200 evidence is on its side. TorchTitan is the wrong choice at this scale today — treat it as a parallelism-pattern reference, not a destination.

01Parallelism config for 10T/200B on 100k GB200

The memory math, not the FLOPs, dictates the layout. With Muon as the primary optimizer (the right choice, see item 5), the per-parameter resident budget is ≈ 2 B bf16 compute weight + 4 B fp32 master + 2 B bf16 grad + 4 B fp32 Muon momentum ≈ 12 B/param, vs ~16 B for AdamW. For 10T params that is 120 TB (Muon) vs 160 TB (Adam) of resident, non-replication-shardable state (Muon quantization, arXiv 2509.23106). Against 100k×192 GB = ~19.2 PB aggregate HBM this is only ~0.6%, which is the whole point: this is a sharding-and-bandwidth problem, not a capacity problem. Pure FSDP/ZeRO-3 fails not on capacity but because its all-gather of the full 10–20 TB weight set is batch-independent and becomes the wall as the FSDP degree grows toward 100k, and because it replicates the all-to-all-bound expert FFNs instead of sharding experts (JAX Scaling Book).

The composition rule is a strict fabric hierarchy: keep every high-volume collective (TP, EP all-to-all) inside the 72-GPU NVLink domain (1.8 TB/s/GPU); let only PP and DP cross InfiniBand (~90 GB/s/GPU, the ~20:1 cliff) (DeepEP, GB200 NVL72). 100k GPUs ≈ 1,389 NVL72 racks. The target layout:

AxisDegreeFabricWhat it shardsMaxText flag
TP (attention/MLA/shared expert)8NVLink (ICI)model dim of dense partsici_tensor_parallelism=8
EP (routed experts)8NVLink (ICI)the 10T expert weights, no replicationici_expert_parallelism=8
FSDP (intra-rack remainder)autoNVLink (ICI)optimizer/grad shard within rackici_fsdp_parallelism=-1
PP (stages across racks)16–32InfiniBand (DCN)layers; moves only stage-boundary actsdcn_pipeline_parallelism=16..32
DP (replicas across islands)~43–87InfiniBand (DCN)replicates the unit; ZeRO-1 opt sharddcn_data_parallelism=-1
CP (long-context phase only)2–8NVLink (ICI)sequence/KV at 64K→1Mici_context_parallelism
 EP×TP=64 ≤ 72 keeps the high-volume collectives inside one NVL72 rack; PP and DP cross InfiniBand only.

With EP×TP=64 ≤ 72 inside a rack, PP=16–32 racks/replica, one replica is ~1,150–2,300 GPUs and 100k GPUs gives ~43–87 DP replicas. This mirrors DeepSeek-V3's 2,048-H800 layout (PP=16, EP=64 across 8 nodes, ZeRO-1 DP, no TP during training) but collapses the 8-node EP domain into a single NVL72 rack because the 72-GPU NVLink domain is what 8 H800 nodes used to be (DeepSeek-V3, arXiv 2412.19437). For the dense parts you can run TP=8 where V3 ran TP=1, because NVL72 makes 8-way TP free of InfiniBand.

MaxText-specific gotchas you must get right:

The logical mesh is ['diloco','data','stage','fsdp','fsdp_transpose','context','context_autoregressive','tensor','tensor_transpose','tensor_sequence','expert','autoregressive'] and DCN axes must appear earlier in the list than ICI axes — your PP (stage) and DP (data) are DCN-mapped and must precede the ICI-mapped tensor/expert. The product of all ici_* must equal chips-per-slice (72). Map ICI → NVLink rack, DCN → InfiniBand.

Never shard experts or TP over DCN

Leave dcn_expert_parallelism=1. Sharding experts over DCN puts the EP all-to-all on InfiniBand — fatal. Likewise dcn_tensor_* and dcn_sequence_* stay 1; base.yml literally annotates them "never recommended," and on a GB200 build TP/SP-over-DCN is a guaranteed comm-bound stall.

Pipeline schedule. MaxText's layers/pipeline.py does circular pipelining (num_pipeline_microbatches, pipeline_delay_activation_forwarding, num_layers_per_pipeline_stage) but does not ship DualPipe/DualPipeV. For a 10T run where the EP all-to-all crosses NVL72 boundaries during the long-context phase, DualPipe's native comm-compute overlap — bubble (PP/2−1)(F&B+B−3W), cost 2× param copies (+20 TB, absorbable) — is worth it, and DualPipeV halves the activation cost (PP/2 devices, PP/2 stages) (DualPipe). This is a real engineering item: either implement a DualPipeV schedule in pipeline.py or accept the 1F1B bubble (PP−1)(F+B) plus zero-bubble ZB-H1 (~1/3 bubble at equal memory, arXiv 2401.10241) as a stopgap. If you stay 1F1B, run PipeOffload-style activation offloading (arXiv 2503.01328) to keep PP=16–32 of a 10T model memory-feasible.

Watch the vocab/output-head imbalance

A 10T model's vocab projection is large and will unbalance any zero-bubble or DualPipe schedule unless you apply vocabulary parallelism (arXiv 2411.05288). MaxText keeps logits_dense on AdamW (item 5) but does not balance its PP-stage cost.

02MoE kernels, all-to-all, and comms overlap

MaxText's MoE substrate lives in moe.py (RoutedMoE, sparse_matmul, permute/unpermute/get_all_to_all_params). The defaults in base.yml are megablox: true, sparse_matmul: true, capacity_factor: -1.0 (dropless), ragged_buffer_factor: -1.0, use_tokamax_gmm: false, use_ring_of_experts: false, use_ragged_sort: false. Here is what to change.

Ragged buffer sizing — the dropless-vs-memory knob

With ragged_buffer_factor ≤ 0 MaxText computes the worst-case dropless size as min(ep_degree, global_experts/top_k) * balanced_size; for a DeepSeek-style 256-expert/top-8 config imbalance is never worse than 256/8 = 32×, and once EP>32 the bound is global_exp/top_k independent of EP. The worst-case dropless buffer is a real activation-memory tax at 32×.

moe worst_case_factor = min( |EP| , global_exp / topk ) Once |EP| > global_exp/topk the bound stops growing — for 256/8 it is 32×, independent of EP.
Set ragged_buffer_factor ≈ 3.0

Set ragged_buffer_factor ≈ 3.0 — the in-code recommended near-dropless, memory-bounded compromise — and accept rare token drops, which are quality-neutral once the load balancer converges (moe.py). You trade a vanishingly small (un-guaranteed) drop probability for ~10× less activation memory than the 32× worst-case buffer.

Grouped-GEMM backend

Three dispatch paths in gmm(): jax.lax.ragged_dot, forked megablox mblx.gmm (quantized, gmm-v2), and tokamax.ragged_dot (Mosaic). On GB200 the Mosaic-GPU path via use_tokamax_gmm: true is the JAX-native ragged-GEMM you want for the expert FFN, but note use_gmm_v2: true "Currently incompatible with quantization" — so if you need FP4 experts you are on the megablox quantized gmm path, not tokamax. Benchmark both against the published Hopper/GB200 numbers from MegaBlocks (1.38–4.35× over Tutel, 1.8–2.4× over dense Megatron-LM, arXiv 2211.15841) and read SonicMoE, arXiv 2512.14080 — the freshest IO/tile-aware grouped-GEMM design, benchmarked on H100 and GB200 — before locking a kernel.

src/maxtext/layers/moe.pyRoutedMoE.sparse_matmul → gmm()three-way dispatchtrimmed
def gmm(self, lhs, rhs, group_sizes):                          # [1]
    gs = RaggedDotGroupSizes(real=group_sizes,
        even_split=(lhs.shape[0] // self.num_experts,) * self.num_experts)
    if self.config.use_tokamax_gmm:                            # [2]  GB200 JAX-native
        return tokamax.ragged_dot(lhs, rhs, gs.real, implementation="mosaic")
    if self.config.megablox:                                   # [3]  base.yml default
        return mblx.gmm(lhs, rhs, gs.real,                     # quantized FP4/INT8 path
                        lhs_quantize_dtype=self.config.lhs_quantize_dtype,
                        rhs_quantize_dtype=self.config.rhs_quantize_dtype)
    with set_xla_metadata(ragged_dot_tiling=self.wi_tile_fwd): # [4]
        return jax.lax.ragged_dot(lhs, rhs, gs.real)           # XLA-native ragged

1Grouped, not batched. group_sizes carries per-expert run lengths; RaggedDotGroupSizes also holds an (n//G,)*G even-split fallback for shape inference.

2Tokamax Mosaic. The GB200 JAX-native ragged-GEMM — but use_gmm_v2 is incompatible with quantization, so it is not the FP4 path.

3Quantized megablox. The base.yml default; the only path that takes lhs/rhs_quantize_dtype, so FP4 experts ride here.

4Tile hints. ragged_dot_tiling passes the wi_tile_fwd_*/wo_tile_* knobs through to XLA.

All-to-all

MaxText uses jax.lax.ragged_all_to_all (not dense a2a) with offsets/sizes from get_all_to_all_params. The ragged Pallas kernels (a2a_ragged_sort/ring_ragged_sort under use_ragged_sort/use_ring_of_experts) gather only the valid prefix rather than the padded buffer — enable these at EP in the hundreds, where padded sort is pure waste. But the harder truth: at 100k-GB200 scale the right all-to-all substrate is DeepEP V2 / the NVIDIA hybrid-ep branch, which measures 726/740 GB/s NVLink dispatch/combine at EP8 on SM100/Blackwell (max-perf) and reduces SM consumption to 4–6 SMs, with FP8 dispatch + BF16 combine halving the dispatch leg (DeepEP README). MaxText has no binding to DeepEP today; writing one is the highest-leverage MoE-systems item after the FP8 GEMM. Two systems landmines: low-latency RDMA dispatch sags to 194µs/39 GB/s at 256 experts (per-token RDMA bandwidth falls as expert count grows), and DeepEP issue #427 reports low-latency dispatch can corrupt under heavily imbalanced expert load — exactly the early-training regime before your bias balancer converges (DeepEP #427).

Step-0 hazard — #427 imbalance corruption filed upstream

Run the high-throughput dispatch path (not low-latency) until the bias balancer converges, or use hash-routed early layers (which cannot hit #427) as a clean warm-up. And treat the 39 GB/s low-latency RDMA floor at ≥128 experts as a hard design constraint — per-token RDMA bandwidth structurally falls as expert count rises.

Routing and balancing

MaxText's get_topk() already implements DeepSeek-V3 sigmoid + learnable-bias aux-loss-free balancing (routed_bias_update_rate, default 0.0, feeding calculate_load_balance_updates, applied post-optimizer via update_state_param), DeepSeek-V4 hash routing (tid2eid lookup, is_hash_routing gating lb-loss off), and expert_group_mask/deepseek_routing for node-limited (group-limited) routing. Set routed_bias_update_rate on a decay schedule mirroring V3: γ=0.001 for the bulk of training, then 0.0 for the final ~500B tokens (DeepSeek-V3). Configure node-limited routing so each token reaches ≤2–4 racks (the GB200 analog of M=4 node-limited routing) and an expert group fits one NVL72 domain, so the RDMA leg only carries cross-group traffic. At deployment you will need EPLB-style redundant-expert replication of the hottest experts (essentially mandatory at 100k GPUs); LPLB — per-batch dynamic redistribution as an LP solved single-SM in ~100µs — is the research-frontier online variant (LPLB). The decisive ratio to remember when deciding whether comm hides under compute is ~6,144 FLOPs/Byte (V4's framing): below it the all-to-all is exposed.

Map one routing group to one NVL72 domain

Set the group structure so an expert group fits one NVL72 rack and node-limited M=2–4 routing keeps the dominant traffic on NVLink — the RDMA leg then carries only cross-group tokens, and you live on the high-throughput curve rather than the 39 GB/s low-latency floor.

03Precision: FP8 now, FP4 QAT path

Ship FP8 first. The reference floor is the DeepSeek-V3 recipe: per-tile 1×128 activations, per-block 128×128 weights, E4M3 on all forward/dgrad/wgrad operands, power-of-two scales, with the FP32-promotion accumulation fix (item 0) (arXiv 2412.19437). The non-negotiable "precision breaks" map — keep router/gating, all norms, attention operators, embeddings, and output head in BF16/FP32; FP32 master weights + FP32 grad accumulators — is invariant across every FP4 paper since, because a single mis-quantized gate flips an expert assignment.

In MaxText, the modern path is Qwix (use_qwix_quantization: true; AQT is deprecated, per base.yml). The get_quantization_rule rules scope quantization to decoder/.*layers.* and route over op_names ("dot_general","gmm","ragged_dot") — note gmm/ragged_dot are precisely the MoE expert grouped/ragged GEMMs, which is exactly where you want low precision and where the router/norms are automatically excluded (quantizations.py). Set quantization: "fp8_full" for static-scaling FP8, or wire the TransformerEngineQuantization recipe te_mxfp8 (block_size=32) for MX-FP8.

Why NVFP4 over MXFP4 — the data-efficiency gap is concrete

NVFP4 uses 16-element micro-blocks with a per-block E4M3 scale + FP32 per-tensor scale, preserving the ±4/±6 FP4 points that MXFP4's power-of-two E8M0 scale clips. The cost of getting this wrong: MXFP4 needs 36% more tokens (1.36T vs 1.0T) to match NVFP4 loss — at a 10T budget that is the difference between a 10T and a 13.6T run (NVFP4 pretraining, arXiv 2509.25149).

The FP4 path is a sequenced upgrade, not a day-1 commit. NVFP4 pretraining (the 12B Mamba-Transformer to 10T tokens, the longest public 4-bit run) requires all four of: ~16% of linear layers in BF16 (first 2 + final 8 blocks), a 16×16 Random Hadamard Transform on Wgrad inputs only (fixed ±1 sign vector shared across all layers), 2D 16×16 block scaling on weights, and stochastic rounding on gradients only (SR on forward weights/acts is detrimental). End-game: switch NVFP4→BF16 at ~82% of tokens to cut LR-decay-phase loss error from 1.5% to 0.5% at ~6% high-precision compute.

MaxText already wires these toggles: te_nvfp4 (NVFP4BlockScaling) and te_nvfp4_no_rht, with a code TODO confirming NVFP4's native 16-block needs fused RHT (currently block_size=128 until unfused RHT lands). The AQT/Qwix vjp_*_stochastic_rounding is per-tensor configurable — set SR on the gradient tensors only. The mixed-precision regex JSON (dense_llm_subchannel.json, per-layer w_bits/a_bits/tile_size) is the machinery that expresses "experts FP4, router/attention/norms high-precision." Follow DeepSeek-V4's production pattern: FP4 QAT on MoE expert weights (the parameter bulk) + the lightning indexer, FP8 everywhere else, FP8 KV with BF16 only on RoPE dims, with a lossless FP4→FP8 dequant reusing the FP8 pipeline (DeepSeek-V4 blog).

FP4×FP8 runs at FP8 speed on today's silicon stated in V4

FP4×FP8 currently runs at FP8×FP8 speed on existing silicon — on GB200 today FP4 experts are a memory/comms win (smaller weights, smaller all-to-all), not a compute win. And heed the precision scaling-law warning: more pretraining tokens makes a model more fragile to post-hoc quantization (δPTQ ∝ Dγ/Nγ), which is precisely why V4 went QAT, not PTQ (Scaling Laws for Precision, arXiv 2411.04330).

04Optimizer: Muon is already in the repo — finish wiring it

MaxText already has Muon (opt_type: "muon" via optax.contrib._muon.muon, with get_muon_weight_dimension_numbers emitting bespoke reduction/output-axis tuples for MoE expert weights, attention out-proj, and QKV). The matrix-only / AdamW-for-the-rest split is correct and matches the field consensus (embeddings, unembed, RMSNorm gains, biases → AdamW). Take Muon: it halves the optimizer state (4 B vs 8 B/param), and — the real reason at 100k-GPU width — its data-efficiency advantage persists and even grows into the over-large-batch regime up to 16M-token batches, exactly where AdamW falls off its critical-batch cliff (Essential AI, arXiv 2505.02222). Be honest about the headline: the "2× FLOPs" claim shrinks to ~10–15% fewer tokens once AdamW is well-tuned; you adopt Muon for batch-scaling robustness and the memory halving, not a 2× miracle.

Punch-list for the optimizer block:

yaml — MaxText Muon block (the settings that are traps if left default)
opt_type: "muon"
muon_consistent_rms: 0.2      # Moonlight RMS≈0.2 rescale; None falls back to width scaling
                              #   (DeepSeek-V4 uses 0.18 — either defensible)
muon_weight_decay: 0.1        # default 0 is a TRAP — Muon needs decoupled WD to scale
# Newton-Schulz: stock optax = 5 steps @ (3.4445, -4.7750, 2.0315)
#   V4 remedy for under-polished output projections: two-stage NS
#     8 steps @ (3.4445, -4.7750, 2.0315)  then  2 steps @ (2, -1.5, 0.5)
#   optax `muon` does NOT expose a two-stage schedule — small worthwhile patch
Three Muon settings, in order

Set muon_consistent_rms: 0.2 (the Moonlight rule: rescale every matrix's update to RMS≈0.2 so a shared LR works across shapes; Moonlight, arXiv 2502.16982; V4 uses 0.18). Set muon_weight_decay: 0.1 (default 0 is a trap — out-of-the-box Muon needs decoupled WD to scale). And patch in V4's two-stage Newton-Schulz: the stock 5 steps at (3.4445,−4.7750,2.0315) under-polishes final-layer projections, which degrade as steeply as M−0.96 in singular-value quantiles at 10T scale (Spectral Scaling Laws, arXiv 2606.04058).

Distributed Muon at 10T. Even bf16 momentum is ~20 TB and must be ZeRO-1 sharded across DP; Moonlight's Distributed Muon all-gathers the full matrix transiently for the NS iteration (overhead in (1, 1.25]× of distributed AdamW). At 100k-GB200 the all-gather of full momentum is the bottleneck — evaluate Dion (amortized rank-r power iteration, exchanges only O((m+n)r), never unshards the full matrix, composes with FSDP+TP) as the scalable alternative if you become DP-sync-bound (Dion, arXiv 2504.05295).

Hyperparameter transfer — and do not double-apply µP

For width/batch HP transfer, adopt u-µP (fuses Unit Scaling with µP, collapses the sweep to ~9 LR-only runs, and trains stably in FP8 at 7B where standard µP diverges from gradient underflow — directly relevant on GB200 FP8/FP4 tensor cores, arXiv 2407.17465). Note Muon's √max(A,B)/RMS scaling already implements much of µP's per-layer LR scaling, so do not naively double-apply. Pick batch size and weight decay from Power Lines: τopt ∝ (D/N)−0.52, Bcrit ∝ D0.5. At 10T tokens Bcrit lands in the tens-of-millions, consistent with K2/V4 peak batches of 75–94M; running at B=Bcrit costs 2× minimum tokens, so size below it (arXiv 2505.13738).

05Stability: the guards are present — tune them, don't add them

MaxText already ships the three stability primitives you need, and the lesson is to configure them, not write new ones.

skip_step_on_spikes
rolling mean ± 6σ
Zeros the update and freezes momentum on a loss/grad spike; preserves optimizer state. Ported from OLMo-core. Turn it on.
z_loss_multiplier
default 0.0 → ~1e-4
Keeps logits from drifting; aggregated in train_step alongside the MoE lb-loss.
use_qk_norm
default false → true
V4's structural fix: RMSNorm on Q and KV to bound attention logits. Chosen over QK-Clip; delivered spike-free training.

skip_step_on_spikes (train.py wrapper, skip_step_interval: 128, skip_step_scaling_factor: 6.0): a rolling Bessel-corrected mean ± 6σ guard that zeros the update and freezes momentum when loss/grad_norm spikes, preserving optimizer state — ported from OLMo-core. Turn it on (skip_step_on_spikes: true). It is the practical spike guard that layers under any schedule and prevents a single bad batch from poisoning the Muon momentum. z-loss (z_loss_multiplier, default 0.0): set it (~1e-4) to keep logits from drifting; it is aggregated in the train_step alongside the MoE lb-loss.

Muon excites exploding attention logits — enable use_qk_norm

Muon excites exploding attention logits (Kimi-K2 saw max logits >1000). MaxText has use_qk_norm (default false) and attn_logits_soft_cap (default 0.0). Enable use_qk_norm: true — this is DeepSeek-V4's structural fix (RMSNorm on Q and KV entries to bound logits), which V4 chose over QK-Clip and which delivered spike-free training. If you instead want Kimi-K2's QK-Clip (per-head conditional rescale by min(1, τ/Smax) with τ=100), that is not in the repo and would need adding; for a V4-shaped MLA model, use_qk_norm is the lower-effort, proven path. The Qwen3-480B config in-repo already sets use_qk_norm — a working reference.

06Dataloader: Grain at 100k-host scale

At ~12,500 hosts the dataloader stops being a per-host concern and becomes a distributed storage problem governed by object-store request ceilings, not bandwidth. A 10T-token corpus at 4 B/token read at 2e7–1e8 tok/s is only 80–400 GB/s — trivial bandwidth — but 12,500 hosts × even 10 reads/s = 125k req/s, far past a flat GCS bucket's ~5,000-reads/s cold-start ceiling (S3: 5,500 GET/prefix), guaranteeing 503 SlowDown/429 storms at launch and after every preemption (GCS request rate, S3 performance). MaxText's input_pipeline/* routes dataset_type → grain | hf | olmo_grain | tfds | c4_mlperf | synthetic; use Grain + ArrayRecord (the only Grain format supporting true global shuffle via Riegeli random-access). The fixes, in priority order:

The single highest-leverage knob: expansion_factor_real_data

Set expansion_factor_real_data > 1 so only jax.process_count()/factor hosts read GCS and broadcast batches over the device mesh (get_process_loading_real_data + PlaceHolderDataIterator). A factor of 8 turns 12,500 readers into ~1,560 — cutting the object-store fanout directly.

  1. Cut the object-store fanout directly. expansion_factor_real_data > 1 (above) is the single highest-leverage knob.
  2. Colocate the loader. colocated_python_data_input: true runs Grain on the accelerator host so shard decode/collation use host CPU/RAM with NVLink-adjacent transfer, eliminating PCIe-roundtrip host stalls.
  3. Use the elastic/pure-function resume path. Per-host process_{i}-of-{N}.json checkpoints break on resize and create N reads on restart. Prefer grain_use_elastic_iterator: true, whose ElasticIterator state is a single global scalar — only process 0 writes process_0.json and every process reads it, so the iterator survives a change in jax.process_count() (multihost_dataloading.py). The in-repo OLMo Grain loader (olmo_data_grain.py) is the even cleaner pattern: a pure-function OlmoIndexSampler keyed by SHA-256(seed,epoch) Fisher-Yates needing zero serialized iterator state — you pass initial_step = train_step * per_host_batch at construction. Note its documented scaling defect: shuffled_global_indices materializes a full numpy permutation (~5.8 GB at uint64 for 724M instances) per epoch per host; at trillions of instances move to OLMo-core's on-disk build_and_save_global_indices memmap (compute once, mmap-share, uint32 — switch to uint64/block-structured above 4.29B instances).
  4. Storage layout. HNS buckets (up to 8× initial QPS), ≥256 MB ArrayRecord shards (one GET amortizes thousands of samples), thousands of name-hashed prefixes to partition the keyspace, and a staggered exponential-backoff ramp so 12,500 hosts don't cold-start the same prefix simultaneously.
  5. Mixtures. Use grain.IterDataset.mix(weights) (not MapDataset.mix) so each component checkpoints independently and the curriculum can be re-weighted after a restart — critical for long curricula (grain_data_processing.py).
  6. Determinism as run identity. Pin data_shuffle_seed and treat it (plus the elastic global-scalar state) as part of run identity so any elastic resize stays bit-deterministic.

07Data pipeline: offline prep, taxonomy curation, and the scaling-law campaign

The discipline at this scale: do all heavy work offline, keep the training loader a dumb deterministic index→fetch→collate function. The proof point is Essential-Web v1.0 — 24T tokens / 23.6B docs processed in a 7-day window with "0 crashes" on a Daft pipeline sustaining 32k req/s/VM (Eventual/Daft). Concretely:

Pre-tokenize and pre-pack offline, always

Pre-tokenize offline. HF Rust tokenizers do ~12.5M tok/s/core, so 10T tokens is ~106 core-seconds — a few-thousand-core-hours embarrassingly-parallel job. Store as uint32 token IDs in ArrayRecord; online tokenization would burn the host CPU the loader needs for shard decode. Pre-pack offline with best-fit-decreasing bin-packing (sort docs descending, place in tightest bin) so packing is deterministic and checkpointable; MaxText's packing/sequence_packing.py produces inputs_segmentation/inputs_position consumed by flash-attention varlen for intra-document masking.

Decide the masking question deliberately: intra-doc masking gives a real perplexity win (arXiv 2402.13991), but DeepSeek-V3 deliberately declined cross-sample masking during pretraining (only at SFT) as an efficiency choice — weigh the splash/flash-varlen kernel cost against the win. Note the splash-attention constraint visible in the OLMo loader: q_seq_len must be divisible by 512, so mask the boundary position rather than emitting L-1.

Taxonomy curation. A 200B-active model is data-bound; the curation taxonomy (domain mix, dedup aggressiveness, quality-classifier thresholds, code/math/multilingual ratios) is set offline and re-weighted live via the IterDataset.mix per-component checkpointing above. This decoupling — heavy global shuffle/dedup/classification in Daft/Ray/Spark, emit large shards, re-weight at runtime — is the pattern.

Run the scaling-law campaign on WSD — the switch points are outputs, not guesses

Run the campaign on a WSD schedule (warmup-stable-decay): a constant stable phase lets you branch short decays to probe the loss frontier continuously without committing the full run, and the river-valley analysis explains why the sharp final decay works. Fit the precision (item 3), batch/WD (item 4), and Muon-vs-Adam iso-loss curves on 399M–4B proxies before the 10T commit. The NVFP4→BF16 switch point (~82%) and the routed-bias γ decay (~95%) are campaign outputs, not guesses.

08Fault tolerance: the item that determines whether you finish

At 100k GB200 the MTBF is brutal. Llama-3 405B logged 419 unexpected interruptions in 54 days on 16,384 H100 (~1 every ~3 hours); linearly extrapolating the per-GPU rate to 100k GB200 gives an interruption every ~25–35 minutes, likely worse given Blackwell's power density and the immature NVL72 copper backplane (Llama-3, arXiv 2407.21783). The governing identity is goodput ≈ MTBF/(MTBF+MTTR), and you cannot raise MTBF at this scale — every technique is an MTTR-reduction. If MTTR is 20 min against a 30-min MTBF, goodput collapses to ~60%; cutting MTTR to 1 min restores ~97%.

mem goodput ≈ MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) MTBF ~30 min is fixed; MTTR=20 min → ~60% goodput, MTTR=1 min → ~97%. Every item below is an MTTR cut.

MaxText already has the machinery in common/checkpointing.py; wire it correctly:

Enable multi-tier checkpointing — peer-RAM restore, not GCS

Set enable_multi_tier_checkpointing: true, local_checkpoint_directory=/local, local_checkpoint_period=20, multi_tier_checkpointing_backup_interval_minutes=20. This writes frequent checkpoints to a per-process local dir (the EmergencyCheckpointManager appends the distributed process_id to the path on GPU) and flushes rarely to GCS (PersistentCheckpointOptions). On restore, SingleReplicaArrayHandler / enable_single_replica_ckpt_restoring broadcasts a single surviving replica across the replica mesh — a swapped-in node pulls weights from a healthy replica's RAM, not from GCS (cf. Gemini SOSP'23) — converting a minutes-scale GCS restore into a seconds-scale broadcast.

Set the optimal interval by Young/Daly: interval ≈ √(2·ckpt_cost·MTBF). At 100k where MTBF shrinks, the cheap local checkpoints must be frequent and the durable GCS ones rare — exactly the two-tier split. CheckFreq-style adaptive tuning bounds overhead to ~3.5% (CheckFreq, FAST'21). Async + OCDBT: save_checkpoint issues ocp.args.PyTreeSave with OCDBT + Zarr3 and explicit chunk_byte_size (target_data_file_size 2 GB, concurrent 96 GB) for parallel I/O; it block_until_readys only to clean the measurement. Keep enable_autocheckpoint so a preemption signal forces a save (PreemptionCheckpointingPolicy composed with FixedIntervalPolicy via AnySavePolicy); maybe_save_checkpoint drains the async save via wait_until_finished() before exit. Grain checkpoint via GrainCheckpointHandler, using the elastic global-scalar state (item 6) so data position is invariant under rescale.

Make DP the elastic outer axis — and measure goodput, not MFU

The layout in item 1 deliberately confines a failure's blast radius to one PP-stage rack inside one DP replica; DP is the only axis you can elastically resize without re-laying-out EP/TP/PP. MaxText's Pathways elastic path (utils/elastic_utils.py, pathwaysutils.elastic.manager.Manager, maybe_elastic_scale_up) implements shrink-the-world-and-continue. Adopt load-time resharding (ByteCheckpoint-style, 54× stall reduction) so you resume on fewer GPUs after losing racks without a conversion job (ByteCheckpoint, arXiv 2407.20143). And measure goodput, not MFU: adopt Google's ML-Goodput-Measurement badput taxonomy (WASTED_PROGRESS, INFRASTRUCTURE_RECOVERY, CHECKPOINT_SAVE/RESTORE, step_time_deviation) as the run's primary KPI — MaxText already wires record_custom_badput_event_start/end into elastic events (ML-Goodput). The bar is Llama-3's >90% effective training time.

SDC and stragglers — the failures with no error signal

Silent data corruption poisons checkpoints with no error signal — keep enough checkpoint history to roll back past a corrupted save, add NaN/Inf + grad-norm guards (item 5 covers the spike side), and sample-recompute a subset of collectives (Meta SDC, arXiv 2102.11245). For hangs, the slowest of 100k ranks sets the pace and a hung rank deadlocks the collective; on the JAX side rely on coordination-service heartbeats and the goodput jitter metric, escalating to kill+elastic-excise.

09Networking assumptions: MRC and Spectrum-X

The network is a first-class failure source, and MaxText assumes nothing about it — the assumptions are operational, not code, but they gate the items above. Two to lock:

Microsecond path failover (MRC) — assume it

Multipath Reliable Connection (MRC) (OpenAI + AMD/Broadcom/Intel/Microsoft/NVIDIA via OCP, open-sourced May 2026) spreads one RDMA connection over multiple SRv6-source-routed paths and blacklists a failed path within tens of microseconds (vs seconds of RoCE routing convergence), handling loss via selective-ACK + packet trimming (OCP). This converts transient link flaps — which would otherwise time out NCCL and trigger a full job restart costing minutes of MTTR — into training-transparent reroutes. If your fabric supports MRC, your perceived MTBF improves because the synchronous collective never observes the flap. Design the InfiniBand/Spectrum-X scale-out (~800 Gb/s/GPU per-GPU scale-out network) around it.

NVL72 is the new blast radius — keep TP modest

A single GPU failure now jeopardizes a whole 72-GPU NVLink domain because TP groups live inside it. For TP=64, 0.1% of GPUs failed drops fleet availability to 94% (NTP, arXiv 2504.06095). This is the systems argument for keeping TP modest (8, not 64) and leaning on MoE expert-parallel sharding — a lost EP rank degrades fewer collectives than a lost TP rank. Pair with NTP-style degraded (nonuniform) TP groups so a domain loss is a soft event. The copper backplane remains a 2025–26 reliability weak point with immature diagnostics; budget for it.

10Build order, in one paragraph

Do the framework bake-off (0) on 1,000 GPUs first — it gates everything. Then, in parallel-ish: land the FP8 GEMM with the accumulation fix and the DeepEP all-to-all binding (0/2/3), because MFU lives there; wire Muon fully (consistent_rms=0.2, weight_decay=0.1, two-stage NS, use_qk_norm=true, skip_step_on_spikes=true) (4/5); stand up the offline Daft pre-tokenize/pre-pack pipeline and the Grain elastic dataloader with expansion_factor_real_data and colocated_python_data_input (6/7); and before you go past ~10k GPUs, prove multi-tier emergency checkpointing + DP-elastic resize + goodput measurement end-to-end (8), because at 100k the MTTR term — not the MFU term — is what decides whether the run converges. Set the parallelism config (1) last, after the kernels and checkpointing are proven, because it is the cheapest thing to change and the most expensive thing to debug at scale.

The one ordering to remember

Item 0 (framework) is irreversible — do it first. Item 8 (fault tolerance) determines whether you finish — prove it before 10k GPUs. Item 1 (parallelism) is cheapest to change — do it last. Everything in between is MFU work that only pays off once those three bookends hold.

Sources

  • MaxText base.yml (MoE/optimizer/precision/checkpoint defaults): maxtext/configs/base.yml
  • MaxText moe.py (sparse_matmul, ragged buffer, routing, all-to-all): maxtext/layers/moe.py
  • MaxText quantizations.py (Qwix rules, te_nvfp4/te_mxfp8 recipes): maxtext/layers/quantizations.py
  • MaxText optimizers.py (Muon via optax.contrib._muon): maxtext/optimizers/optimizers.py
  • MaxText checkpointing.py (multi-tier emergency checkpoint, OCDBT): maxtext/common/checkpointing.py
  • MaxText olmo_data_grain.py (pure-function index sampler): maxtext/input_pipeline/olmo_data_grain.py
  • MaxText multihost_dataloading.py (elastic global-scalar iterator): maxtext/input_pipeline/multihost_dataloading.py

  • ByteCheckpoint (load-time resharding, 54× stall reduction): arxiv.org/abs/2407.20143

  • S3 performance (5,500 GET/prefix): docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3

  • Vocabulary parallelism (output-head PP balance): arxiv.org/pdf/2411.05288
  • Intra-document masking perplexity win: arxiv.org/pdf/2402.13991
