frontier models & technical reports
How to read this list

Treat the why line as the filter, not the title. A title tells you the topic; the annotation tells you the single fact, table, or trick that makes the resource worth the click over the dozen near-duplicates you have already seen. Where a resource is a framework file (MaxText, Megatron-Core, TorchTitan), the annotation names the exact symbol or flag to grep for.

01Frontier models & technical reports

The detailed model reports, retrospectives, and framework files that ground the architectural and systems bets — the MoE scaling laws, the optimizer existence proofs, the data recipes, and the readable JAX re-implementations of primitives most assume live only in private CUDA. 46 resources.

What this section deliberately omits

The canonical primary reports cited inline in Chapters 01–07 and Appendices A–C — the DeepSeek-V3/V4 reports, the NVL72 spec sheet, the headline scaling-law papers — are not repeated here. The entries below are the second-order corpus: the retrospectives, framework files, issue-tracker bugs, and community extractions that sit one layer behind the obvious citations.

  • MAI-Thinking-1: Building a Hill-Climbing Machine (full 109-page technical report PDF) — The only public, detailed report of a from-scratch 1T-total MoE trained on production GB200 (8,192 GPUs) — your exact substrate. Contains the EGFLOPs/EGTime scaling-efficiency metric, dense+MoE interleaving ablations, attention zero-init trick for router balance, full FP8 numerical recipe, and the 'YOLO' distributed…
  • Epoch AI — 'What did it take to train Grok 4?' data insight — Independent, methodologically transparent estimate (246M H100-hours, 310 GWh, ~$490M, with stated 90% CIs and explicit caveats) — far more rigorous than the press numbers, and a reusable template for back-of-envelope costing your own 100k-GB200 run.
  • Epoch AI — 'Notes on GPT-5 training compute' (Besiroglu/Erdil) — Reasons explicitly that GPT-5 (~3e25 pretrain / ~5e25 total) used LESS compute than GPT-4.5, decomposing the RL-vs-pretrain split — the clearest public evidence that the frontier shifted from scale-up to RL+over-training, directly relevant to sizing your 200B-active bet.
  • SemiAnalysis — 'H100 vs GB200 NVL72 Training Benchmarks' (TCO/MFU/reliability) — The candid statement that no large run had yet completed on GB200 NVL72 in 2025, plus the 1.6–1.7× cost-per-GPU and 1.6× break-even hurdle, is the most important risk datapoint for anyone committing 100k GB200 to a single run; the paid section benchmarks Llama4-400B and DeepSeek-670B MoE specifically.
  • MuonClip / Kimi K2 optimizer write-up (Moonshot) — The 1T/15.5T zero-spike training result with MuonClip is the strongest existence proof that a non-AdamW optimizer can stabilize trillion-parameter MoE pretraining — a live alternative to the AdamW+zero-init recipe MAI uses, and decision-relevant for de-risking your run.
  • DeepEP legacy performance docs (deepseek-ai/DeepEP) — The raw per-expert-count dispatch/combine bandwidth and latency tables (43–58 GB/s RDMA, 77–314 µs LL kernels, 24-QP/20-SM details) that let you compute actual all-to-all cost, rather than the rounded numbers in secondary write-ups. The bandwidth-vs-line-rate gap here is the real bottleneck story.
  • Insights into DeepSeek-V3: Scaling Challenges and Reflections on Hardware (arXiv 2505.09343) — DeepSeek's own hardware-co-design retrospective, distinct from the V3 report. It quantifies the NVLink:IB 4:1 cliff, the IB-then-NVLink forwarding dedup trick, node-limited routing rationale, and explicit asks for next-gen interconnects — the closest thing to a blueprint for mapping EP onto an NVL72 scale-up domain.
  • Scalable Training of MoE Models with Megatron-Core (arXiv 2603.07685) — A 2026 production systems paper introducing Parallel Folding (separate TP/EP meshes for attention vs MoE via dual DeviceMesh), the Flex dispatcher (DeepEP/HybridEP), and measured 1,233 TFLOPS/GPU on GB200 for DeepSeek-V3-685B — directly answers the EP-vs-TP conflict the reader will hit.
  • Robust LLM Training Infrastructure at ByteDance (arXiv 2509.16293) — Concrete fault-tolerance engineering at the 10k+ GPU scale (fast failure detection, in-memory checkpoint, elastic reconfiguration) that justifies making DP the elastic outer axis — operational detail absent from model technical reports.
  • DeepSeek LPLB (Linear Programming Load Balancer) — A newer, much less publicized successor to EPLB that solves per-batch dynamic expert imbalance online via a single-SM interior-point LP on cuSolverDx/cuBLASDx in ~100µs, consuming DeepEP buffer stats. It targets the dynamic imbalance EPLB cannot, and almost nobody outside DeepSeek's repo watchers knows it exists.
  • SonicMoE: Accelerating MoE with IO and Tile-aware Optimizations — Dec 2025 paper (Guo, Mishra, Cheng, Stoica, Tri Dao) that benchmarks new grouped-GEMM kernels directly against MegaBlocks and CUTLASS on H100 AND GB200 with IO/tile-aware optimizations. The freshest non-DeepSeek grouped-GEMM work and explicitly Blackwell-targeted; unlikely to be on the reader's radar yet.
  • Megatron Core MoE 2026 Q2 Roadmap (GitHub Issue #4815) — Pre-discloses DeepSeek-V4 support internals (mHC, sqrt-softplus/Hash MoE scoring, CSA+HCA hybrid attention, Muon with FP8 primary weights), Qwen3.5 Gated Delta Rule kernels, DeepEP v2, and the MoE ECHO/Paged-Stashing trick for CUDA-graphing dropless MoE — a forward look most readers won't have seen.
  • deepseek_batchsplit_fp8.py in MaxText (quantized_psum_scatter custom VJP) — Concrete proof that MaxText reimplements DeepSeek's FP8-communication wgrad trick (BF16 reduce-scatter forward / FP8 all-gather backward) in pure JAX with a custom VJP — exactly the systems detail an expert assumes only exists in DeepSeek's private CUDA, now readable and forkable.
  • MaxText attention_mla.py Indexer (DSA reference in JAX) — A production, readable JAX re-implementation of DeepSeek's DSA lightning indexer with the gradient-isolation (stop_gradient) barrier, FP32 weights_proj enforcement, KL-divergence indexer loss, and the [rope,nope]-vs-[nope,rope] RoPE-layout subtlety made explicit in comments — far clearer than the V3.2 paper's prose.
  • Hardware-Centric Analysis of DeepSeek's MLA (arXiv 2506.02523) — Quantifies the MLA absorb-vs-materialize tradeoff (the mla_naive_kvcache flag in MaxText) at the roofline level — the missing analysis for deciding whether to cache the 576-elt latent or reconstruct full K/V per head on GB200 memory hierarchies.
  • Practical FP4 Training for Large-Scale MoE Models on Hopper (arXiv 2603.02731, anon repo MXFP4-Hopper) — The only recipe that gets FP4 benefits (memory + comms, −14.8% activation memory, +12.5% throughput on DeepSeek-V3 671B) on hardware with no FP4 tensor cores, via bitwise integer-domain FP8↔FP4 conversion and hierarchical scale alignment. Directly relevant if any part of the fleet is Hopper, or for the all-to-all…
  • Spectral Scaling Laws of Muon (MIT, arXiv:2606.04058, June 2026) — Layer-dependent power-law exponents for momentum singular values (final layers ~M−0.96) give the first principled, predictive justification for why DeepSeek-V4 needs extra Newton-Schulz polishing steps on output projections at scale. Very recent, almost certainly unseen.
  • OLMo-core skip_step_optimizer (Ai2) + Critical Batch Size Revisited (arXiv:2505.23971) — The reference implementation MaxText's skip_step_on_spikes is ported from (production spike-guarding logic, not a paper), paired with Ai2's empirical CBS protocol that replaces the distrusted gradient-noise-scale heuristic with direct measurement — the practical pairing for stability + batch-size selection.
  • mHC: Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections (arXiv:2512.24880) + community reimplementation — The primary DeepSeek-V4 residual-stability paper is very recent (Dec 31 2025) and the Birkhoff-polytope/Sinkhorn doubly-stochastic constraint is the load-bearing trick. An independent open reimplementation lets you ablate the n=4, 20-iter Sinkhorn, α=0.01 gating choices before committing 100k GPUs — most readers kno…
  • MaxText qk_clip_utils.py (production JAX QK-Clip for MLA) — A working, sharding-aware QK-Clip that operates on MLA wq_b/wkv_b kernels (√γ on the nope slice, full γ on the qR tail, V-slice untouched) — the exact per-component handling the Kimi K2 paper describes only in prose. Most readers have read the K2 report but not seen a correct framework implementation with the attn_…
  • mHC-lite OpenReview (id=5IJX6kvOif) — the lite variant MaxText actually ships — MaxText's mhc.py cites this 'mHC-lite' note, not the main DeepSeek paper — it uses the explicit permutation-matrix (Birkhoff vertex) parameterization. Useful if you want a cheaper residual-stabilization path than full Sinkhorn-projected mHC and want to know exactly which approximation a major framework chose.
  • Towards a Comprehensive Scaling Law of Mixture-of-Experts (arXiv 2509.23678) — The first scaling law to jointly couple all five MoE axes (N, D, N_active, granularity, shared-expert ratio) in one fitted form, and it explains why the entire production frontier (DeepSeek-V3.1, Kimi-K2, Qwen3) independently converged on G=8–9 — directly contradicting the G=64 implied by the better-known 2402.07871…
  • Towards Greater Leverage / Efficiency Leverage (arXiv 2507.17702, Ling team) — Reframes MoE scaling as 'efficiency leverage' EL=Cdense/Cmoe with a closed-form dependence on activation ratio, granularity, and compute, showing the payoff amplifies with budget. Includes a fully-specified validated pilot (Ling-mini-beta, 3.4% activation) — a rare end-to-end recipe from a non-DeepSeek lab.
  • Nemotron-CC-Math (arXiv 2508.15096) + Lynx-based math extraction — The non-obvious lesson is that the bottleneck for web math is not the quality classifier but HTML EXTRACTION — Trafilatura/Resiliparse silently corrupt inline LaTeX and flatten code. Rendering through Lynx (a 1992 text browser) then re-LaTeXing with Phi-4 is a recipe most teams have not internalized, and it delivere…
  • Olmo 3 / Dolma 3 technical report (arXiv 2512.13961) + Duplodocus — The most fully-disclosed end-to-end SOTA stack of late 2025 with exact mix proportions, a new trillion-token global-dedup tool (Duplodocus), and the deliberate choice of quality-aware upsampling (up to 7×) over flat top-k filtering — a complete, auditable counter-template to the 'discard 90%' orthodoxy.
  • MaxText OLMo Grain loader (olmo_data_grain.py + olmo_data_grain_resume_test.py) — A production-grade worked example of stateless, pure-function deterministic resumption (initial_step offset, repr-excludes-offset trick to satisfy Grain's sampler-equality check) that sidesteps iterator-state checkpointing entirely — the exact pattern you want at 12,500 hosts, plus in-code documentation of the 5.8 G…
  • OLMo-core data loading guide + NumpyDataLoaderBase / build_and_save_global_indices — The canonical open-source implementation of an on-disk global-shuffle index (global_indices.npy memmap computed once, mmap-shared) plus a VSL (variable-sequence-length) curriculum and stateful deterministic resume. This is the scaling target the MaxText OLMo loader explicitly says it should adopt; few outside Ai2 ha…
  • Analysing the Impact of Sequence Composition on Language Model Pre-Training (arXiv:2402.13991) — The controlled study quantifying that intra-document causal masking beats both naive packing and semantic grouping on perplexity — the evidence base for deciding whether to pay the splash/flash-varlen kernel cost for no-cross-doc-attention, which DeepSeek-V3 declined for pretraining. Directly informs the packing-vs-…
  • Mid-Training of Large Language Models: A Survey (arXiv 2510.06826) — The first dedicated survey to formalize mid-training as a phase and give a unified taxonomy (data distribution / LR scheduling / long-context extension) across DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3, Llama 3, OLMo 2, MiniCPM, Phi-4, SmolLM2, Granite 3.0. Useful as a single cross-report comparison table rather than reading each report s…
  • Scaling Llama 3 Training with Efficient Parallelism Strategies (ISCA 2025) — The systems paper (not the model report) that quantifies context-parallelism pathologies at scale: 65.75% of CP exposed latency is slowest-rank wait under document masks, and the exact 2×CP load-balanced sharding fix. Directly load-bearing for a 100K-GPU long-context plan and rarely cited alongside the model card.
  • How to Train Long-Context Language Models (Effectively) / ProLong (ACL 2025, arXiv 2410.02660) — Shows long-context capability is dominated by data engineering, not token budget — matching Llama-3.1-8B with ~5% of its long-context tokens, with concrete ablations on short-data retention, code-repo packing, and per-length sample counts. The most actionable contrarian result for budgeting the extension phase.
  • What's the deal with mid-training? (Vintage Data blog) — A sharp practitioner essay disentangling the conflicting uses of 'mid-training' / 'annealing' / 'continued pre-training' across labs, including the vocabulary collision where SmolLM3 mid-training means reasoning injection vs OLMo 2 mid-training means the data-quality anneal. Helpful for not getting confused across r…
  • DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp full report (arXiv 2512.02556) + DSA bootstrap recipe — The under-read bridge between V3 and V4. The exact 1000-step / 2.1B-token indexer warm-up procedure (training a sparse indexer against the frozen dense model's attention distribution before adapting the full model) is the cheapest way to retrofit sparse attention onto an existing dense-attention checkpoint, and it i…
  • Huawei CloudMatrix384 serving papers (arXiv 2506.12708 and 2508.02520) — Even an NVIDIA-only team should read these: CloudMatrix384's flat UB all-to-all domain is the structural twin of GB200 NVL72, and CloudMatrix-Infer's peer-to-peer disaggregated prefill/decode/cache with EP320 is a worked example of serving DeepSeek-class MoE on a flat-interconnect supernode, with real per-NPU throug…
  • MiniMax-M1 CISPO algorithm (arXiv 2506.13585) — CISPO (clip importance-sampling weights rather than token-level ratios) is a less-known but cleaner alternative to GRPO/DAPO for long-generation RL, demonstrated end-to-end on a 456B linear-attention model RL-trained for only ~$535K on 512 H800 — a concrete, cheap reproduction target for long-horizon reasoning RL.
  • Qwen team's Gated DeltaNet ratio ablations (Qwen3-Next blog + analysis) — Goes beyond the marketing to document why the specific 3:1 (75% Gated DeltaNet / 25% softmax) ratio was chosen, including the head-to-head where Gated DeltaNet beat Mamba2 and sliding-window attention on in-context learning — the load-bearing detail most secondary coverage omits.
  • Sebastian Raschka, 'A Technical Tour of the DeepSeek Models from V3 to V3.2' — The single best cross-version synthesis tracing the precise architectural deltas (MLA → NSA/DSA → sparse indexer) with code-level fidelity, useful as a map before diving into the individual primary reports; flags exactly which changes are systems-motivated vs quality-motivated.
  • DeepSeek-V4-Pro HuggingFace technical-report discussion thread — Community extraction of V4 config details (Sigmoid→Sqrt(Softplus) gating, hash routing in early blocks, CSA per-m-token learned compression, FP4-on-experts-and-indexer) that are buried in the dense arXiv HTML; a fast index into the report's systems sections before reading the PDF.
  • Moonlight / Muon-at-scale technical report (Moonshot/Kimi) — The first public account of Muon trained past 1B (16B MoE, 5.7T tokens) with the two non-obvious fixes — weight decay + 0.2·√(max(fan_in,fan_out)) update-RMS matching + ZeRO-1 distributed Muon — and a measured ~52%-compute Pareto gain over AdamW. The concrete recipe a GB200 MoE team would adapt.
  • dlrover — 'What is the MFU for DeepSeek-V3 training?' (derived 21.4% FP8 MFU) — Reconstructs DeepSeek-V3's effective MFU from published GPU-hours using ATorch FLOP profiling (not the 6ND approximation), landing at ~21.4% FP8 / 42.9% BF16 over a full 14.8T-token MoE run — the single most realistic public MoE-at-scale efficiency anchor, far below any MLPerf dense-405B sprint.
  • IEEE Spectrum — 'Intel and Nvidia Square Off in GPT-3 Time Trials' (MosaicML extrapolation) — Sources the rarely-cited Abhi Venigalla (MosaicML) estimate that an 11-minute MLPerf record extrapolates to ~2 days of real full-scale training — the cleanest public quantification of the sub-convergence sprint gap that makes MLPerf non-frontier-representative.
  • AMD ROCm Blog — 'Technical Dive into AMD MLPerf Training v5.1 Submission' — The only non-NVIDIA primary LLM-training signal at this generation: MI355X within 3–6% of B200/B300 FP8 on Llama-2-70B LoRA and 8B, with full software-stack deltas (ROCm 6.4→7.0, FAv3 via AITER). Useful as a credible second-source datapoint and a hedge against single-vendor benchmark framing.
  • DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report
  • DeepSeek-V3 paper (cited for bubble-free pipeline scheduling)
  • LLaMA 3-70B HuggingFace config.json
  • LLaMA 3.1-405B HuggingFace config.json

02Hardware, GPUs & accelerators

The silicon and accelerator references — measured (not marketing) tensor-core and HBM numbers, the competitive TPU/Trainium/AMD threats to a 100k-GB200 plan, the FP4/NVFP4 recipes, and the JAX-on-Blackwell plumbing a GPU-only team actually starts from. 50 resources.

  • SemiAnalysis — 'xAI's Colossus 2: First Gigawatt Datacenter' — Independent reconstruction of the only ~100k-GB200-class cluster actually being stood up (power-buildout math from cooling capacity, GB200 NVL72 counts, the RL-methodology angle) — concrete reference for the datacenter/power side of a 100k-GB200 deployment that NVIDIA marketing and the press omit.
  • Microbenchmarking NVIDIA's Blackwell Architecture (arXiv 2512.02189) — Independent measured (not marketing) FP4/FP8/BF16 tensor-core efficiency (~96% of peak) AND the inconvenient 51.8% realized HBM bandwidth, plus tcgen05.mma vs wgmma instruction latency. This is the single best source for grounding MFU/MBU assumptions instead of NVIDIA spec sheets.
  • Practical FP4 Training for Large-Scale MoE Models on Hopper GPUs (arXiv 2603.02731) — Rare 671B-MoE FP4 training study with concrete loss deltas (+0.61% vs BF16) and a forward-FP4/backward-FP8 recipe specifically for expert GEMMs, achieved on Hopper without native FP4 cores — a lower bound for what Blackwell gives for free.
  • SemiAnalysis: Google TPUv7 (Ironwood) deep dive — Only rigorous third-party teardown of Ironwood's OCS optical topology, transceiver count, and a hard TCO comparison (~30–44% below GB200/GB300) — the actual competitive threat to a 100k-GB200 plan, with numbers you can't get from Google's blog.
  • SemiAnalysis: Huawei CloudMatrix 384 vs GB200 NVL72 — Quantifies the brute-force tradeoff (4.1× power, 2.5× worse power/FLOP, 49.2 TB HBM) of the only non-US rack-scale MoE platform, and details the three-plane UB/RDMA/VPC fabric — useful as the worst-case power-vs-bandwidth reference point.
  • AWS Trn3 UltraServer launch + SemiAnalysis Trainium3 deep dive — Reveals the non-obvious detail that Trainium3 FP4 throughput equals FP8 (no 2× FP4 speedup, 512×128 vs 128×128 systolic arrays), which quietly disqualifies it as a true 4-bit MoE training challenger until Trainium4 — a tradeoff buried under the headline 4.4× perf claim.
  • Balancing Pipeline Parallelism with Vocabulary Parallelism (arXiv 2411.05288) — Addresses the often-ignored imbalance the embedding/output head injects into PP stage timing — material for a 10T model where the vocab projection is large and would otherwise unbalance a zero-bubble/DualPipe schedule.
  • DeepEP hybrid-ep branch (NVIDIA-authored) — Most readers know the DeepEP main branch but not this NVIDIA-contributed branch that re-architects the NVLink+RDMA path with TMA copies and warp-level pipelining to nearly eliminate SM occupancy, plus experimental NIXL/UCX transport. This is the most GB200-relevant DeepEP variant and is easy to miss.
  • DeepGEMM MegaMoE kernel internals (DeepWiki) — A code-level walkthrough of the V4 fused mega-kernel's SM100 implementation (three warp roles, TMEM, UE8M0 2D-TMA scales, SymmBuffer, MegaMoEScheduler) that goes far beyond the V4 paper's prose. Essential for anyone actually porting the fused dispatch-GEMM-combine kernel to Blackwell.
  • Tokamax RaggedDotGroupSizes / ragged_dot (used by MaxText) — MaxText routes its production grouped GEMM through google/tokamax's ragged_dot with a RaggedDotGroupSizes object carrying both true group sizes and an even-split fallback, plus a forked megablox gmm-v2 for quantized paths. This JAX/Mosaic ragged-GEMM stack is a credible non-CUDA alternative most CUDA-centric readers…
  • MoE Parallel Folding (arXiv 2504.14960) — The precursor paper to 2603.07685 that contains the actual per-config H100 MFU tables (EP/ETP/TP/PP/CP values, FSDP-vs-MCore comparisons, 1024-GPU strong scaling, FP8 631.7 TFLOPS) the big report omits. The +68% fine-grained-MoE folding gain is the load-bearing data point and only appears here.
  • Megatron-Core MoE README (NVIDIA/Megatron-LM main) — The canonical source-of-truth for the exact flag surface (--overlap-moe-expert-parallel-comm, Flex/DeepEP/HybridEP dispatcher selection, aux-loss-free per-expert bias, TEGroupedMLP, CUDA-graph scopes) you need to actually reproduce the paper's configs — more authoritative than any blog.
  • Megatron-Bridge low-precision discussion #3460 — A GitHub Discussion (not a doc) carrying the actual TFLOP/s and accuracy tables for FP8-CS vs MXFP8 vs NVFP4 on B200, including the load-bearing instability finding that fully-NVFP4 (F0L0) diverges and you must keep the final layers in BF16. This is operational tuning knowledge rarely surfaced in official docs.
  • Turbocharge LLM Training Across Long-Haul Data Center Networks with NeMo — The only NVIDIA primary source on multi-datacenter NeMo training: Hierarchical AllReduce, DP/PP-across-sites, 96% scaling efficiency at 21ms RTT, and an explicit 500k+ GPU projection. Directly relevant to whether a 100k-GB200 fleet can span domains/sites, and most readers won't have seen it.
  • NVIDIA blog: Accelerating Long-Context Model Training in JAX and XLA (CollectiveBackendAssigner / NVSHMEM on GB200 NVL72) — The only public source documenting NVSHMEM plumbed into XLA:GPU collectives via the CollectiveBackendAssigner pass, with hard +36.3% numbers on a real GB200 NVL72 + MaxText. Directly load-bearing for whether XLA's GPU collective scheduler can compete with NCCL/Megatron on long-context, and a template for the MoE all…
  • Tokamax (google PyPI kernel library, v0.0.12) — Google's new cross-platform (GPU Triton/Mosaic + TPU Pallas) kernel library with a single MoE ragged-dot GMM that runs on both backends and built-in serialized autotuning. It is the quiet bet to unify TPU/GPU kernels under one JAX library and a direct MaxText dependency — most readers track Pallas, not Tokamax.
  • MaxText benchmarks/xla_flags_library.py — The actual, curated XLA flag sets used in production benchmarks: Continuation Fusion, SparseCore collective offload, AG backward pipelining, SDC checker. Shows the stark maturity asymmetry between TPU (dozens of overlap flags) and GPU, and is the single best cheat-sheet for reproducing MaxText MFU.
  • NVIDIA/JAX-Toolbox (ghcr.io/nvidia/jax:maxtext containers, dated tags, GB200/GB300 CI) — The canonical NVIDIA-maintained path to run MaxText on Blackwell: pinned JAX/XLA/cuDNN/TE containers, NVFP4 recipes (te_nvfp4 / te_nvfp4_no_rht), and nightly CI on B200/B300/GB200/GB300. This is where a GPU-only team would actually start, and it reveals exactly which hardware NVIDIA itself tests.
  • Pathways on Cloud — IFRT proxy / single-controller porting docs — The actual mechanics (IFRT proxy client/server, gRPC, colocated Python, elastic training primitives) of the runtime that makes 9,216-chip TPU runs resilient — and, by omission, the clearest evidence that this resilience layer is TPU-centric, which is the crux of the JAX-on-GPU orchestration risk.
  • Efficient MoE Pre-training at Scale on 1K AMD GPUs with TorchTitan (PyTorch blog + Primus-Turbo) — Shows TorchTitan running DSV3-671B at 96% efficiency to 1,024 MI325X via AMD's Primus-Turbo/ROCm-DeepEP fork — demonstrates the stack is genuinely vendor-portable, a non-obvious de-risking option for a GB200 buyer.
  • nebius/ml-cookbook pytorch-dsv3-mxfp8 (reproducible DSV3 B200 configs) — The actual runnable TorchTitan configs behind the '+41% DSV3 on B200' blog (EP=32, MXFP8 grouped GEMM, DeepEP) — a turnkey starting point most readers won't know exists outside the blog footnote.
  • Rafi Witten — High Performance LLMs 2024 class (TPU performance engineering) — A full lecture course on TPU/MaxText performance engineering by a Google engineer, cited in the scaling book's further reading. Goes deeper than the book on practical XLA/MaxText MFU tuning and is largely unknown outside the TPU community — directly relevant since the reader's MaxText repo is on-disk.
  • HuggingFace Ultra-Scale Playbook — The GPU-native counterpart to the scaling book (PyTorch/nanotron lens), with interactive memory/comms calculators and empirically measured collective bandwidths on H100/B200 clusters. Complements the TPU-centric formulas with the GPU all-to-all/SHARP realities the reader will actually hit on GB200.
  • JAX Shardy / Shard-Map deep-dive docs (the new GSPMD successor) — The book references 'XLA's Shardy compiler' but the operational details of shard_map for manually overlapping MoE all-to-all dispatch/combine with expert matmuls live in these docs, not the book — the key API surface for implementing the rack-local EP the GB200 estimate depends on.
  • Stas Bekman — ML Engineering Open Book — Practitioner handbook with real measured InfiniBand/NVLink collective throughput, failure-mode and straggler diagnostics, and checkpointing throughput numbers for 10k+ GPU runs — fills the operational gap (network debugging, MTBF at 100k GPUs) that the analytical scaling book never addresses.
  • Tokamax splash-attention (referenced in MaxText) — MaxText now optionally swaps the stock JAX Pallas Splash kernel for 'Tokamax' splash kernels (use_tokamax_splash) — a little-publicized Google TPU kernel library that an expert planning a TPU fallback to the GB200 plan would not know exists.
  • NVIDIA 'Train Models Faster with JAX and MaxText Using NVFP4 on Blackwell' technical blog — The exact bridge between the NVFP4 paper and the reader's likely stack (MaxText/JAX on GB200), documenting the TransformerEngine-JAX NVFP4BlockScaling integration the reader will actually configure — non-obvious because most NVFP4 coverage is PyTorch/Megatron-centric.
  • DatologyAI BeyondWeb blog + paper (arXiv 2508.10975) — The clearest trillion-scale synthetic-data ablation: rephraser quality saturates by ~3B params, generator quality doesn't predict output quality, and STRATEGY DIVERSITY (not generator size) is what prevents synthetic plateau. Reframes synthetic-data cost away from giant generators toward prompt/strategy engineering.
  • Nonuniform Tensor Parallelism (NTP), arXiv 2504.06095 — Directly reframes fault tolerance for GB200 NVL72: quantifies that TP=64 + 0.1% failed GPUs ⇒ 94% fleet availability, and proposes running degraded (nonuniform) TP groups so a single GPU loss degrades rather than kills the whole 72-GPU scale-up domain. The single most NVL72-relevant fault-tolerance paper for this e…
  • Meta 'Silent Data Corruptions at Scale' (arXiv 2102.11245) + FleetScanner/Ripple follow-up — The empirical basis for SDC prevalence and the two-pronged FleetScanner (out-of-production) / Ripple (in-production) detection strategy — the failure mode that silently poisons checkpoints and is invisible to every watchdog. Pair with NVIDIA's OCP SDC-in-AI whitepaper for Blackwell-era hardware direction.
  • Su's 低精度Attention可能存在有偏的舍入误差 (biased BF16 rounding in attention), archives/11371 — Goes beyond the well-known BF16-breaks-RoPE result: identifies a systematically biased (not random) rounding error in low-precision attention accumulation from round-to-even breaking with 3+ operands. Directly load-bearing for FP8 attention kernels on GB200/Blackwell and almost unknown outside Chinese ML circles.
  • TorchTitan paper (arXiv 2410.06511) — the 'MFU is undefined under Float8' stance — Explicitly refuses to report MFU when low-precision is enabled because the metric becomes ill-defined — a crucial methodological caveat that invalidates naive comparison of FP4/FP8 MLPerf 'effective TFLOPS' against BF16 framework MFU tables, exactly the trap a GB200 planner could fall into.
  • A Domain-Specific Supercomputer for Training Deep Neural Networks (Jouppi et al. TPU paper)
  • TPU Deep Dive (Henry Ko)
  • How to Optimize a CUDA Matmul Kernel for cuBLAS-like Performance (Simon Boehm)
  • Writing TPU Kernels with Pallas (JAX docs)
  • JAX Pallas TPU matmul kernel docs
  • SemiAnalysis: NVIDIA Tensor Core Evolution from Volta to Blackwell
  • SemiAnalysis: NVIDIA Blackwell Perf / TCO Analysis
  • H100 DGX SuperPod Reference Architecture (NVIDIA)
  • GB200 DGX SuperPod Reference (Network Fabrics, NVIDIA)
  • NVLink Switch Hot Chips 2022 Presentation
  • NVIDIA H100 data center GPU (spec sheet)
  • Cloud TPU v6e specifications
  • Google Cloud TPU v5e system architecture docs
  • Google Cloud TPU v5p system architecture docs
  • Google Cloud TPU7x documentation
  • TPU v4 paper (Jouppi et al., arXiv 2208.10041) — referenced for networking/topology details
  • Systolic array animation (YouTube)
  • Google Cloud TPU pricing (FLOPs/$ figures, Feb 2025)

03Networking, interconnect & collectives

The fabric layer — photonic and circuit-switched collectives, the NCCL Device API, the MRC/SRv6 resilient-transport stack, and the operational tooling (flight recorder, in-process restart, communicator shrink) that decides whether a 100k-GPU run survives a link flap. 17 resources.

  • PCCL: Photonic circuit-switched collective communication for distributed ML (arXiv 2509.15450, Cornell, Sep 2025) — Inverts the entire collective-synthesis paradigm: reconfigure the photonic topology per collective ROUND (microsecond MZI switching) instead of synthesizing topology-aware algorithms. Directly targets the MoE all-to-all-vs-allreduce topology conflict with hard numbers (7.5× all-to-all). Almost nobody outside the opt…
  • GPU-Initiated Networking for NCCL (arXiv 2511.15076, NVIDIA, Nov 2025) — The primary-source spec for NCCL 2.28's Device API (LSA/Multimem/GIN) and the GDAKI vs Proxy backend split. This is how DeepEP-style device-initiated all-to-all becomes a first-class NCCL citizen with elasticity and fault tolerance, written by the NCCL team itself rather than a blog summary.
  • MRC + SRv6 Resilient AI Supercomputer Networking (arXiv 2605.04333) — The actual 18-page paper behind the OpenAI MRC press cycle, with the real numbers (770 Gb/s, 5.09/6.54 µs, 256-EV spraying beats 16 RoCEv2 QPs, 131,072-GPU Clos) that the news coverage omits. The OpenAI blog 403s for automated fetch; this is the citable substitute.
  • Oracle 'First Principles: Acceleron Multiplanar Networking' engineering blog — The clearest articulation of the disjoint-multi-plane alternative to rail-optimized fat-tree, splitting each GPU NIC across fully independent planes to kill fate-sharing. This is the substrate MRC sprays over at 800K-GPU scale and the most concrete non-NVIDIA scale-out architecture document.
  • OCP Optical Circuit Switching white paper (April 2026) — Vendor-neutral OCP treatment of OCS for AI datacenters (the Google-pioneered approach) with power/cost deltas and switching-time tradeoffs, useful for grounding the circuit-switched-vs-packet-sprayed contrast beyond Google marketing.
  • 'A (mostly) Unbiased Review of the Ultra Ethernet Specification v1.0' by Tom Herbert (Medium) — A protocol-engineer-level critique of UET 1.0 internals (packet trimming, reordering, CC) from someone who has implemented kernel networking, surfacing the design tensions that MRC's SRv6 + write-only-RDMA choices were made to sidestep. Far higher signal than the official UEC overview decks.
  • Megatron-Bridge Resiliency docs (NVRx in-process restart, straggler detection) — The concrete, version-pinned (PyTorch ≥2.5.1, NCCL ≥2.26.2, ft_launcher, torch_dist) requirements for fault-tolerant 100k-scale training that determine whether a run survives — and the explicit note that no single feature covers all failure modes, so layering is mandatory.
  • nvidia-resiliency-ext in-process restart usage guide — Documents the exact restart-loop state machine, the per-component restart latency budget (TCPStore barrier 0.5s at 16k ranks, total 1.5–3s), and the RankAssignment/group-of-8 exclusion semantics. This is the mechanism that determines goodput at 100k GPUs and is buried in a GitHub Pages site separate from docs.nvidia…
  • NCCL 2.27 ncclCommShrink resilient-training blog — Explains the low-level NCCL primitive (ncclCommShrink, default vs error mode) that makes in-process restart possible without full communicator reinit — the layer beneath NVRx that most framework users never read but which dictates the real recovery floor at scale.
  • SGLang issue #22583 — NVSHMEM/SymmetricMemory all-to-all vs NCCL/DeepEP — Candid engineering thread showing the pure-SymmetricMemory all-to-all does not yet beat NCCL/DeepEP for large payloads — a crucial reality check before betting EP comms on the SymmetricMemory path multi-node.
  • PyTorch NCCL Flight Recorder blog + analyzer tutorial — The actual hang-localization tooling, with the side-TCPStore trick that forces even the hung rank to dump its trace buffer and the heuristic analyzer that names the culprit collective/rank. The operational detail that turns a multi-hour hang investigation into minutes.
  • MaxText src/maxtext/common/checkpointing.py and utils/elastic_utils.py — Reference implementation tying Orbax EmergencyCheckpointManager (local + persistent tiers, per-process process_id paths, SingleReplicaArrayHandler restore-broadcast, replicator.errors sentinel handling) to Pathways elastic scale-up/down with Grain ElasticIterator state that survives process_count changes. The exact…
  • OCP Multipath Reliable Connection (MRC) protocol spec — May-2026 open-sourced transport (OpenAI + AMD/Broadcom/Intel/Microsoft/NVIDIA) that does tens-of-microsecond path failover via SRv6 source routing and packet trimming — converts transient link flaps from NCCL-timeout-triggered job restarts into training-transparent reroutes. Newest and least-known piece of the 100k…
  • Overlap Communication with Dependent Computation via Decomposition (Collective Matmul, Wang et al.)
  • JAX Pallas GPU Collective Matmul docs
  • NCCL (NVIDIA Collective Communications Library)
  • Wang et al. — Overlap Communication with Dependent Computation via Decomposition in Large Deep Learning Models (collective matmul, ASPLOS 2023, DOI 10.1145/3567955.3567959)
The two networking entries to read first

If you read only two from this section, make them the MRC + SRv6 paper (the citable substitute for the 403-ing OpenAI blog, with the real 770 Gb/s / 131,072-GPU-Clos numbers) and the NCCL Device API spec (how DeepEP-style device-initiated all-to-all becomes a first-class, fault-tolerant NCCL citizen). Together they define the transport floor for a 100k-GPU run.

04MoE systems, kernels & frameworks

The deepest cut of the chapter's own subject matter: NVFP4 pretraining recipes, MoE dispatch and grouped-GEMM kernels, the pipeline schedules, the optimizer-state quantization levers, and the framework files (MaxText, TorchTitan, Megatron-Core) where the real flag surface lives. 35 resources.

  • Pretraining Large Language Models with NVFP4 (arXiv 2509.25149) — The definitive NVFP4 pretraining validation at the exact 10T-token horizon, with the full recipe (Random Hadamard, 2D weight scaling, stochastic rounding, sensitive-layer carve-outs, BF16 decay tail). Most readers know NVFP4 only as an inference format.
  • DualPipe / DualPipeV repository — The actual schedule code plus the bubble/memory comparison table (1F1B vs ZB1P vs DualPipe vs DualPipeV). DualPipeV's PP/2-device V-shape is the under-discussed memory-saving variant most readers know only from the V3 report's prose.
  • PipeOffload: Improving Scalability of Pipeline Parallelism with Memory Optimization (arXiv 2503.01328) — Sea AI Lab's follow-on to Zero Bubble: activation offloading that makes deep PP memory-feasible at large microbatch counts — the missing piece for running PP=16–32 stages of a 10T model without blowing activation memory, and a real alternative to DualPipe's weight duplication.
  • On Quantizing the State of the Muon Optimizer (arXiv 2509.23106) — Quantifies Muon's single-momentum 4 B/param footprint and shows 8-bit Muon cutting optimizer memory ~74%, the precise lever for the 120–160 TB optimizer-state budget that dominates a 10T model's resident memory.
  • DeepEP issue #427 (low-latency imbalance correctness) — A concrete, load-bearing correctness bug: low-latency dispatch can silently corrupt under heavily imbalanced expert load — exactly the regime during early training before the bias balancer converges. The kind of operational landmine you only learn from issue trackers, not papers.
  • LLM-PRISM: Silent Data Corruption from Permanent GPU Faults (arXiv 2604.10390) — Couples RTL-level GPU fault simulation with a stochastic injector embedded in Megatron-LM to quantify how SDC silently corrupts gradients at scale — the failure mode that actually bites a 100k-GPU run and which the throughput papers ignore entirely.
  • Megatron-Core MoE feature page (flag-level reference) — The exhaustive flag-level reference (flex/DeepEP/HybridEP dispatchers, aux-loss-free expert bias, --moe-router-padding-for-fp8, fine-grained offload of expert_fc1/moe_act, MTP). Engineers gravitate to the NeMo recipe docs and miss that the real MoE knobs and the HybridEP-for-NVL72 guidance live in the Megatron-Core…
  • DrJAX: Scalable and Differentiable MapReduce Primitives in JAX (arXiv 2403.07128) — MaxText ships drjax as a dependency and supports a 'diloco' mesh axis — DrJAX is the underpinning for DiLoCo-style outer-loop / federated and cross-datacenter training in JAX. Highly relevant for a 100k-GPU planner thinking about multi-region or communication-reduced training, and almost nobody connects DrJAX to Max…
  • TorchTitan MOE_SHARDING.md + DTensor placement diagram (in-repo) — The single clearest primary spec of how routed/shared experts map to DTensor Shard/Partial placements across the four EP×SP configurations, with an Excalidraw source — far more precise than any blog and rarely cited.
  • TorchTitan minimal_async_ep (SymmetricMemory-native all-to-all-v) — A DeepEP-free, pure-PyTorch SymmetricMemory all-to-all-v for MoE dispatch with explicit symm_mem buffers and microbatch overlap — the clearest signal of where PyTorch-native EP comms is headed, and almost unknown outside the repo.
  • torchft (meta-pytorch) — checkpoint-free FT with Lighthouse quorum, DiLoCo/Streaming-DiLoCo — The PyTorch-native answer to 100k-GPU failure rates; the quorum-as-transaction design and async peer recovery are the architectural prerequisite for the reader's scale and compose directly with TorchTitan HSDP.
  • HipKittens: Fast and Furious AMD Kernels — Shows the ThunderKittens tile abstraction ported to AMD CDNA (MI300-class) — directly relevant to any frontier-lab hedge against NVIDIA lock-in, and almost nobody tracking FA4/CuTe-DSL has read the AMD-side tile-DSL story.
  • Long-Context Attention Benchmark (arXiv 2510.17896) — Independent kernel-to-distributed-context-parallel benchmark across FA3/cuDNN/sparse variants — a neutral apples-to-apples reference (not vendor numbers) for choosing an attention backend at 100k-GPU scale, including context-parallel scaling behavior.
  • Pretraining Large Language Models with NVFP4 (NVIDIA, arXiv 2509.25149) — The definitive 4-bit pretraining study at the exact 10T-token horizon the reader is targeting, with the four-technique convergence recipe, the NVFP4-vs-MXFP4 36%-more-tokens result, and the 8.2T-token BF16 switch-over — quantitatively the most load-bearing source for a 4-bit go/no-go decision.
  • MaxText quantizations.py TransformerEngine recipe table (te_mxfp8 / te_nvfp4 / te_nvfp4_no_rht) — Source-of-truth showing block_size=32 for MXFP8, the NVFP4 block_size=128 with an explicit TODO to drop to 16 'when unfused RHT is supported', and a no-RHT toggle — a concrete, current signal about which NVFP4 features are production-ready in JAX vs still gated, which no paper tells you.
  • A One-Stop Guide to Scaling Laws in LLM Quantization (Ruizhe Wang) — A rigorous independent synthesis reconciling Kumar et al. (pessimistic on 4-bit) with the QAT scaling law (2505.14302) and the empirical NVFP4 success — exactly the tension the reader must resolve before committing 10T tokens to 4-bit, and it surfaces the QAT-specific scaling law that the canonical precision paper p…
  • OCP Microscaling Formats (MX) v1.0 specification — The normative definition of block-size-32 / E8M0-scale MX formats that NVFP4 deliberately breaks from (16-block, E4M3 scale). Reading the spec makes the NVFP4 design choices legible as a critique of MX's power-of-two scale and 32-element block, and clarifies what is and isn't hardware-portable across vendors.
  • Megatron-Energon feature docs (.nv-meta, packing/grouping/joining, object-store streaming) — The PyTorch/Megatron-Core counterpart to Grain that most JAX-centric readers haven't evaluated: deterministic blending + savable loader state + native S3/GCS/Azure streaming over sequential WebDataset tar shards (the most object-store-throughput-friendly format), with built-in packing — a credible alternative if the…
  • NVIDIA — 'Reproducing MLPerf v5.0 Training Scores' (exact configs to back out MFU) — Publishes the literal run strings (GA112TP4PP8CP2, GBS=896, MAX_STEPS=450, eval-loss 5.6, FP8_PARAM_GATHER, TP_COMM_OVERLAP, NGC 25.04) needed to independently compute MFU from MLPerf time-to-train — and exposes how short and tuned-for-the-test the runs actually are.
  • FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention (Dao et al.)
  • Efficient Memory Management for LLM Serving with PagedAttention (vLLM)
  • Distributed Arrays and Automatic Parallelization (JAX docs)
  • JAX shard_map documentation (JEP 14273)
  • JAX explicit sharding notebook
  • jax.checkpoint (gradient checkpointing) docs
  • OpenXLA Shardy compiler
  • JetStream (Google inference engine)
  • JetStream PyTorch
  • Megatron-LM (NVIDIA)
  • Scaling Book GitHub Repository (jax-ml/scaling-book)
  • StableHLO (OpenXLA)
  • JAX TensorBoard profiling guide
  • JAX experimental layout (AUTO layouts) notebook
  • Colab: generate full JAX/TensorBoard Transformer trace (also Worked Problem 2)
  • Source notebook the section is taken from (JAX shard_map / parallelism docs)

05Optimizers, scaling laws & theory

The optimizer and scaling-law theory you transfer to the 10T regime — the adversarial checks on the Muon 2× claim, the distributed-Muon and quantized-Muon variants, the HP-transfer laws (η, batch size, weight decay), and Su Jianlin's Chinese-language corpus that unifies muP and Muon as one spectral geometry. 16 resources.

  • Essential AI — Practical Efficiency of Muon for Pretraining (arXiv:2505.02222) — The single best adversarial check on the 2× claim: shows the real iso-loss gain is ~10–15%, but more importantly proves Muon's advantage persists into the over-large-batch regime (to 16M tokens) — the actual reason to use it at 100k-GPU scale. Includes the telescoping muP-for-Muon algorithm.
  • Dion: Distributed Orthonormalized Updates (Microsoft, arXiv:2504.05295) — Directly answers the 'how to shard optimizer state at 10T' question: amortized power iteration + low-rank error feedback that never unshards the full matrix and cuts DP-sync from O(mn) to O((m+n)r), composing with FSDP+TP+DDP. The scalable alternative to Moonlight's all-gather-based Distributed Muon.
  • Power Lines: Scaling Laws for Weight Decay and Batch Size (Cerebras, arXiv:2505.13738) — Reframes weight decay via the AdamW EMA timescale τ=B/(η·λ·D) and gives clean fitted exponents (τ_opt ∝ TPP−0.52, B_opt ∝ D0.4, B_crit ∝ D0.5) plus the D=D_min(1+B/B_crit) penalty — exactly the transfer recipe for picking batch size and λ at 10T tokens.
  • NorMuon (arXiv:2510.05491) — Identifies a non-obvious Muon pathology — orthogonalized updates have highly non-uniform per-neuron norms letting some neurons dominate — and fixes it with cheap per-neuron second-moment normalization, +11.3% over Muon under FSDP2 with comparable memory. A plausible drop-in upgrade to the default Muon.
  • Jeremy Bernstein — Deriving Muon — Derives Muon as steepest descent under the spectral/modular norm and shows its hyperparameter-transfer properties overlap with muP's (dualizing the gradient in the correct norm IS muP LR scaling). Essential for understanding why naive muP+Muon double-application conflicts and how u-muP resolves it.
  • StepLaw / Predictable Scale Part I (arXiv 2503.04715) — A 3,700-model, ~1M-GPU-hour optimal-HP scaling law that explicitly validates <0.5% prediction error across sparsity levels, giving directly-usable η(N,D) and B(D) formulas plus evidence of a tolerant convex plateau — the empirical backbone for HP extrapolation at the 10T scale, far larger than the usual small-scale-…
  • Parameters vs FLOPs: Scaling Laws for Optimal Sparsity (Apple, arXiv 2501.12370) — Isolates sparsity as an independent optimization axis and argues optimal sparsity approaches ~1.0 for large models with total params rising and active params falling at fixed compute — the cleanest quantitative justification for pushing a 200B-active model toward extreme total-param counts.
  • Scaling Laws for Sparsely-Connected Foundation Models (Frantar et al., arXiv 2309.08520) — The under-cited 2023 origin of the 'optimal sparsity grows with training data' result, with an explicit (1−S)b_S functional form fitted on T5/C4 and ViT/JFT — predates and grounds all the 2025 MoE-sparsity work and is useful as the foil that shows the result is architecture-general, not MoE-specific.
  • Power Lines: Scaling Laws for Weight Decay and Batch Size (arXiv 2505.13738) — Extends HP scaling to the usually-ignored weight-decay axis and its coupling with batch size, closing a gap the LR/batch-size laws leave open — relevant because at 10T tokens the effective-learning-rate / WD interaction materially affects the late-training loss the WSD decay phase recovers.
  • Essential-Web Algorithm-1 heuristic rules + distillation appendix (in arXiv 2506.14111 PDF) — The appendix exposes the exact pre-classifier heuristic gates (frac_chars_dupe_Ngrams thresholds, top-2/3-gram fractions, word_count<50) and the two-pass annotation + NMI-based teacher-selection methodology — a concrete, copyable spec for building your own distilled annotator at ~90k GPU-hours rather than re-derivin…
  • Scaling Instruction-Tuned LLMs to Million-Token Contexts via Hierarchical Synthetic Data Generation (arXiv 2504.12637) — Concrete recipe for stepwise RoPE scaling to 1M tokens with explicit document-concatenation counts and shrinking per-length sample budgets (2000@180K → 200@1M). Fills the gap between 128K production recipes and true million-token training that the major model reports gloss over.
  • Tyler Romero — English translations of kexue.fm (Scientific Spaces) + NanoGPT Speedrun Worklog — Su's posts are paywalled-by-language for most Western readers and his SPA blocks scrapers; Romero maintains faithful English translations (e.g. the Muon Guide) AND a living worklog of the NanoGPT speedrun. The single best bridge between Su's analytic Chinese-language corpus and the speedrun engineering it informs.
  • Su's MuP之上 (Beyond MuP) series — #1 archives/11340, #4 archives/11729 — The clearest available exposition of why Muon and muP are the same spectral-norm geometry from opposite directions. Most readers know muP (Yang) and Muon (Jordan) separately but not this unification, which tells you how to co-design init + LR-transfer + optimizer for a trillion-scale run.
  • Yang, Simon & Bernstein — A Spectral Condition for Feature Learning (arXiv:2310.17813) — The 'higher-order muP' Su builds on: derives μP from the single condition that weight matrices and their updates have spectral norm ~√(fan_out/fan_in). More actionable for setting per-layer init/update scales than the original Tensor Programs derivation, and the theoretical justification for Muon's spectral trust re…
  • Su's 从梯度最大化看Attention的Scale操作 (archives/9812) and 从JL引理看熵不变性Attention (archives/9588) — Two independent re-derivations (gradient maximization; Johnson–Lindenstrauss) that recover the same log-n attention scale as entropy invariance. Triangulating three derivations gives unusual confidence that the YaRN/QK-norm/logit-cap family of fixes is solving one underlying problem, not three.
  • Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al.)

06Data, curation & dataloading

The data-side levers most teams under-invest in — streaming sparse-attention indexers, topic-vs-source mixture optimization, the 'beat the data wall' rewriting work, and the shuffle-locality and offline-pipeline patterns that decouple heavy global dedup from the training loader. 6 resources.

  • StreamIndex: Memory-Bounded Compressed Sparse Attention via Streaming Top-k (arXiv 2605.02568) — A 2026 follow-on to the DSA/CSA line that makes the top-k selection memory-bounded and streaming — directly addresses the indexer-memory blowup problem at 1M context that DSA's dense top_k over the full sequence does not solve.
  • WebOrganizer (weborganizer.allen.ai) + 'Topic Over Source' (arXiv 2502.16802) — An interactive explorer over 24 topic × 24 format web domains plus the counterintuitive finding that mixing over TOPIC-defined domains beats the universal default of source/provenance-defined domains. Directly actionable for RegMix/CLIMB-style mixture optimization at frontier scale, and the live dashboard lets you s…
  • Apple BETR — 'Language Models Improve When Pretraining Data Matches Target Tasks' (arXiv 2507.12466) — Beyond the 2.1×-over-DCLM headline, its scale-law finding that LARGER models need LESS aggressive filtering is a direct warning against tuning your 10T/200B-active mix on a 7B proxy's optimal filter threshold — a mistake that silently costs frontier compute.
  • REWIRE — 'Recycling the Web' (arXiv 2506.04689) — Most teams treat the filtered-out 90% as waste; REWIRE shows rewriting that discarded tail beats both raw web and pure synthetic and is the most direct 'beat the data wall' lever for a unique-token-constrained 10T budget — orthogonal to and stackable with rephrasing the kept data.
  • MosaicML Streaming shuffling algorithm reference (py1b/py1br/py1e/py1s/py2s) — The only place that documents the exact shuffle-quality-vs-download-locality tradeoffs and the shuffle_block_size=max(4e6/NCN,1<<18) formula. Most engineers know 'StreamingDataset shuffles' but not that the default py1e deliberately shuffles within blocks to preserve object-store read locality — the design lesson th…
  • Eventual/Daft case study: how Essential AI built Essential-Web v1.0 (24T tokens, 0 crashes) — A rare public account of a trillion-token offline data pipeline with hard numbers (24T tokens, 23.6B docs, 32k req/s/VM, 7 days, 0 crashes) — the offline-prep-then-stream pattern that decouples heavy global shuffle/dedup/tokenization from the deterministic training loader. Note the URL moved from daft.ai to eventual…

07Economics, clusters & industry analysis

The cost-and-cluster reference for sanity-checking your own MFU and TCO against runnable, NVIDIA-reference layouts. 1 resource.

  • NVIDIA/dgxc-benchmarking repository — NVIDIA's reproducible DGX Cloud benchmarking recipes (per-model READMEs with exact parallelism, batch, MFU and container tags) — the closest thing to ground-truth, runnable configs behind the marketing TFLOP/s numbers, useful for sanity-checking your own MFU against NVIDIA's reference layouts.

08Courses, handbooks & playbooks

The long-form teaching material — the first-principles GPU-performance and architecture courses worth working through before the rest of this list makes full sense. 4 resources.

  • Making Deep Learning Go Brrrr From First Principles (Horace He)
  • The Illustrated Transformer (Jay Alammar)
  • Stanford CS336: Language Modeling from Scratch (slides & videos)
  • Cornell Virtual Workshop: GPU Architecture

09Other notable resources

The cross-cutting remainder — transparency indices, the checkpointing and SDC-detection literature, the inference-arithmetic classics, the linear-attention lineage, and the operational GCS/storage limits that decide whether a 12,500-host job survives its own launch. 26 resources.

  • Stanford CRFM Foundation Model Transparency Index 2025 — Alibaba/company reports — Systematic, audited disclosure scoring across labs — the fastest way to see which frontier-run facts (compute, data, params) are actually documented vs. asserted, so you can calibrate confidence on every entry in this landscape.
  • SPARe: Stacked Parallelism with Adaptive Reordering for 100k+ GPU fault-tolerant pretraining (arXiv 2603.00357) — One of the few papers addressing the reader's exact 100k+-GPU resilience regime, complementing torchft with parallelism-aware reordering under failure — directly relevant and rarely surfaced.
  • Efficiently Scaling Transformer Inference (Pope et al., arXiv 2211.05102) — The mathematical parent of the scaling book's inference chapter, co-authored by the same Reiner Pope. Has the original PaLM 540B serving partitioning notation (the 2D weight-gathered / weight-stationary layouts) that the book compresses; essential primary source for the latency/throughput Pareto math the reader need…
  • Colfax Research FA4 deep-dive — The only write-up that actually exposes FA4's internals an expert needs: exact Horner exp2 coefficients (Sollya-fit), the conditional-rescale threshold idea, warpgroup layout (2 softmax + 1 correction), the 2.25 PFLOP/s peak math, and TMEM/2-CTA backward dQ-via-DSMEM details the abstract omits.
  • Silent Data Corruption in LLM Training (arXiv:2502.12340) — Controlled study using 15 known-bad production nodes showing SDC is invisible in the loss curve, that ABFT checksums catch only 1/15, and quantifying submodule corruption rates (up to 2.89e-2) and gradient NSR (5.1%). This reframes 'mysterious late spikes' as a hardware-detection problem and motivates shadow-replica…
  • Auxiliary-Loss-Free Load Balancing (arXiv:2408.15664) — the standalone paper behind V3's router — Readers know V3 uses bias-based balancing but often skip the dedicated paper that isolates why aux loss harms quality (interference gradients) and validates the bias-update rule independently up to 3B. It is the cleanest justification for choosing global aux-loss-free balancing over sequence-level aux loss at tril…
  • GCS request-rate & access-distribution guidelines (and HNS for AI/ML) — The exact, often-overlooked cold-start QPS numbers (5,000 reads/s, 1,000 writes/s, double-every-20-min, 8× for hierarchical-namespace buckets) that determine whether a 12,500-host job's launch and post-preemption restart triggers a 429/503 storm. The ramp-up-and-prefix-fan-out guidance here is the difference between…
  • Gemini: Fast Failure Recovery with In-Memory Checkpoints (SOSP '23) — The canonical primary source for the in-memory/peer/redundant checkpointing the dossier needs: the actual replica-placement-probability math and host-device coordination to overlap snapshot with training traffic. Often cited by name but rarely read in the original; the placement-optimality proof is the non-obvious p…
  • ByteCheckpoint (ByteDance, arXiv 2407.20143) + GitHub repo — Production-grade open implementation of parallelism-agnostic checkpointing with load-time resharding — the missing capability for resuming a 100k job on a different chip count after losing racks, without a separate conversion job. The 54.2× stall reduction is a concrete target to benchmark against.
  • WSM: Decay-Free LR Schedule via Checkpoint Merging (arXiv 2507.17634) — Argues the WSD decay phase is equivalent to a form of checkpoint merging and proposes eliminating the explicit decay by merging stable-phase checkpoints instead — a non-obvious reframing that interacts directly with model-souping and continued-pretraining branching strategies.
  • StepFun Step-3 + StepMesh (arXiv 2507.19427, github stepfun-ai/Step3) — The only report that frames attention design as an arithmetic-intensity matching problem (MFA=128 deliberately between MLA=512 and GQA=32) and ships a production Attention-FFN-Disaggregation serving stack with a zero-SM RDMA library. The hardware-tier sparsity thresholds (H800 needs ≥5.8%, H20 0.7%) are unavailable…
  • Su's 线性注意力简史：从模仿、创新到反哺 (archives/11033, 2025-06) — A current (mid-2025) synthesis of the linear-attention lineage into modern gated/delta variants reappearing in hybrid frontier stacks, written by someone who built several of the early variants. Useful if the 10T MoE considers a hybrid linear/full-attention layout for long-context economics.
  • GLU Variants Improve Transformer (Shazeer)
  • Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need (Multi-Query Attention)
  • GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models (Grouped-Query Attention)
  • Self-attention Does Not Need O(n^2) Memory (Rabe & Staats 2021)
  • Fast Inference from Transformers via Speculative Decoding (Leviathan et al.)
  • Accelerating Large Language Model Decoding with Speculative Sampling (Chen et al.)
  • Transformer Inference Arithmetic (kipply / Carol Chen)
  • Domain Specific Architectures for AI Inference (Fleetwood)
  • Character.ai Research: Optimizing Inference
  • Efficiency/memory hardware-affordances reference (arXiv:2007.00072)
  • bfloat16 floating-point format (Wikipedia)
  • Message Passing Interface (MPI) — Wikipedia
  • Perfetto Trace Viewer link (simple Transformer profile)
  • Colab: Worked Problem 1 profile (two-matmul / all-reduce)
Using this appendix as a reading queue

The fastest path through 201 entries: read §01 (Frontier models) and §04 (MoE systems) end-to-end since they ground the rest of the report; skim §02 (Hardware) and §03 (Networking) for the specific number you need; and treat §05–§09 as targeted lookups indexed by the why line. The framework-file entries (MaxText, Megatron-Core, TorchTitan) are the ones to open in an editor, not a browser tab.

Sources
